# Davina
## stories from Davina Casillas
## told by Matthew Temple

> The world tried so so so hard to break her and it didn't work!—Kristina  

WHEN I WAS FIVE MY DAD KIDNAPPED ME.

He came with his wife Marilyn. Her daughter Janine. They came to visit me in Pasadena and take me away for the day. They told my mom they were taking me clothes shopping at the mall and to dinner. But once we left my mom's house, we didn't go back—ever.

We rode a carousel at the mall and did get new clothes. But after dinner the car didn't go back home. It kept driving and driving, out of the cities and into the desert, and I was falling asleep in the back seat next to Janine.

In the morning I asked my dad when he was taking me home.

"You're going to stay with me for a little while."

It was scary because I didn't know where we were going. But I was with my dad so I knew everything was going to be ok. It wasn't till a few days later, after we had reached our destination, that I started wondering if I was ever going back to live with Mom.

In Arizona, Janine and I played hand games in the back seat of Dad's car.

Marilyn turned to look at us.

"At least tell her where we're going."

Dad looked at me in the rearview mirror.

" 'Vina?"

My face perked.

"We're goin' to the rez."

"What's the rez?"

"It's a place where the police won't be lookin' for you."

"Does Mom know?"

"Well. Your grandmother knows, ok?"

I didn't ask any more questions. I wanted to see the rez.

- - - -

And I did. It was my dad's wife's Hopi reservation in Hopi, Arizona. It was a little village on the side of a cliff, all the houses adobe, all set really close together—so close you could jump roof to roof and so close you couldn't walk between them on the ground.

As we drove in, kids looked up at us. They were playing in the dirt and doing dances. Dad drove real slow and waved at some of the grown-ups sitting in front of their houses. The houses were all different shapes, everything in white, everything bleached by sun. The steps that went up to them felt like they were cut right into the mountain.

That first day we arrived it was sunshine and rain, and the first thing I noticed about our particular house was the smell. The adobe mixed with desert rain, softening in my hands as I went up the steps, and I let myself explore. Neat little kitchen, stairs that went up to the roof. To this day I love the smell of wet clay or pottery. It puts me back when I was five, back on the rez for the first time. All the houses smelled that way when it rained.

We had no running water. There was a well with a pump. The houses were in a courtyard formation and everyone had their own outhouse. Each one had a picture on the door so you could know which one was yours. Ours had a crescent moon and three stars.

Beyond the outhouses were the cliffs—they were higher than the tallest buildings I had seen in California—that's where everybody throws their garbage. Down there were piles and piles and piles of garbage: bed frames, mattresses, old cars rusted out with holes in the seats, the glass smashed to pieces, nothing working. Me and Janine and these Hopi indian kids would play on the edge of the cliffs. It was fascinating to look down at everything people had left there.

We played in empty adobe houses. We played in desolate flat areas. Out from the village, down in the ground, there were special places for the kachina—a mound of dirt with a ladder sticking out. You'd go down the ladder into a cave. Me and Janine went down there one time and there was a sacred gathering—all sitting in a circle, all hot and sweaty, a big group of people. I knew how special it was and how lucky we were to be there. It was sacred.

When the kachina would come out you weren't supposed to look at them. They would run from their caves into the middle area between our houses. They were dancing, and *that* you're allowed to watch. You're not supposed to touch a kachina but if one of their feathers touches you by accident, it's good luck to you. They have feathers all over their bodies and a wooden mask and they wear moccasins. They're mostly naked, with a thing around their waist, bare chested, and each one carries a rattle.

One time when the kachinas came to the middle of the courtyard where it was dirt and not sand I remember thinking: *kachinas are men, not women*.

The men would dance in a circle. People were sitting on the roofs of their houses. The roofs are flat, and the sides of the houses had built-in stairs. (If you know where to step, you can climb all the way up.) The kachinas would be dancing and singing—bells, rattles, drums. There were baskets of fruit and flat bread, and pies in baskets, given to random people. They would throw fruit up to the roofs for people to catch. Some of the ladies would be on the ground in their most beautiful dresses, hair back in buns, wearing silver jewelry, turquoise, tapping a stone mortar and pestle to make music. Everyone dancing. Everyone watching. Everyone catching their goodies from below—even candies for the kids. I was so in awe of the kachina, looking at them from every angle, but you can only see their eyes behind holes in the masks. My family got a basket full of fruit and flatbread. I remember even today, there were bananas, oranges, apples.

Another day, there was a ceremony where kachinas were coming out of their caves wearing real buffalo heads with horns sticking out and the buffalo skins trailing down their backs. They were running a big race in the flat area between their caves and our houses. I heard the screams and saw the dust fly up behind them. I thought about Mom and I wondered what she was doing *right that minute*. I wondered if she was thinking about me. I wondered if she missed me. But mostly I looked at the buffalo heads, racing, racing. I thought it was awesome.

- - - -

I remember a pair of white people at the rez. A guy and a girl. We were eating dinner (which was lamb—we ate lamb almost exclusively on the rez). The guy had a camera around his neck and they banged on our door. Marilyn opened it.

"And what can I help you with?"

Dad half got up from his chair. He motioned, "Come in, come in. Don't just stand in the dark."

Marilyn closed the door behind them.

"Found yourself in the wrong place," my dad said.

"No, we know where we are," said the guy with the camera.

"It's just *leaving* we're having trouble with," said his wife.

"No," my dad said. "It's not a question. You found yourself in the wrong place."

"Do they have laws," the woman asked, "where they can kill you on an indian reservation and like it's outside the normal courts?"

"You sit down. Have some lamb."

So me and Janine gave up our chairs and sat on the first step leading up to the roof. We ate our lamb and watched as the adults got more friendly and they all laughed about "the wrong turn" they had supposedly made to get here.

"Your neighbor," the guy said. "I think he was going to kill me."

"We don't like to see white people around here."

"Why not?"

I knew my dad was thinking: *It's because all you came here to do is take pictures! Buy souvenirs. Catch a glimpse of some freaky indians.* But what he said was, "Eat with us and then we will help you find your way out."

That was the only time I remember seeing white people on the rez.

- - - -

Mostly I spent my time hanging out in a big group of kids. When I was with them I saw how brown everybody was compared to me. We'd walk to a little store right there in the village which had sugar and coffee and basic stuff. Us kids would go into that store and get packages of Kool-Aid. We would stick our fingers in it and eat it. It was super sour and we'd all be making sour faces with colored lips and colored fingers, each of us with a different flavor.

Other than that village store, and our adobe houses, and the kachina caves, it was nothing but desert. Village dogs would follow us kids around. We'd find old rusted-out cars and play in them, getting into nothing, really, just being little kids and wandering around the earth.

It seemed like we stayed there a long time. Every day we'd make our trip to the village store, standing around the ice cream freezer pressing our noses and lips against the glass door. We had pennies and nickels we begged from our parents, so Kool-Aid packets were all we could afford. But they were enough, and on the walk home we'd stick our tongues out to each other to show our colors.

The german shepherd that followed us wagged his tail and stuck his tongue out, too.

Everything seemed so safe even though our parents weren't following us around. There was no danger of strangers.

But there was the danger of looking over the side of a tall cliff. We would try to climb down the side of the cliff a little bit. Slide down craggy areas on your butt. That's fucking scary..god. I can't believe we did that.

- - - -

My name is Davina Casillas. Technically I don't have a middle name—it's blank on my birth certificate. But I guess if it was anything it would be Christina because one day when I was about seven my dad took me to the edge of a cliff and told me to choose a name and for whatever reason I picked that. Then he told me to scream it into the wind, as loud as I could. He said that would make it real. I think my dad had a flair for the dramatic, being all spiritual and indian and all that. And I guess it *was* dramatic, standing against the sky from the edge of a cliff where you can die at any moment, picking a middle name, screaming it at the wind.

Great father-daughter moment, right? Well whatever, Dad, thanks for letting me choose a name and then sending me off never to see me again haha!! Are we spirit twins or perfect strangers?—I can never tell.

It's funny, outside of that day at the cliff, I never have thought of myself as Christina—more as like an Isabel or Isabella.

About twenty years ago (I would have been 22 at the time), I went to visit my dad in Arizona with Ashley and Zach so he could meet his grandkids. He took me to my once step mom's house and left us there for two days!! Ever since then I've pretty much been like fuck you.

Dad decided he wanted to go to the University of Arizona, so we moved to Flagstaff. We were in student housing close to the U of A. I went to school in Flag and the winter was very cold—I remember that.

My stepsister Janine was kind of mean to me. So was Janine's mom. Janine was a bully. She locked me in a closet after making me and this kid I liked take off all our clothes, then she kept us in there for an hour. Then she told on us and I got in trouble with Janine's mom.

"Do not *fuck* with me."

I ran out of the bathroom straight to the table beside the couch and I pulled the phone off that table and sat there and held that phone in my lap, rehearsing my call to the police. I cried, knowing that just minutes before, my life had almost ended, and as my naked body dripped on the living room floor, I felt I knew something that most adults would never know: that all life is, is just being held underwater until you drown, unable to stop the inevitable, all while staring at your toes..and I never even painted them.

When Dad got home from class he put an afghan on me and asked me what was wrong.

I just shook my head and cried.

I slept by the phone in case Marilyn tried to kill me but I woke up in my own bed, alive, and after my near-drowning Marilyn kept her distance from me. Janine was the same way for a while. I think they believed me when I said I would call the police.

I had good times in Flag, though, hanging out with friends. Janine let me hang out with her—as in, she *tolerated* me sometimes. Janine was older by three or four or five years. When we weren't at school we'd wander around the campus area. There was a cafeteria where you had to use a card to get food. We lived by a foresty area that was all pine trees. Pine sap and pine trees really get to me now—they're such a trigger. Janine and I would wander around campus, going inside buildings, riding on elevators. He steps straight to Jamie. He points the gun in her face. He looks down at her shoes. They have a little bit of ball bearing grease on them. Ron pulls the trigger on the caulking gun, putting a dab of the grease on Jamie's nose.

"No, don't touch it."

Jamie's arms just naturally go for her face but Ron bats them down.

He squirts the ball bearing grease at her again, this time on her cheek.

"Don't touch it."

Jamie starts to cry.

"Oh, so you see how difficult you make life for me when you mess with my tools. This is for a job. Know what a job is? It's how I pay for your food, you ingrate bitch."

He lets out some of the grease in her hair.

"Without this—" he greases her up some more "—I will be unable to afford those lobster-flavored ramen noodles for you ungrateful pieces of shit. You *like* those lobster-flavored noodles, don't you? Well start imagining a life without them."

Jamie wipes a tear away with her hand.

"Now you sit there."

Ron pushes my sister down into the reclining chair.

"And don't touch your face..*or* your head..until *I* fucking say so. You two sit on the couch and watch her. Make sure she doesn't..*fuck*..with..*my..fucking..gun*."

He sets the caulking gun in Jamie's lap.

Then he smears the ball bearing grease all over her face, her chin, her eyes. He's using his hand on her like he was wiping himself off on a tree. He covers her lips in it, and she reflexively wipes some off of her nose so she can breathe.

All the while Jamie is looking at me out of the corner of her eyes—she is looking up at me where I am slightly more elevated on the couch, and she is looking at me for cues to emulate—if my hands slightly grip the edge of the couch, hers grip the edge of the chair. If I look casually to the ceiling, Jamie does the same. I just mention this because while I will never forget to mention that my little sister Jamie was indeed a horrible brat, flip that coin and it is also the case that she idolized me above everything she ever knew—which I hated—but that poor little thing wanted to be *just like me*..always.

Janine's face drifts, trying to connect with me, and some of the ball bearing grease rubs off her face onto Ron's hand.

Ron smacks her with his hand, then wipes his hand on the back of her neck.

"Fuck. You stupid bitch. You got ball grease on my hand."

- - - -

My mom walks in.

"What's going on?"

"Nothing, baby, what do you need?"

"I don't *need* anything I just thought you and I were going out to get more shit."

"Ready in a minute, baby."

"So you want to meet me in the car?"

"That's right, yeah, I'll meet you in the car."

"Jamie baby, what happened to your face?"

"Jamie, don't you say a word."

"So you'll meet me in the car?"

"Yes! I'll meet you in the goddamn car. Go! Go! Get your ass in the goddamn car, Lisa. I don't know how I can make it any clearer to you. Get in the fucking car, I'll be out in a minute, and we will go to the store and get everything you need. In a minute I'll be there. But right *now*, I'm in here. I'm taking care of some business and I'LL BE WITH YOU IN A MINUTE."

Lisa goes out the front door. She doesn't even have shoes on.

Ron points at me. His eyes are so strained, he looks like he's about to have an aneurysm.

"Watch her. Davina, listen to me."

"Yes, I'm listening."

"*To me.*"

"Yes, I am. I'm listening to you."

He takes a step closer.

"Don't get smart with me."

"I'm not smart, I'm listening—I'll do whatever you say."

"Ok, good. Your job, while I'm gone, is to keep everyone *right where they are*. That goes for your fucking retarded *brother*, too. So..pop quiz: what do you do if someone has to go to the bathroom?"

"Let them go to the bathroom?"

Ron takes one step closer.

"No, you weren't listening. When I say I want everyone to *stay where they are*, what I mean is *exactly what I fucking said*. Everyone stays where they are. So..pop quiz number two: what if someone has to go to the bathroom?"

"We stay right here."

"Good! So what happens if your sister's *eyes* burn out of her *fucking skull* because she has ball grease in them?"

"I call 911."

Ron steps right up on me, I mean close, like he wants me to suck his dick or something.

"That's the wrong answer, Davina. Now, I think you know what the right answer is. And I'd like you to tell me now."

"The right answer is that I let her eyes burn out of her fucking skull."

"You're a smart kid, Davina. I'll see you in an hour."

Ron goes out the front and I hear the car start and drive away.

"You're not going to leave me with this shit on my face, are you?"

I've already told you that my sister is the devil—pure evil. And sitting there on the couch, looking at her try to keep her composure while her eyes were probably burning like hell..I'll admit, a tiny part of me enjoyed that sight. But right is right and you don't fuck around with people's health.

"Go wash it off."

Jamie stood partway up.

"I'll handle it."

She put her hands together like she was praying—and I know for sure she never said a prayer in her life except maybe that God would make a new flavor of ice cream that backstabbing, fucking evil bitches like her would like. But this was a prayer to me and she said:

"Thank you, Davina."

"Don't thank me. Just wipe that shit off. And wash your hair."

Jamie went down the hallway.

I told my brothers to go play.

It could be days before either of them came out. They had their own bathroom, their own supply of food. Us kids would eat whatever we found in the cabinets. Sometimes that meant we drank green tea as our only sustenance for weeks. Sometimes it meant we shared cans of smoked sturgeon until it was all gone and I'd find Jamie going through the trash, retrieving the fish cans and tasting drops of oil that were left in them. She'd stop to see if I was going to get mad at her, licking her lips of sturgeon oil, kneeling beside overturned trash cans in the back yard, but there was no judgement in my look—we did what we had to. We were resourceful. I could usually get Mom to buy a big bag of rice, then I'd cook it and spice it and call it "spicy rice." We *lived* on spicy rice.

We never ate out. Ron and Lisa rarely bought groceries. Most of the money went to meth—that was house priorities.

I hear Ron and Lisa's door open. Ron leans around the corner into the living room.

Ron gives me a giddy thumbs up.

## 16
Sometimes there was absolutely no food at our house. Ron and Lisa had food of course but us kids literally didn't have anything to eat for days or weeks at a time. Our house was next to a Greek fast-food place, though, and we hung out and found food in the garbage cans. We hung out in the lobby of the restaurant. Sometimes if we stayed there long enough the owners would give us some extra food, like food that got cold 'cause it sat too long. I guess they felt bad for us—they never kicked us out.

I remember back then me and my siblings hiking all day at Lytle Creek. Do you know where that is? It's this kind of camping and fishing place outside of San Bernardino. Sort of between there and Rialto?

Well, anyway, the little kids got so sunburned they had huge blisters on the tops of their shoulders. It was me and the kids and my mom and Ron. We didn't have anywhere else to go. (Lytle Creek is cheap.) And it's one of my favorite places to go, ever, across my whole life. I loved going there when I lived in California. There are levels of campgrounds: cheap ones where you just put your tent—or one level up with hookups for campers and RVs. We had a cheap one on the side of a hill. It was incredibly hot and there weren't many trees. I woke up one morning and the first thing I saw was a scorpion.

Mom and Ron were being all smoochy—probably copulating as they were in the same sleeping bag—so me and the kids tried to climb up the hill we were camping on. That hill was covered in scrub—low-down dirty bushes, kind of deserty. I'm the adventurer of the family. I go off ahead and everyone else follows behind me. At one point, we got stuck in an area that was all thorns. We were trapped in this rough, spiny, scratchy, thorny place. I felt so bad because I got my brothers and sister into this huge scratchy mess. When we got out, everyone was covered in welts. We were all young enough, though, that everything was still an adventure, even new kinds of pain—I remember my brothers just touching the red spots and being like, "Wow, that hurts!" They didn't even care.

There was a medium-sized creek we played in the most. It had picnic tables on both sides..and so many families. Most of them were Mexican. They put their beer and watermelons in the river to keep them cold. It was a nice happy family party atmosphere. I could smell all the food that everyone else was cooking. The four of us must have looked pretty pathetic because people brought us plates of food. Even though I was hungry, I never thought of actively begging. Me and Shane and Jessie and Jamie were sitting on some rocks at the side of the creek, looking at all the activity of everyone else, eating and serving and cooking food—and we're starving. Then some families brought us food and we ate and ate and ate and it was so delicious.

I decided we would hike the bank of the river and see how far it goes. I love that kind of exploration—just taking off somewhere I've never really been before. To see new things with every step. To take in information through every one of my senses. We walked upstream. The sides of the creek were covered in blackberry bushes. A lot of the time we'd just be standing there picking and eating blackberries. When we got thirsty we drank water straight from the creek. When we got hot we'd play in the creek to cool off, splashing water on each other's backs and on our faces. We hiked that creek for hours and hours without seeing anyone. Eventually we found this clearing where there were no trees in a large circle around the creek. We all stood at its edge and looked at it. It felt like some magical place in the woods..I think you could call it sacred. All around us tall trees perfectly shaded the clearing. Me and the kids sat in a circle. I said, "This place is amazing," and the four of us held hands. You know sometimes how a dream is magical to you but when you tell it to someone else the *magic* just doesn't translate? This clearing was like that, and you're just going to have to take my word for it.

- - - -

Then a weird thing happened. This lady came out from the far end of the clearing and I was still inside my mind of magic and wonder and how it really was a miracle that had brought us to this clearing.

And this lady is wearing a bathrobe. She's got curlers in her hair. And she looks like she's really in a hurry to tell us something important but she comes right up to us kids and says:

"This is for RVs you got to go back to your campsite."

I looked around.

I didn't *see* any RVs.

I didn't see any *hookups*.

"We can sit here if we want to," I said.

"I've gotta call the super, then. My hands are tied. You tied my hands. See?"

She wrings her hands around in front of her face.

"So *go*," she says. "What's the matter with him, he retarded?"

I stand up.

"Say you take that back."

This lady laughs.

"Oh, what, what're you gonna do?"

"I'm gonna scalp you."

She gets in my face.

"Doesn't that require a *knife*?"

I cup my hands together in a certain way and blow.

"You hear that whistle?"

"Yeah."

"Well my stepdad heard it too. He's on his way up here, now, bringing me my knife."

"Kid, you ain't gonna scalp me. Your generation watches too much TV. You're living in a fantasy world."

"Well *you're* living in a fantasy world if you think you own this clearing. The *trees* own this clearing. The *grass* owns this clearing. The *spirits* own this clearing."

"Are you on drugs?"

"No, but my stepdad *is* and when he gets up here he's gonna eat your face off like a monkey. Did you know that a chimpanzee has more face muscles than a human?"

She looks down at me critically.

"That's what I mean about TV—your generation jumps from subject to subject every ten seconds. You can't concentrate. Your little brains are fried on Big Macs and speed."

"But I *haven't* changed the subject," I inform her.

The lady puts her hand over her eyes and peers at the low edge of the clearing. She takes a step back.

"Just make sure you're gone before the super arrives."

I stand up.

"You don't own this clearing!"

The lady is walking away, her back to me.

She says, "Fucking natives."

"*Wha'd* you say?!"

"You heard me, kid."

"Bitch! I'mma find your camper and burn it down with you in it!"

That's what I said. But we left. Oh, yes—me and Shane and Jessie and Jamie hiked all the way down that creek, leaving the sacred circle behind. I was just a kid, but I knew that people in the expensive RV-camping area were serious about keeping poor kids like us out of their sight. That's how rich people are—it's an inconvenience for them to be reminded that poor people even *exist*.

I hope some rich people go out and buy this book and read it from beginning to end. Then I hope they donate all their money to Greenpeace and go back to their mansions in Bel Air and kill themselves except this time without the Manson family—that's my hope and if you are rich and you read this I hope you will honor my wishes.

That's what I was thinking all the way back to the tent. And before you analytic types point out that there's no way I could have thought that because back then there was no *book*—I had a *time machine*, ok? And I went forward and read my own memoir then jumped back in time and thought about those rich people killing themselves because they read my book. You know? Try not to pick everything apart—try to enjoy yourselves.

When we got back, Ron and Lisa were picking each other's scabs, flicking bugs away that weren't even there.

I told them that we hiked up the creek and found this amazing circle of protection cut out between the trees and then this bitch with hair curlers, in a bathrobe, started talking shit to us.

"What did she say to you?"

"She said that *natives* like me think we own the place. Fucking bitch!"

"What did she do?"

"She left and I told that bitch to *keep walking!* I was like: '*Keep walking*, bitch!' "

Lisa says, "Davina! Stop talking so loud."

Jamie tells them we had to leave because that circle is for RV people only.

"Is that what she said?"

"Yes."

"Well fuck those RV people. Let 'em have their clearing."

"No one owns that clearing," I say.

Ron says, "Yeah, well, maybe in a Henry David *Thoreau* sort of sense—" he's waving his hands around to indicate how frivolous and perhaps homosexual Thoreau is "—maybe in *that* sense no one owns that clearing, but what I'm saying is—Davina—let it go. Let the bitch have her clearing, ok? What do you expect to get out of talking with that woman anyway?"

"I'd kill her."

"O-*k!*"

"Then I'd kill her super."

"Super? What's her *super*? Are you talking about super *powers* here? Lisa, we got to get this kid some serious psychotherapy."

"Her *supervisor*, her *superintendent*—she didn't specify."

"You're gonna kill that bitch's supervisor?"

"Yes."

"Davina, that's crazy."

"She claims to have special *rights* to that land—when she doesn't. She kicked us off that land! THIS IS *MY* LAND!!" I shout, imagining that pink curler bitch can still hear me from all the way up there in her rich camper area.

"D. Did you forget that me and your mom are carrying? So like we don't want any involvement with the police, ok?"

"She's bringing the police?" That was Lisa, totally fucked on crystal methamphetamine. "That means we have to get rid of our stuff!"

"No. *No!*" Ron says. "We *don't* have to get rid of our stuff!"

He gets really close to my mom, grabs her arms, and the bloody scabs on each of them make print marks on the other like a stamp.

"Just chill the fuck out. You're on crystal, you're in the middle of the woods, and you're freaking out a little."

"You don't have to treat me like a baby—I've done this a few times before, you know."

"I know. But at least I'm not making an *ass* of myself like every other fucking member of this so-called 'family.' Pretty soon we're all going to turn into *him*."

He points to my brother Jessie who *is* deaf but not retarded, as Ron assumed. People simply didn't understand disability back then—if you had Down syndrome, you never left the house. If you were in a wheelchair, you couldn't work—businesses didn't have wheelchair ramps.

"So what did you do, then, Davina? Hike your ass back down the 'Creek of Mystical Desire,' *out* of that clearing as soon as possible, like a fucking *pussy*? Honestly, Davina, do you have to fight with *everyone*? You couldn't smalltalk that bitch with the curlers? *Hello!?* It's called *making friends*—something I doubt *you've* ever done in your life. But no, *you* gotta deny what *everyone else* says—*you've* gotta be right. Well lemme give you a hint: *sometimes* being in a relationship means *lying to the other person*. Just *lie* to the bitch! Tell her her hair looks *great* and you were all *just about to leave* the Magical Cavern of Forest-ness and she and *all her rich friends* won't have to look at *your ugly face* no more."

"My face isn't ugly."

"Yes it is, Davina, and it's time you admitted that. You're *too old* to still think you're gonna be Miss America one day."

"I don't think that."

"Would you just fucking *shut* up, Davina? *Shut. Your. Mouth.* Nobody wants to hear your *shit* anymore, understand? No one cares."

I looked at my mom. She was lying on her back in the sleeping bag with Ron, eyes open, mouth open, and I could see tiny little flying bugs landing on her lips and she didn't do anything about them.

Ron saw what I was looking at and said:

"That's 'cause *you* didn't zip up the tent."

It was night by that time. All the kids were sunburned but my brothers had it the worst—they went shirtless the entire day. My sister was wearing a tank top. They all got these blisters on the tops of their shoulders. They were like little water beds, so puffed up and filled with fluid. That's the worst sunburn I've ever seen.

The whole time it felt like just us kids on a big adventure—nothing bad, nothing wrong—just a good fun time. We stayed there about a week.

Me and my mom went there too, later, when the kids were gone. Bikers hung out at that park and Mom had been friends with them for a long time. She and I were always welcome. There's this show called *Sons of Anarchy—*have you seen it? The clubhouse on that show reminds me of the Hells Angels house we went to. It was pretty dark and kind of grim inside that house. There were huge clouds of smoke everywhere. It was homey in a weird way, but there was also this *don't fuck around in here* kind of vibe.

Mom fucked the bikers and we had money for food and drugs.

That's just how everyone took care of each other.

## 17
I don't remember going to school in that house. I was always just there with the kids 24/7 with Mom and Ron gone. I didn't always know what the adults were doing, I just knew that it was my job to take care of the kids. We would run around the small property. There was a tin shed out back and we would play house and talk and play pretend. Me and my sister were always in charge and, because my one brother was kind of deaf, we would make up our own sign language and teach him what it meant. We spent a lot of time there just fucking doing nothing.

Me and my sister once rolled up a bunch of leaves and sat in a tree and lit the roll with a match and tried to smoke it in the shape of a cigarette because we wanted to smoke a cigarette. We were laughing and coughing—that's how fucking bored we were.

I remember always being in the dark—in our room, in the shed, watching TV in the living room—it was always dark. Mom and Ron started doing tree trimming of palm trees as work—another reason for them to never be home.

Ron had an old beat-up truck in front of the house filled with palm fronds. One night I was sleeping on the couch. I woke up. There was bright orange light in front of the house. I got up and looked out the window. The truck was on fire—well, the palm fronds were. I woke up my mom. Fire engines and cops came. And wouldn't you know, it was Jessie (you remember my stepdad who liked to cum on my ass). He was released from jail and the first thing he did was track our asses down. I don't know if he wanted to see my mom or he wanted to see me but he obviously wanted our attention—and he got it. After he lit those palm fronds on fire we got the fire department, multiple police cars, a helicopter shining lights everywhere hoping to find his ass. There was even a canine unit, and that's what finally got him. This German shepherd found Jessie right near the house hiding under a tree. The dog had him by his forearm, Jessie was screaming. It didn't make any sense but I was sure it was for revenge against me. When the cops put him on the ground and cuffed him, Jessie was looking straight up at me where I was standing on the porch. He was muttering something between his lips. I couldn't hear it but I assumed it was something like, "I'll get you, bitch, and this time I really will rape you. I don't care how old you are, I'll rape your fucking *eye sockets* if it's the last thing I do." That could have been what he said. I don't know—I have a vivid imagination.

Jessie went back to jail, longer this time. His sentence for setting those palm fronds on fire was a year and a half. His sentence for sexually abusing me for five years?—three months.

- - - -

A guy named Benny lived at that house sometimes. I think he was related to Ron or was his friend. I had a crush on him (naturally) because I always had a crush on fucking adult men. He didn't want to fuck me (Yay! Hats off to him!) but we did make out and heavy pet and flirt. He drew the line at fucking me and didn't cross it.

One time watching TV in the dark, Benny and I were holding hands. I noticed he was bringing something out of his back pocket and he was trying to sneak it out so of course I was curious what it was. I kept my eyes straight on the TV so he wouldn't know I could see him and then suddenly I felt this cool goop on my hand and it was lotion. He put his hand on mine and our fingers were interlocked and basically our hands were fucking. Every time he moved, more of the lotion would get on me and then I made my hand into kind of a hole and he was fucking that hole with three of his fingers. I didn't know if this was his introduction to sex—like his symbol that we were about to fuck—or what. But we didn't fuck that day. Benny packed up his lotion and went into the bathroom for a while. I mean obviously he had to get off somehow and he didn't want to fuck me because I was underage or his friend's stepdaughter. When he came out of the bathroom his dick was still semi-erect. It was a bulge in his jeans and I did want to fuck it. Benny looked at me, up and down, and then rushed out of the house.

The next day he said, "You almost got it." By which I assumed he meant that he almost lost his shit and fucked me.

We moved to a new house, this huge old farmhouse that was in a neighborhood now but used to be in an orchard. It had a barn out back. When we moved in, we pulled open the barn doors and there were two Mercedes in the garage.

"Those are fucking stolen," Ron said.

"Why do you think that?"

"Because, Lisa, people just don't leave Mercedes lying around in a *barn*."

Mom was rubbing them and touching them and practically orgasming over them.

"You like that car, huh?"

She was rubbing her breasts on the hood.

"I fucking love this car. Let's find the keys."

"No. What you're gonna do is call the police."

"Why?"

"Why?? Because we've seen 'em now. We're accessories after the fact if we don't say nothing."

Mom was peering through the window. I wanted to kick her in the ass or break that window around her fucking face.

"Lisa?"

No answer: she's checking out the interior.

"Lisa!"

"What?"

"Call the fucking *po*-lice, will ya?"

"I don't see why we can't enjoy them for a little while." She said that as she was walking toward the house to make the call.

- - - -

"Thank you, ma'am," this cop is telling her twenty minutes later, in person.

He shook her hand.

"You did the right thing," he says.

My mom rolls her eyes.

"How about we make a deal where I give *you* something..and in *return* you let us borrow one of your cars once a week. Any day—*you* pick the day."

"I don't understand what you mean, ma'am."

This tow truck is loading the first Mercedes onto its flatbed.

"Can we keep one of your cars?"

"A *patrol* car?"

"*No*, silly."

She slaps him in the shoulder like she's drunk, which she isn't.

"The Mercedes," she whispers into his ear.

"Ma'am. Please take a step back. Are you taking medication for a psychiatric illness, maybe bipolar or schizophrenia..something like that?"

"No. You think I have schizophrenia?"

My mom laughs.

"Are you drunk?"

"No, I'm not."

"Are you taking any other drugs that I should know about?"

"*That you should know about?*"

"Are you taking any street drugs, Mrs...?"

"Lisa. No, I don't take anything like that. It's just the stress of the move and you can see my kids..they never tell you that when you become a parent..that they're going to *be there twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week* and it's *week in* and *week out* and sometimes you just need a little *something* to calm you down."

"What are you referring to?"

"Like I might take a Xanax every few days."

"You have a prescription for that?"

"Of course I do. What are you suggesting?"

"Not suggesting anything, ma'am. Just doing my job. I need to go check with the tow truck driver. Have a good day and take care of those kids! You have a beautiful family!" he shouts.

Under her breath my mother says, "No I don't."

Then she turns around. I'm standing right there.

"What do you want? If it was up to *you, you'd* have us all in jail, wouldn't you?"

I turn my face away. My mother grabs me by the cheeks and turns me around.

"When a father touches his daughter, that's not rape," she says. "When I was a kid, rape meant fucking *rape*. Like: when you rape a baby you have a huge man's cock and he *fucks* a baby's *tiiiiiiiiny* little cunt *so bad* when she grow up she can't have any kids. Or those guys..those guys like the California Strangler who cut you in half..cut your body *in half*, Davina. And then while you're bleeding to death they fuck you. *That's* rape. And then do you know what they do to you? They cut your head off and put it in a plastic bag in the freezer so they can have something to remember you by. Then they put your legs—your legs and your chest—in the back of a pickup truck and they drive along the highway. And then when no one's looking, some guy in the back of the truck throws your torso and your legs out on the side of the road. *That's* rape. Did Jessie do anything like that to you? I'm asking you a *question*, child, and I'm advising you not to test me because you see that cop over there? I will strangle you by your pretty neck and go to jail right here, right now, because *that's* how little I care about your scheming ass. You *schemed* to take Jessie away from me and by Jesus you did it! I could hardly believe it! Me and Jessie doing lines at the house in Texas then here come the *po*-lice. Take my man away in handcuffs. I'm thinking: *why?* Then here you come, Davina—" she pushes my forehead back when she says my name "—you and Principal Barney Fife, tooling along in his shit mobile, and I knew, *I knew* you had made this all up just to get Jessie away from me. You been scheming against me since the day you was born. You know I never believed in any of that Pentecostal shit down there, like the devil and God and shit, but I do now. I believe it right in this moment. 'Cause I am looking at the devil and the devil has a name. Her name is Davina, Davina! Yes the devil has a name!"

She was dancing around in a circle singing my name.

I saw the cop looking at her as he drove off, and I backed away slowly from my mom. I didn't want to get hit, or have her pull my hair, or hear anything else she had to say. I wondered how much of her was in me. I wondered if I would be like her when I grew up. So I thought about her, and the things she did; and I thought about me, and the things I did. And I was suddenly happy. Because I was nothing like my mom.

- - - -

I backed away from her and I quietly went into the barn. I climbed up to the loft. From there I could see my mom still dancing around, cursing me. The tow truck and police car were gone and Ron was taking pictures of the remaining Mercedes with a camera that I happened to know was also stolen.

I sat down in the corner of the loft where there were piles and piles of old magazines. Some were in mildewed boxes. Some were spread out on the floor. There were *Saturday Evening Post*s and *Good Housekeeping*s and I loved it—I love old shit. I sat up there flipping through the pages. Some of them were warped and stuck together but I would gently pry them apart with my fingernails—I didn't want to miss a single image. I loved looking at the way things were back then. Crazy advertisements: a rocking chair for twenty-five cents! Or a sewing machine for a buck twenty-five. I could stare at the gentlemen for hours. Everyone wore a suit every day, it seemed. I saw myself in that world, a more proper world..maybe a world with more rules. Or maybe I just thought mothers back then wouldn't slap their children. I'd blame the drugs but back then there was coke in Coca-Cola! And stuff like heroin wasn't even illegal. So it wasn't that, but there was some property of older America that I was pretty sure I would like better than present America.

The tow truck returned and I stood up to look out over our driveway. As the driver was loading the second Mercedes my mom was literally on her knees bending forward like Muslims pray, and she was crying at the fact that someone was taking away this car that wasn't even ours. It had never *been* ours. And yet, she was *so* attached to it. But even then, I understood how she felt. Did you ever have a dream where you get something in the dream, something wonderful, and as you start to wake up you think: this thing is so wonderful, I have to take it with me into waking life? Then you wake up, and you have this loss experience. You feel loss over something that you never could have had. You know what that dream's about? Being poor.

- - - -

That house had a basement in it, just a dirt room really. The floor was made of dirt and mud. The walls crumbled if you touched them. It was as if someone had put the most fragile layer of sand-like stone over the foundation. I liked to pick at the walls, but I kept it to a minimum because I didn't want the entire house to fall in.

I would go down there to that basement and sing really loud to my favorite songs on the radio. Everything smelled of dirt, and after a while spent down there, I did too. I love that smell of damp dirt. The only things that weren't dirt down there were the ceiling and windows. I would twirl around and sing and hide from the kids. I would go there to get space from them because without it they drove me crazy.

I was always hungry there—there was never any food. That was the house where I learned to fast, first for a day, then a week. There are many lessons in this life and that is one of them: learning how to manage your consciousness while you are without food for an extended period of time. Most people, after a day of fasting, are severely unbalanced mentally. Almost no one can fast for three days voluntarily—they go crazy. But when you're fasting due to the fact that your mom and her boyfriend have a frighteningly expensive meth habit, you're not fasting by choice. There's no lifeline, no secret cache of food hidden away that you can get to if things get really bad. Things just get bad, and bad, and worse, and worse, and bad again. You have to manage your time—normally meals provide a punctuation to the day, they're part of what helps you make it through time, essentially, because you're always (at least subconsciously) anticipating the next meal. You're usually a little full from the last. It's one of our clocks. Eating makes the time manageable—it makes the day seem shorter. But when it's Sunday afternoon, and you know your mom won't have money till Friday night, the time is nothing but long.

You have to break it into pieces. You go outside to the back yard, and you sit in the grass and play with purple flowers. And you might eat a few of those, not for sustenance but to make your body feel the act of eating. It's quite useful. Or you can gnaw on a stick, or your finger, or your arm. Just chewing tricks the brain into thinking you've had something to eat, and after a while an hour has passed.

My sharp-as-a-tack sister Jamie (the devil, the evil one, the spirit of darkness, the demon)..anyway Jamie once told me (many years later) this thing about time, and even coming from a very smart person, it's the smartest thing I've ever heard her say. She said, "The bad thing about time is that if you're doing something you like, like watching a great movie or smoking a cigarette or having an unbelievable fuck..the bad thing is that time is always passing, so the great thing you're enjoying is always going away, second by second."

That great fuck is like sand in the hourglass. Always going away. I knew what she meant.

"But," the demon said, "the flip side is that if you're doing something you hate, or you're in a situation that sucks or is hard for you, that sand is flowing through the hourglass, and nothing can make it stop. Every hard situation you find yourself in is ending one second at a time, and eventually, inexorably, brilliantly, all that tortures you will end."

Ok, Jamie didn't say, "inexorably," or, "all that tortures you will end." Do you ever notice how smart people limit their vocabulary when talking with regular people?—so they don't stick out as much? Well Jamie did that shit religiously—even around me, which was silly, since every five-syllable word she knew, she learned the moment she heard it drop from *my* mouth. So what's above, that's my elegant way of saying what she originally said ghetto-style. And when she said it to me (this is in Pennsylvania, years later), I doubt even *she* understood how profound a thing it was she just said. People as smart as Jamie say so many profound things, they don't even understand what profound *is* anymore. They're not even listening to *themselves* as they spout this shit out, but *I was* listening to Jamie when she said that. And I heard her wisdom in my own way. I just wish I had heard it twenty years earlier so I could have used it when me and the kids had to fast for weeks because Mom and Ron were using all our money to buy crystal.

But when we fasted that way, as kids, I already had my own understanding of the concept contained in Jamie's future words. It was about spacing out the time, slicing it into little increments that added up to a day. You had to think of that slice only, or you'd go crazy. If you thought of a whole week hungry..thought of it all at once, you would never make it. You had to become zen master of every movement of your body, every decision to go outside or go inside, every movement of where to sit, to stand, to lie, to lean. And you had to become zen master of your thoughts—the thoughts were the most important thing. It's almost like preparing to die. You let go of serving the needs of the body. You stop shitting. You drink a lot of water. Around day fourteen you reach a strange peace—and this is what I think death must be like. Your body has started digesting your organs after three days. At fourteen you become, by force, a much more spiritual being.

One way I thought about it, when I was hungry in that house, is that most of the time people are not really living, they're just postponing death. You die after three days without water. Think of that..think of how close we all are to death. We're just three days of water away from it. We know we need water, and it feels good to drink it, but we're also subtly postponing death every time we take a sip.

I drank lots of water in that house and I peed all the time. The fact that Mom and Ron spent most of our money to buy drugs made me hate them. (Even more than I already hated them.) If they had given me twenty dollars a week I could have fed the whole house. When the adults are so fucking out of it on meth that they forget their own children have to *eat*, that makes me angry. Especially now, looking back on all this. Really at the time I just accepted it as my lot in life. I wasn't about to fight Ron or Lisa. But now that *I'm* the adult, and I review the past, I *am* mad. At Ron, Lisa, Jessie, and a bunch of other adults I'm about to describe in this book. Most of the adults I met in my childhood either wanted to fuck me or starve me. My dad kidnapped me when I was five—what kind of father does that? I had fun on the rez and all but what about my mom—didn't she miss her kid? Never mind—scratch that. She probably didn't give a fuck.

That bitch is crazy, I'm telling you. I think she thinks of me more as a dog than a human—I'm her pet. I mean sure, she wanted me back from the reservation but as soon as I got there it was like: here is your nice room with toys and murals and scary dog hallucinations out the window. And by the way this is Jessie. Introduce yourselves. Hi, I'm Davina. (We shake hands.) I'm Jessie. I'm going to be molesting you for the next five years.

*All* my mom's boyfriends were crazy. I mean like they would probably be helped by electroshock therapy. One time at the San Bernardino house my brother broke a glass bottle outside. That's just what kids *do*, right? It's not like he threw it at somebody's *head*. Ron gets home. He sees the glass. He gets super mad. At first he didn't even talk to my brother, he just gets all the glass bottles he can find and he brings those out of the house and he smashes every single bottle in the driveway so that all of us would have to walk over it if we wanted to go in or out. He was screaming the whole time:

"See what you did? You made me smash all these glass bottles over the driveway because this is the only way you'll understand. Come here Jessie."

"No!" Jessie is saying as Ron drags him into the driveway.

"Now walk."

Jessie was already barefoot—we always were.

Jessie looks up from the glass-filled gravel driveway..to me. I'll never forget that look if I live to be a hundred. I wanted to help. *I should have helped.* But I didn't. I just stood there like a fucking weakling.

Ron said, "Go ahead."

Jessie took one step forward.

"Keep going, you've got the skill—you can *do* this, you retarded piece of shit."

I held my sister's hand. I looked down at her. She was crying.

I knelt down beside her.

She said, "What is happening to Jessie?"

I turned her so she was looking straight at me.

"Shut that kid up!"

"Shhh," I said to Jamie.

I stood up and we watched together. I heard Jamie stifling her cries.

When Jessie walked all the way to where the glass ended, Ron said:

"Now do it again. Just like we're in the military. You're gonna walk this path until you learn *not* to break glass in *my, fucking, driveway*. Keep walking, you Mongoloid motherfucker. It's no wonder you're so ugly 'cause my best friend *Jessie*—and I hate to insult my best friend—but *your* father, *my* friend, for all the positive things about 'im, he is one ugly son of a bitch."

Ron kicks my brother.

"Faster! Run! Get to that side! Get to this side! I'm counting to three. If you don't get from here to there and back on the count of three I'm gonna crush your skull with a baseball bat. You know the bat in the garage? The red one made out of wood? I'm gonna go Mickey Mantle on your ass."

He knocks on my brother's head.

"You know what that means? It means I'm gonna set your skull nice and still, and you're gonna be standing there thinking about what retards think about—which is probably nothing—and I'm gonna take that bat and sever your head from that skinny little neck of yours and then your brains are going to be scattered *all over the yard* and some of them are going to make it *all the way to the barn* and stick to the *side* of that barn. It'll be like spaghetti noodles!"

Ron has his hands up in the air and is hopping around on one foot.

"Do you know how to tell if spaghetti is done? You throw a piece of it against the wall, and if it sticks, it's done. We're gonna do that with your brains. And when the spaghetti brains are done, your dumb fucking brother and your dumb fucking sisters are going to eat your retard brains out of the yard, with no utensils or anything—they're gonna scoop them up with their hands and slurp them up like gummy worms and so on and so forth until the entire side of the barn is *licked clean* by your brother and your oh-so-lovely sisters—"

"Stop," I say.

"*What?*"

"Stop fucking with him."

Ron comes over to me.

"Do *you* want to walk the driveway next?"

"His feet are bleeding. Look."

"Davina, I would think after what you went through with your last stepfather that you wouldn't want to mess with me. I ain't got no natural taste for kid pussy but I think I could develop one. Your momma told me you's still a virgin. You lied and said you weren't to get that motherfucker in jail..but you's still a virgin—ain't cha. Now I's had a lot of sweet pussy in my life but I ain't never had a—how old you is?"

"Thirteen."

"Yeah, that's about what your momma said. I think I know your type, Davina."

"You don't know me."

"I said I think I know your *type*."

"Just let him off the glass."

"Ok, son, get off the glass. The rest of you get inside or else I'll tell Lisa no more food for the rest of the week. Go on!"

Then it was just him and me—him wearing boots, me barefoot.

"You're worried about your brother's feet, aren't you?"

"*Yes!*"

"Don't worry, I'll let you go inside in a minute and help him. We're not *going* to no doctor so I suggest you take a needle and some dental floss—"

"*I will!*"

"Oh, you thought of that already. I'm sorry. I forgot you're the smart one."

"So what type do you think I am?"

"Well, first of all I think you still *is* a virgin."

"Why?"

"Because of the way you *walk*, Davina. You've still got that walk of someone who has *something to hide*. And the only thing a little *girl* has to hide is her *virgin little pussy*."

"That's not true."

"Oh no? Well what else she got to hide?"

"Her thoughts."

"Her *thoughts?! Girl*, I can hear your thoughts from a *mile* away."

"Then what am I thinking?"

"That's you'd like to kill me."

I looked away. Ron turned my head, by my chin, back to him.

"There's nothing wrong with that. If I was you I'd want to kill me, too."

"See there you're giving away more than you need to. *I* never *mentioned* suicide. *You* did. That tells me what *you're* thinking about. You need to play your cards closer to your chest, girl! No, what I think, is I think *you want to be raped*. Lisa told me you like older men. But I think that's only half the picture, with you."

I roll my eyes.

"What's the other half?" I ask.

Ron says, "That you want to be killed."

- - - -

Ron stares at me for a few moments before he continues.

"I think—and I'm no psycho-doc—but *I think* you's *ready* to die and the thing that would give you the greatest satisfaction *in all the world* is to be *fucked and then killed*."

He stares me down, but I'm not even looking at him. I'm imagining scenarios. I've imagined them before. A man on top of me, choke-fucking me till I pass out. Then he turns me over and fucks my tiny little ass until he cums. Somehow I'd be watching this from overhead—maybe because I'm already dead. And my dead soul gets off watching this man fuck my corpse. I can't think of anything that turns me on more, it's true. I have one where a man breaks into the house but I'm sitting there, naked, in the rocking chair, reading the dictionary. He has a knife. He starts with my face. He cuts x's into my nipples. "Davina."

"What?"

"Where did you go there?"

"Just..imagining..things."

"Well your brother's calling you. He says his foot's hurt. You better get up there. Davina!"

"*Ok!*"

"Don't forget Krazy Glue."

"Right, and a needle and some dental floss."

"That's right. And don't use the winterfresh, that's your mother's favorite."

"I'm pretty sure there's not even any dental floss in that house."

"Well do you want to stand here speculating or do you want to save your brother's life?"

"Also, the only kind of needle we have is not the kind you use to do stitches."

"Try to make do. You're a very argumentative child, Davina. Girls shouldn't be like that."

I gave him the finger as I walked toward the house. I couldn't get out of my mind my helpless little brother being made to walk over a driveway of broken glass by my mom's boyfriend. When I went inside and saw Jessie's feet, I cried and hugged him and went to get the Krazy Glue. I basically Krazy Glued the entire surface of both the soles of his feet. He smiled. I would have done stitches for the bigger cuts but as I predicted, the only kind of needle in our house was the kind you stick in your arm.

While all this was happening my mother was in her room with the door closed, probably shooting meth.

I looked out the window and saw Ron take off his boots. Then, with his arms raised for balance, he tiptoes across the driveway. He had a huge smile on his face and he was saying, "See, I can do it," over and over. I guess he thought he was still in Vietnam or Korea or Panama or whatever the fuck war he fought in.

I could imagine Ron in war, but not Jessie. Ron would be satisfied with killing innocent gooks and stepping on their dead faces but to get Jessie riled up in a war like Vietnam, you'd have to take him to some nice village with some pre-pubescent girls so he could get his rocks off. Which is, I guess, what some of the guys in Vietnam did.

Ron's war was against us kids, that was absolutely certain. I was always playing hospital with the younger ones after Ron had roughed them up. I wanted Mom's help, but I knew not to open that door when she was high..which was one-hundred percent of the time that she wasn't out clearing palm fronds with Ron.

Ron liked to fuck with us kids—that was his entertainment. His imagination for torture methods was endless.

Once he got mad at my sister and locked her in her room for three days. No one was allowed to talk to her. Me and my brothers would lie on the floor and look at her under the door. We were too afraid to speak, but whenever we got food we slid some of it under the door to share with her. We couldn't slide a whole sandwich through—the space under the door was too small. But we'd tear off our crusts and push those through, till our fingers could no longer reach but hers could, and she pulled the crusts through to her side. While the rest of us had food to eat, all Jamie had were crusts until Ron decided to let her out of her room.

Once upon a time our family left that San Bernardino house and went on a *Magical Mystery Tour*-style vacation. We went to the beach. My parents were intoxicated all the way to the ocean. Before we left the house, Mom went around closing the doors to all the bedrooms, and while she managed to get Ron and the kids on the correct side of the bedroom doors, she locked our cat, Ruby, inside one of the rooms. We were gone ten days, and for ten days Ruby was locked inside that bedroom with no food or water. When we got back and discovered she had been in there starving for ten days, we tried to comfort her—but that cat wouldn't come near us. Ten days without water should have killed her. But it didn't. What it did, though, was permanently change her personality—she was never again a lap cat. It was years before she trusted us to pet her. Also, she never, ever went in that bedroom again. I always wondered if, even on a subconscious level, Lisa accidentally locking Ruby in that bedroom for ten days without food or water was Ron's inspiration for locking Jamie up (in that same bedroom, by the way) for three days.

Did Jamie's personality change with her brief banishment? I guess I'll just say that after that experience she relied on people less—even people she knew loved her.

By the way, if this sort of thing is happening in your household, stop reading this book and call 911. This isn't funny, it isn't acceptable, it isn't even legal. You deserve parents who love you and take care of you—actually even that is the bare minimum. Parents should *adore* their children. And while you may not have been abused, were you, are you, *adored* by your parents? No? Well have a good cry with me now 'cause neither was I. At least they didn't lock your helpless little sister in a bedroom with no food or water for three days. And that gives *me* a helpless feeling, too—knowing poor little brilliant Jamie, that goat blood-drinking embodiment of Satanic evil, was so alone and so empty in that room and there was nothing I could do but lie on the wood floor, pass her my crusts, and whisper to her something I didn't even believe myself, which is that things would get better.

I went to school while we were at that house. It would have been an easy thing for them to fix, too—just hold back some of the drug money and get me that special shampoo. Once when I was on the playground with my few friends—and in this case a "playground" meant a slab of concrete with a fence around it—anyway I'd be standing there with the other two people in the school fucked up enough to be my friends, and this kid McDowell (that's all anybody ever called him)..this kid McDowell would come up to me. So McDowell goes back to his boys and he's pointing over at me and my two friends and they had their arms around me and I turned with my back facing the boys because all this talk about my lice really did make me cry.

My two friends tried to make me stop crying so McDowell didn't have anything else to make fun of, but all I thought about was my parents. If *you're* a parent, even if you're poor, get your kids the lice cream. I know it's expensive and they'll scream and cry when you pull out that tiny little lice comb and run it through their hair—but it really does make a difference.
