## 20
I met a guy whose dad owned the house we were living in. He came by some days to do landscaping. He really really *really* liked me (a lot). He threw pebbles up to my window to get me to come out.

I was scared.

He looked Mexican.

We made out, one time, kissing in the barn.

My sister told on me, and both me and the gardener got in trouble. Ron was yelling at the two of us. Mom was standing by his side, doing nothing.

"Do you know how old she is? Did you even ask?"

"No I am sorry sir I didn't mean to break your rule."

"My *rule*? It's called the Republic of the State of California and it's *their* rule, not mine. It says you can't get with this girl until she's eighteen. Does that girl look eighteen to you? Even if you shaved off your mustache I can tell you're at least forty."

"You want me to shave my mustache?"

Ron slaps him in the face.

"You are a dumb motherfucker. Learn to speak English. You tell your father that we're moving out of this 'house' as soon as we can. And *you*, Davina—Jesus goddaughter. This man is here to put in bushes and flowers and do some things for his dad. All anyone is asking of *your* horny ass is that you *leave the help alone*. Got that? So don't come downstairs looking for trouble when grown men show up at the house."

"But he threw rocks at my window."

"I don't care if he sent a goddamn *carrier pigeon* with a message in its beak that said, 'Come suck my dick.' *Leave the man alone.* You don't have sex with the gardener. I can see why you caused your mother so much trouble with Jessie."

I hated hearing that name. It changed my whole color.

"Oh yeah. Hearing that brings it all back, don't it? You, Jessie, late at night. And you would lift up your dress and run off."

"I never did that."

"Well according to Lisa, you did."

"You know you teased him, Davina. You played your part." This is coming from my mother.

"I did not tease him."

"You didn't give him a show of your little girl panties when you ran through the living room?"

"No."

"So, Sebastian or whatever your name is, why don't you go back home and tell your father—in Spanish—that we're not living here anymore if you throw any rocks at our daughter's window or whatever you do to get her to come downstairs with you. We don't want you making any Mexican-Cherokee babies 'cause that's just more people than we can take care of. Fucking..one-third white, one-third Mexican, one-third Cherokee. What the fuck are you gonna call the little thing? Davino-Gonzalez-Running Sky? *Hombre!* Am I getting through to you?"

"Yes."

"Tell your papa no more yard work."

"Ok."

"And you no come around no more—comprende?"

I grabbed his arm and was sobbing. I make a friend, they take him away. And his name was *Dylan*.

"Go. *Go!* Tell your father. And get the fuck off our property. This is ours as long as we pay the rent."

Dylan went on down the driveway.

"And son!" Ron yelled.

Dylan stopped walking.

"We got supplies. We're well dug in here. You wouldn't believe the arsenal of offensive and defensive weaponry I have stockpiled in that barn. We're almost indestructible. Watch out for that mine field! Your daddy may have all the money but *we're white*—at least some of us are."

Ron looked at me.

"*Most* of us are at least half-white. So keep that in mind, if you ever find yourself digging in our flower beds trying to plant a geranium or some shit. 'Cause the way I have this place rigged, you're liable to come face to face with an M18A1—know what that stands for? It stands for *I don't want no Mexicans fucking my daughter!*"

Dylan turns his head.

"You've made your point, sir. I won't be back."

"Good. Lisa, you backing me up on this one?"

"Yes I am. I agree one-hundred percent."

"Then why don't you teach your daughter how to fingerfuck that Cherokee pussy of hers so next time it ain't her and the mailman."

"I'm not Cherokee. I'm half *Papago*. You think everyone who's indian is a fucking Cherokee."

Ron rolls his eyes and makes a hand motion like: *whatever*.

Ron goes to his and Lisa's bedroom and Lisa follows.

Honestly, when she's high, it's like she's not even there.

- - - -

I cried all afternoon in the barn just lying on the stack of *Good Housekeeping*s and wondering how I would ever be able to meet someone while I was still a member of this family.

The next day Dylan came to my school and said something to my history teacher that made her let him come to my desk. Everyone around us could hear what he was saying but I didn't care. He told me that he loved me, not just in a sexual way, and even though it was Ron who told him he could never come back to the house, Dylan apologized to me that he wouldn't be coming to see me again. He said he had to honor my parent's rules.

I hugged him.

I told him I understood.

Then he left the classroom and that was the last time I ever saw him.

I cried all through history class and I guess they could tell that something was really wrong because for once not a single person in that classroom said a mean word to me. Not to get too mystical, but I think the reason no one said anything is that the way I was crying was the way *they* needed to cry to release *them*selves from all the fucked-up shit in *their* lives. That's that kind of deep personal place that when you touch it, it scares the shit out of people. Because no matter how old we get, part of us is still that screaming baby who just came out of our mom. And we didn't come out into a happy, cheery place like Disneyland—we came out into chaos so deep it doesn't have a name. We came out into a place, parts of which are truly evil. And at some age, if you're really really lucky, you realize that some of that evil is in *you—you're* the reason that someone else is bawling their eyes out right now, gut wrenching, empty.

You look into your soul and see everything you hated that your parents did, sculpted in nice little miniature packages for you to take out and unwrap and repeat with your kids, in your life, in your self.

That's what I was crying about in history class, and if I explained to any of those kinds most of them would have got bored and walked away. But a few of them would have listened, and those few who would have understood me then are the same ones who are reading this book now.

The other reason I cried is that I just really liked kissing Dylan.

I was never going to get to kiss him again.

And for that reason, for that moment, I was the saddest girl who lived.

- - - -

We weren't always hungry. Our parents expanded their business from just clearing palm fronds to doing tree trimming of all kinds. They did this solely to increase the amount of money they had to spend on drugs, but sometimes us kids would get a few dollars and there was a place called The Hamburger Stand which had twenty-five cent hamburgers. It was just the bun and the meat and ketchup and mustard. The four of us kids would walk down with a few dollars and come home with like twenty hamburgers and we'd eat them all.

In the evenings, Mom and Ron would sit in front of the fireplace. The whole house was basically empty, so they'd sit on a couple of kitchen chairs. There was no carpeting anywhere. Us kids would sit behind them to grab some of the heat from the fireplace. The adults would pass a bottle of whiskey between them—the kids couldn't drink. When it got late the adults went to their room to shoot meth. Three kids fought over two chairs but I just sat on the tile by the fire and watched the embers. There were faces in there, and little cities and roads that would change as the oxygen blew over the coals. I felt if I looked deep enough into the embers of any fire, I could see what the universe was made of, and who made it. Fire has always had special significance to me. Like, where other people just see energy being released, or light, or heat, I've always been drawn to every fire I've ever seen. Some doctor would say it was mental illness but I always have, and do to this day, feel fire and light have a special significance that deserves our attention. Well it deserves mine anyway.

Sometimes Ron or Lisa would put the whiskey bottle down on the hearth when they went to their room. Nights like that we split it, four ways, and I made sure it was fair.

Usually I woke up lying on the hearth, right next to a pile of ashes. And I was glad I slept with the fire. Don't you have spiritual relationships with trees, with mountains, with streams and the rocks within them? I've always kept rocks from where I've been, and I still have some old ones that are very special to me.

- - - -

There was a couple (a drug relationship of my parents). I liked them—they were some of the only adults who were nice to me. They had a van and one day they saw me walking home from school and offered me a ride.

"I should probably say no."

"Why should you probably say no?"

"Because my mom might get mad?"

"No..! Why would she get mad. She knows us, she trusts us. You've been around us before, Davina, have we ever hurt you?"

I shook my head.

"Then get in the van!"

The two of them were all smiles.

For a minute I thought they might kidnap me or lock me to a table in the back and do experiments on me. I had seen this one TV show about a couple in Arizona or Utah that kidnapped women and girls and strapped them to this table and stuck things up their vaginas. The pictures of these people's workshop was pretty ridiculous, too—they had all these surgical tools like you'd find in a hospital and some dentist tools too. When the girls first woke up the killer would play this tape for their victim and it was all like, "Be calm. You are here to die. We have selected you for your beauty and we will be exploring your body for the next several months. If you need to go to the bathroom, just do it. At least one of us will come by every day and if you poop or pee on the surgical board it's ok—we will clean you up. In fact, we take special interest in cleaning up all your waste products and their orifices." That's pretty much how the tape went. Then they'd keep these girls for like two or three months and basically sexually torture them, like electrocute their nipples and their clit and shit. And it turns out this wasn't being done by a bunch of guys—it was a guy and a girl. They were like boyfriend and girlfriend.

Anyway so I'm looking up at my parents' drug friends' fun van and they're like begging and waving their arms and saying:

"Get in the car Davina!"

I smile at the woman.

She says, "We've got peppermint!"

I take about three seconds after she said that to evaluate the likelihood of getting killed by my parents' drug friends. I make my choice: I get in the van with them.

I look around the back area: no chains, no surgical tools.

"Davina baby have a shot of this."

The woman hands me a bottle of peppermint schnapps.

"I can't."

"Why not?"

"My mom will smell it on my breath."

"Just tell her you ate a peppermint."

The woman smiles.

So I put the bottle to my mouth and she holds it there so that I'm chugging this bottle of peppermint schnapps. I swear to god I almost threw up in these people's van.

"Just drive me straight home."

"That's exactly where we're going."

"Good, 'cause I saw this thing on TV and it makes me nervous about being in vans."

"I think I saw that same show, Davina, and it scared the crap out of me. Was it the one about alien vivisections where they take a cow and try to mix its DNA with a human's?"

"No, I think this was a different one," I say, gulping their schnapps.

"What was yours about?"

"What was what?"

"The show you saw. That scared you about being in vans."

"It's kind of a lengthy explanation and I wouldn't want to bore you."

"I understand, honey, there's a lot of scary stuff on TV. Don't worry about me and Martin, we're not aliens or anything."

"Ok. I'm not worried."

"See, feel my skin."

The woman holds her arm out to me.

I look at her, confused.

"Pinch it. See, they can make themselves *look* like humans but it's just a hologram so if you actually *touch* an alien, their skin will be real smooth, with no hair. See those hairs on my arm? Try to grab one—that'll *prove* I'm not an alien."

Right so I grab this woman's arm hair and by her way of measuring it she doesn't seem to be an alien.

I drink like half that bottle of schnapps by the time we get to my house.

"Thanks for the ride!"

"Just tell your mom you ate a peppermint candy!" this woman yells.

I knew my mom was not going to believe that, so I decided to tell the truth: that her friends Martin and Martin's wife gave me a ride home and we drank *a little* peppermint schnapps on the way. I was working through all the outcomes I could imagine, figuring out how bad things were about to get for me given that I had been drinking peppermint schnapps with Mom and Ron's drug friends, when I opened the front door and there was an epic fight going on between my parents.

- - - -

They were coming down off speed. Both were in the kitchen but I could only see Ron. He was reaching out his arms to my mom.

"Lisa. Just think for a minute. Just *think* for a minute, ok?"

And then my mom said, "YOU DON'T KNOW WHAT THIS FEELS LIKE!!"

"Yes I do, yes I do. I been there, baby—trust me, I been there. You just gotta get though a couple more hours and you'll be fine. You want me to see if I can find some Vicodin?"

"We don't *know* anybody with Vicodin."

"But do you want me to at least *try*?"

"Tell me you're hiding. Tell me you have some hidden."

"I don't. I don't this time. That was the last of it."

"What can we sell?"

Ron looks at me.

My mom says, "What are you looking at?"

"Davina."

Mom comes around the corner. She's holding the largest knife we own. It's serrated, the rest of the blade narrow and straight.

"Hey Mom."

"Hey yourself."

"I'm just gonna go upstairs and do my homework."

"*No*, you're gonna stay right here 'cause I want to you see something."

Then my mom takes the serrated knife and drags it across her left arm. I mean she was really digging in. Blood is *gushing* out of her arm. Not drips—giant *gulps* of blood hitting our hardwood floor. You could see her heartbeat in the blood coming out of her arm.

Pump.

Pump.

I thought the second one seemed smaller.

Then she moves the knife to the other hand to try to slash her right arm but she doesn't get this one as deep—I guess because the arm now holding the knife has been weakened by the gash. But she still manages to cut herself on the right side, a few small serrations wildly dripping blood.

"What do you think of that?" she laughs.

And then she laughs and laughs some more.

Ron comes to her side with a dish towel. I don't know what he thinks he's going to do with that.

"Nice knowing ya, 'Vina. You got the crazy genes from me, so don't worry, it's not your father."

Ron is trying to tie the dish towel around my mother's arm.

"'Vina, you know what to do, right? Take care of the kids. And take care of *you*." (It came out like a Hallmark card.) "I'M SORRY *I* NEVER DID!"

Then she laughed again—insane.

I was about to say it's ok and let's get you to a hospital but she ran past Ron, into the kitchen, and down into the basement.

Ron picked up the bloody towel from the living room floor. He went into the basement and closed the door.

- - - -

I'll tell you what I thought then. I thought nothing of my mom. I certainly didn't think shit about Ron. I thought: *why is my mom dying in my sacred basement space?* That was *my* space where I went to get away from things when things got stressful and now my speed-freak mother has gone into that space to die. I wasn't sad. I wasn't scared. I was miffed because my one place I could go in the whole house to get some peace was now going to be the place my mother died, and that was going to ruin my spot.

I didn't feel bad for her because she wanted to kill herself. Meth does that. I mean, among all drugs, meth has been shown to make long-term users want to kill themselves. It's the same thing as that football head-injury thing—you damage your brain enough times in the right spot and you just naturally want to kill yourself. Meth and football should be outlawed.

Mom stays downstairs, screaming at Ron about how she's going to *die* in this basement and *not* to call the ambulance because *they're* in with the *cops* and if the cops come then her babies won't have no momma no more. It's the most times in my life I've ever heard Lisa talk about us kids. I guess, in the very specific circumstance of thinking she was about to die, she did care about us.

But she didn't die. Ron ran up and down the stairs enough times with towels and knives and scissors. I don't think he really knew what he was doing, but he managed to save my mom's life. He brought her upstairs that evening. I was sitting on the floor in the living room. Ron had his arm over my mom's shoulder and she was leaning on him and looking at the ground. They went upstairs and my mother never looked at me the entire time.

When they were gone I went into the kitchen. The basement door was open. The steps were stained with blood, every one of them, all the way down. Blood had *pooled* in the dirt floor. There were footsteps everywhere, half-blood, half-dirt.

I closed the door.

I didn't want anything to do with that basement anymore, not because it's where my mom tried to kill herself, not because it was sad or painful for me that she did that there.

It was because she ruined the space.

She took a space that was happy and that no one but me ever went into, and she basically fucked it up with her selfishness. I wouldn't care if she killed herself, even today, as long as she did it far away from me.

## 21
The day after Lisa tried to kill herself, she and Ron left the house and there was *nothing* to eat. Me and the kids were starving. We scoured the house for anything we could find that was edible. On the top shelf in one of the kitchen cabinets we found a bowl of cut-up peaches—but they were fermenting. You could smell the fermentation from a few feet away. But we ate them all, every last one. We were like dogs, silent while we ate, only the sounds of lips and tongues and teeth devouring the meal.

Mom and Ron came home with a surprise that day. Boxes and boxes and bags and bags of food. The weirdest things: containers of soups, ravioli, candy bars, sodas. I guess she was trying to make up for trying to kill herself yesterday but *oh my god* it was so awesome. We helped them carry everything into the laundry room.

I asked my mom where all this came from.

"From a food truck."

"Did you pay for it?"

"No, we stole it, hun!"

She laughed hilariously but I knew she was telling the truth.

We were ecstatic carrying everything inside. When we got everything inside the laundry room, Ron shooed us out and then he locked the door—a padlock with a key.

Everyone's face went sour.

"I know, I know," Ron said. "But we gotta make it last."

He looked at Lisa.

"How long we gotta make this last for?"

Lisa counted with the fingers on her bandaged hand—the one she had slashed the deepest.

"Don't hurt yourself Lisa."

"I'm not hurting myself."

Lisa continued to count. Eventually she said:

"Two months."

All the kids screamed with delight!

Then Lisa said, "PLUS OR MINUS A MONTH."

Ron said, "Huh?"

"It means it could be *one* month or it could be *three*," I said.

"Can we have some now?"

"NO! You cannot have any of this now. We're gonna be on a strict schedule for food and the schedule starts..when does it start?"

"It starts when I say it starts now leave us alone."

- - - -

We kids went to our rooms and Ron and Lisa went to theirs. Soon the smell of crystal meth filled the hallways on the second floor. I got my siblings and we quietly went outside to the window that looked in on the laundry room. We turned over a trash can and I climbed up there and opened that window and soon I was throwing Hostess CupCakes and Ding Dongs and Ho Hos down to the kids like I was The Wizard of Oz or something. When I jumped down I had a special Jolly Rancher stash in one of my pockets—you deserve a few perks as the oldest. Also in my back pocket I had a stick of beef jerky which the kids instantly noticed while I was closing the window.

Jamie (that epic horror-bitch of all time) said, "No fair! You got beef jerky!"

I slapped her in the face with it.

"Who gave you that Twinkie you're holding?"

"You did."

"Exactly. Shut the fuck up and eat your Twinkie."

"But I don't like Twinkies."

"Then give it to Shane!"

Jamie obviously hated that suggestion because she unwrapped and ate her Twinkie right away.

We enjoyed the moment, filling our bellies with junk food, but it had to be quick.

"Give me your wrappers! Jamie! Pick that up! Do you know what will happen if Ron or Lisa finds a Hostess wrapper out here?"

"They would kill us?"

"Basically, yes. Hand it to me. That's right. Everybody hand your stuff to me."

"I'm not finished."

"Well finish fast. Take it out of the wrapper and give me the wrapper! *Hurry!*"

When I collected all the wrappers I took them to our trash can and lifted out several layers of trash. I shoved the wrappers down deep into the trashcan and prayed that no one would find them. Then I put the rest of the trash back on top.

That food they got us didn't last for two months. It didn't last for three. It didn't even last us *one*. A week later we were all starving again. For some reason Ron and Lisa couldn't steal from the food truck—they had a new driver or something.

I knew you didn't get hungry on meth, so I found myself spending more time in the hallway outside my parents' door. I would even sometimes suck the air from the space under their door, hoping to get high so I wouldn't be hungry.

- - - -

I remember one day I was talking to myself, wandering around the back yard. If you had seen me, you might have thought I was crazy—and, quite possibly, I am. I would broadcast the weather to myself, announce what songs were coming next on the radio, and I liked to make up stories about my future—where I would live, who I would fall in love with, and how I would die. I've had to grow *myself* a lot, you know? I mean I've had to *teach* myself—*everything* really. I never had a mom who would give me "the talk" or tell me what her first kiss was like. It's like I'm a new strain in the family, culturally, because nobody ever taught me *shit*. I've had to make stuff up for myself and that involves a lot of talking to yourself.

So I'm in the back yard, and I climbed a small tree. I had never given much thought to this tree (as you'll see).

I'm sitting in the branches, singing to myself and looking around and looking down and I realized there was fruit hanging off the branches of this tree. I picked one and smelled it. It was kind of squishy and brown, but it was a fig. I ate it. And—you're going to think I'm exaggerating, but—it was the most delicious thing that I ever ate in my entire life.

I started picking them and shoving them in my mouth. The kids came out and I told them to climb up with me. So the two little boys and even evil Jamie climbed up as high as they could. It was almost dusk—the sun had just set over the top of our house. It was that type of orange light you only get at sunsets because of all the pollution and the angle that the sun shines through it. When I looked at that orange I closed my eyes and I felt the heat on my face. I just remember there were no parents and, for a night, we had plenty to eat. We were all sitting in the fig tree stuffing our faces with its fruit. When I looked down on my siblings they had that same orange light on them and I felt love for them. I could see them as little children and I guess the point is that I could see *myself* as a little kid, as the little kid I'd always be—innocent, creative, playful, and yes, loving.

We sat in the branches till dark.

We ate and ate and ate.

I'm pretty sure we cleaned that tree out.

## 22
We used to go and play at a nearby school playground. All of us kids playing and swinging, having a good time. One time this group of black guys came and played basketball. They were ghetto from the way they talked, to the way they dressed, to the way they acted toward us. Between games, one guy came over to where we were. He took off his Kangol hat and put it under a swing that no one was using at the time. Then he went back to playing basketball. My brother swung on that swing and kicked the sand and some got on the hat. The guy came over and stood in front of Shane.

"You wanna mess with me?"

Then he pushed Shane backwards off the swing.

I got off my swing and got right in the guy's face.

"That's my *brother* you're messing with!"

"Oh, that's your *brother*?"

The guy pushes me.

"Well tell your brother not to kick my hat."

"He never kicked your hat!"

The guy picks up his hat from the ground and dusts it off.

"Then why it got dirt and wrinkles in it when just a second ago, when I dropped it off here, it was perfect."

"Don't leave your hat on the ground next time."

I bend to help my brother up.

The guy comes around the back of the swing and pushes my brother to the ground. He kicks him: once in the face, once in the side.

"Stop!" I yelled.

"Oh so it's *you* I should be dealin' with?"

"That's right."

"And what you gonna do about my hat?"

"*Fuck* your hat."

Then I feel it. This fucktard punches me twice in the face. My hands go up.

"If you love your sister then don't be kickin' a brother's hat!"

He kicks my brother again.

"*Stay down, motherfucker!*"

I push the guy, push him way back.

"Don't be pushin' *me*, bitch!"

"Just take your hat and go the fuck away. Go play with your boys."

"You ain't know *nuthin' 'bout* me and my boys so why don't you shut up about that."

"Just go *play* fag basketball. Half-court bullshit. I've seen you—you're making like one in five."

He grabs me by my hair and swings me around. I'm on all fours trying to get up but he's kicking me every time. I can't breathe.

I'm looking up into the sky, and this black guy is standing in front of the sun. He brushes off his hat meticulously. Then he bends down and spits in my face.

"You were referring to my hat earlier."

I wipe his saliva off my face with my wrist.

"What about it?" I say.

"It ain't no *hat*," he says.

He puts it on his head, slightly adjusting the angle, then he gets it right.

"Ain't you gonna ask me what it is?"

"No."

I start to get up.

"*Stay down*, bitch."

He holds out his hand, not to help me up, but to show me how low he wants me to say.

"If I walk away from here and my boys see you standin', it's on me. See? So when I walk away from here you gots to be lyin' on the ground *looking* like I kicked your ass. And this," he says, removing it from his head, "ain't no fuckin' *hat*. It's a Kangol. You wanna touch it?"

- - - -

I shake my head.

"You should, bitch. It's fuzzy, see? Fuzzy like my 'nads up against yo' ass!"

He laughs.

"What about your retarded brother? He wanna touch it?"

"*He's not retarded!*"

"Ok! Alright, bitch, no need to get excited. I just thought he might be on the spectrum or something."

"The autism spectrum?"

"Yeah. Or some other spectrum."

"I'm surprised you know what a spectrum is."

"Just 'cause a brother *black* don't mean he don't know what a *spectrum* is. You make too many assumptions, white girl. I prob'ly knows a lot of stuff *you* don't, you know? I don't like being boxed in by my race."

I stood up.

"I'm not boxing you in by your race."

"Then why I don't know what a spectrum is?"

"'Cause you act like a *fool*."

The guy put two fingers to his mouth, thought a while. Then he waved for his boys to come over. They dropped their basketball and Jamie ran fast, right out of the situation. She ran off the grass, over the sidewalk, and down the street towards home.

"GO AHEAD AND RUN, LITTLE WHITE GIRL, WE'LL GET YOU TOO. So whose ass we gonna kick today, you or your little retard brothers."

"Don't listen to him, Jessie. You are not retarded. Don't let anybody ever tell you that."

The black guy cocks his head.

He smiles.

"Then I guess it's gonna be you."

- - - -

His boys were there and the alpha said, "Don't mess with them little boys. They on the spectrum. But we gonna beat this girl till she die, 'cause she let her little retard brother fuck up my Kangol. See? You see that? This hat ain't rightly proper no more on account of the brim of the hat be havin' wrinkles 'cause this mixed-breed little *bitch* let her little brother stomp all over it."

I looked at them. There were four of them. One of me.

"Go ahead and kill me."

I told my brothers to stay out of it.

"You want us to *kill* you?"

"Why not."

"You a *depressive* bitch, ain't you?"

"I don't know."

"You don't *know* if you're depressed?"

"Look, if you fags get off on going four against one *on a girl* then go ahead. Beat me up. I'm a virgin too."

"You a virgin?"

"Yep. So fucking rape me. Fucking kill me. I don't care what you do as long as you don't hurt my brothers. He didn't mean to kick your hat."

"Well that don't change the fact that he kicked it."

"You put it *under* a swing. What did you think would happen?"

"Also, if you recall what I told you two seconds ago, it's not a hat—it's a *Kangol*, bitch. Don't make me tell you again. And I don't appreciate being called a fag by no virgin. Is you white, anyway? Or..what *is* you?"

I look away in the direction Jamie went. How long can I keep this going before she gets Lisa?

- - - -

"Is you really a virgin, bitch?"

"Are you?"

He laughs.

"I bet you've fucked a lot of virgins, haven't you?"

The guy stands there, confused. His friends are looking back and forth.

"Have you ever had a virgin suck your dick?"

"Sure, lots of times."

"You're a fuckin' liar."

"Bitch, are you crazy? It's four against *one*, and you continue to in*sult*?"

"You wanna get your dick sucked now? As a deal?"

"What kinda deal is that?"

"I suck your dick and you leave me and my brothers alone."

"What about that girl that run off? She a virgin, too?"

"Sicko! Do you know old she is?"

"I kind of don't care."

"Unzip your pants and I'll suck you off real quick."

His friends laugh.

"I don't know, I don't know. You seem like a *crazy* bitch to me. How do I know that when I unzip my pants and you see that beautiful black cock, that you won't try to bite it off?"

"Do you know how hard it would be to *bite* a cock off?"

"Maybe those wimpy white cocks *you* be fuckin' with."

Mom and Ron always left early, so in the mornings I would help the kids get ready for school. God, it was so depressing. We never had any food there. Mostly we ate at school. At home, we always ate mayonnaise sandwiches—which is exactly what it sounds like—and you would not believe how delicious that is when you're starving. I still like them to this day as a comfort food.

When Lisa and Ron were gone, we would pile up on the bed and watch cartoons and prime-time television and talk to each other. The two boys would be roughhousing..making up pretend scenarios..fighting like they saw on TV. I wanted to be alone but I never got any alone time, always being responsible for the kids. They were up my butt.

Whenever I could, I would leave the Foothill Motel and walk around the street: there were liquor stores, motels, fast-food places. My school was nearby in the other direction. I'd go into motels and ask what their rate was, pretending that I could afford to stay there, just enjoying the fantasy that I could..that I could have a room to myself again someday.

I admit I had a fantasy of sucking off the guy at the desk of a fancy hotel so I could stay there for just one night. But there weren't any fancy hotels near where we lived.

When I went on these walks, I would go around the backs of fast-food places and look through their dumpsters for extra food. I was so hungry, I imagined going inside McDonald's and telling them I was hungry and *begging* for food. I never did that, though—I guess pride prevented me.

One time I was kneeling with my back against a dumpster, just looking at the sky. This dude comes by and says:

"Just enjoying some time alone."

"Well you can't sit here."

I kick the dumpster.

"That's destruction of property," the guy says.

"*Nothing was destroyed! My* little foot didn't even make a *dent* in your dumpster, *asshole*—so *how the fuck* is *that* destruction of property?"

This guy goes inside the restaurant. Fucking asshole. But I get out of there because I'm sure his faggot ass *will* call the police. And I'm not about to get arrested for standing in the parking lot of a fucking McDonald's.

We stayed at the Foothill Motel long enough that we got to know other families who were staying there. Our kids would run around with their kids. I'd trail along, keeping an eye on them, making sure everybody was safe. There was nobody my age but I made friends with some of the adults. I was careful not to go *in* their rooms lest it turn into some kind of sex-slave scenario, but they would give me food sometimes. Sometimes I'd eat Taco Bell flavor packets, I was so hungry. I took the bus to school. I had friends: stoners, kids who listened to metal. I fit in with them. I started ditching at that time—a lot. In the mornings when I got to school sometimes it would be really super foggy—you couldn't see four feet in front of you, couldn't see across the courtyard, you couldn't see anything. Me and my friends would go to school and see that it was foggy, then run off and run around smoking pot. No one bothered us. We'd skip a few periods and then sneak back into the school through the field.

Spending time with them was the only part of my day that wasn't total boredom. We all liked each other: boys with boys, girls with girls, girls with boys. Nothing was serious. I mean nobody fucked each other—not that I know of. But sometimes we'd hold hands on the way back to school, while we were still in the fog. You don't think of hard core metal kids, stoners, holding hands like little kids. But I think we were just comforting each other. It was validation which we needed because we were like no one else in the school.

I did smoke pot at that time, but I wasn't doing a lot of drinking *except one time* Lisa and Ron were gone all night and I was with the kids. I tried to figure out if I was in trouble but Ron wasn't upset—he was content to laugh his head off at me. And Mom was asleep in the bed. That was normal. Mom would always be sleeping off her wired after they'd been up all night partying.

## 24
While we were living in the Foothill Motel, I met this guy—my parent's dealer—big Mexican guy named Louie. He was always nice to the kids and me. He would buy us food sometimes, take us to McDonald's. He took me with him to the store to get milk and bread and mayonnaise and bologna and cheese. When we brought home the food, the kids jumped around like hyenas, they were so excited.

"So what's your favorite thing to eat?" he asked me.

I wasn't sure if he meant something other than food but I just said, "A mayonnaise sandwich."

The kids were devouring the bologna and cheese and Louie moved closer to me on the bed.

"Show me how you make it," he said.

I blushed.

"You want me to show you how I make a mayonnaise sandwich?"

"Uh-huh."

I laughed.

"Ok."

So I made a mayonnaise sandwich and Louie watched every motion. He even supported my arm when I was spreading the mayo!

"That's pretty good," he said. "But you have to put the mayo on both sides."

It sounds stupid to me now, but that was a window to a wider world to me at the time. Mayonnaise (and every other food) was always so scarce for us that before that moment I never thought of putting it on both sides of the bread. I would never use twice as much of something as I had to. As I was spreading mayo on the second piece of bread, I imagined my mom getting mad at me when she found out.

When my parents were "working" (or just mysteriously not there), Louie drove us kids to the park. My brothers and sister would jump out of the truck but Louie held me back. He'd hold onto my arm so I couldn't escape and I'd watch my siblings running off to play.

Louie would tell me to shut the door.

I did what he said. I stayed looking out the window.

"Why do you not look at me, Davina?"

I turned.

"I'm looking at you."

"Are you scared of me? Because I like you very much and it would hurt my feelings very badly if you were to become upset with me in any way."

"I'm not upset with you."

"Do you know how beautiful you are to me?"

"You must not have looked very close because everyone at school says I'm quite ugly."

"But that is simply what schoolchildren say, to disarm each other. They want to disarm you of your power, Davina, because they can see that you are growing into a powerful woman—much more powerful than they will ever be."

"Really?"

"Yes."

"Are you sure I'm not ugly?"

"You are not ugly to me."

"To you."

"Davina, you are not ugly at all. You have a thin self-confidence, is all. You should find ways to build your self-confidence."

"How do I do that?"

"You succeed at things. You pick things to do, you do them..and once you do them you build positive self-esteem. Understand?"

"Nnn..I mean..yes..in the abstract."

He reaches over and runs his hands through my hair.

"You have beautiful hair, my little flower. Is that ok with you, if I call you my little flower?"

I look out at my brothers but especially Jamie. The three of them are running around a tree. I wonder if men have talked to her like this.

"Sometimes I have dreams," Louie says. "They are about a beautiful little flower. And her name is Davina. She is the most beautiful in a huge field of beautiful—but this one is the most beautiful. Does it bother you I have dreams about you?"

I turn to him.

"No."

"I am glad to hear that, sweet Davina. You visit my thoughts often. I dream of you naked, the slight curvatures of your little body: your hips, your breasts. When I dream of you, your hair is covering your breasts, so that I cannot see your nipples, and I want to pull the hair back and see everything you have to offer. Shall I go on?"

I nod.

"I see us being together. I know you are a virgin, Davina, and I want to be with you for your first time. There are men who would be rough with you—I would never be rough. Do you mind that your mother has told me that you are a virgin?"

"Yes, I mind. I totally fucking mind. Don't you guys have other stuff to talk about than me?"

"What he was *allowed* to do. Did she tell you that? *She let* him do those things. She knew all along what he was doing! Did she mention that part? And now she's pimping me out to you? What are you giving her? Rent money? Free meth? How much? I want a cut, I want fifty percent."

And what do you know, one day soon after that Louie came over when it was just me and Mom. The other kids weren't there. Mom came up with some reason she had to leave right then. She stepped outside and closed the door. Then I heard her lock it.

So picture this: me and Louie are sitting on the bed together and he asks me:

Louie pulls out a glass pipe and puts a little rock in it. Speed? Crack? I never knew what it was. He showed me how to hold the pipe and when to breathe. I smoked, then I passed the pipe to him. It came in waves, it made me feel lightweight, it killed some of my anxiety, and it made me want to lie down..so I did, I lied back on the bed. I noticed the bed had been made (which Ron and Lisa never did) but this thing that would normally annoy me was just an interesting fact.

I was fourteen when I became homeless. It was the beginning of my ninth grade year. We were living at the Foothill Motel. After school one day, I called my mom to ask her if I could go to a friend's house. She said no.

"I want you here, Davina. I want to talk to you about something."

That freaked me out. I thought I was in trouble. I didn't want to hear what she had to say—the thought of that was too scary. So I made a snap decision that I wasn't going to go back.

I said, "Bye, Mom," and I hung up the phone.

I was with a group of friends. I went up to my good friend Carrie, and she asked me if my mom was letting me come over.

I shook my head.

"Oh! Fudge!"

"It's ok," I said. "I'm not going home today."

"Does that mean you're coming over?"

"Yes," I said with bright eyes.

Carrie hugged me.

Everyone was glad the group wasn't breaking up and they were saying, "Yay!" and "Excellent!" and "Nice one, Davina!" They were all happy and celebrating it, but I don't think they knew what I meant: *I wasn't going home..ever.* I thought I was pretty cool for making that decision. I was done with going home—I was done with that shit. But I didn't even think about where I was going to stay.

The first night I stayed at Carrie's house. Carrie lived in this awesome apartment complex. It had gated entry, a pool, and the apartments looked like they were only a year old. We went to her apartment. She had a bratty younger brother. He was ten years old. Carrie and I hung out, ate some fancy microwave dinners, went swimming in the pool. Carrie smoked cigarettes but I didn't.

"You wanna try one?" she asked me, as we were sitting on the edge of the pool.

"Sure."

So Carrie handed me one and I twirled it around my fingers.

"Put it in your mouth?"

So I did and she lit it and on my first inhale I stood up, feeling lightheaded and dizzy. I threw up on the side of the pool.

Carrie laughed her ass off.

I was too mortified to speak.

We moved down aways to get away from my throw up—which wasn't really throw up, just some stringy liquid because I hadn't eaten lately.

Carrie was still laughing when she offered me another one.

"Come on, cow girl."

I coughed.

"I don't think so."

"Alright, but if you change your mind they're right here."

She patted the pack of cigarettes beside her.

"Are you staying here tonight?"

"Can I?"

"Don't worry, my mom works late. You can stay in my room and she'll never know."

"So is it ok?"

Carrie shrugged.

"It's ok with me."

I remember that night so clearly because it was The First Night I Didn't Go Home. Carrie and I swam some more. Went inside. We completely avoided her brother because Carrie had a TV in her bedroom. It felt like nothing mattered. I was amused at the thought of Lisa wondering where I was. She was gone so much without telling me where *she* went or when *she'd* be back that I felt we were even. For the first time ever, I felt good. And I felt free.

Carrie and I stayed up late and her mom still wasn't home. Carrie was lying in the bed and I was sitting on the floor.

"I feel like going to sleep," she said.

I didn't respond.

"Are you sleeping on the floor?" she asked.

I shrugged.

"Come sleep up here with me."

For a flash of a second I thought of Janine, of rubbing butts together in Flagstaff, and all the adults laughing at us. But I didn't want to sleep on the floor, so I decided to give Carrie a chance. I turned off her TV and the overhead light and went to her bed. At first I just lied on top of the covers, but Carrie said:

"Get underneath, silly!"

A quick flash of Jessie, his hot cum squirting on my tiny back.

But Carrie put the covers on me and she rolled over on her side, facing the wall, so we were back to back.

I lied awake for the longest time, waiting for something bad to happen, like Carrie wanting to have sex or her mom coming home and checking on her daughter and seeing me in bed with her. But nothing happened. Carrie was still and soon I heard her breathing change from awake breathing to the deep, even breaths of sleep.

I felt alone, and for the first time in my life I was aware of how much I loved that feeling.

- - - -

That lasted two or three nights. I'd get up in the morning and go to school, borrowing Carrie's clothes. Then Carrie's mom found out I was staying there. Before she found out, we were unsupervised. We cut each other's hair and did makeup and babysat Carrie's younger brother. But the brother told the mom and the mom said I couldn't stay there anymore. For a couple nights after that, I would sneak in over the gate and stay with her anyway.

Carrie gave me this cool haircut that was all the eighties hairband style—where the top sticks out but the rest of your hair is really long. We used to go to Kmart and take soda cups from McDonald's and take small makeups or earrings or necklaces in our empty cups and then just walk out with them. The top of Carrie's dresser was covered from edge to edge with stolen makeup.

I stopped going to Carrie's house and I stopped going to school. I thought my mom would find me there and I didn't have any more school clothes. So instead of going to Carrie's, one night, I just walked. I didn't know where I was going—I was just going forward. The first night I found this big park and I walked around the edges and looked at what was going on. There were people barbecuing, people walking their dogs. I found a giant tree and sat under it, watching everyone and dozing off until finally I woke up and it was dark and there was no one in the park that I could see. But I knew there had to be other homeless people here, so I sat perfectly still as my eyes scanned the park for movement, and that's the way I stayed till morning.

- - - -

That park had a daily cycle, and I learned it. Thirty minutes after dawn, homeless people would come to the park and wash in the park's bathrooms. I was one of those. And at that time of day, the water in those sinks was freezing—it felt terrible to splash it on my face, but I did it anyway. Then there were two sets of baby and dog walkers, one around ten (which was the mothers) and one around seven (which was the fathers). Then there were Hispanic maids pushing strollers with white babies in them—they would come in the middle of the day. There were the hardcore/street kid/punks who wore all black and slept and traveled as a pack and had big dogs for protection. They did meth when they could afford it. They didn't approach me and I didn't approach them. There were the party Mexicans, who would back up a truck with a cooler full of beer and a boombox. They'd sit out on folding chairs and get drunk off Coronas and Michelob. There were people who played frisbee (late afternoon). There was a prophet who came every day to the gazebo. He sat on the steps and read aloud from the Bible, warning us that the end was near, singing us the Psalms, telling us how the Earth was formed. He read loudly so I assumed he was working on his projection. That man carried a black trash bag with him and his shoes were torn up and he didn't seem to have a job he needed to get to so I assumed he was homeless. I imagined his route through the city, maybe sleeping in a shelter, buying coffee with food stamps, walking to the gazebo, reading, then he would walk off in the same direction he came from, so I assumed he had someplace to sleep over there.

One day this yellow convertible kept driving around and he would slow down when he came by my tree.

"How much for an hour, beautiful?"

"Keep going! I'm not a fucking prostitute!"

This douchebag with blond-dyed hair cocked his head to the side and threw up his hands.

"Too bad."

"Yeah, too bad for you, fuckhead."

He drove off.

I wasn't to that point yet—selling my body for food. I hoped I never would be. When that yellow convertible pulled up I hadn't eaten for eight days.

- - - -

Sometimes kids from my old school would ditch and come to the park I stayed in. We would all hang out and walk around rich neighborhoods and sneak inside empty houses and smoke weed and drink. We'd sit on the living room floor in a circle, which made it kind of ritual. Not Satanic or anything, just a ritual of poor kids who would never live in houses like these because we'd be working at McDonald's or Carl's Jr. or Walmart or something. Leaving the sweet smell of weed and a bunch of empty liquor bottles was our way of saying, "We're here," to some rich assholes we'd never meet.

Fuckers. They're driving a car that costs more than my *life*.

"Davina! Yo! Spacegirl! You still staying with your friend?"

"No, her mom found out. I had to move out."

"So where you staying?"

"The park."

"The park where we picked you up today?"

"Yeah."

"Do you need a sleeping bag, yo? 'Cause we got sleeping bags at our house."

"Doesn't somebody need them?"

"Not as much as you. Yo, I don't mean no disrespect, I'm not saying that you *need* a sleeping bag or anything. I'm just sayin', if you need something, ask."

"Cool," I say.

"Yo, pass that girl a drink, I think she needs one."

You can say what you want about ditchers, losers, potheads, drinkers, druggies, freaks. They may not conform to your idea of what a person should be, but those are the only people who ever treated me well in my entire life.

- - - -

One time we were hanging out in the park and the cops showed up. Everyone scattered but me. They were all ditching—they didn't want to get in trouble. But I figured since I lived here and didn't go to school anymore, why should I run?

These two cops pull up in their patrol car.

I'm kinda walking with my hands behind my back, acting cute.

"Nice day today," the one cop says.

"Very nice," I say. "Very fucking nice."

"What's your name?"

"Isabel."

"You live around here?"

"A couple blocks away."

"Who were those kids who you were hanging out with?"

"Just some friends."

"Any of them cutting school?"

"Very likely that they are, some of them. I wouldn't know."

"You cutting school?"

"No, I don't go to school anymore. I graduated early."

"You must be pretty smart."

I walked up to the patrol car and leaned on the window opening. They were listening to oldies on the radio.

"Don't you have a CD player?"

The second cop answers—the one in the passenger seat.

"The city voted it down. We're on duty so we're not supposed to have fun."

"You're not supposed to *have fun*?"

"Meaning that listening to our own music would put us at too high a comfort level, so the people would rather that all we had was a radio. Hence no CD players."

"Well that's dumb," I say.

"Tell me about it. Fifty bucks a car. Fifty bucks a car is all it would cost them."

"That sucks! I'm gonna write a letter."

"Who you gonna write a letter *to*, Isabel?"

"The city! I'm gonna get my mom to write it, actually—demanding that they give you guys CD players."

"Every little bit helps."

"Yeah, thanks Isabel. Enjoy this weather."

I wave goodbye and I'm turning, walking back into my park. Those cops could have arrested me and taken me back to my mom. Those kids I hung out with? They weren't cool. I was friendly with those cops, confident—made *them* the issue instead of me. It's like with sharks and dogs and every other kind of animal—people are no different—they smell your fear. But I didn't let 'em smell *my* fear. I walk like this was *my* land, and they see nothing wrong with me being here. I guess I *was* pretty good at being cool.

- - - -

At night I would walk around because I didn't know where to sleep. I walked around neighborhoods daydreaming I lived in one of the nice houses. I talked out loud, telling myself these stories pretending that the people who lived in a certain house were on vacation and in my imagination I would go in and make myself something to eat—nothing complicated, just a sandwich. Then I would go upstairs and peek in each of the bedrooms to see which one felt like mine. When I went in the closet, it just so happened there were clothes there in exactly my size. When there's no one out on the sidewalks, you might as well talk to yourself—you're the only one listening! I made up these elaborate fantasies for every house I liked. Each one had a family to go with it—a family that liked me. I rarely had brothers or sisters—I preferred to be the only child. I had a mother in these dreams, but my father was blurry—I guess I didn't know what an ideal father looked like.

Sometimes people would catch me talking to myself, narrating my fantasy lives. Someone would come out to walk the dog or they'd be walking past me. At first, I'd stop talking when someone was listening. But after a few days I didn't care.

Something you should know about me: I don't care what you think. I mean, if I love you, I might *consider* what you think. But I'm not making my decisions based on other people's opinions. I've always been that way, but I think being homeless makes you that way even more. I mean you're washing your face in a water fountain at five a.m.—you know what people are thinking about you. You're not part of the system. You don't have a place to live. You don't have a job. You don't pay taxes. You don't get to go to the doctor if something breaks. You're out of society, and it's *your* fault.

My main impression of society when I was a kid was an image of like this huge tank of cannibalistic snakes. Everyone eating everyone else's tail and calling it progress. Just blood and scales and mouths filled with other people's tails. Munching and munching. And finally getting to the end where no one can eat anymore and everyone's dead.

As I walked around the neighborhood, though, looking at all these rich houses, my fantasy always ended with a bed. Pillows, covers. And thick covers, too—heavy ones. With covers, it's not just that they keep you warm, it's the weight of them—they have to feel like a hand, a huge, warm hand that keeps you safe throughout the night.

When I got too tired to walk, I would go back to the park. I was never alone there, but I found ways of walking so I was mostly unseen. I would make my way behind trees and in the shadows they held from the moon, and I would go to the very center of the park where there were bathrooms that were usually unlocked at night. I'd go inside very carefully, looking that no one else was there. When I got to the very back of the room, I would sit and lean against the wall. There were no lights in the bathroom—all it had were windows at the top for light from the sun and moon and stars. I would go in there and try to sleep, but all I ever got was a few minutes at a time—some noise would wake me up or the weight of my own head falling over to sleep would wake me up. That happened over and over throughout the nights. It was so dark in there that even though I knew I could reach out and touch the nearest stall, I couldn't see it. I was scared because everything was unknown and nothing was protecting me. When you're sleeping cozy in your nice bed you at least have the illusion that you're safe. When you're sleeping in an unlocked bathroom in an open park in the middle of a city, there's no illusion. If someone bigger than you and stronger than you comes along and wants to rape you or kill you or dismember you—or whatever people are doing these days—they're going to do it. And no one's going to find out until the morning when some jogger comes in to take a piss. She goes to the back of the restroom and sees my dismembered corpse, eyes missing, fingernails chewed off, head separated from the neck and (oh, that's nice) someone took a shit where my brain used to be.

So I'm sitting in the cold, and it gets colder the later the night goes on. I would sit there, perfectly quiet, and sometimes other homeless people would come in and use the bathroom and I would be sitting against the back wall and they wouldn't even see me. *That's* how quiet I was.

There was also a baseball field with bleachers—I slept there too. I would go as far under them as possible and lie down and hope that no one would see me. I didn't sleep a lot, just kinda rested my eyes. I tried not to be seen by cops or other people. Sometimes I would go to the back of Carrie's apartment complex, where there was a brick wall with drainage holes. I was pretty thin at that time and I'd wriggle under the drainage holes and come out inside the parking lot. I found that to be a safe area. I slept right up against the wall.
