"Because you know how good it feels when you're high. Come on, let me get you off."

"Forget it."

"Charles! Eddie! Just let me out for two minutes. I'll suck you off real good and then you can lock me up again."

"Nice try."

"I'm serious, Eddie. I'll be real good."

"Nice try, bitch, but do you know what?"

"What?"

"You were always just a pet to me. You're just a little. Fucking. *Dog.* With a tight ass. That's all you are to me."

I sit down on the pile of mattresses and put my head in my hands.

- - - -

"You know what makes me happy?" he yells.

"What?" I say to myself.

"What makes Eddie happy is seeing that tub of red water after I fucked you yesterday. It means that I own your ass and I can fuck you till you *bleed*. I can fuck you till you *die*. You hear that, little girl?! When you first came to me you were tight as a fucking *cat*—if you've ever *fucked* a cat. I couldn't *believe* your *ass* when you first came to me—I thought: this must be a blessing from *God*, and what did I do to deserve it? Then I saw through..I realized I *did* deserve you, your fuckin' twat your mouth your ass your hands your hair your everything. I deserve your *everything*—you hear me in there?"

I said, "Yes," very quietly.

"Baby, I'm sorry for fuckin' up your asshole so much last night. I ain't blind—I saw that bloody water. I ain't saying I'm sorry or anything. I'm just sayin': thank you for your sacrifice. I can't imagine what it must be like to be a bitch with an asshole that's in such demand."

I felt the back of my head. There was a knot there, and cuts crusty with blood. That's from my head hitting up against that spicket every time Charlie jammed his cock into me. Yes, some of that blood was from my asshole, but most of it was from my head. Charlie had stretched out my asshole long ago.

I covered my ears so I didn't hear what else he had to say.

I just let the sweat gather in my hair and my armpits and the pits of my knees. You know I never had any ideas about getting married or even what it'd be like to have a boyfriend. I might have had them as a very little kid—I don't remember. By the time Jessie came along all that was forgotten. I was in hell, daily, with Jessie. And now I'm in hell again.

Me, banging on the garage door, screaming for someone to let me out.

I said, "I'm dehydrating!!"

No answer.

I bang on the door again with all my force.

Then it's just that record playing *Home Sweet Home* over and over and over and over..

Charlie would come check on me from time to time.

"Bitch, you awake?"

I pretended to be asleep.

"You ain't fuckin' those neighbor boys now!"

"I never was," I say to myself. "I never was."

"What's that?"

"I NEVER FUCKED THOSE BOYS!!"

"That's typical: a bitch pretending to be asleep when she ain't really asleep just so she can hear what you're saying. Bitch, you just earned yourself another hour."

He restarts *Home Sweet Home*.

- - - -

It was dark before he let me out. I was weak from not eating. I was dehydrated. Charlie had to help me stand. I was bent over in the kitchen drinking from the sink when Charlie said:

"You got blood on the back of your head Davina."

"I know," I said.

Then I drank some more. The only sound in my head was Charlie's laughter when he let me out of the garage. He thought it was *hilarious* that he had kept me in there all day.

"Baby I thought you could sleep with me tonight."

"Why would you think that?"

"Because I'm going into the Conservation Corps."

"When do you leave?"

"Two weeks."

"And then when are you coming back?"

"Don't know."

I looked at this man—and I really didn't want to see him go. Call me stupid or fucked up or chalk it up to my experiences as a child, but I loved Charlie—and I told him that, that night, when we were in bed together. I whispered it in his ear: "I love you." And I meant it.

## 38
Soon after that, Charlie left. He had a green rucksack. It looked like was going into the Army. He even dressed like he was going into the Army, which is not how the California Conservation Corps dressed. We dropped him at a train station and he went north.

After that, things were very quiet for me in the house. There was no one left to torture me, and as you can see I'd been used to a certain amount of torture in my life from an early age. Charlie called sometimes, and though I was always last, he always asked to talk to me after he talked to his sister and mother. When we talked we'd say "I love you." Charlie was better at a distance.

We had a peaceful existence, me, Yvette, and Yvette's mom. I had my own room at that house. It was a weird room—it wasn't meant to be a bedroom but rather a sunroom. One wall was all glass. I liked to change clothes with the lights on and wonder if anyone out there was watching.

Without Charlie around to occupy me, Yvette and I became more and more like sisters. Yvette would fall asleep with the radio on. I'd go in and change the station and Yvette would wake up to Led Zeppelin all surprised. I got to be close to all of Charlie's relatives, his older sister, his cousins. It was like I was part of the family—kind of.

- - - -

Towards the end of the summer, Charlie's mom decided to move because she couldn't afford the house we were living in. She also couldn't afford to feed me anymore—she found out that I wasn't 18 and that I was a runaway. Personally, I think she knew that all along and decided to look the other way. She sat me down in the kitchen to tell me all this.

"Do you know how much I love you, Davina?"

She held my hands.

Then she cried.

"I'm sorry, baby, I love you being here but I just can't take care of you anymore."

She looks at me through teary eyes.

"Do you understand?"

I nodded. I was crying, too.

"Davina. I've got to take you to the police station. Because..look, I know you ran away from home and the police did tell me some of what your real home is like and even so I doubt ours is much better and I know Charlie didn't treat you right. I know that, ok? Maybe he'll learn, while he's away, how to treat a woman. I know you loved him."

"Yeah."

"Maybe you two can meet up again in the future, if you both want that. Can you forgive me? You're such a sweet girl—you're sweeter than my own kids. But if you were my daughter and I was wondering if you were out there somewhere, I'd worry every night."

"The only reason my mom wants me back is so she can claim child support."

"I'm sure that's not true."

"It is. And I can tell you right now, living with you is a lot better than living with her."

Yvette's mother sobbed at that one.

"Please understand, baby, I'd keep you forever if I could."

- - - -

Then she got up and wiped her tears away and took deep breaths by the kitchen sink.

"Do you *have* to take me to the police station?"

"Where can I take you?"

"Back to my park."

"*What park*, Davina? I can't just take you to a park."

"I don't know what the name is but I can show you how to get there. It's the park I stayed in when I was first homeless."

"Oh, Davina, you're breaking my heart. Little girls like you shouldn't be homeless."

I thought about running out the door, just running and running and running until I came upon a new park and a new Charlie and a new truck stop to live in. But instead I just said:

"When do we leave?"

Yvette's mom turned to face me.

"As soon as you get your things."

"Where's Yvette? Can I say bye to her?"

"Yvette is at a dance lesson. Get your things, ok?"

Yvette didn't even *take* dance lessons. But I went upstairs, stole a Hello Kitty backpack from Yvette's room, and I packed some of the nicest clothes Yvette's mom had bought me. I went into Charlie's room and stole some weed, a glass pipe, and a knife he had left behind. I took a long look at his room and wondered if I'd ever see him again.

I went downstairs. Yvette's mom was already holding the door open for me. I don't even think she noticed the Hello Kitty backpack. She was crying a lot.

"Don't feel bad," I said.

"I *do* feel bad. Because I met your mom, Davina, and I know a little bit about what your home life was like. I know about Jessie too, and—well, I know a few things. I'm not trying to get in your business, Davina, but I see why you ran away. I'd run away from that, too. I would. And I would *love* to keep you—I swear it Davina, I love you like my own child. But you're not 18 so it's *illegal* for me to keep you. If it wasn't for that we wouldn't be making this trip. It's a *stupid* law—it really is. You don't have any drugs or anything on you? Good. I'd hate to take you to the police station just to get *arrested!*"

It was like that all the way to the police station, Yvette's mom talking hysterically. I'm surprised she could drive. I imagined being locked in a cell while they figured out what to do with me. Instead, we walked into a room with a bunch of desks and some chairs right inside the door.

Lisa was sitting in one of them.

She didn't get up.

She didn't hug me.

She just looked away, and she and Yvette's mom filled out paperwork with a cop half their age. Then he looked at me and smiled, like he had just done some sort of good deed. And I walked out with Lisa, and Yvette's mom walked out alone.

Yvette drove away without a wave or a look, and I knew it wasn't because she didn't care—she just didn't want to complicate the situation.

Me and Lisa stood by the car. She had come alone. I expected her to rip into me with some derisive shit she had learned from *her* mom, or maybe to slap me across the face. But she just looked at me—and looked and looked and looked—and I felt like I was receiving some alien telepathy that said: *hate kill hate hate kill hate hate*. Her telepathy was asking me why I was ever born and why I was such a thorn in her side and why she had to *waste her time* to come pick me up at the police station. I'm not saying I read minds—I'm not even saying I can read eyes—but when a certain, creepy, evil individual is around me, I can.

- - - -

And that was the end of me being a runaway. It was the summer where we flirted around but there was no intercourse (except with Charlie). But with the boys in the next house, and with Yvette, it was innocent, and beautiful, and fun. I felt what it was like to be attractive to—but not molested by—those guys. That was a first for me.

One day I remember the most. The twins' dad took us on a trip to the beach. On the way there we stopped at a restaurant. In the area where you wait to be seated, there was a novelty game. It was the kind where you put in your birth date and it tells you your fortune. When it was my turn, I put in my birthday but I forgot I was pretending to be 18 so I put in my real birthday and everyone was like freaking out, screaming and laughing at me that I didn't know my own birthday.

I screamed, "I put in the wrong year!"

"What year were you trying to put in?"

I was sweating so much and my face turned bright red.

Then someone said the supposedly right year and I put that in and the excitement blew over. I was so scared that right then that one little mistake had ruined everything, that I was going to have to move away from my peaceful house and back with Lisa. I was so afraid I had messed it all up but for that moment, at least, everything worked out.

I always did wonder if the twins' dad noticed this event and told Yvette's mom about it.

So after I put in my fake birthday, I squeezed this copper-looking thing and my fortune came out. You wanna know what it said?

It said: "Help! I am being held prisoner in a Chinese bakery!"

Now listen, people, some fortunes are shit and some fortunes are great, but I've never forgotten that one, from that day. Probably mostly because of the part right before that where I put in my real birthday and everyone freaked out, but also because of this: perspective. You know, that shit'll save your life. You might be a banker, you might be rich, you might be poor, you might be homeless living in a mansion. Or, of course, you might be a prisoner in a Chinese bakery.

Things to think about.

## 39

Mom moved me back in with the kids and Ron (remember the guy that put ball bearing grease all over my sister's face and was Jessie's best friend?—yeah, that guy). I mean what kind of guy immediately gets with his best friend's woman when his best friend goes to jail? Backstabbing motherfucker—or maybe it's kind, I don't know.

We lived in every motel in San Bernardino. I don't know why we moved. Maybe they were passing bad checks, maybe we were running from someone—that just wasn't the kids' business. But we moved a lot. Once we ran out of motels in San Bernardino, we stayed at motels deeper in the city.

- - - -

Something I want to mention from around that time is going to a motel room with two brothers and them leaving me stranded hours from home. I walked most of the way back from Anaheim to the Foothill Motel. When I told Lisa what happened, I tried to make it sound like I was pressured into going that far away. Lisa didn't believe it—she said she told her mom the same story when she was young.

There were these guys who went door to door selling gold-plated chains out of suitcases. It was necklaces, bracelets. They came to my door. They showed me the chains. The outside was good quality gold and the inside was tough metal that wouldn't break, they claimed. After about twenty minutes it was obvious I wasn't going to buy anything, but we were all getting along. They asked me if I wanted to go along with them, hang out, party. I said yeah—I didn't know they were going so far away.

We went to a motel room in Anaheim. Once we got there it was like *dead silence* between us. One of them was in a chair, the other was lying on the bed, and I leaned against the door. I started having flashes of double-penetration rape and lacerations in this girl's face. She wasn't me, but she might have represented me. This imaginary girl's face was all cut up by one of those knives that flips out really quick. I was thinking about how long it would take to bleed from lacerations to your face and then I was checking these boys' pockets looking for lacerating knives and then I was like Davina this is all in your imagination so I just said:

"Does anybody want to go swimming?"

We went downstairs but the pool was locked. I climbed over the fence (I've climbed a lot of fences in my life). I have memories of going to swim at a younger pool and climbing over that fence too. So me and these two guys went swimming, then went back to the hotel room.

I thought the younger of the two brothers was cute. I was flirting with him, sitting by him on the bed. Then the older guy got in bed with us. It was clear he wanted to have a threesome. Now I'm in bed messing around with both of them, but playing hard to get with the older brother—I didn't want to sleep with both of them at the same time. The older brother got upset because I was letting the younger brother kiss me but not him. He got disgusted and stormed out.

Me and the younger brother made out—I can't remember if we had sex. I passed out.

- - - -

When I woke up in the morning they said:

"Well, Davina, there's the good news and the bad news."

I stared at them, half awake.

"Which one do you want first?"

"The good news or the bad news."

"Right."

"Alright how 'bout the good news."

"There's an unusual choice," says the older brother. "Don't you want the bad news first so that after you hear it, the good news will cheer you up?"

I scratch some sleep out of my eyes.

"What?" I say. "Just give me the good news. I don't have time for this shit."

"Well," the older brother says, "you'll have plenty of time..once you hear the bad news."

"Go. Go. Good news. Gimme the good news."

"Go ahead, little brother."

And the one I was making out with (and possibly fucked) says, "The good news is that we enjoyed our time with you. We had an excellent time. You are a very fun person to be with."

I wasn't exactly sure what he meant by this since I didn't exactly remember what we did, but I said:

"Ok."

"Trust me," the younger one says. "It was *more* than ok."

"Well good for you then. What's the bad news?"

"The bad news," the older brother says, "is that we can't take you home."

"Hmn?"

"We can't take you home."

"Why not?"

"Because as you can see it is nine o'clock. We normally start our routes at seven. We have to get going to make our quota or Father will be disgusted with us."

"*Disgusted?*"

"Yes, disgusted."

"Just drive me home on the way to your routes."

"Unfortunately, our routes are in the other direction today."

"So take some extra time! How am I supposed to get home?"

"Take a cab. Call somebody."

"*I don't have any money.*"

"I guess you'll have to walk."

"From *Anaheim*?!"

"If that is where we are, then yes."

I point at the older brother and speak directly to him.

"This is a shit move. And you know that this is a shit move. Are you religious?"

"Yes, I am."

"Do you think your god is happy with you right now?"

He blinks, once.

"You have the room till eleven," he says, and he leaves.

The younger brother set his suitcase on the bed and opened it. He took one of the gold bracelets out of its compartment. He kneeled before me. He polished it. And he gave it to me.

He said, "We're sorry about all this."

Then both those brothers are gone.

It's funny, when you're a kid you know exactly who you've kissed. You make lists. You order them. You know how many. Then it's the first person you fucked—you'll never forget that. Then you're a teenager or an adult, whenever it happens for you. The lists are obsolete. You don't remember all the people you fucked. You have people you fucked where you don't remember their name. Then you've got people—like this jewelry-selling younger brother, where you're not sure you fucked them at all.

Well, those brothers drove off and I stayed in that hotel room till *exactly* eleven o'clock. I got under those covers and masturbated to that little brother's image, imagining that we both came together and it was fireworks beyond belief and everything. After I came, I felt like shit. You would too if you were stuck in a hotel in Anaheim.

I didn't call my mom because what would she do? Help me? Not likely. Come get me? Not likely.

In fact, fuck that bitch.

- - - -

I left the hotel and wandered around town. A guy in a van with a bubble teardrop window offered me a ride. I got freaked out though and didn't get in the van.

I started walking. I found the freeway and figured out which way to go and I walked. I didn't want anyone to see me because, if you don't know, it's illegal to walk on the freeway. So I went up on a woodsy embankment and I was trampling through this foresty stuff not too far from the road.

I walked forever.

Every mile marker was a *fucking* eternity.

I got over being mad at those boys and got into being mad at myself—so hungry for adventure or dick or friendship or whatever that I'll wander off with *anybody*—literally anybody—without even asking if my ticket is round trip.

Some miles I was mad at myself.

Some miles I was mad at my mom.

I thought of Jessie, and Ron, and the pastor at the Pentecostal church.

Then I stopped getting mad and I thought about getting a face tattoo—things like that. Like moving to Hawaii. Americans can move to Hawaii, right? How much fun would that be? Gettin' tan, smokin' bud—becoming a little surfer girl. You *know* surfer girls get mad dick.

I had fantasies of being raped by a truck driver who offered to give me a ride. It would almost be worth it. My ass..for a ride home, plus whatever food and beer you have in the cab. Give you an extra blowjob if we make it all the way to my house.

This is how it went, the day I walked from Anaheim, walking through thick forest (which slows you down—like walking through sand), spending time with my thoughts (which I like to do 'cause it organizes them), and watching as all these people right next to me, who had more money than me, could go wherever they wanted—in the blink of an eye.

At one point I saw a gas station and I walked down to the freeway. Before I even got to the gas station someone pulled over and rolled down their window.

"Is that your car broken down on the side of the road back there?"

"Yeah."

"Lemme give you a lift to the gas station—you can make a call."

I was scared as shit: either this guy was gonna chop me up and eat me or the cops were going to put me in jail for hitchhiking. But I got in and he took me to the gas station. No chopping was involved—he let me out and he got back on the freeway.

I waited five minutes and started walking again. My mind was troubled with the possibility of encountering the police—I'm half-white, though, so I gave myself a fifty-fifty chance of surviving if a cop caught me walking in a no-walking zone. I felt like I was going crazy, I had so many thoughts. I even *wanted* a cop to kill me. When I started thinking about people I had known and maybe me back in Flagstaff with Janine and my real dad, that time Janine had me rub my butt against hers..when I thought of these things, I cried. I wasn't really sure what I was crying about, but it felt good.

All that way until the gas station and a mile beyond, I did all that thinking and that little bit of crying I just described. But past a point, thoughts are useless; and past a point, tears are useless too. So you know what I did? I stopped thinking and I stopped crying and I walked my ass home.

- - - -

I remember walking up to the hotel room where we were living that week. My arms and legs were scratched from walking through the woodsy part by the freeway. It was daylight again—I had walked all day and all night and into the next evening. Mom was getting out of the car with Ron and they each had a bag of groceries.

Mom said, "Where have you been?"

And I said, "I've been walking for a really long time."

So many times when I think about things I'm surprised that I'm still alive right now. And that I don't have any STDs. Because I got myself into so many strange situations. And I slept with so many strangers.

But what are you going to do? I mean, it's not like my mom set a good example. We're moving from this motel to that motel—we never stayed anywhere longer than a week. You know? It's not like we're having family dinners and the parents are helping with the homework. More like the kids are passed out on the floor while Mom and Ron are sitting up in bed shooting crystal methamphetamine.

"Davina?"

"What."

"You awake? Come here. I'm gonna give you some cash—"

"I'm not awake."

"I want you to go down to the liquor store, get me some rubbing alcohol."

"What time is it?"

"'Vina! Do what you're told. Now get up here."

I kneel beside the bed. There's fucking..cotton balls..needles..about twenty plates with little piles of crystal meth on them. I reach for one of the plates.

"Do you want me to consolidate this for you, this is a mess."

"*No no no!* Don't touch it!"

Lisa grabs my arm.

"I'm giving you more than enough. Just buy one bottle of rubbing alcohol. *Bring me change.* Don't be buyin' no Ho Hos, ya hear?!"

"Yeah, I got it."

"And *never* touch a meth head's plates."

"I've never even seen a meth head use *plates* before."

"Well *these* meth heads do!"

My mom and Ron crack up laughing.

- - - -

Pretty soon I'm walking in the dark on Main Street. None of the streetlights are on. It's not very far from the Foothill Motel to the liquor store—like fifteen minutes. I don't even know what time it is, I'm just trusting two meth heads that they didn't send me on an impossible mission. I can see the light of the liquor store ahead on my left. Every once in a while I casually spin around so I can check that nobody's following me. All the way up to the store it's safe—I see nobody. Then at the liquor store there's this black dude leaned up against the door *totally* casing me. He doesn't take his eyes off me.

I say hi to indicate I'm not afraid of him.

He says nothing back to me—not a good sign. He just looks at me and lets me know that he knows I'm there.

I pull open the door next to him and go in the liquor store.

When I come out, black dude is hiding behind a sign—those easel-shaped signs stores put on the sidewalk? Yeah, he's behind that like he thinks it makes him invisible.

I walk by the sign hoping that the fact that we're *right next to* the liquor store will be a deterrent from him trying anything.

As soon as I passed the sign he grabbed me around my chest and stomach, holding me with his arms.

I screamed as loud as I could.

I tried to unwrap his arms from around me and I was holding onto that bottle of rubbing alcohol *with my life*—if I came home without that, *Lisa* would probably rape me. This guy got me so we were facing, and then he was kneeling so we were on the ground.

I never stopped screaming. I don't know what I said but I screamed right in that motherfucker's face and I looked directly in that man's eyes and I communicated whole *volumes* of shit to that guy—shit like I'm'onna follow you back to your house after you rape me and eat your grandmother's *brains* while she's sleeping. Shit like: motherfucker, you rape *me, I'll* unleash a pair of demon meth freaks who will cut your dick off and *smoke* that shit. I literally told him:

"My mom will *cut your dick off* and smoke it in her meth pipe."

He looked at me funny but still held me down.

"My mom is fucking *crazy*. You don't want to *meet* my mom. She'll have a fat Mexican get you high on ice, then he'll fuck your little asshole and your ass'll be so high you'll be beggin' that Mexican for more. You won't even know you *have* an asshole—you'll be trippin' so high your ass'll think you some eight-year-old girl gettin' her pussy fucked the first time and you'll be *begging for more*."

This black dude who was holding me down loosened his grip.

"Is you crazy, bitch?"

"*What*, do you, *think*."

"I think you sound like a crazy bitch."

"Have you ever fucked a crazy bitch before?"

He shook his head.

"What's your name?" I said.

He said, "Pippin."

"Well, Pippin, I am *not the bitch* you want to fuck tonight. Can we agree on that and go our separate ways?"

"Yeah." He said it real quiet, and with something like respect.

He let me go.

We both got up.

Pippin was dusting off his clothes and went back to leaning on the liquor store.

I still had my rubbing alcohol. I still had my change.

Every step I took I was surging with adrenaline. You don't *fuck* with me. You *do not fuck with me*.

When I got to the end of the block I screamed. It was something animal, something prime, something that reached all the way back to Pippin's soul and his gut and even the Vietnamese guy who owned the liquor store heard it and everyone on the block who was awake heard it and everyone on the block who was asleep—they heard it in their dreams. And it meant: *I am alive. I WILL NOT DIE TONIGHT!!* It was the battle cry of an urban fifteen-year-old girl. And Pippin, I do not recommend you *fuck* with a fifteen-year-old girl. 'Cause fifteen-year-old girls is crazy. Pippin, *you is crazy*—but fifteen-year-old girls *is crazier than you*.

- - - -

Before I could even get through the door at the Foothill Motel, Mom was shaking her hands.

"Gimme gimme gimme gimme! Davina! Get me the rubbing alcohol or I will die."

I walked around the bed and handed the rubbing alcohol to her.

"Aww, Davina, you never buy the store brand."

"But you *always* tell me to buy the store brand."

"Not for the important stuff! Yes, you buy the store brand *by default* but there are certain exceptions like Cheerios, Kleenex, and rubbing alcohol."

"I'm sorry. I didn't know."

"Yeah, baby, have you ever tasted store-brand Cheerios?"

"I don't know."

"They taste like shit. Just let that be a lesson to you. We're poor but we're not *that* poor. Always buy name-brand Cheerios."

"Ok, I will. I'll always buy name-brand Cheerios."

She didn't even hear me. They were too busy cleaning their needles.

"I almost got raped tonight."

They still didn't hear me. They were both shooting up and this took their entire field of concentration—they were like a tightrope walker with another tightrope walker balancing on the shoulders of the first—that *Cirque du Soleil* shit that requires ultimate concentration. Once my mom was good and high, she said:

"You almost got *raped* tonight?"

"Yeah."

"On your way to the liquor store?"

"On the way back."

"Well what happened?"

"I bought the rubbing alcohol and then this black guy was hiding behind their sign—"

"Did he touch you?"

"Yes! He had me on the ground and he was about to rape me. He might have wanted to kill me, I don't know! I'm not sure what his exact intentions were—I forgot to ask."

"So how did you get out of it?"

"I screamed at that motherfucker!"

"What did you say?"

"I don't remember! I just told him to get the fuck off me or bad things would happen."

My mom busts out laughing, so hard she knocks over one of the plates. She slaps her thighs.

"Oh, Davina. You always were a screamer. Ever since you were little—I didn't know half of what you were saying! But you said it so *convincingly*—I mean how do you tell your child she's insane?"

She laughs some more. Then she shoots up and it fucks up her laugh. Then her laugh makes it hard for her to shoot up. Fuckin' loser.

I leave the change on the bed. Ron has said nothing this whole time but as I'm lying back down on the floor and arranging a sheet over my body, he says:

"So you say this guy was black?"

I look at the sleeping children—they are completely oblivious. Even Jamie, the demon, looks so calm. I wish her sweet dreams.

"So this guy. At the liquor store. Was he black?"

"*Yes*, he was black."

"That figures."

I pull the sheet off my head and sit up.

"What do you mean?"

"Nothin'. Just that if he had been some white dude he wouldn't have tried to rape you."
