About growing up in motel rooms and being butt-raped by the future father of my daughter. I want you to know that people *live* at truck stops—they live there. I want you to know that it's a lot easier to become homeless than you think. And I want you parents out there to know that you're having a major effect on more than just your children. I'm a scumbag because of those motherfuckers. Even though you're reading my book, if I saw you on the street I probably wouldn't like you—just for some stupid reason, like you're too much of a high-class trust-fund baby and I think you should die. I wanna kill people. I do. Other kids my age are having happy happy turkey day with their whitewashed asshole families. I'm standing on the balcony of the Foothill Motel in San Bernardino, California, wrapped in a bath towel waiting for my stepdad's butane-anaconda shit to blow over. So like, if you have your own bathroom, I want to kill you. If you have your own bedroom, I want to kill you. If you're *ever* going to have a life that isn't scraping by on loans from drug dealers and applying for heating subsidies for your house so you don't *freeze to death* in winter, and begging the government for food stamps because no job will hire you but you still have to *eat*..basically if your life is easy because you were born into the non-starving part of the system, then I want you to die a horrible death—like having bleach injected into your veins, for instance. I've heard that kills you right away.

## 42

I never even took a pregnancy test. I just passed out one day and my mom asked me if I was pregnant and I said:

"I have no idea."

She said, "You are."

And I believed her. We didn't have a doctor—we just had my mom's intuition. She could just look at me and *tell* that I was pregnant. It was as simple as that.

"We better tell the father."

"Eddie?"

"You never mentioned no Eddie to me!"

"Charlie."

"Holy child, how many men you fuckin'?"

"No, Charlie *is* Eddie. It's Eddie's. It has to be."

"Well let's go tell him the happy news."

We had a car at that time but I didn't remember where Eddie's house was. Mom drove, we left the kids at home, and I acted like a human dowsing rod as we wandered through neighborhoods.

"Not this street. No, not this one either. It's up here to the left—no, I mean right, I'm sorry."

"Kid, I find it a little odd that you can't remember the way to your baby daddy's house."

"It's different when you're walking! Cars go too fast!"

So Lisa slowed way down.

"Don't upset that child of yours. When a mother gets upset, it upsets the child, too."

"I *know*, Mom—I'm not dumb."

"Well *forgive me* if I try to pass on a little wisdom from the older generation."

"This is it," I say. "Up here on the right."

Mom pulls up in front of their house. Their house is perfectly kept. Our car looks like something from a war movie. When I ring the bell, their mom comes to the door. I guess they didn't have to move after all.

"Davina!"

"Is Eddie home?"

His mom yells, "Charlie! You've got a visitor."

Minutes later me and Charlie were on his bed, and he was trying to have sex with me.

"Charlie, wait."

His hand was down my shorts.

"Charlie, stop!"

"What's wrong, Baby D?"

"I'm going to have your baby, that's what's wrong."

"Never kid a kidder!" he said.

"I'm not kidding."

"Never fuck with a fucker!"

"Listen! I'm not fucking with you!"

"You're straight-up pregnant?"

"Yes."

"Who's the father 'cause I know it ain't me."

"Baby, it *is* you."

He pushed me. We were both lying on the bed but he pushed me by the shoulders, away from him.

"Well?" he says.

"Well what?"

"Well what're you gonna do about it?"

"I don't know."

"You don't? Well let me *tell* you. You're gonna nip that motherfucker in the bud."

"We don't have money to do that."

"That's why abortion clinics should be free—don't you think? I heard in China or Vietnam or someplace that abortions are free. You can literally walk into an abortion clinic in South Korea and they'll suck that fetus right out of there with a turkey baster. They even give it to you in a little glass bottle filled with formaldehyde. It's a keychain—so you can remember your abortion forever. You can even give your kid a name, and talk to it, and show it to your friends. You carry it around like a Tamagotchi but that's *ten times* better than a Tamagotchi—don't you think? Try getting *that* done in Texas. Did you know that in Texas they *lock pregnant women up* for nine months just to *force* those bitches to carry that shit to term? *That's* how anti-abortion they are in Texas. And if they find out you're not really pregnant, or you have a miscarriage or you use the bed springs to give *yourself* an abortion, they'll send in an officer—usually a junior officer on the force—and they'll tell that guy to *get* you pregnant—or else it's his *job*. *That's* what I'm telling you—*that's* what America's become. Fuck this country. Why don't you get some money from your dad?"

"I don't know where he is."

"Of course you do. He's on the reservation smoking peyote and drinking himself to death. Why the hell did I get involved with a half-Cherokee bitch like yourself—it's a fuckin' mystery—pussy ain't even that good anyway."

"You thought so at first!"

Then Charlie punched me in the face.

"Go find some other dad—"

"What the fuck did you do that for?"

"—in some other part of town to be your daddy. You and I both know that kid ain't mine."

He punched me again, even though I had my hands over my face. And he punched me in the stomach and I rolled off the bed (I guess to protect the baby).

Charlie was standing over me, kicking me with everything he said:

"If you knew how many dumb bitches came around here claiming that I was the father of their imaginary baby..well, baby, you ain't the first. You leeched off me at the truck stop. I *supported* your dumb ass for *months*. You *need* to learn some self-reliance, bitch. Ain't you ever read Thoreau? Just because you're homeless in a park and got a fifteen-year-old twat—that *everybody* got—does, not, make, *me*, the, father, of, *your*, supposed, child."

"Emerson," I say.

"What?!"

"*Self-Reliance* is Emerson, not Thoreau."

"Oh *really*? I'm supposed to trust a homeless, drugged up, high school *dropout* like yourself to tell me the difference between Emerson and Thoreau?"

Then he kicked me really hard. I was covering my stomach. All I wanted was to get out of there and he had me pegged like a spider in a corner of his room.

"If I say *Self-Reliance* is Thoreau, Davina, then *Self-Reliance* is Thoreau. That's practically all we *did*, at CCC, is read about self-reliance. So I don't need no little girl to tell me who wrote it. When's the last time *you* went to a library?"

I didn't say anything—I just protected my body.

"Well up in the mountain at CCC, we got ourselves a little library about all sorts of things. Did you you that I'm a certified Junior Trails Specialist now?"

"No I didn't know that."

"Well I am. See these stripes? You gotta *earn* those stripes. And not the way *you're* used to earning things—Jesus—you've gotta be the nastiest bitch I ever fucked, despite your age."

He kicks me again.

"How many drug dealers you think you fucked, so far?"

"I don't know. Just let me go."

"Oh I'll *let* you go. Just as long as you admit that you ain't even *carrying* no baby. You don't look pregnant to me. Say it."

"Say what?"

"Say, 'I'm not pregnant, Eddie, and I'll never come and find you again.' "

"I'm not pregnant."

"The whole thing."

"I'm not pregnant, Eddie..and I'll never..come..find you again."

I'm obviously crying by now.

Eddie stepped back.

I carefully got up, holding my womb.

"There ain't no baby in there."

He lets me pass and I leave his room. As I'm going down the hallway towards the front of the house I can hear him grumbling to himself about how he, "never shoulda fucked no indian pussy 'cause they'll come in your sleep and drag your ass to a reservation where there are no laws that protect a white man and they'd kill you just for fucking one of their daughters and if there *was* a baby they'd kill that too 'cause they're *savages* who don't know how to live a decent life among white people.."

And that's where I couldn't hear him anymore.

I was never Eddie's girlfriend—I was his prisoner.

Eddie's mom wasn't around so I let myself out.

"How did it go," my mom asked, as I was buckling my seatbelt.

"It went fine," I said, and that's all we ever spoke about it.

## 43
When I was seven months pregnant, we went to Texas.

My mom and Ron were in trouble with the cops. Everywhere we went, they were looking for cops. They spent so much time looking in the rear view mirrors they hardly paid any attention to what was going on in front of us. They had done something that was against the law, something that could get us pulled over. We were fugitives now. And on top of that, we drove to Texas in a stolen car.

Picture me, seven months pregnant, both my brothers and my sister had the chicken pox and we were all crammed together in the back seat of this tiny stolen car. It was so fucking gross—it was hot, everyone had sores, I had this huge protrusion sticking out of my belly.

I had just said bye to Brian. To say he was my boyfriend is an understatement. He was my lover, my friend, my partner (just about). He was my first true love—he fucked me bent over a tombstone in San Bernardino, California in the small hours of a June morning in 1989. I still dream about it. And he gave me his Levi's jacket to remember him by—which was a big deal. He stole that jacket from a biker store and when bikers catch you stealing their shit they don't call the police. He had risked bodily harm for that jacket and it was his favorite one, the one he wore every day—my point is he wouldn't have given it to me if I was just some bitch he liked to fuck. We loved each other. Leaving him tore my heart apart. It did. That might sound overly dramatic or nothing like what a girl would say but that's the most accurate way I can say it—leaving Brian tore my fucking heart *to pieces*..it shredded my raw ability to love. Another Brian was not about to just accidentally walk into my life anytime soon. He was that special.

On the way to Texas, I sat in the back seat of that stolen car with chicken-poxed siblings on both sides of me, holding Brian's jacket to my face and smelling it to remember my love. I knew the more I smelled it, and the more time that passed, the less his jacket would smell like him. Someday I would go to smell it and it wouldn't smell like Brian at all. I would hate that day, and I might even bury the Levi's jacket like you bury a person when the light goes out of them—what good are your lover's clothes when your lover's smell has left?

- - - -

I can't overstate my devastation at this move to Texas. I was *forced* to leave my boyfriend—and Brian was my first true love. This wasn't someone I just liked to hook up with. He wasn't just a cock and a pair of legs—someone you don't even like but you speed dial at 2am to come over and give your pussy a thorough beating. I know how you people do it—but this wasn't like that. Brian and I had it all, the whole package—emotional, physical, intellectual, spiritual—we had a real relationship. We cried together when I told him I was going to Texas. We even made plans for me to stay with him and not go with my family, but we didn't have money—ultimately Lisa won and took me with her.

When I went to tell Brian our family was leaving, I cried so deep I almost choked—my throat wouldn't let me breathe! I felt it was a sign from my body that I was doing the wrong thing by leaving California. I had all these *Romeo and Juliet* fantasies where I die in Brian's arms while EMTs do an emergency surgery to remove the fetus from my womb. The baby dies on-site and Brian partially decapitates himself using the med techs' portable bone saw. My mind was mixed-up—I was pregnant—to me, leaving was death.

The event put me in shock—I was catatonic. When Brian saw me, he knew I was there to say goodbye and he took off his stolen Levi's jacket and put it on me like it was the most natural thing in the world—something broken couples had been doing for thousands of years. It smelled so much like his sweat, and I was proud to be wearing it. I hugged him with the baby between us—even though it was Eddie's baby, I wasn't with Eddie, I was with Brian, and if that baby was born tomorrow it would be just as much Brian's as anybody's.

- - - -

I held Brian's jacket to my nostrils all the way through California, all the way through Arizona, all the way through New Mexico, and all the way into Texas. His scent was strongest in the armpits, so that's where I held my nose.

Now my mom isn't stupid. She knew Brian gave me that jacket to remember him by and she knew how important Brian was to me. She saw me smelling his armpits all the way from Cali to Arizona to New Mexico to Texas, and she saw that not once did I take my nostrils away from the sweaty, Brian-smelling armpits of that stolen Levi's jacket. So she *knew*, by simple observation, that jacket was important to me—I'm sure she's had similar souvenirs in her lifetime.

So you know what that bitch does?

Somewhere in Texas, while I'm *asleep* in the back seat, she carefully relieves me of the Levi's jacket—which I'm sure I was *tightly* clutching in my arms—she takes it from me..and I'll never know *exactly* what happened since I was sleeping, but when I woke up Brian's jacket was gone, the car was parked at a Flying J truck stop, and Mom was doing bumps of meth off the back of her hand. I looked around to make sure I didn't accidentally misplace the jacket.

"Where'd you get the meth?"

"Why, do you want some?"

"Where's Brian's jacket?"

"Honey. Davina Dear. You are of the age—well I guess if you're old enough to *have a baby*, then you're old enough to start keeping track of your own things."

She turns around and smiles, then does another bump. I can see, through her eyes, that she's laughing inside, laughing because to her *she won*. Stealing my jacket without me noticing and trading it for meth was like her hitting a free throw from the foul line. I wondered if she had to throw in a blow job as part of the transaction. Might as well sell everything—if she was willing to get ass fucked in the men's room at the Flying J, she might not have had to steal my jacket to trade for drugs.

I've forgiven my mother for a lot of things—I've had to, to be able to move on with my life. But when I saw that jacket was gone, my only link to Brian sold for a little bit of methamphetamine, my heart broke—again—and there's only so many times that can happen before you just let go of all the sanity you have and you fall off the edge.

And you keep falling forever.

I'll never forgive my mom for stealing that jacket.

She of course never mentioned it or apologized.

That was my introduction to Texas on this particular road trip.

I didn't get chicken pox and haven't had them to this day. Maybe being pregnant jacked up my immune system..I don't know.

- - - -

Let's go back a little. Somewhere in the desert, before we get to Texas, we're traveling in the little car that Ron stole (then stole plates for). It's just sand and scrub brush for hours. Then right in the middle of nowhere we see this camper (the kind you put on the back of a truck—but a really nice big one). We pull off the road. There's no driveway.

Ron turns back around to us kids.

"We're gonna stay here for a couple of nights."

"What is this place?" I ask.

Lisa turns around to me.

"Don't ask questions—just be grateful you have a place to stay."

I shut my mouth 'cause there was obviously no winning that one.

As we pulled closer I saw there was an old man and another camper with an awning for shade. All around us was desert—hard, white sand. And there were all these buckets surrounding the campers, tons of them, and they all had tiger eyes in them—these beautiful stones with the subtlest swirling patterns in them. I couldn't stop looking.

The old guy said:

"Go through them—take as many pieces as you want."

I looked at the old man and smiled. I was the only one who cared. The kids didn't care—to them this was "just a bunch of rocks" (that was a choice phrase from the demon Jamie, and I'll never forget that she said it). Lisa also didn't care—just more confirmation that I was born into the wrong family (possibly on the wrong planet). I picked out four or five of them and the old man looked pleased. I went to him and he looked at the stones in my hand. He touched them, turning them around to see all sides, and he looked at me and made a deep nod, and I was glad to have his approval.

You see, picking out stones is a spiritual thing. I'm not talking about God. I'm saying there's something that some people get—and most people don't. It's that a stone—even a dull stone—isn't just a stone. It is part of the Earth, which is one as a whole..it is a whole organism. And little beautiful stones matter. And snakes trekking through the desert matter. All of it deserves our respect, because we are connected like a heart to a kidney—the body needs them both. So, to me, disregarding sun-washed buckets of the most beautiful rocks I had ever seen..that was sacrilege. It was missing the point of life altogether.

See, even back then, I knew that there are two kinds of people: those who rush right past life, and those who stand in worship of it. Our society tricks us into rushing—it tricks us into believing there is something at the end of the maze. But—*shhhh*—here's a secret: there's nothing at the end of the maze. Everything is right here. You just have to stop seeking yourself out there in the world. Your *self* is not out there in the world. It is right here with you—and it's been here all along.

We stayed there in the desert at this haven for criminals. Ron parked our stolen car behind old grandpa's RV. I think we stayed there three days, just stayed in the bigger RV. For all my claimed spiritual enlightenment I was bored as hell. The kids were getting on my nerves so I took this portable tape player and played a tape of Quiet Riot—we listened to it over and over. *Metal Health* was the name of the tape. My mom and Ron were on one side of the camper doing their quiet whispering and talking on their bed and me and the kids were on the other side in our bed hopping around, trying to keep ourselves entertained.

We ate about one meal a day—canned food—like me and the kids would eat one can of spinach, or one can of kale. That's us *sharing* one can of kale, not each of us getting one. The best day by far was when we got SpaghettiOs.

The old man hardly opened his mouth the entire time we were there. Three days later we were on the road for Texas.

- - - -

In Texas, Ron and Lisa would do this weird thing. Ron was driving. We would stop in front of a house. This was in nice neighborhoods with cul-de-sacs and new sidewalks where no one had written their name. So Ron would stop, Lisa would jump out and run up to the house. She'd start by ringing the doorbell. If someone came, she made up some bogus story like she was looking for her aunt who ran away from home without telling anyone. The front door to the house would close and Lisa would run back to the car. We did this street after street. Sometimes Lisa would check their mailbox. Sometimes, when no one answered the door, Lisa would go around back. A few minutes later she'd come around the side yard, get back in the car and say, "No."

After enough neighborhoods and enough mailboxes and enough "No"s, there finally came a "Yes." And with that yes, Lisa broke into the house through a kitchen window and opened the front door. The rest of us unpacked our stuff into a house that was definitely not ours.

The adults didn't answer any of our questions. *Whose house is this? Are we squatting?* That was the kids' questions, though—I already knew what squatting was from my homeless days. The only thing they told us was that if the cops found us they would go to jail for a long time. Honestly I didn't care if Ron went to jail—he never did me nothin'.

During the day, we slept. Then around 9pm we'd drive into nearby towns and Ron and Lisa would steal things out of people's cars: purses, sunglasses, quarters and dimes. They'd steal an umbrella just because it was there. They didn't just steal from cars, though—they stole debit cards from old people walking down the street. An old couple would be walking along the sidewalk and our whole family would be following right behind them in the street. Then, depending on who was driving, either Lisa or Ron would jump out and be like:

"Heeeeeeyyy, how y'all doing tonight?"

The couple would hesitate.

"We're doing fine." (Some ancient warbly voice.)

"What brings you into town tonight?"

"We're going to see a movie."

"No you're not. Stop walking. There. Now you, hand me your purse—the whole thing. Do not think about it—just do it. You sir: wallet, now."

"Or what?"

"*What* what?"

"What are you gonna do about it?"

"Well, that's a very good question, sir. And I'd be happy to reply. If you don't hand over your wallet and the purse in three seconds I'm going to shoot you in the face, and you in the face."

"You don't even have a gun."

"Technically you are correct, sir. But you see that woman in the front seat of that car? She's badder than the baddest gangster during prohibition and if you do not hand over the wallet and the purse *right now* she's gonna pull out her little Tommy Gun and start spraying bullets all over this beautiful little sidewalk. See those beautiful carnations? They're gonna have bullet holes all over them. And these azaleas? Same thing. Bullet holes everywhere."

"Listen, son, I was alive during prohibition and I doubt you even know who the baddest gangster during prohibition *was*."

"Of course I do. It was Al Capone. Everybody knows that."

And Ron would snatch the lady's purse and grab the guy's wallet and get back in the car. By the time the police arrived, we were in the next town over casing another couple who looked like they were born a hundred years ago. They threw all the loot into the back seat for me to filter through.

"Next time get their PIN number."

"Davina, this is an adult operation for *adults* to perform. You have a simple job: look through the shit and pull out everything that's worthwhile. That means we don't need old lady stockings or hearing aids or motherfuckin' ACE Bandages. We need credit cards, we need debit cards, we need cash, we need jewelry. So stick to *your* part of the job and we'll stick to ours."

"Ok but I'm just saying if you had the PIN number to this debit card you could get cash from it."

I'm holding up a debit card from the old man's wallet.

I mouth the word, "Useless."

Lisa says, "Davina's right. Get the fucking PIN next time."

"What if they lie?"

"Just tell 'em you'll kill their kids or something."

"I ain't tellin' em that. You tell 'em that. Anyway it's your turn next time."

We must have robbed all the old people in Texas. Then we took their debit cards to bank after bank, withdrawing the maximum amount per transaction.

Mom and Ron also figured out this scam where they stole phone cards, then called over and over again until they figured out the codes. Then they would sell the cards or trade them for dope.

It was a day-to-day-type life. We could never leave our stuff in the houses we squatted in because what if the owners came back? Plus we had to run the phone card scam and rob old people every day due to my parents' meth habit. We must have made a ton off those old people's debit cards, but me and the kids never saw it. We never went to the grocery store or rented a hotel room—it was like the adults had a moral objection to spending money on anything but speed.

- - - -

One time we were parked behind an abandoned gas station with the windows open—way out of town. Ron and Lisa were in the front seat shooting up. The boys were asleep. Jamie was looking out the window.

I told my mom I was hungry.

She didn't answer.

I told her again.

She answered by handing me a 3x5 card with a line of speed on it.

I looked at her like: *why is my mother offering me speed?*

She said, "Cover one side of your nose. Then sniff the line up real quick. Go fast and breathe in like you're a vacuum cleaner—sniff as hard as you can. It's gonna burn. It's gonna burn real bad. But you won't be hungry anymore."

"I've done speed before, Mom."

"Oh you have! Well fuck me! You could have stopped me before the explanation! When did you ever do speed before?"

"When I was homeless."

"You were never homeless. You just chose not to come home."

I didn't feel like arguing so I just snorted the motherfucking line.

Jamie was watching me the whole time.

(Implication: *I'm* the big sister. *She's* the little sister. She looks up to me to see what's ok to do. And I'm *completely* shirking my responsibility by snorting a line of what is quite possibly the most dangerous drug in the world *right in front of my little sister*. By the way, if you didn't understand that before I explained it to you then you should probably be reading another book.)

I handed the 3x5 card back to my mother. And that was the beginning of us doing speed together, even though it was only a handful of times and even though I was pregnant with Ashley, I did it..I did..I did meth while I was pregnant with Ashley.

The adults always smoked pot with me—that was a given. Doing chrys while I was pregnant was not something I ever planned to do. But I was hungry. I was hungry after days and days of not eating anything, and a way presented itself so that I wouldn't be hungry—and I took it.

- - - -

Of course our little crime spree had to come to an end, and here's how it happened. We were all playing at a park and Lisa asked me if I wanted to go for a walk around the block. So just the two of us walked the block around this park and when we got back to where we started there were two police cars—my brothers and sister in the back of one car, Ron in the back of the other. I didn't realize it until a few minutes later: Mom had sold Ron out. She had called the cops. Our walk around the park wasn't to take in the fresh air, it was to save me and Mom's ass.

I often think about that move she made. She sold out her boyfriend. She sold out her other three kids. But she saved me. I probably read too much into it—it could have just been because I was pregnant. But it also could have been because she liked me the best—I like to think that's the reason.

## 44
After that I was my mom's puppy dog. The first thing we did was visit Ron was in jail. Me and Mom were in a courtyard looking up three stories to see Ron's face in a tiny window. They were discussing court dates so I tuned out and watched the other visitors. There were little kids carrying balloons and releasing them into the sky. What the fuck's that going to do? You release a balloon that floats by daddy's window and he gets to think about it for the next twenty years—while you're in college, have your first fuck, all those Christmases where Daddy's not at home 'cause he's locked up for holding a meth rock at the wrong time. Just don't get me started on this fucking system. Just don't. Big wig war criminals never even get charged for their crimes while Ron will spend the rest of his life in that fucking cell for holding a tiny rock of crystal methamphetamine.

My mom and Ron have settled their court-date conversation. Ron's face disappears for a while. Mom squeezes my hand.

"He really loves me. I think he really loves me," she says.

Then Ron's face appears at the tiny window. His hand reaches up. There's something white in it—a piece of paper. Ron throws it as far as he can from the building but it falls straight down.

Mom and I run to the side of the building before some other visitor can get it and read it first. We're kneeling at the base of the prison wall. Mom picks up the scrap of looseleaf and opens it.

It says: *Will u marry me?*

Mom was instantly emotional, crying and shit. I mean I've never seen a grown person cry that much. And over *Ron*—a man you just turned over to the *police* to save your own ass? I think my mom must have borderline personality disorder or some shit. Or maybe it's something they don't have a name for yet. Or, as I suggested earlier, the problem here might not be about the individuals involved, but something more systemic, on a larger scale.

Anyway, we ran back out in the courtyard where Ron could see us and I was just standing there solemn while my mom jumped around and flashed her tits to Ron's *tiiiiiny* face in that *tiiiiiny* window and she turned around and slapped her own ass like you slap a horse's ass and she turned to face him and she said, "Yes, baby. Yes Ronald. I will! I will marry you!!"

"*Ronald?*"

"Shut up baby. Be happy for us."

Mom shimmies partway out of her pants and shows Ronald her ass.

He whistles with two fingers in his mouth.

"That's my baby's ass!!"

Then other inmates yell:

"Show us the other side!"

"That's a sweet piece a ass you leavin' behind, bro."

"Bet she got a fine pussy on 'er, too."

"Oh she does. A *fine* piece. Can't wait to get out of here and fuck that sweet piece of ass! You know how we do!"

Lisa shouts, "That's right, baby. Soon we'll be fuckin' again and you'll be cummin' and pullin' my lil panties down and fuckin' my sweet lil puss."

"I'll take a piece a that!" some inmate yells.

"No! My sweet little pussy only has Ronald's name on it. The rest of y'all can't have any!"

"We can have anything we want, sister—all depends on who gets out first. What your boy in for?"

"He could be in there *a million* years and I wouldn't open my legs for you."

"You only been here ten minutes, you already showed us your ass."

"Fuck you!"

"Lisa!" Ron says. "It's ok. Chill. Chill. Don't get into it with these motherfuckers. You're mine, baby."

	And you're mine, baby doll."

"Good. Now take Davina out of here. This ain't no place for a girl who's pregnant."

"You're right, baby—god you're always right. If I had listened to you, you prob'ly wouldn't be in there now."

"Aww, it's alright."

"I want you to listen to me, baby, Ronald baby. Things are gonna be different—"

"I know they are baby. You don't have to tell me that."

"Would you shut up and listen? This might be the last time I see you in a while. I'm takin' 'Vina back to California."

"But you'll be back for our wedding."

"Of course, of *course*—I would *never* miss that. But me and D's gonna be gone for a while. First we're gonna get off the junk. I mean we're gonna smoke the rest of what I got but after that we're stopping. And we're just smoking it—no needles. We'll do lines of course—but just that and the pipe. After that, we're going *cold turkey*—no rehabs, no programs, just: zip, zap, zoom. And we're gone! We're clean, Ronny baby, we're *clean for life*. So don't be doin' none a that junk in there—I want you *clean* when you come out. I'm talkin' clean as a whistle..God don't make no dirt..what's that saying? God made dirt, and dirt don't hurt. No wait—I'm thinking."

Lisa hits her head.

"The one I'm thinking of is: God don't make no junk—"

She shakes me.

"Davina, help me. Why don't you loan some of those smarts out to your mommy sometimes? Do you know the expression I'm thinkin' of?"

"No, I'm sorry, but I don't."

"Well you don't have to act all *official* on me. Ron! We gotta go Ron, she's having contractions. I love you, baby—don't you ever forget that."

She waves the torn piece of looseleaf in the air.

"And I'll be thinking about your question!! Stay cool in there!"

As soon as we were out of that courtyard Lisa ripped Ron's marriage proposal into about a thousand pieces. She threw them over her shoulder and they became pieces of litter, to be walked on, to degrade in the rain, to grow thin, to break into even smaller pieces, and ultimately to join the dirt surrounding Darrington State Correctional Center of Darrington, Texas. We never went back to visit Ron. After that day, I never even heard Lisa mention his name.

- - - -

We went to see my brothers and sister in a low government building with chain link fence around its perimeter—from now on, the state would have custody of them. It was a room with tornado-proof glass, and we saw the boys before we saw Jamie. They could hardly hear us as we said goodbye to them. I was tapping on the window and using the sign language we made up as kids, but they must have had them on some kind of psychiatric drugs because they didn't understand a word we were saying.

Then they took the boys away and they must have known what was going on because they were screaming and flailing their arms and trying to escape.

When Jamie came in she was completely composed, silent. She sat in the chair with her legs crossed and her hands folded on top of her knee. We said, "I love you," and "I miss you," and "I can't wait to see you again," and do you believe that bitch sat there the whole time without saying a single word? She didn't change her facial expression—it was blank and she made no eye contact with either of us. When her time was up, she got up and walked away without a word.

That day I went from seeing my brothers and sister every day to hardly seeing them ever again in my life.

That makes me hate my mom like you wouldn't believe.

## 45
After that we literally walked from Texas to California. I was seven months pregnant (at 15 years old). I was so pregnant and so hungry—I swear we never fucking ate.

You have to imagine the walking we did. We walked from the time we woke up to the time we went to sleep. Sometimes we walked at night because the sun was too hot during the day. We slept in the woods, we slept right up against gas stations, we slept in the grass by the side of highways and the sound of trucks zooming beside us first was a nuisance, then became a sort of metronome, rocking us to sleep. When my mother grabbed my hand and said, "Let's go," that meant it was time to *go*—right then. It might have been that she saw someone else walking the road or it might have been that she got shocked awake by all the speed she was doing. Whatever it was, when she said, "go," I went—I followed her like a little puppy.

I gave up asking her about food. We had no money—zero dollars—so there was no point in asking about it.

"There are other options," my mom would say.

"Like what?"

"Like you do a line of speed."

"But my baby."

"Didn't stop you before."

"Ok. I'll do it."

"That's my girl."

A quick note here for any of you who are thinking, "I would never do drugs when I was pregnant." Well, I hope that's true for you. I hope you all have happy pregnancies in fancy hospitals and three-bedroom houses to go to afterward and raise your impeccable Harvard-bound families in relative peace. Just know that's not how it goes for most of us. We don't have money for a doctor—we don't even have money for a cheeseburger. And when you're hungry—truly hungry—all those nice little morals you committed yourself to..they disappear.

But anyway yeah have a nice pregnancy you silver spoon motherfuckers—America's not like that for most of us. I hope your baby's little brain is damaged forever from you eating GMO corn and your whole family gets taken down in an accidental drone attack. Fuck you rich motherfuckers.

So yeah, I did speed when I was pregnant with Ashley. Mom and I knelt by the side of some highway. Mom put some in the needle for herself. She shot the vein. Her eyes rolled back in her head like she was off her fucking *brain* with that shit. Her hands were spasming. Her shoulders went completely limp. She finally came back to reality enough to take the needle out of her arm.

Then she chopped up some powder off a few small rocks and held it in her palm. I covered one side of my nose and did a big fat line out of my mother's hand.

It hit instantly. That feeling of angel wings in your lungs, the lightness all over you. I even imagined my young little Ashley (though I didn't know her name) and I saw that speed penetrating the barriers between me and her and I wondered if undigested speed would hit my baby's little brain and get her high and make her feel not-hungry too. I was too high to feel guilty, just high enough to wish everything good for everyone, even my mom, even my baby. I just wanted us all to be not-hungry.

"You feelin' that, baby?" That's my mom talking to me.

I nod. I actually smile.

"Good, baby, good. We just got a little more walking to do and we'll get there."

"What state are we in?"

"We're still in Texas."

"Mom."

"Yeah."

"Did you do speed when you were pregnant with me?"

Mom sat for a while. Then she said:

"Don't worry about the past—worry about the present. You ready to walk?"

"Yeah?"

So we did. We walked. And walked. And we walked some more.

- - - -

My only goal was sleep: when will we have walked enough that we could sleep? Of course finding a position in the weeds and roots of trees was impossible while I was carrying Ashley. I needed to be in some soft bed in a safe home with extra pillows to prop myself up on. But that never happened. I carried her months seven and eight walking through the American southwest, and as we got into New Mexico there were no trees to sleep under. Just sand, and a vague hope we didn't get bit by rattlesnakes. I never even went to the doctor when I was pregnant—except in California when my water broke and Mom finally agreed to take me to the hospital.

But at night in Texas you could hear the crickets and the wild animals. Sometimes you could feel them crawling so close to you—sniffing you out because *you* were an animal, too. Strange animals from the city lying down with possum and armadillo and crickets and snakes. *They* had babies in the wild—why couldn't I? I felt more at home with those animals than my mom, than any of my brothers or sister, than any of my fathers or stepfathers. I wish I was born with animal skin, skin to protect me from the elements—I would crawl around at night and slurp up snakes like they were spaghetti noodles, let them digest in my stomach. I would rub noses with possum and if I found a big cat I would rub it behind the ears. I mean that was my fantasy—I was 15 years old, Jesus.

- - - -

One day I remember we hitched a ride with this crunchy, vegetarian-type couple in one of the tiniest cars I had seen at the time. It was like a Toyota hatchback with a back seat designed for little green gnomes from the forest—not actual people. Also, we had to move half the couple's stuff (which was filling up the whole back seat) onto one side of the back so that me and Mom could sit there. Mom sat in the seat. I sat on Mom's lap. My pregnant belly was pressed against the passenger seat and sometimes when we'd hit a bump in the road me and my pregnant belly would push the passenger seat forward and the woman occupying it would say:

"Oh!"

"I'm sorry."

"No problem, dear, let me see if I can move this seat forward to help you. Oh, no, I don't think that's going to work. So where are you people from?"

My mom answered: "We're from California but we got zapped by my boyfriend in Texas and we're tryin'a get back home."

"Oh I see. And what do you mean when you say you got 'zapped' by him?"

"Oh, it's a long story. I wouldn't want to bore you."

The husband (or who I assumed to be the husband) was one of these super-cheery positive-thinking types who belonged in a Jimmy Dean commercial selling sausages and croissants with a fresh glass of orange juice. He said:

"We've got nothing but open road and there's never a bad time to hear a good story!"

So Lisa begins: "So like, we're in California and this motherfucker named Red Dog is all up my ass about this grand I owe him. He won't stop at anything. He's visiting the house—and when I say house I mean the shittiest little motel room you can imagine. Name of motel will stay 'nonymous if you don't mind."

"That's ok," the Jimmy Dean guy says. "We don't know many motels in California."

"Well ain't you lucky. Anyway, this motherfucker—this Red Dog—starts harassin' my children. He shows up at this Thanksgiving turkey roast they is havin' for the neighborhood and like..I send my daughter down there to get some turkey and some gravy—"

"Nothing like good-ol' country gravy, right Davina?"

The Jimmy Dean sausage touches my leg. I pull it back.

"Right. So Red Dog is everywhere. I can't even send my kid to the park to get our Thanksgiving meal—this motherfucker is *serving* the meal. Now you have to understand—and I'm telling you this in complete confidence—but Red Dog, or whatever his real name is, is a *serious* drug dealer. The kind'll have you killed if you fuck up—does y'all know what I'm saying?"

"Oh yes, dear."

"Absolutely, Miss Lisa."

"So I'm sittin' in this hotel room in San Bernardino and I says: I gotta get these kids out of danger. I can't even stay in this goddamn *state*, if you read me."

"Loud and clear, Miss Lisa. Loud and clear."

"Right. So the lot of us go to Texas."

"And who all is this?" the woman asks.

"My whole family. Don't ask me all their names 'cause I'm a little off my rocker, if you know what I mean."

"You've got mental problems, sure, sure," the man says.

Lisa hits him in the back of the head.

"No I ain't got no mental problems. I'm high off the rock, if you take my meaning."

"Oh," Jimmy Dean says, "you mean you're high off the crack rock."

Lisa hits him again. The car swerves a little into the other lane.

"Now you're just showing your ignorance," Lisa says. "Do I look like I smoke crack?"

"No, I was just guessing—"

"He was just guessing," she woman says.

"Well if you want to guess, why don't you guess about something important, like where you're going to get my daughter some food. She *is* pregnant, you know."

This couple was so agreeable. Either they were scared for their lives and they didn't show it or they were Christians getting high off some do-gooder deed. We drove through the window of a Whataburger and these people bought us one of everything on the menu, I swear. Me and Mom were like:

"And a chocolate shake."

"And a bowl of chili."

"Also, on that second burger, lots of jalapeños if it's not too late."

"And we need some Cokes—the largest kind you have—caffeine and sugar, baby, we're gonna get your baby started *right*! Too bad we can't just IV this right in your bloodstream."

Mom and I look at each other for a second..*naaaaaah*.

We spent like five times this couple's daily food allowance. They seemed happy with helping us out and we were just entering New Mexico when my baby started kicking. Everyone felt my belly and we were all one big happy family until James Dean exited us all off the highway into some podunk town called Hope, New Mexico.

I asked my mom, "Where are we going?"

"I don't know. Let me handle this."

She interrogated them and at her queries they told all.

Sure enough those motherfuckers *were* Christians—and the very worst kind. They weren't pentecostal but they believed in hell and damnation and sin and not having sex before marriage and all that crap. They pulled into this gravel parking lot in front of some white building with a steeple and giant red lettering that said, "THE MISSION."

The Jimmy Dean/James Dean guy had his hands held out to us like as soon as we got out of the car he was going to baptize us in the gravel parking lot. This is around dusk.

"We truly *are* blessed to have found Davina and Lisa on the road today. We offer them sanctuary in our modest rooms and a time of cleansing when they can detoxify themselves—body and spirit Oh God."

My mom let me off her lap and she took all the uneaten Whataburger scraps she could find.

"We even offer protection for Davina's unborn baby, that she be born healthy and right—"

"Don't bless my baby," I say.

Mom says, "Davina, be nice."

"We know her baby's tiny brain has been through *turmoil*, Dear God, and we ask you to—"

"DON'T TALK ABOUT MY BABY!!"

The man stopped talking.

Mom comes out of the back of the car with all that's left from Whataburger. She can hardly carry it but she managed to get a couple of sodas.

"The Lord blesses you on your journey. We are truly blessed to have the three of you—"

I step forward.

"You say another word about my baby and I will kill you between where you stand right now and that ugly-ass building behind you."

The preacher opens his mouth.

"Uh!" my mom says. "I'd believe her. When that girl gets an idea in her head, she *will* carry it through. My best advice for *you* is turn around, go on into your little mission, and pray that we don't come back here later tonight to kill both you and your wife in your sleep."

"She's not my wife."

"Well that don't make no difference! When you're giving someone a ride, you're just giving them a ride—not trying to convert us into some foul religion. Think about what you did: you set a *trap*. You trapped us and brought us here. Fuck your mission."

It was one of the coolest things I ever heard my mom say. But don't think that makes up for all the fucked-up things she *did*. I still hate that bitch with a vengeance.

"Come on, 'Vina. We don't want no trouble."

I realized that I was walking toward the priest.

"You know what you remind me of?—since the first moment I met you!?"

"What?" he says.

"You're like one of those guys on TV who sells Jimmy Dean sausage." Here I laughed like a maniac. "You know how everyone in those commercials acts so pathetic and tired 'cause they haven't had their Jimmy Dean sausage yet? That's what you are: you're tired, you're pathetic, you're out of gas. And every religious person I've ever met was either tired like you or a liar. I don't know which is worse."

I turned my back on that lazy, yawning, pathetic Jimmy Dean sausage of a man and walked toward Mom. Of course I flipped him a middle finger over my shoulder. And I hoped he was watching, too. But by then he had probably turned his back on us and headed to "THE MISSION," which as far as I could tell, wasn't helping anyone tonight.

We walked out of Hope, New Mexico that night. Then we walked, and walked, and walked. We were careful not to get picked up by any religious fanatics. In fact we refused what may have been perfectly safe rides just because someone had the wrong bumper sticker on their car. Anything that indicated religious or political persuasion was a no-go—we ignored liberals, conservatives, libertarians, and atheists alike. Better to die in the desert sun than listen to any of them!

- - - -

I thought about my baby—I thought about her every step. I hoped she was comfortable in there and that she'd make it back to California and not be born on the side of the road. Sometimes I'd talk to her or sing her little songs. I thought she was gonna be alright.

Mom walked ahead of me and I walked behind. We walked right on the side of the highway, right next to the cars.

One of my strongest memories of my mother is of her filing her nails as we walked through the desert. She was constantly high on meth—she got to where she could shoot up *while* we were walking—we didn't even have to slow down. The whole time we walked, she was tweaking and working on her nails. She didn't even look at the road—she had all that obsessive meth energy focused on her nails. She'd press on these ridiculously long fingernails. But she didn't use the adhesive that came with 'em—she Super Glued those motherfuckers. Then she painted 'em these dark blood colors. We'd stop in little towns and Mom would sniff out the drug people. She sold them some meth and she and I would have enough money to go to eclectic little hippie craft shops where they're smoking pot behind the counter. She'd buy craft supplies. Then we'd walk into a McDonald's and buy cheeseburgers off the Dollar Menu. I'd want to sit down and eat, me and the baby. Maybe we'd find someone at this McDonald's who could take us farther down the road. But Mom was like:

"'Vina. We got to go. You can eat 'em on the way."

So I followed her out of the air conditioning of the McDonald's back onto the side of the road where we walked, where we sweated, where we were just lost motherfucking souls.

I threw my burger wrappers on the asphalt. Give me a cafeteria I can eat in, give me a fast-food restaurant even, or give me my own kitchen and I'll keep that fucker clean, clean, clean—I'll always throw my trash away in the *designated receptacle*. But if this is my life—walking the desert, sweating out all the water this baby needs to survive, following a mom who isn't even feeling this pain 'cause she's high every hour of every motherfucking *day*—then yeah I think I'll throw my trash on the ground.

I could tell you more about the next three days but it was all the same: walking, stopping anywhere we could to get water, sleeping in the sand on the side of the road, pissing and shitting just over the cattle fence where everyone in every car passing by could see us. Thousands of cars just speeding by with their air conditioners, radios, adjustable seats—just cruising the American highways as fast as they wanted. No one stopped to help us. *Cops* drove past us. People with Jesus stickers on their cars drove past us. I even saw a fucking limousine.

But I learned quick not to find joy in self-pity. I was determined to have that baby in California, in a nice hospital bed with trained doctors and nurses there to make everything go right. So I held my head high and let the sweat drip down through my hair and every time I took a step I said, "I'm gonna walk this baby to California." I said it out loud, too. My mother must have heard it but I doubt it registered. She was too busy shooting crystal methamphetamine into every imaginable part of her body and working various craft projects—necklaces, bracelets, earrings—just like repairing and modifying jewelry that she stole.

- - - -

A trucker picked us up somewhere in Arizona. Mom sat in the front and I was happy to stretch out in this bed thing in the back.

"So how's you too related?"

"Mother and daughter, what does it look like?" my mom said, cranky off the meth.

"Mother and daughter. That's real nice, real nice."

Mom looked at me and rolled her eyes.

"So how long you been travelin'?"

"Since Texas."

"Oh. Why'd you go to Texas?"

"Do you really want to know or are you just makin' conversation? 'Cause if you really want to know I'll tell ya—but if you're just makin' conversation, then why don't we let my daughter sleep. We been on this road for a long long time and you're the first decent person to pick us up so if you want to make conversation, I'll make conversation with you but if you don't mind *let's keep our voices very very quiet so my daughter can sleep—she's pregnant if you didn't see*. So what the fuck question did you ask me?"

"Why you went to Texas."

"Oh, right."

I got myself comfortable in the bed. It was night when he picked us up.

"We went to Texas to avoid the law."

"Well, then, you're my kind of people," the driver said.

"What do you mean by that?"

"I just mean that sometimes I dally in areas that might be *slightly* outside of what is considered perfectly legal by this government."

"Like what?" my mom said. "Sex? Drugs? You kill people?"

"Kill people? I would never do anything like that."

"Can you tell I'm high?" my mom said.

"What are you high off?"

"Crystal," my mom said, and she laughed her head off.

"You mean meth?"

"Yeah, crystal meth. You wanna try some?"

"Oh, I would have to decline."

He had a southern accent: Louisiana.

"Your choice, buddy. I'm just trying to make this worth your while."

"What do you mean?"

He talked slow and steady, which some people would interpret as stupidity, but it wasn't.

My mom leaned over and undid his belt.

"What are you doing now, little lady?"

"Giving you a blowjob."

She unzips his pants.

"I'm gonna have to stop you there, my new companion."

"Why? What? You don't want your dick sucked?"

"No ma'am I do not."

"Are you queer? Faggot? Got a small dick? Herpes? I don't care."

"It's none of those. I just—I got a woman back in Ruston and if she found out I got a blowjob from a whore I think she'd break it off with me."

"First of all, I'm not a whore. I'm just a woman offering you a BJ in exchange for a ride so me and my pregnant daughter aren't walking along the edge of a highway all night. Second of all, HOW IN THE FUCK IS YOUR WOMAN GONNA KNOW??"

"Oh she'll read my eyes, I reckon."

"You gotta learn to be a better poker player, buddy."

My mom zipped this guy's pants up and sat straight in her own seat. Eventually she leaned against the window and fell asleep. I stayed awake. This truck driver ignored my mom but every once in a while he'd turn around and sneak these peeks at me. At first I'd close my eyes real quick so he'd think I was sleeping but after a while I kept them open so *he* knew..that *I* knew..that he was looking at me.

- - - -

We rode with him for hours before he said anything.

"Hey. You. Kid."

"I'm about to make my mother a grandmother so I don't think 'kid' applies anymore."

"What's your name?"

"It's Isabel."

"Isabel, will you do something for me?"

"Depends."

"Are you wearing any panties?"

"No, I don't happen to be wearing any panties."

"You ain't smearing shit and baby blood all over my cab, are you?"

"No! My ass is clean, since you asked, and there's no 'baby blood' until the baby comes *out*. At least that's what I understand."

"You ain't never had no baby before?"

"No."

"Me either."

"So what do you want me to do? Finger my asshole with three fingers, pull out some shit and lick it off? Fuck my pregnant vagina with the handle of one of these screwdrivers you got back here? You got a thing for pregnant chicks? Pregnant kids? You want me to make myself cum and squirt all over your bed so you can smell it when you go to sleep at night, and you can get yourself off knowing that a *fifteen-year-old pregnant girl* got herself off in your sheets? That get you off?"

The driver shook his head.

"You two are a couple of psychopaths."

"We're not psychopaths—look it up in the dictionary."

"There is something I want to see."

"And what is that?"

"I wanna see you and your mom have sex with each other."

"What, like fingering, kissing, 69ing—what are you into?"

"Anything, anything. She's your real mom, right?"

"Yes unfortunately she is."

"Well?"

"Well what? I guess you better wake her up."

"I'm not waking her up."

"Oh yes you are. If you think I'm waking up my mom to tell her that even though you passed on her earlier offer of mind-blowing oral sex, that you now want us to fulfill your mother-daughter fantasies while driving 70 miles per hour down some anonymous Arizona highway, forget it. You wake her up."

So this guy pokes my mom gently in the shoulder.

"You're going to have to go harder than that, mister."

So he pokes harder.

"Dude, she's been awake for like five days from shooting meth. Do you know how hard it is to wake someone up from a meth nap?"

So this idiot truck driver punches my mother in the arm.

She instantly wakes up.

"WHAT THE FUCK, MAN?!"

He swerves the truck but there's no one else on the road.

"WHAT THE FUCK DO YOU WANT?!"

"Well, Isabel suggested—"

"Who is Isabel?"

"Your daughter?"

"Oh! Isabel! Of course. My baby Isabella—I like to call her that sometimes—what did Isabel suggest?"

"It's prob'ly better that she tell it."

"No way dude. It was your idea—*you* tell it."

"Well," this guy says, "I'd kinda like to see..you know..you and Isabella..you know..like..maybe you could get back there in the bed and maybe..like..do stuff."

I hated hearing this guy talk. He was like an old hat that someone had stepped on.

"You wanna see me and my daughter have sex?"

"Um..yeah."

"While she's pregnant?"

"I don't know! I don't know! It was just an idea!"

"Just an idea, huh? Your idea *sucks*. You refuse to let me blow you (which I take as a personal insult by the way). You refuse a blow job and now is *this* the deal? You won't give us a ride unless I have *sex* with my daughter?"

"No, that's not the deal. But I'll go farther."

"If I have sex with my daughter you'll take us *farther*?"

"Right."

"What's your name?"

"Todd."

"Well, *Todd*, I would like you to know that I have met some slimy motherfuckers in my trips around the sun but you and your Louisiana accent, your stupid over-greased, *thinning* hair—you know what comes after thinning, don't you? Balding. So this pre-balding, slimy, incestuous truck driver who wants me to fuck my pregnant daughter so you can get off while you drive your stupid little truck down the road—and don't think I ain't notice what kind of truck you was drivin' when you picked us up. A *Walmart* truck. You musta sold your soul to drive for them..huh? Or was this the only job you could get? Try being a *man*—try driving a *logging* truck or one of those wide loads that carries pieces of the *space shuttle*. No, you couldn't hack it, so you're driving peaches and ramen noodles and Real^®^ American Cheese from Santa Fe to LA to Santa Fe to LA. Loser. I'll give you one more offer and then we're gettin' off this fuckin' truck. Pull over, you and me go to the bed in the back, Isabella waits in the front, and you can fuck my ass. Then you drive us *all the way* to LA, with no more favors. What do you think of that?"

"It's no good."

"*Why* is it no good."

"Has to be the pussy, not the ass."

"What's wrong, you scared of a little ass fucking?"

"I'm not scared, I just prefer the pussy."

"Well look who's picky now—he just prefers the pussy. Did you have some bad experiences as a child with the ass? Daddy fuck you there? Brothers fuck you there? You got older brothers?"

Todd swerves the Walmart truck to the side of the road, bringing us to a scarily immediate stop.

"Get out," he says.

"Todd."

"Get. Out."

"Look, I'm sorry if I offended you with the 'big brother' thing but it's ok, Todd, a lot of us was raped by broom handles and Coke bottles and big brothers with big dicks. Did they hurt you?"

"Lady, I have a shotgun set against the door near my left leg and I am telling you that if you and your daughter don't *shut your mouths* and get out of my truck it will not end well for you."

Neither of us made a move.

"It will not end well for you," he repeated.

Mom got down to the gravel and helped me make the step down so she and I were standing side by side. Mom kicked his door shut and his faggot-ass Walmart truck chugged itself back on the road.

This is nighttime. The only light is from the stars.

Mom and I kept walking toward California until we found an underpass. We didn't even talk about sleeping there—we both just knew that we would. In the city there would be other people sleeping in the same spot—out here we had the peace of knowing we were the only humans around.

We went up the concrete to the very top. There's a place there where it flattens out where you can sleep without rolling all the way down the incline. It's scary 'cause you're closer to the noise from the road above—it's so loud that you wake up thinking you're going to get run over. The other advantage of sleeping at that part of the underpass is if someone else does come along, you're above them and they're below you—the classic advantage of high ground.

"Do you think that guy had a gun?" I ask, after we're both settled in our sleeping positions.

"I don't know," my mom says. "Works either way."

That was one of the worst nights of my life sleep-wise. I kept adjusting and readjusting and with the baby I was never quite right. I looked at my mom for help but she was in a deep sleep, snoring and everything. I wondered how many times she had slept under bridges.

My sleep came in increments: 15 minutes, half an hour, an hour. During one of those sleeps, in dirty-ass concrete under this highway, I had one of the most memorable dreams of my life.

- - - -

In the dream, I was working at McDonald's. I was standing in the lobby. A lot of people were waiting in line. I was watching this lady, a mom, who had a five-year-old girl with her. They were talking happily—they looked at each other so intently, you could tell they were really present in each other's conversation. The little girl went to the bathroom while her mom stayed in line.

The I felt someone hug me down around my legs and all of a sudden I was filled—my whole body—with this incredible bright light and this intense feeling of love and adoration.

I never felt so loved in my entire life.

This was a *consuming* feeling.

I lift up out of my body.

I look down—

—and it's the little girl.

*That's* who's hugging me.

The mom comes over and apologizes.

The mom says, "I'm so sorry. She's blind, you see, and she thought that you were me."

## 46
I woke up to the realities of the underpass: dust that felt like spider webs, the springs from a mattress (not the mattress, just the *springs* from a mattress), old clothes so beaten they could never be worn again (not even by homeless people). I woke up in a bed of junk food wrappers—Doritos, Combos, Fritos, Ho Hos—every one of them empty. There were styrofoam cups, empty 40 ouncers. If there had been a drop of beer in any of them, I would have taken a sip. But those bottles had dried up long ago.

My mom didn't say anything. She stood up and helped me up and we started walking. I guess that's something she and I have in common. Not all mothers have it. But when I came, my mother just *knew*. If you want the truth, it made me feel like *god*—like one of the gods who sometimes come to Earth and takes the shape of one of us but instead of sitting on the couch watching cable eight hours a night, these people change the world. Sometimes it's in small ways, like just feeding one person when that one person is hungry. And sometimes it's in big ways, like being martyred for leading a revolution that will make all people free.

Maybe you'll chalk that up to me being crazy (which I am). Maybe you'll chalk it up to me having done some drugs (which I have). But that little blind girl in the McDonald's of my dreams carries more love than anyone on this planet can *imagine*. She's my little Matrix girl, my love Oracle. All throughout my life, when I've felt low, I think about that dream and that little girl who hugged me, and thinking of that makes me feel better.

And as the years have gone by, the most fascinating thing about that dream is that, presumably, it came from me. This little girl with Tesla watts of love is a figment of my imagination—a figment of my mind—a figment of me. I made that little girl up because I needed a hug—a love hug. It wasn't coming from Lisa—so it had to come from me. My sleeping mind wasn't going to let me die that night under that underpass—no. My sleeping mind that night gave me a symbol I would never forget no matter what happened to me my whole life.

A little blind girl, hugging me like she thought she was hugging her mother—and this symbol given to me before I birthed my first child. That little girl's hug gave me such a powerful feeling of *acceptance*, that I swear to you, in that moment, I became just a smidgen more unstoppable.

I thought of that, as we walked the highway that night. What if a truck swerves and hits us—don't worry about it, I'm unstoppable. What if there's a snake in our path—don't worry about it, I'm unstoppable. What if the baby comes too soon? What if we literally dehydrate ourselves on the walk tomorrow? What if Mom leaves me, just gets in some stranger's car and goes somewhere without me?

Unstoppable. Unstoppable. Unstoppable.

*I am an unstoppable force.*

I let my mind play with that idea—which I wasn't even sure was true. But it struck me as a very good thing to think *even if it wasn't true.*

We only walked till eight before a guy in a super-hot sports car picked us up. It might have been a Lotus. Look, it might have been a Lamborghini for all I know—I don't know cars. We got to this guy's diner which was waaay out of Phoenix and not that crowded. I caught this waitress looking at me extricate myself and my pregnant belly out of the very small front seat of this sports car. She treated us nice when we got inside though. My baby was so hungry I bypassed the menu and invented this hamburger that had three half-pound patties of flame-broiled beef, seasoned with black pepper, red pepper, and Himalayan sea salt. Between two of the patties was to be a slice of Swiss cheese. Between the other two patties was to be a slice of muenster. The burger was to be liberally topped with sliced jalapeños and my baby had requested that it be served not on a bun but rather on sliced and toasted Texas toast.

Our waitress had red hair. She was writing down everything I said.

I looked at the waitress and also the guy who was supposedly paying for all this—my thanks was for both of them.

"Not sure about the Himalayan sea salt—"

"Yes, it has to be the pink kind. Not white. Not red."

"Well, like I was saying, I'm not even sure we *have* Himalayan sea salt—of any color. So if I can't find that, do you still want the triple burger?"

"Yes."

I nodded.

Then everyone else ordered, the three of us talked about things that only hitchhikers know about, our food came, I finished off my triple burger for two—every speck was gone from my plate—and we paid and got out of there. My mom even offered to leave the tip with the one dollar bill she had in her position but the guy we were with said:

She handed Lamborghini guy her one, thin, cloth-like dollar bill and our man handed it to the waitress while he paid our bill and the rest of the tip with his American Express *Black* card. Me and the baby looked up and wondered what the limit was on an American Express Black card—my guess was like five-hundred thousand dollars. I wondered how much he added to my mom's one-dollar tip but I couldn't see from where I was standing.

The three of us stepped outside the diner. Immediately the Arizona sun was on our skin, little beads of sweat appearing everywhere. The Lamborghini was one of the only cars in the parking lot, and I guess the question was: *were we getting in it?*

"It was the most delicious hamburger I ever had in my life."

I lifted my shirt. And this man, this sports car-driving man, this multi-hitchhiking man, felt my belly the way a woman wishes the dad of her baby would do. It was like the caress of skin you see in a soap commercial.

When we got back to San Bernardino I guess our credit was bad—there were no more week-long luxury stays in the Foothill Motel. We stayed a night here, a night there—sometimes we were homeless; sometimes, for a few hours, we were not.

One time we went to this apartment with a bunch of people. It was almost like a waiting room atmosphere—there are people on the couch, people standing. Mom was talking to someone at the table and I leaned up against a wall, watching everyone.

A guy comes in the door, nervous looking. He walked in and (I think) got confused that there were so many people. He pulled a gun out of his waist and waved it around, pointing it at people in the room. He was saying something but it made no sense—yet he was angry at the same time. "Um, excuse me. Excuse me. What's your name?"

"Brad."

He said it like it took the same energy as it would to move a ton of bricks.

Now Brad is pointing the gun at my mom.

"So Brad. I need to make a call. Do you think it might be ok if I used the phone?"

Everyone else in the room is scared shitless, not making a move.

"What's it gonna be? Can I make my call or what?"

"Sure. Go ahead."

"Ok, great—and you're not gonna shoot me or anything? Tha'd be great."

Lisa's hand dips into her back pocket.

She shows him her address book—it's battered, spine broke, kept together with a rubber band.

The gunman loses interest in my mom and she goes to make her phone call. Then the man looks at each person in the room, and when he looks at you, he points his gun at you, myself included.

When my mom hung up the phone she went downstairs and I followed her. I was slow due to being eight months pregnant at the time. We went along the sidewalk with a quickness.

Mom was like, "Oh my gosh I don't believe that just happened—that was so scary!"

And it was: scary, weird, tense, and as surreal a moment as there is.

"The police," I said. "So they can come get that guy."

My mom busted out laughing in her hideous, hateful, just derisive as a motherfucker sort of way.

Lisa laughed again.

"Then who did you call?"
