"We're not a couple."

"There are two of us—is that not a couple?"

"Hoss I'm not having sex with you in a Port-a-John!"

"Well where then?"

"Nowhere."

"I wish you would let me finish telling you about the magic nature of Port-a-Johns."

"Can you let me out of here and *then* tell me about the magic?"

"No, I'm afraid you would run away."

"You're right, I would."

"So anyway," Hoss says, "when I was in the Army there was this cute little private who bunked across the hall in basic."

"Basic training."

"Yes. So all day I would see this private, in uniform, and you know there's nothing sexier than a US Army private in uniform."

"Maybe a Marine, but yeah, I got you."

"So at night this private would be undressing and I couldn't take my eyes off—"

"Wait a minute. Guys and girls in the same bunk? Bullshit."

"I never said it was a she. So we used to stand where we could see each other while we were undressing at night and then this one time we were outside being PT'd by this fuckin' slave-driving motherfucker of a drill sergeant—"

"PT is physical therapy, right?"

"Physical training, but yeah. I'll never forget that man's name. It was Leon, but he let me call him Leona which I never told him why but I used to have a crush on this little girl named Leona in church youth group so when I was fucking Leon up the ass—and this is with the drill sergeant right outside the Port-a-John calling the shots like *one and two and one and two* and I was fucking Leon in the ass like *one and two and one and two* and I was thinking of Leon but I was also thinking of that little girl Leona from church group who had one of her hands burned in a fire when she was young and she had scars all over her fingers and the scar ran partway down her arm. It's weird, but back when we were that age nobody would play with Leona because of her scar but to me that scar was the coolest thing and she was—like—a hero in my mind. I never did get to fuck her—at that age we were both too shy. But I got to fuck Leon—we fucked every day in that Port-a-John and believe me it was a whole lot nastier and a whole lot more fucked up with flies and shit and the toilet seat broken..a whole lot more fucked up. Try eating Army food for nine months—see how your Port-a-John stinks then!"

I was dizzy.

I said, "Hoss, will you please let me out of here?"

"Ok, since you're pregnant, I'll give you this one pass."

"Thank you."

"We don't say 'thank you' in the Army. Don't say 'please' either. You just do what you're told. Somebody saves your life, you don't thank them—they were just doin' their job."

Hoss takes a step back. As soon as he's out of the way I step down out of the porta-potty and head for the campsite. Hoss runs up next to me and puts his arm around my shoulder.

"The crux of what I'm saying to you, Davina, the *linchpin*, is that someday you'll be a little bit older, you'll be a mother, a few more watermelons will have gone down your spout—you know what I mean? I'm just saying that youth doesn't last forever and, when you're older, sex won't be as available to you then as it is now. I'm not forcing you to do anything—am I? No. All I'm saying is: while you're young, you should appreciate being able to have sex anytime you want, right then and there. You feel me?"

I didn't even respond. There was to be no fucking tonight. Not by the horse paddock, not in the porta-potty, not in anyone's tent. I was crabby and I wanted to go to bed.

- - - -

The next day when Hoss saw me, he said, "You missed out last night."

Yeah, missed out on getting fucked in the ass while you pull my hair and call me Leona, all to the delicious smell of other people's weeks-old shit and urine sprayed over the walls and toilet seat—and cap that off with an orchestra of flies and mosquitoes landing on my back and hands and arms and legs and my butt. I'd be itching for weeks as a reminder of me and Hoss's rendezvous that luckily never happened.

I did ask Hoss a question, something I had wondered about since yesterday:

"What happened to you and Leon?"

"What?"

"The guy you were fucking in the Port-a-John during PT."

"Oh. We got away with it a few times. Then some ass-wipe private tells the drill sergeant what's up. The door to the Port-a-John was locked, but that drill sergeant ripped it off like he was taking the next tissue out of a tissue box. The whole platoon saw. Me standing. Leon bent over the toilet fixture. My dick up Leon's ass."

"So wha'd they do?"

"They kicked us out."

"Of the Port-O-Let?"

"No, Davina, of the Army."

That's the last thing Hoss ever said to me. The Hell's Angels moved on and Mom and I had to find a new place to go. Before they left I saw Hoss and my mom flirting very physically—Hoss put his hand down the front of my mom's jeans and she was like cupping his dick through his pants. Hoss didn't care who he fucked: pregnant teenager..pregnant teenager's mom—whatever. They slipped inside *our* tent that *we* brought and I can only assume they "did the deed," involving every hole of hers and every protrusion of his—and possibly the other way around. All I know for sure is when Hoss and Mom finished their business there was cum on *my* sleeping bag and I was definitely not alright with that. Picture me screaming, holding my sleeping bag over my head, yelling at Hoss as him and the rest of the Hell's Angels rode off in a cloud of dust. He didn't hear a word I said, which is a very very good thing because what I said was: "CUM ON YOUR OWN SLEEPING BAG YOU NORDIC FUCK!!" I mean look at this guy: he fucks my mom, pulls out 'cause he's too pussy to risk having a child with my insane mother, and he squirts it on *my* fucking sleeping bag? The guy is a menace to society.

"I wonder how many girls he's raped in porta-potties."

Apparently I said that out loud because my mom asks me what I'm talking about.

"Nothing..nothing," I say. "So where are we staying tonight?"

"Are you worried, my little 'Vina?"

"Yes, I kinda am."

"Don't we always have a place to stay?"

"Yes."

"Then why are you worried?"

She cups my face in her hands but I pull away.

"Yeah we always have a place to stay but it's always last minute. It's always for one or two nights."

"Your worry is natural," she says, like she's a doctor or a fuckin' university lecturer. "Because you have a baby to think about. You're pregnant, you're in love—"

"*In love?!* What are you talking about?"

"Charles! Eddie! Charles Edward Brown, the father of your baby!!"

"We're not in love. He kicked me in my stomach so I'd have a natural abortion!"

"Davina what are you talking about, 'a natural abortion?' "

"He kicked me in the stomach so my baby would die."

"It must have been an accident."

"It was hard, and it was multiple times, so it wasn't no accident!"

"Men do silly things sometimes."

"Mom. Where are we gonna stay tonight. That's three of us: you, me, and the baby."

"Have you thought up names for what to call him if it's a boy?"

"Mom. Try to focus. We're homeless. I'm about to have a baby."

"We are *not homeless*. Anyway, having a baby isn't that hard—you'll find that out once you have your first one. Everybody makes a big deal about the hospital and getting the right doctor—the doctor doesn't do anything useful except say "push." Anybody can do that—Charles Edward Brown can do *that*."

I listened to my mom go on and on about how Aborigines just squat in the bush, Africans just squat in the bush..so how come *Americans* think we're so special that we need hospitals and ambulances and doctors and nurses and all kinds of *specialists* just to have a god damn baby!

I wanted to tell her: *This is my first time and I'm nervous about how it's gonna go*. I wanted some comfort from her but I knew I wouldn't get it. It was like trying to get spring water from the tap—it's just not going to happen. No matter how many times I tried with her, I could never get a connection—you know: love, empathy, any of those emotions that normal people feel. Most of my life I thought it was directed toward *me*. But after a couple of decades I now know that her behavior had nothing to do with me. I wasn't the cause of it. I wasn't to blame for it. She just doesn't have the emotional strength to give much of anything to anyone.

## 49
One thing she did have a lot of was contacts. Drug contacts. And one great thing about drug contacts is they'll usually let you crash for a couple of nights. Or sometimes a couple of months.

After Mom and I packed our stuff we walked out of the campsite and Mom found a payphone. Within an hour someone picked us up and by that night me and Lisa were sleeping in a tiny camper in some lady's yard.

It was a drug house, by which I mean that drugs were bought and sold there..primarily to people this lady, the owner, knew. And it being a drug house (perhaps obviously) meant that it was ok to do drugs there. The owner didn't mind. And even yet another thing that this being a drug house meant, is that everybody who lived there, and most of the people who came there to buy, were spending waaaaaay too much of their money on drugs, thereby leaving them less money to—oh, say—feed their kids. You've seen this earlier in my story. Now I saw it in Timmy's story.

Timmy was one of the kids who lived at this drug house. Timmy was..I'm guessing..seven. I have no idea if or how he was related to my mom's friend whose house it was, but he might have been necessary to keep around because he was helping the finances by being a dependent of somebody. (Remember, I'm fifteen at this time so I don't know much about tax law.) The best I could tell, the lady who owned the house was helping out Timmy's mom by letting Timmy live here while Timmy's mom was shooting crack somewhere.

Mostly Timmy was an annoyance to me. He would make up songs including my name that insulted me by calling me a whore for having a baby at 15 and his lyrics were all these pathetic little couplets and that's what drove me crazy the most. Have you ever been woken up to the sound of a seven-year-old child singing insults to you in *couplets*? If you haven't, give it a try sometime. Let me know your results. But you won't be able to because you'll be in an insane asylum!

So day after day, week after week, I'd be hiding out in this tiny camper, by myself (Mom was always going on errands), and my only company is this little piss rat of a boy who would throw things at me whether I was in or out of the camper—stuff like a Barbie with a mechanical pencil up her cunt. Or a Combos bag with a rock in it. He would push sticks at the back of my shorts—I'm assuming his mission was to locate my asshole. It bugged the shit out of me that this seven year old had figured out that he had the man up on me because he lived there and I was the guest so he had more power than me with the lady of the house.

- - - -

One day me and mom wake up to Timmy's degrading couplets and Mom says:

"Want to go shopping?"

"YES!" I said.

I even hugged her, which is rare.

So Mom and I go on this all-day shopping trip, walking the entire time..we were so sweaty but it didn't matter to me because we were *away from the fucking house*.

We walked across the highway and Mom took me to the Levi's store. She said I could pick out one thing—only one—and she would pay for whatever it was.

I went up and down every aisle.

I asked the cashier, "Do you have any new stock that I might be able to look at before I make my decision?"

"New stock?" she asks.

This is a typical American white girl: red hair, clothes that look like a Catholic school girl uniform even though she's never attended a day of Catholic school in her life, and a look on her face that says *I am better than you.*.in every way that a look can say that. This girl isn't that much older than me—I could work at this store.

"Yes," I say. "New stock. Any new items that haven't been put on display."

She looks like I just asked her to recite the quadratic equation or explain general relativity or something.

"Well," she says, "if they're new items, that's why we have them in the back..I guess."

I'm pretty sure this girl isn't going to get into her first choice of colleges. I ask her where the shoes are and she points. I go to the shoes. I'm above getting into it with that bitch. I know she's going to have a better life than me because she's white with red hair and so many freckles. A good college probably would make an exception for her based on the freckles alone, and that dumb-ass bitch will get into college but I won't. I wonder how many times that bitch has been raped.

Mom is waiting in the shoes. She's holding up two pairs of moccasins. They're knee high. One pair is black, one pair is brown.

"Oh. My. God! They're perfect!"

"I know. There's just one question!"

"What is it?"

"Do you want black or brown?"

"I want brown!" I say, and I'm sure that redhead bitch cashier can hear me.

That redhead bitch's voice is inside my head singing, "You got pregnant at fifteenwhen I got an abortion!" Then she laughs and laughs and laughs and it's all in my head except the part where I'm in the shoe section of the Levi's store laughing about the fact that Mom and I each got our color. I got brown, she got black—the world is in order.

We wore our moccasins to the register.

"Do you want me to box those up?" the redhead asks.

"No, we're gonna wear these out."

Mom put her old shoes on the counter. Then I did.

The redhead had to box up our old stinky shoes but—and I swear to god this is true—that bitch and I made eye contact the entire time she was doing it. *Look who's laughing now, bitch!*

Right outside the store was a mailbox. My mom balanced her old shoebox on its top.

"Put your old ones up here," she said.

I did. We left both our old pairs of shoes on top of that mailbox and we walked all the way home wearing our new moccasins. I never needed to ask why we left our old shoes behind. That had been my mom's tradition forever. If you have money for shoes, you leave your old ones behind for someone who doesn't.

It wasn't so bad when Mom was around, but when she left that night to go on errands and excursions and job and trips and all the many things she called them..it got worse. It was that little bitch redhead's voice, in my head, and it was like I knew exactly what she would say to me if no one else was listening. She would rub it in that my mother had to pay for our shoes in cash, instead of with a credit card. She even made fun of the fact that our quarters were old—not shiny—and that our dollar bills were ones—not twenties—and that the corners of some of them had gone missing.

It made me remember being with Mom in a corner store, buying groceries with change. We bought eggs, mayonnaise, penne pasta, and frozen orange juice. That was for the whole week. And we paid exclusively in coins, and the cashier glared at us throughout the process like we were the scum of the earth.

That's how the rich people are in this country—they act like they *did something* to deserve to be rich. Bitch, you ain't done nothin' but be born to a rich daddy.

In that camper that night, I slept with my moccasins. The outside had velvety part and smooth parts, the laces were leather, and they had the most excellent traction—perfect for city walking or hiking in the country.

I called that redhead bitch Rebecca. And it seemed like Rebecca and the corner store cashier *knew each other*, because I could hear those two talking to each other. She was white, he was Indian, and they were both higher class than me because of it. Americans aren't racist towards Indians because if they got to this country they had to have *money* to get to this country—and it's hard to be racist toward people who have more money than you. It's the same thing with black people: a white person will kiss a black football player's *ass*—if he's good. It's not the rich niggers these neo-KKK motherfuckers have a problem with. It's the ones with no education and no jobs begging for change outside a gas station. But I guess where contemporary white racists maybe have a little gap in their thinking is it's kinda hard to go to college when you were born in a crack house.

Rebecca and that corner store cashier had something like a channel in my brain where they could speak to each other and I could overhear them. They talked about matters of race and class and even some things that I didn't understand but I heard them inside my *head*, not through my *ears*. If I heard them through my ears I think that's schizophrenia, but in my case it's just a bit of paranoia and a busy, busy mind that sometimes makes it hard to go to sleep.

Eventually I did.

- - - -

I woke to the sound of the camper door opening and by the porch light I could see it was Timmy—the seven-year-old crack house kid who if he was black he would look like a starving Somalian?—yeah, that kid. Here he comes, jaunting into the camper and putting his hands on my moccasins (that were still in bed with me).

I *rip* those motherfuckers out of his hands.

"I *want* them. I *want* them," he says.

I hit him in the face with the back of my hand so hard he fell into the camper door, his force *opened* the camper door, and his poor little malnourished ass landed in the dirt. I reached over, pulled the door closed, and locked it.

Timmy said, "I'm telling my mom."

I said, "Timmy, how many front teeth do you have left?"

"Two."

"Wake me up again, you'll have one."

He started walking away but I was so angry, not even about him, but about everything, that I went one step further with that pathetic little kid.

"Timmy."

"What?"

"I'm gonna let you in on a little clue."

"Ok."

"That bitch in there you call your mom?—she ain't your mom. Your real mom left you here when you were little and said she was gonna go buy some drugs? Well, she never came back, so Linda or Bulinda or whoever you livin' with in there—"

"Bindy."

"Ok. Bindy. Bindy is just watching you 'cause your mom is her friend."

"So where's my mom now?" He asked it in such a sincere way that I felt awful for saying the things I was saying.

But I kept going, because it fed some sick fire in me.

"Your mom? She prob'ly lost *all* her teeth, and she's in South Carolina suckin' some big fat nigga's dick."

"Why?"

"If you can't figure that out, I ain't telling you."

"Well when is she coming back?"

*Holy shit.* This kid was killing me. I figured out right then I wasn't cold hearted enough to tell him his mom was *never* coming back—she was prob'ly dead or in jail.

"Timmy, just go to bed."

"But I want your moccasins."

"Get Bulinda to buy you some."

"It's Bindy."

"Whatever, Binda."

"*Bindy.*"

"Tommy if you don't let me sleep I'mma come out there and beat you into the dust."

I guess that did it 'cause he went away.

I held my moccasins tight and somewhere in the night Lisa came in. There were two beds in the camper so that was nice. I heard her talking about something and I figured she was talking to herself or she had shot so much meth she was talking to her hallucinations but I fell back asleep pretty fast and that was the last time I woke that night.

- - - -

In the morning I shot up in bed because I was no longer holding my moccasins. Mom was sitting in bed opposite me, brushing her hair. On her feet, fully laced, were her black moccasins.

"Where are my moccasins?" I said.

Lazily she said, "I don't know, honey."

I stood up and threw my sheet on the floor. They weren't on my bed. It was a tiny camper so it took me about two seconds to see that they weren't inside. I checked outside the camper—there was nothing there. I sit down on the ground by the camper, and you know what I see? Little Timmy, little crackboy Timmy, come around the house with a pink hula hoop in his hand and on his feet are my perfect, new, leather moccasins. They're so big on him he can't even lace them all the way up. He walks up to me like he's part of a circus show and does the hula hoop right in front of me, singing songs about me and my baby and how one of us was gonna die but he didn't know who—all this shit. I looked at his neck. You could see the Adam's apple protruding and all his tendons and veins showed clearly, like you were looking at someone who hadn't eaten in a year. He wasn't wearing a shirt. His belly was imploded, every rib was visible. I felt like I was looking at a corpse.

I waited till he finished his song and dance and then I said, "Timmy, give me my moccasins."

And Timmy said, "They aren't yours anymore."

I stood up.

"You unlace those motherfuckers now and hand 'em to me *or I will kill you*."

But all Timmy did was extend his decrepit hand and point a finger toward the camper where Lisa and I were staying. *And I knew*. I knew what had happened before she even told me.

Timmy smiles at me and hops off like a bunny back around the other side of the house.

I go back inside the camper. I sit on my bed. I look at my mom.

She combs her hair. I think she's doing it slowly on purpose just to avoid telling me how *my* moccasins became *Timmy's* moccasins.

We sit there at least three minutes.

Then my mom finally says:

"Look, Davina, I traded them for some blow."

I say nothing. My forehead is hot. My armpits are hot. My feet are burning and I want to scream, run, yell, *kill* my fucking mother. And as much as I hate crying in front of that bitch, I do, I cry. First it's just a little and then it gets bigger and bigger and I lie on my side and grip the sheet so hard I rip a hole in it—*that's* how mad I am. My mom sold my moccasins for blow! It makes a pit in my stomach that aches like when you lose a lover or someone *dies*—*that's* the kind of loss I felt about those shoes.

I'm lying there waiting for her to say something like *I'm sorry*, or *I'll buy you a new pair*. But she didn't say any of the things I wished she would say. She doesn't have the emotional capacity of a ten year old! I'm lying there and I hear sniffing. I look up and that bitch is doing bumps of the coke she traded my shoes for. Like, right in front of me.

I settle my crying, and you know, I'm just about the age where I can take care of myself. Maybe it's time to move out, get a job, support myself. Looking back I was so unrealistic—I was eight months pregnant. But I wasn't thinking about that then. I was thinking I'll tell my mom I'm going for a walk, zip right on over the overpass, go find that bitch cashier in the Payless and ask her for an application. I'll buy myself that exact same pair (only better), I'll get an apartment and be done with Lisa. Fucking traded my moccasins for blow. Unbelievable.

- - - -

I'm about to get up and go on my walk to meet Rebecca and get a job and begin my new life where *I'm* taking care of myself, not a mother who's 10 times crazier than me. Think about it: Lisa comes home to our camper in the middle of the night. Her meth high is wearing off. The house is closed, but this little seven-year-old corpse named Timmy is sleeping on the porch. Lisa wakes him up.

"Timmy, I know it's late but I just need a couple of lines."

"The house is locked."

"Can you break in?"

"Yeah, I guess I could do that. How much do you want?"

"Just a couple of lines, Timmy. All I gotta do is make it through the night."

"I mean in dollars. Like five dollars? Ten dollars? We already have bags made up for twenty."

"Well that's the problem—I don't have a twenty."

"What can you give me?"

I imagine my mom dismissing the idea of giving him a blowjob because of his age.

Then Timmy says, "What about those?" and he points at her moccasins.

So what does my mother do? Does she trade *her* moccasins for drugs? No. While Timmy is breaking into Bindy's house to steal some of *her* drugs to trade for something *he* wants, my mom comes into the camper and steals my moccasins out of my sleeping hands.

Timmy comes back. Lisa comes back. They make their trade.

Then Timmy notices something is wrong.

"I wanted the *black* ones," he says. "Brown is for girl cowboys."

"No it's not. It's for macho handsome cowboys like you," she says.

Then my mother notices a problem. She holds the bag Timmy gave her up to the porch light.

"This is coke, you asshole."

Timmy shrugs and starts putting on his shoes.

Anyway I'm lying in bed imagining how all this went down and it strikes me as pathetic that in the process of stealing my moccasins and a bag of Bindy's coke, neither Timmy or my mom ended up with what they wanted. Timmy got brown instead of black. Lisa got coke instead of meth.

I'm almost smiling and I'm secretly wiping my tears off my face with my pillowcase. Yeah. Through with these people. Let 'em have their dysfunctional druggie relationships all they want. I'm going to the Levi's store, gonna apologize to Rebecca for calling her bad names in my imagination, and ask her for an application.

I sit up.

I throw the sheets off me.

My feet hit the floor.

And then I realize: I can't go to the Levi's store.

I can't go anywhere.

I have no shoes.

## 50
When we were at that place I was *so* pregnant. I mean I never knew how pregnant I was since we never went to the doctor, but I could feel it—this baby was about to come.

During the day we were allowed inside the house. Every day, as soon as I could, I would go up on the porch and kick Timmy to wake him up. He was like our dog—a dog with moccasins. Timmy and I would sit on the couch and watch TV. It was me—like the fattest woman on the Earth—and Timmy, who looked like he belonged in east Africa with flies swarming around him and some film actor's voiceover telling you that you could save him for only twenty-five cents a day. Sometimes our legs would touch and neither of us would pull away but it wasn't like an attraction thing..it was more like a brother and sister thing.

I hate to say that, because *to this day* I'm pissed at him and Lisa for stealing my moccasins. But it was hard to be mad at Timmy—a seven year old that Bulinda made sleep on the porch. She treated him like a dog! And to be honest, I thought of him that way.

When there was nothing on TV, Timmy and I played Mario—the original one on the original Nintendo. We never had a Nintendo in all our years of motel hopping, but Timmy had grown up playing it and he knew all the shortcuts. I was trucking around world 1-1 like a one-legged kangaroo..I had to look down at my hands to see which button to push to make Mario jump. Tim showed me all the secrets, the bonus rooms, the hidden levels. My favorite was when you found a blinking star—if you jumped up and got it, it made you invincible. That was a feeling I liked a lot.

Timmy tried to fuck me once—I guess that's what you would call it. We were sitting on the couch playing Mario and I had just grabbed a blinking star so I was going to be invincible for the next ten or fifteen seconds or something. Then I saw Tim's hands near his crotch and he had unzipped his shorts and his tiny little penis and scrotum were out. His underwear looked like they hadn't been washed in a *year*. Timmy was stroking his ChapStick-sized peen with two fingers and it got semi-hard and then Timmy looks up at me and in this Humphrey-Bogart-type voice he says:

"Did you know I'm a pimp?"

"What?! Timmy put your fucking dick away I don't wanna see that!"

"And you're one of my whores."

My invincibility on Mario was running out, wasted on this wannabe pimp.

Timmy kept stroking his dick until it got as hard as it looked like he could get it, approximately like a stick of string cheese.

"Lay back, baby. I'm gonna do you like you wanna be done."

I paused the game.

"Timmy, you ruined my invincibility, and lemme give you a tip: when you try to sweet-talk a girl, I would not suggest using Prince lyrics as your first line of attack."

"But I didn't use any Prince lyrics!"

"Not that Prince isn't one of the greatest musicians in the history of the world..and a *genius* as a producer—"

"But I didn't *use* a Prince lyric."

"Uh, 'I'm gonna do you like you wanna be done'? I'm not an *idiot*. That's Prince and the New Power Generation, the *Love Symbol Album*, and I know you have the tape because I hear you playing it on repeat every time Bulinda lets you in the house when she's passed out on Vicodin. So put your tiny dick away and let's play Mario."

I unpaused my game. I was still invincible but I had only blown past a few enemies before Timmy's crotch was in my face and he got about three and a half centimeters of his tiny dick into my mouth and it was hard enough that he got a few strokes in! *Ugh*—I'll never forget the semi-soft cheese feel of the dick that seven-year-old fucked my mouth with for like one second before I pushed him on the floor, dropped my controller, and stood up off the couch and kicked that little kid like I was a gangster in a fucking gangster movie. I came to my senses when I heard something *crack*—probably one of Timmy's ribs. I realized I could *kill* this kid if I wasn't careful so I stopped kicking and walked out of Bulinda's house—the Mario invincibility song was still playing as I closed the screen door behind me.

- - - -

That night I dreamt I was sucking a baby cock, holding this infant up to my face and trying to get his tiny penis hard. I was strutting my head like a peacock, a pigeon—any kind of bird—forward and backward, in and out on this tiny cock. It was a nondescript baby—it didn't *look* like Timmy. But I know that dream is about Timmy, and I have that dream every few months—apparently my subconscious loves to torture me with the idea that it's somehow my fault that Timmy's cock ever went inside my mouth. I can't even look at a male baby without thinking of that dream. And I feel guilty that for three seconds Timmy's cock was in my mouth—it wasn't one, ok, it was more like three—but whatever: I feel guilty that I helped that boy get even three seconds of pleasure from the wetness and the hotness of my mouth. Voices run through my head that say: *You suck baby cock!* and I believe them—I believe they're true.

- - - -

The next day I still went inside the house to play with Timmy once Bindy had gone. Timmy and I now had a nonverbal agreement about where he could put his penis and where he could not. That whole next day I let him play Mario by himself, one player, without having to share with me—that was payment for his broken rib. As for me, I stared past the TV and discovered a tiny slice of the world you could see from inside that room.

If you looked out the living room windows, you could see a freeway, and across the freeway was an apartment complex. I would stare out those windows for hours, pull up a chair and watch people coming in and out of their apartments..and I'd daydream about being a mom and having a baby and my mom not being there and it was a perfect image.

I specifically remember this one time I was watching and I saw a lady coming out of her apartment with a little girl maybe four or five years old. The lady was getting her kid strapped into her car and the kid was all bouncy with the cutest ponytail. I was imagining where they were going, what they were doing. I was so envious. I wanted what they had. And that's the most stressful thing there is: knowing that somewhere out there people have cars and jobs and money—and you don't..knowing that out there people are living like they do on TV, in Venice Beach and Santa Monica, with the ocean right there and parents that love them and good relationships with their brothers and sisters, and lots of sex and *lots* of money. Those were the main two things: sex and money. If you had those two everything else would take care of itself. I watched that mom and her little kid drive off, to buy new diapers or maybe for a play date! She made everything look so easy: getting in the car, buckling the seatbelt, and driving off down her street to some more pleasurable place.

It made me mad. It made me so mad I was cross-eyed and palms sweating. I decided to look for a job.

- - - -

I had shoes again at this point. Mom had borrowed Bulinda's car and took me, not to the Levi's store, but to the Payless. I was barefoot in the car. I was barefoot in the store. Mom said:

"Stay right here, Davina."

She walked up and down every aisle, picking up boxes and looking inside boxes and looking *under* boxes. She switched price tags between an expensive pair of shoes and a less expensive pair—which one of the cashiers totally saw.

This cashier, who was fat beyond fat, gripped the sides of her cashier station and sucked in all her breath and *squeeeeeezed* herself out of the tiny opening meant for people like that rich bitch redhead at the Levi's store.

This lady goes up to my mom.

"I saw you switch 'em. Now switch 'em back."

My mom thinks for a second.

Then she punches that fat bitch in the face.

And the bitch *goes down!*

There's blood running from her face and she's rolling from side to side to get enough momentum to stand up.

Once she gets a hand on a shelf my mom kicks her again and the bitch goes down again.

I'm standing in the front of the store trying to act like I don't know my own mom.

The other cashier picks up his phone. Dials zero. Asks for non-emergency police.

I go down the aisle and I gently tell my mom:

"They're calling the police."

"Ok, ok, let me think."

"Let's just leave."

"But we need to get you a new pair of shoes!"

She puts her head down and leans against the shelf. Big bitch is crying and cupping her broken nose. She starts to wail.

My mom kicks her again, trying to kick her in the cunt but her cunt is inaccessible through all the fat rolls. Mom says to me:

"Two options: One. I give you a dollar limit and you pick out a pair of shoes real fast before the cops get here. I'm talking, like, the next forty-five seconds you find those motherfuckers."

"What's option number two?"

My mom doesn't answer for a long time. She listens to the fat bitch of a cashier wail about how hard it was to get this job and her doctor knows she has emotional problems and the pills, the pills..she should have put them on the edge of the sink so that she would see them when she brushed her teeth because she always forgets to look in the medicine cabinet and now everything is fucked up and she's gonna lose her job and it's all because of her ex-husband—

"Wait right there," my mom says. "Don't even do that. Don't even start with that shit."

"What shit?"

"You can't blame this on your ex-husband. Ok. I got a shitty ex-husband too. Ex-boyfriend anyway. When we blame them we *give them power* over us. Secondly, you gotta get yourself off all that pharmaceutical shit. That shit is no good. No good!"

My mom kicks the fat woman in the stomach but not hard at all—more like a nudge.

"Mom, the police are going to be here any second. So.."

"So what?"

"So what was option two?"

"Oh, yeah," my mom says, taking a cigarette out and lighting it. "Option two is we take this bitch's shoes and we get the fuck out of here before the cops show up."

Mom and I look at each other for one second and I know I come from her. As much as I hate my mom, when I watch what she does, I see myself doing the same things. I convince myself, alternately, that we *are* alike or that we're not alike. Obviously it's both.

So I kneel down by that fat cashier bitch's feet and I pull off her sandals. They're entirely too big for my feet, as in you could fit *four* of my feet into *one* of hers—easily. They're pale blue sandals with the kind of toe catch that will cut through the skin between your first two toes the first day you wear them. But after that you toughen up. I take those fuckers off her feet and say:

"Mom. Let's go!"

Mom is in some kind of a nicotine trance but she hears me and follows. I walk fast. I hold the sandals at arm's length. They're worn so thin on the insole because of the way our cashier's knees rub together when she walks that I'm not even sure that I can wear them. As me and Lisa exit the Payless, the other cashier looks at us and my mom says:

"Don't *fuck* with me!"

Then we're in the car, and we're driving off, and Mom opens the window so both our hair is blowing in the wind.

"Well, how are they? Do they fit?"

I flash back to the woman's feet we stole them from: corns, warts, foot fungus. She can't even reach her feet when she bathes.

I put the sandals on anyway, pushing my toes all the way to the front. About four inches of shoe sticks out behind my heel. I show my mom and she cracks up. I crack up, too. Even though she traded my moccasins and the shoes she got me to replace them are ghetto as fuck, I felt like it was something we did together.

There used to be space in my heart for my mom. There used to be *huge* spaces. That day there was a big space. And it's no secret if I tell you now that from that day when I was fifteen, to this day when I'm 42, all that space has disappeared.

## 51
So I had shoes when I went out looking for a job. They weren't the *best* shoes and they certainly weren't my moccasins, but I could walk in them (barely) and they got me down the road.

This job search thing was all me. No one asked me to look for a job. I just felt panicked. Imagining me Mom and my baby living in the camper of Bulinda's house almost gave me a panic attack.

I found an ad in the newspaper for a lady who wanted a babysitter. It wasn't within walking distance, so I had no idea how I could actually *get* to this job if they offered it to me, but Bindy gave me a ride to the woman's house for the interview.

The lady's name was Mrs. Davis.

"Should I call you Mrs. Davis, or..?"

"You should call me Mrs. Davis."

We both sat down. Me on the couch, her on an old-fashioned plushy chair with neat wood carvings on the feet.

Mrs. Davis had a grid of sticky notes on the table beside her. She chose one and stuck it to her knee.

"So," she said.

We both smiled.

"Davina."

"Yes."

"How old are you?"

"Fifteen."

Mrs. Davis wrote something on the note, then her eyes came back to me. She was staring at me, slack-jawed, eyes wide open.

"Are you in school?"

"Not currently."

"Why aren't you in school?"

"Well, we're new to the area," I said cheerfully.

I scratched my leg. A fingernail ran over a scab. I started bleeding.

"So we're still evaluating schools, you know, I mean you can't just *go* to any school because it's the closest one to your house."

"Of course," Mrs. Davis said, offering me a tissue.

"Oh I'm fine."

"For your leg."

"Oh! Right, that, yeah, sorry. You know those mosquitos! I must be allergic."

She waits for me to finish wiping the blood off my leg and brings me a trashcan to throw the tissue in—I guess so she won't have to touch it. Once we were all situated she said:

"You've obviously read the posting.."

"Oh yes. It sounds like just the sort of thing I'd be interested in. And good at."

"..so you know the position is sort of a nanny."

"Yes, exactly. I can do that for you."

Mrs. Davis looks confused. She tilts her head to one side.

"So you have experience taking care of children."

"I've taken care of my brothers and sister my whole life."

I started tearing up. Because? Because it was true, up to a certain point, that I had taken care of my younger siblings like a mother and how fucked up it was that I had to do that. Because I hadn't seen them in what?..a year?..months? And because I could see where this interview was going, and where *every* interview was going to go—and that meant that right here in this moment, on Mrs. Davis's couch, my dream of getting an apartment and a job and being free of Lisa and having a wonderful life for me and my baby was crumbling.

"Davina?"

"Yes?"

"You look like you're about to have a baby soon."

I smile.

"How far along are you?"

I shrug.

"Eight or nine months. Didn't really have the doctor thing going on this time."

"Well, let's be honest: you can't look after my child if you're taking care of your own! Davina, baby, you seem like a lovely girl. And with your experience taking care of your younger siblings—with that and your confidence—and you seem very smart."

"Really?"

"Yes."

"How can you tell?"

"I can tell from the way you talk."

I smile at this woman and she smiles back and her smile is a big "no" with a side of classist pity. What did she see in me? A kid who was *so* pregnant and *so* young, but with so much enthusiasm (I hope) and a will to find work so I could take care of my baby. I was definitely not the interview candidate she was looking for—she wanted Mary Poppins and got me instead. This woman had a nice-looking house with real paintings on the walls and a pedestal holding a statue of an elephant cut from wood that they picked up on their last vacation to Kenya—the tusks are made from real ivory. To her I probably looked like a waif off the street—which I was.

I stood up (me and baby).

"I'm going now."

"You don't have to."

"Sorry for bleeding during our interview."

"It's alright, Davina. Take care of yourself."

Obviously I didn't get the job. I mean I knew that when I walked out the door. Bindy was gone so I sat on the sidewalk waiting for her. A few times Mrs. Davis came out to check on me.

"Do you want some water?"

"No, thank you."

"Some lemonade?"

"No thanks, Mrs. Davis. That's really nice of you."

"Do you need to use the phone?"

"Nah, my ride'll be here in a minute."

"You're welcome to wait inside."

"I'm fine here."

She finally gave up after so many "no"s and left me, pregnant, sitting on the sidewalk in front of her house. She probably only wanted me to wait inside because I was fucking up the property values.

- - - -

Bindy finally came and her ass was *way* too high to drive. She parked with one wheel on the sidewalk *right* next to me. I had to roll out of the way so she wouldn't hit me. Mrs. Davis came outside to help me get up.

I said, "I'll do it myself!" and didn't let her touch me.

"Please be careful," Mrs. Davis said. "If you have a fall on our property then—"

"Don't worry, lady, I'm not gonna sue you."

"Oh no, that's not why I said that."

And Mrs. Davis took a step back.

And I got up off the grass.

"Then why did you say it?" I asked.

Mrs. Davis was silent for a long time.

Then she said, "I'm sorry. That is why I said that. I'm sorry, Davina. That was a very low thing for me to do, to assume that just because you fell you would sue us."

She covered her eyes with her hand, blocking the sun, blocking her view of me, blocking her view of the car I was leaving in and the person who was driving it. She went inside her house and locked the door—I could hear the bolt click from where I was standing in her yard.

Bindy said, "Ya comin'?"

I went to the driver's side and opened the door.

Waves of meth smoke came at me.

"Slide over," I said.

"You ain't even got a license."

"Bindy, slide the fuck over and let me drive."

"Better watch your mouth or you and Lisa will have to find someone else's camper to sleep in."

"I didn't mean to offend you."

"Well you did offend me. How about this: you get to ride in the trunk."

"No one's riding in the trunk. Slide the fuck over so we can get the fuck out of here."

"What's the big hurry?"

She puts the meth pipe up to her mouth.

I smack it down. It lands in the passenger seat and rolls into the crack.

"Bitch," Bindy says, "you can *walk* home."

She backs up off Mrs. Davis's lawn.

"Why'd you smack my pipe down? That's good crystal. Now it's on the seat of my car. I can't smoke it down there."

I wedge myself into the driver seat, pushing Bindy over enough that I can close the driver's side door.

Bindy corners herself as far away from me as possible, against the seat back and the door. She points at me with witch fingers.

In a raspy voice she says, "You're not old enough to drive."

"Ok," I say. "Let's do a little test. What year is it?"

"Nineteen forty-five," she says without the slightest hesitation.

"And *that's* why you're not driving."

I put the car in gear and I slam on the gas and I get us the *fuck* out of that neighborhood.

- - - -

One time my mom took me to this big old movie theater where they showed three movies for a buck fifty. This was before movie theaters had stadium seating, so the distance between the back row and the screen was enormous. And that's where we sat—the very back row. Mom gave me money for snacks, then she turned sideways and within five minutes she was sleeping. There were only two other couples in the whole theater. I got popcorn and stuff and came back to sit with my mom. I watched *Nightmare on Elm Street* one, two, and three, in order, with bathroom breaks in between. The screen was dark like they needed to change the light in the projector. The movies were also out of focus. I would have complained but I thought the only person working was the old man at the concession stand, and he was busy reading his newspaper. The two other couples in the theater were making out far ahead of us in this sea of chairs.

My mom slept the whole time. I think she felt safe in there.

After the movies we found this tiny corner of sidewalk that was being repaired and the cement was still wet. We pressed our hands into it and I wrote my name and she wrote her stripper name: Stormy.

Then in that exact spot in downtown San Bernardino, years later, when I was living with Chris (I'll explain Chris in a minute so hold your horses *amiga*!)..anyway Chris and his friends came home from doing whatever and they were all laughing because they found that little corner of sidewalk that Mom and I had put our names in. One of Chris' friends happened to be named Storm and they were teasing saying, "I didn't know your girlfriend dated Storm! Haha it was weird for me to hear about, thinking how that little piece of concrete had stayed there all those years, and eventually someone had come by who knew who I was.

- - - -

I still carried on as though I was not pregnant for long into my pregnancy with Ashley. It's almost as though I was disconnected from my womb. I was pregnant the day we (or I) watched *Nightmare on Elm Street* and when Mom and I wrote our names in the sidewalk. I was still acting like a kid, but I was about to become a mother.

We left Bulinda's house the day we saw Bindy shooting up Timmy with meth. Bindy was in her chair. Timmy was sitting on the floor in front of her. Me and Mom were on the couch. Bindy just packed a syringe, took Tim's arm, and gave him a fat shot of meth. I guess this was too much for even my mom, 'cause she grabbed my hand and pulled me off the couch. On the way to the camper she told me never to shoot meth and I promised I wouldn't.

When Bulinda shot Tim up, his eyes were rolling around all weird and he started touching his nipples and playing with himself. That's all I saw but it was fucked up to see a seven year old get high. I imagined Bindy shooting up babies, getting little infants off on meth—one of their first experiences in life would be getting zapped by one of the worst drugs on Earth. Some of the babies could handle it, they'd shit their diapers in joy at that lighter-than-light feeling it gives you in the lungs. And some of the babies couldn't handle it—the second that crystal hit their hearts, they'd die.

Mom and I slept the night in the camper. By morning we were gone.

## 52
After that we stayed with a lady my mom knew somehow. This is in San Bernardino. One night my stomach started killing me—it was hurting so bad. I sat up in bed and realized my water broke.

I was like: *Crap, I'm going into labor.*

When I sat up in bed I was in my mom's friend's house—my mom was two blocks away at her boyfriend Larry's house. I got up from bed, dripping everywhere. I went to the living room but my mom's friend was passed out on Smirnoff and Unisom. There was porn on TV—perfect-looking Latino boys cumming in each other's asses, then squeezing it out, then the other one sucking it off of his partner's asshole before any of it hit the ground. They were kissing and passing shit-laced cum between their mouths when I closed the front door and began my two-block trek to Larry's house, as the labor of my first child began.

I was barefoot—walking on asphalt.

I stayed in the very middle of the street—I thought this increased the chance that someone who came by would actually help me.

At times I was doubled over in pain so bad that I couldn't take another step. At those times I held my sides and my belly and lowered my head and waited in the streetlight.

I thought at times that the baby would come before I ever got to Larry's house, and we would both die from blood loss in the very center of some suburban street and the next person who came along would be a photographer and they would get a high-res picture of me and my baby, bled out on the asphalt of an American city. Someone like *National Geographic* would pick up the photo and write a whole story about us in an anthropological vein. We would both be dead—but, hey, we would both be famous.

That's what I thought about on the trek to Larry's house. At some point it crossed my mind that they wouldn't even be there—they might be at Jack in the Box taking advantage of some stellar late-night menu featuring breakfast burritos and one-dollar tostadas. Maybe I was just hungry. I looked up into a streetlight and wondered if blood loss or labor in general was causing me to hallucinate—I saw ghouls flying out of the light just like in the next to the last scene in *Raiders of the Lost Ark*. You know the one where all those Nazis die because they think they can control the power of God, but Indiana Jones saves Marion by telling her not to look at what comes out of the ark, no matter what. That was what it was like for me on the street so I didn't look at the light, I just kept contracting and dripping—there were times when I was crawling on all fours on these asphalt streets and then I started yelling because I didn't want my baby to come out of me on Crescent Cove Drive because then all of its life this fucking baby's birth certificate would say that its place of birth was Crescent Cove Drive—not Baylor University Hospital in Dallas, Texas or some other respectable place of birth. You either home birth or you hospital birth—this is America—we don't have our babies in the middle of the goddamn street. My yelling brought me zero good. One guy opened his door and I said:

"Help?"

He turned his back on me and spoke to someone inside his house. He said:

"Don't worry it's just the start of the zombie apocalypse."

Then he closed his door! Fucker! And I was rolling in the street, holding my knees and lying on my back with this extreme pain inside my body and I knew it was my baby trying to come through my vagina and I could not let that happen here.

I turned over and crawled—it was the only way I could move—standing hurt so much that I couldn't stand and walk at the same time. And over what seemed like an eternity (which was prob'ly only half an hour), I made it from Mom's friend's house to Mom's boyfriend's house.

I knocked on the door—nothing happened.

I rang the doorbell—nothing happened.

The garage door was closed so I had no idea if they were even home.

I tried the door handle—it let me in. I was walking now, with a pained limp, one hand permanently attached to my side. I took two steps into the house and the whole place smelled like sex and meth.

"Mom?"

No answer.

"Lisa! It's Davina. I need help!"

No one answered but I could hear the two of them fucking upstairs.

My journey up the stairs would have been completely unnecessary if those two could just fuck *sober*, like normal people. But they were so high off of so many drugs that if they ever *could* have an orgasm it would have given them a heart attack. I yelled all the way up the stairs and left bloody streaks of handprints on the wall above the railing. The water from my amniotic sac left long strings of goopy wetness all up Larry's stairs, all through his upstairs hallway, and by the time I made it to the bedroom I was panting but I no longer needed to clench my side to numb the pain of walking.

I opened Larry's bedroom door, imagining I looked like the last person standing at the end of a 70s horror movie. Larry and Lisa were both naked and Mom pulled a sheet over their bottom halves.

"Have you ever heard of knocking?"

"My stomach hurts. I'm pretty sure I'm gonna have a baby."

My mom laughed and slapped her boyfriend's thigh.

"They're so *hysterical* at this age! One little thump and they think they need to be *rushed* to the hospital. Larry, I'm *so* sorry about this. I invite you to share a night of pleasure, *assuring* you that it's ok with my friend that she watches my daughter, assuring you that my *daughter* will *stay at my friend's house* and not be an issue. But, Davina, you *are* an issue. You've been pregnant what? Six, seven months?"

"It's more like eight."

"Eight? Ok, let's make it eight! You're not in labor, Davina—if you *were* you could hardly stand. Your baby's just kicking, and if you can't handle some occasional poking and prodding then, really, you don't deserve to be a mother."

"My water broke."

"Are you sure?"

"I think so."

I look down and the sides of my legs are still slick from it. There's blood there, too.

Mom and Larry look at each other.

Then Larry looks at me.

He says, "Let's get you to a hospital!"

Lisa says to Larry, "I'm so sorry about this."

"No, it's ok!"

"I've been planning and planning and I wanted tonight to go perfectly but..*Davina!*..of *all* the nights! Are you sure you didn't just piss yourself?"

She kept at me all the way to the hospital. Her message was clear: *I embarrass her. I'm an eternal embarrassment to her. I'm nothing but a big problem for her. I'm something to be apologized for. My existence and my pregnancy are things she apologizes for to her boyfriend.*

I looked out the window, took the pain, and thought of Lisa as little as possible.

- - - -

When I was in labor with Ashley, my mom was in the delivery room with me, and because of this, I showed zero emotion. I kept perfectly quiet even when I was in extreme pain. I hardly made any sounds: no talking, no crying out—I don't even think I talked to the doctor when he asked me questions. Being that this was my first time giving birth, and being that the mother who never cared for me was right there in the room, watching me do it, I didn't want to show myself emotionally. Sometimes I closed my eyes and every time I opened them, she was there, hovering around. I cried silently. I never made any loud noises even when Ashley finally came.

When my oldest and only girl was nineteen I gave us matching tattoos—a Sublime lyric on each of our left arms: *The time is coming for us all to pay.* Maybe that ended up being more prophetic than I knew.

The nurses held Ashley up for me to see. The first thing I noticed was she had her dad's chin.

I said that to my mom: "She has her dad's chin. She looks just like her dad."

Lisa looks at the baby and then she looks at me, like she has something really sarcastic to say. I was bracing myself for it. I was ready to yell: "I'M FIFTEEN AND I HAD THIS BABY WITH NONE OF YOUR HELP!" Then I was gonna cuss that bitch out and tell her to get the *fuck* out of the delivery room. But Lisa didn't say anything. She just turned her back and went into the hallway. I heard her ask someone where the nearest place was to buy cigarettes.

As soon as I thought she was gone I let myself cry. The doctors and nurses were cleaning me up. I didn't care if they saw me crying. I just thought how awfully my mother had treated me and how wonderfully I was gonna treat this kid.

In the recovery room I was relieved. It felt like I was pregnant forever. I have so many memories that include me being pregnant: Charlie kicking me when I was down, trying to kill his own child. Or us on the way from California to Texas, me sitting in the back seat and everyone had chicken pox but me. Now, nine months flies by like an airplane, but back then it was step, by step, by step. And what about that guy who fucked me when I was pregnant, staying in that one couple's garage. I wondered if he remembered me. Doubtful. Just another harmless fetishist who liked fucking pregnant women. Ok, I know that's not true. I know he didn't really want to fuck a pregnant girl—he was just hard up for pussy and any slut would do.

- - - -

So in the hospital a couple people came to see me. My mom was there. Also the mom of my boyfriend Brian (who was my first true love), and Brian's sister—but not Brian. I was trying to ask his mom and sister where he was, but nobody was listening to me. They were all gaga over the new baby, holding her and talking baby talk and passing her from person to person. Everyone was saying how *cute* she was—which was ridiculous. Babies are only cute to their parents. To everyone else they look like bloody hairless rats. They're purple. I mean, really, if they're not yours, they look like that shit from *Alien*: it punches through your stomach, scares the shit out of everyone, screams "IEEEEEEEEEEEEEE!!" in a high-pitched voice, then runs out of the room so it can mutate in private, becoming everyone's worst nightmare. I love my kids, but I'd say that's a pretty good description of the birth and rearing process.

Anyway everyone was saying how cute my little Ashley was, and I couldn't get a cup of water or anyone to tell me where Brian was or five minutes to hold my own baby. But, that's the way it goes. Once you have children, the focus switches to the next generation. You're no longer *it*. And it stays on your kids until *they* have their kids. Anyway.

I never wanted to name her Ashley. But my mom filled out the birth certificate. I could see her across the recovery room using a nurse's clipboard and she and the nurse were talking. *I* wanted to name her Rhianna, but I read my mother's lips as she was talking to the nurse and what she said was: *Ashley Nicole*. And that's what she wrote on the birth certificate. And that became her name.

My mom's reasoning was transparent: Ashley Nicole was a popular name on all the soap operas back then. Apparently a lot of people name their kids based on soap operas because there are a lot of Ashley Nicoles around that are Ashley's age.

I don't know why I didn't stop her, why I didn't yell across the room and say that, "Her name is Rhianna, not Ashley Nicole!" I could have gotten the name I wanted for *my own kid*, not the name my mother wanted, but I was weak. Not just weak from the delivery, but just weak as a person. I was never assertive back then—especially with my mom. She's evil, I tell you, she wouldn't let her daughter pick out the name for her own kid. But it was my fault, too, for being so scared that I didn't stand up for myself. I guess I've let my life slide: not done what *I* wanted to do. I let other people lead because I really don't care. And somewhere deep from childhood, I believe that my wishes don't matter.

Mom came over with the birth certificate and waved it in everyone's face.

"Ashley Nicole! Ashley Nicole! What a *beautiful* name for such a beautiful girl!"

What could I do? The naming was done. Once it's on the birth certificate, that's your name.

My mom came to the bed where I was lying. She held the birth certificate in my face and she say saying something but I couldn't hear it. My mind automatically blocked out Lisa's voice to save me from additional agony. Lisa turned the birth certificate around and I saw by her lips that she was reading the name aloud:

*Ashley Nicole. Ashley Nicole. Ashley Nicole.*

She had a genuine smile on her face but the eyes, looking at me, were pure evil. She was so satisfied to have gotten the name she wanted, but that wasn't enough for her—she had it rub it in.

By this time I wasn't mentally even in that recovery room. I was in some protected, dissociated space, where I couldn't be hurt by any of these people because I wasn't even me anymore. I was just an actor, playing a new mother on one of Lisa's soap operas. Ashley wasn't even a real baby—she was a stunt baby!

Pain brought me back, though—I was extremely sore from the delivery.

My mom was still there waving around the birth certificate.

*Fucking bitch.*

- - - -

I turned my head to the other side. Brian's mom and sister were watching over me.

"So where is Brian?" I asked.

Brian's mom said, "Oh, he wanted to be here but he had something else to do."

"Just tell her," Brian's little sister said.

I look to Brian's mom.

"Davina, I don't know how to tell you this, but—"

The worst possibilities flashed through my head: drug overdose, car accident, busted by cops.

"—but he got a new girlfriend."

"What?!"

"He's seeing someone else now and she didn't think it would be appropriate for Brian to come."

I haven't had lice since then, and I don't dwell on this particular trauma any more than I dwell on any other trauma. I kept walking a little, then I would turn and shoo them really hard and they would turn and run then come right back! So I ran and I just kept running and I had to cross a very busy street that happened to be just free enough for me to run across without stopping! I ran all the way across the street and then I stopped when I got to the other side to turn and look and see where the puppies were..if they turned back yet..but they didn't! They followed me across the street and just as I was turning to look I watched three of the puppies get hit by a car and another one was just trotting all happily and unaware across the busy street.

I'll never forget the way those puppies looked getting run over—it hurts my heart to this day. Every time I think of them I cry. And that one lone puppy who lived, once he made it across the street, I picked him up and I hugged him even tighter knowing his brothers and sisters were dead. He was licking my face and my ears in front of the 7-Eleven and I told him I would never let him go—that I would never let anything bad happen to him. He was simply filled with enthusiasm to see me, to follow *me*. It was his *innocence* that rang a bell with me. They were all so innocent. They depended on me. And with one stupid assumption on my part, three of them were dead.

I took my one living puppy home and I was crying and yelling at Lisa. I must have seemed insane. I was yelling at her with sounds that weren't even words—that's how fucked up I was over those puppies dying.

And don't think that this analogy was lost on me: the Davina running through busy traffic is my mom. The litter of puppies are her children. And I'm the one who lived—not because someone was taking care of me, but from luck—*luck. That's* why I'm still alive.

Those puppies had imprinted me as their mother. They would have followed me into a house on fire. My fifteen-year-old mind didn't quite understand that, and by me going across that street, I led them to their deaths.

Killing those puppies is the most guilty I've ever felt. I was young. I was stupid. I hope you don't think I'm hateful now.

I remember when we lived in our apartment in Yuba City..in the summer with the windows open you could smell peaches on the breeze. One of my friends who I worked with at McDonald's offered to take me to a mental health clinic. She had brought Ashley home from playing with her daughter and while the kids were outside this friend of mine said:

"I know you been poppin' those pills."

"Ultram? Here. Try one."

"You know I had a habit once."

"With what?"

"Pills."

"What kind?"

"The addictive kind. Vicodin, baby."

"You want one of these?"

"Honey, one of those wouldn't do *shit* to me. How many you taking at a time?"

"Twelve."

"Baby girl, baby girl. You can't be doing that to your body. Plus with your kids—"

"They don't know. None of them know."

"'Vina you're fooling yourself. *They* know before *you* do."

"Did Ashley say anything?"

"Ashley didn't say nothin'. I could see by the look on your face when I came in this room that you were on something."

I looked at the floor.

Angelina (my friend) grabs my hand.

"I know a mental health place right down the *street* from here. They can help you out, I promise."

"I take pills—what do I need a mental health clinic for?"

"Because taking pills is a mental illness."

"It is?"

Angelina nods.

Half an hour later we go into this drab-looking box of a building. It looked just like a plasma donation center—beige paint on the bottom half and rusted red on the top, with rusted red doors. There wasn't a single window on the place. We sign in at the waiting room and I fill out a clipboard full of forms.

"How many times have I attempted suicide??" I ask Angelina.

"Just put zero," Angelina said.

"I don't think I'm in the right place. I've never tried to kill myself."

"It's ok," Angelina says. "They treat all kinds of problems."

I stared at her for—like—ever.

She grabs the clipboard out of my hands.

"Next are some medical questions," Angelina says. "You answer, I'll write. Have you ever had a heart attack?"

"No."

"Does diabetes run in your family?"

"Not that I know of."

I grab the clipboard.

"Blindness? Syphilis? Do you smoke tobacco or use chewing tobacco on a regular basis."

I went up to the desk and handed this girl my forms. I mean she was about *my* age.

She circled the whole bottom of the page I was on.

"You have to fill in *all* of these."

"Or else what?"

"Or else the doctor won't see you."

She hands me back the clipboard and goes back to her work.

- - - -

Twenty minutes later we're being led into the doctor's office, having filled out every bit of that paperwork they required.

A nurse shut us in.

Angelina and I both sat down in chairs in front of the doctor's desk.

The doctor—his nameplate said Dr. Kongara—was looking at the paperwork I just filled out. He didn't look up. He didn't say hi. He looked at the paperwork—every page, every question, every answer. Then he finally raised his head and said:

"Which one of you is Davina?"

I raised my hand. (Reflex from school, permanently imprinted—I'll be raising my hand to the time I'm ninety.)

"And who is this, your lover?"

We both laughed.

"Angelina's my friend."

"You don't mind if she sits in?"

"No, I guess—she's the one who brought me here."

"Ok then. Let's begin. Davina, why are you here today?"

"She's popping pills," Angelina says.

"Please. Let the patient speak for herself. Is this true, you are abusing some sort of prescription medicine called Ultram?"

"Yes."

My face blushed. I hadn't thought of myself as *abusing* anything.

"How many of these pills you take per day?"

"I don't know. Like eight, or..sometimes twelve."

Dr. Kongara writes that into my file.

"Well, sometimes twenty."

"Twenty?"

He leans back in his chair.

"When you take these twenty in a day, do you take them all at once or sporadically?"

I don't know what to say.

"I'm here to help you," the doctor says.

"So no police are gonna like bust through that door and take me to jail?"

"The only reason I would call the police is if you were a danger to yourself or others. You don't seem to be a danger to others—do you have plans to kill anyone?"

"Hell no."

"Do you have plans to kill yourself?"

"No," I said quietly.

"Because too much Ultram at one time could kill you. So I wonder, since you are taking twenty a day, if some part of you does not want to be here anymore."

"Like am I taking the Ultram to kill myself, even though I don't know that's what I'm trying to do?"

"Exactly, my young friend."

I think for a while.

"No, I don't think I'm subconsciously trying to kill myself."

"Then why are you taking so many?"

"I don't know? Maybe 'cause I'm bored? I mean how could I be bored—I have three kids to take care of."

"Where are they now?"

"Their father has them. And actually we have to get going because he's *terrible* with babies and we have one who's fairly new."

"Does the father work?"

"Yes."

"How many hours?"

"Sixty?" I say.

"Eighty," Angelina says.

"It sounds like you need some help around the house. And you need to do something that is enriching to yourself. Your children can be wonderful, but they cannot be your sole source of entertainment. I will be brief since I know you have to go, but Davina, listen to me on two things: One, find a friend or a neighbor or someone you trust to come to your house and help you with the kids—even if it's for two hours a day. Can you do that?"

I said yes—what the hell else am I gonna say?

"The second thing is, Davina, you need better stimulation for your mind. You are a smart person."

"How do you know?"

"Excuse me?"

"I'm not trying to be rude but how can you tell that I'm a smart person?"

"I can tell by the way you answer my questions. So you need enrichment for your mind. If a jailer put you in solitary confinement or you landed on a desert island and had no one to talk to, you'd go crazy, right?, because you'd have no one to talk to."

"I talk to my kids."

"They don't count. You love them and take care of them and *you are their mother*—so they need you. Talk to your friend Angelina more often—that is what I am prescribing to you. Go to the library and check out books that are hard for you to read. You need a challenge, Davina, something that is so hard you could possibly fail. Now I know you need to get going so you can take care of your children so what I want you to do—and I need your help with this, friend Angelina, because I can tell Davina is stubborn—go downstairs and sign up for a therapy appointment with one of our student therapists. They are not licensed yet but they are very good."

"What about the pills?"

"What? The Ultram?"

"Yeah, what I came here to talk to you about."

"Don't worry about those."

"What do you mean don't worry about them?"

"What I mean is, you get help with the kids, you enrich your mind with some kind of challenge, and you will stop taking those pills on your own."

- - - -

That was Dr. Kongara—nothing like what I expected. And even though I measured the guy as being (so far) 100% bullshit-free, I didn't have any intention of signing up with some student therapist. Angelina tried to force me into signing up but I wouldn't let her. She was first out the door of the clinic and walking backwards into the street she yelled at me:

"You know you've got *one chance* to make this change and all you have to do is see a *therapist* once a week and what do you do?—*nothing*."

I was crossing the street with her now.

I said very quietly, "I'm sorry."

But as quiet as it was, Angelina turned me, both of us still standing in this empty street.

"You're sorry? *I'm* sorry. I'm sorry for my uncle—died from pills and alcoholism. I'm sorry for my cousin—died from shooting up cocaine *into her neck*. And guess who had a twin sister? My cousin who shot up cocaine *in her neck*, had a twin sister—identical twins. You know what the other twin did, the one who was still alive? She waited about a year, went to therapy, got off drugs (supposedly). Everything was going well until the anniversary of her twin's death. Anniversaries are rough, aren't they? The twin who was still alive went into the bedroom the two of them used to share. There's a mirror on the back of the door. She looks into the mirror, and she doesn't see *herself*—she sees her sister, the one who killed herself in this very spot! And she imagines what it must have been like to be looking at your own beautiful face and *miss the vein*, shooting straight into your carotid artery and killing yourself instantly. And the second twin, staring in the mirror on the anniversary of her sister's death, pulls out a syringe and *does the exact same thing*."

Angelina is red faced. Cheeks puffy. Crying.

I go up to her and put my arm around her.

"Let's get out of the street."

"Ok I will as long as *you* go to counseling."

Angelina poked me.

"Uhh..girlfriend..you just poked me in the boob."

She pokes me in the boob again.

"Promise me you'll go to counseling."

"I will if you'll stop poking me in the boob."

"Shut up, I'm serious. You can't be taking twenty fucking Ultram a day. You'll kill yourself and then who will I have to poke in the boob?"

We both laugh.

"I'm serious. Try taking ten a day and see if you can hack it. You know—*I know*—it's tough having kids."

"Kids are hell," I say.

"Yes they are," my friend says. "No doubt."

We walked back to me and Chris's place and Angelina said quick hellos and quick goodbyes and then she went to the door. Chris let her out and as she was leaving, Angelina held up her hands with all ten fingers extended and looked at me like a circus mime.

"Got it. Ten," I said.

## 58
