That will make me forget that I'm a mother and a lover and that *people depend on me* for their life, when I can hardly take care of my own.

- - - -

Now there are things you can expect your ten-year-old daughter to handle, and there are things you can't. This is one of the things you can't:

One night Ashley had a friend over. We were all hanging out in the kitchen. I was holding Phillip, nursing him. Zach was running around under my feet. Ashley and her friend were singing *at the top of their lungs* some song they'd learned at school about a boy and a girl who go into a barn, take off their clothes, and he starts "poking" the girl.

I asked Ashley if she even knew what poking was.

"Yes I do. It's what you and Daddy do when you're alone in bed. Sometimes. But lately you're not in the mood 'cause you have Phillip and Zachariah and me and taking care of kids is a handful even though I'm not a kid anymore I'm ten. So yes, to answer your question, I do know what poking is."

"Well good for you."

"And I do it all the time with my friends."

"Ashley Nicole! Say that isn't true."

"Ok fine: it isn't true."

"Tell me you're telling me the truth."

"I'm telling you what you want to hear. You said tell me it isn't true so I told you it isn't true."

Phillip runs into the other room.

"Go check on him. Somebody!"

Ashley's friend says, "I'll go." And she follows Phillip.

"Do we need to have a certain talk?" I ask Ashley.

"I've already heard that talk."

"From who?"

"My friends have told me what their parents said during this so-called 'talk.' I know it all now, Mom, you don't have to tell me."

"Well I'm glad you know it all."

"Yep, heard every detail!" Ashley says, and I get so mad..I get so mad it can't be rationally explained or blamed on Ashley or anyone at all except for me and my crazy mind. *Heard every detail!*—That's a bitch-ass thing to hear your ten year old say about sex. Everything came back to me as I looked at her innocent face. Jessie cumming on my back. The delicate way he would lift up my Sunday dress. His hot cock sliding between my butt cheeks. His pre-cum lubricating me so that he could squeeze my cheeks together and pretend that they were..whatever.

Ashley had been talking to me a mile a minute while I was remembering Jessie delicately lifting up my sun dress and delicately pulling down my panties so he could see and touch and smell and lick and fuck what was definitely not his. I saw Ashley's mouth moving but I didn't hear the words. I interrupted her:

"I've had enough of this shit."

"Mom, what are you talking about?"

"I'VE HAD ENOUGH OF THIS SHIT!!"

"Mom, are you ok?"

"Here. Hold Phillip."

I handed him to Ashley and went to me and Chris' bathroom. I took the bag of pills I had stashed under the sink. In the kitchen, I dumped the bag of sample packs on the counter.

"How's he doin'?" I asked Ashley.

"Oh he's fine."

"Good."

I popped every single one of those sample packs and I filled a glass of water from the tap. Ashley's friend was back in the kitchen now, Zachariah at her side.

"What's going on?" the friend asked.

"I don't know," Ashley said. "She's going crazy or something."

I scooped up handful after handful of the pills and swallowed them with water. I kept going till there were no pills left. I was sure I had taken enough to kill myself—but you have to be careful about that. Sometimes people who do suicidal things don't really want to die—they want the pain to stop. I'm pretty sure I'm in this latter category.

Anyway I *had* taken enough pills to kill me, which is a gesture that deserves to be taken seriously, regardless of the precise intentions. I said to Ashley:

"Do you know what to do in case of an emergency?"

She nodded.

"Well we're having one now."

And I went to Chris' and my bed and I laid down to die.

- - - -

I don't remember what happened after that 'cause I had taken so many fucking Ultram that I was in a blackout like when you drink too much alcohol. I later learned that the chain of events was: Ashley called Chris—he was at a friend's and he rushed home as soon as he heard the news. Apparently he didn't think I needed medical attention. 911 was not called—no ambulance was sent to the house. The next day I was lying in bed with my back to Chris and he was talking gently with me:

"Look, I don't know how much you can hear me right now, but I want you to know I'm not angry—I'm not upset with you in any way. I'm glad you're ok. I know how hard it is to take care of the kids, especially at night when they're hyper. If you need, we can find someone to help. And maybe Ashley doesn't need to have friends over when it's just you. The thought of you not being here..I mean I'm no doctor, I don't know why people kill themselves, but if that's what you wanted to do, I think we should call that psychiatrist—what was his name? I mean are you worried for yourself? Do you even want to be here with me, and the kids, and everything we do? Do you think this was just a one-time thing, or are you worried you might do it again? I kinda need to know."

I closed my eyes and there were tears seeping out between my eyelids. I rolled over on the bed so Chris could see my face, and I was so scared at what I had to say if I was to answer his question honestly. Not scared of what it would mean to him—I was scared of what it meant about me.

"I'm worried," I said.

"That you'll try to kill yourself?"

I nodded, and the tears flowed more, and Chris hugged me.

- - - -

That same day I checked myself into a mental hospital. It wasn't some fancy schmantsy mental hospital, it was just a floor of a regular hospital that they had turned into a mental hospital: no sharp edges, chairs made out of foam, and a cord on a pay telephone so short that no one could possibly hang themselves with it. Unfortunately that also made it very difficult to make calls—you were always leaning into the phone to get close enough to the hand piece.

I stayed there for two weeks. It might have been shorter if I had answered the admitting questions differently, but once you answer "yes" to questions about self-harm and harming others, it's no longer your right to check yourself out of the hospital—even though you checked yourself in.

The kids came to visit me a couple times. It was pretty embarrassing. Zach was asking me why I was staying here and it was impossible to answer in a way that he would understand. Ashley stared at me with her arms folded. Chris tried to be the glue that held our family together and that seemed ridiculous to me. I laughed at him when he talked and eventually he stopped talking. When visiting time was over, I was relieved. I got to go back to my room, put a sheet over my head, and lie there thinking about how crazy the world is. Or maybe it's me who's crazy, considering I'm locked up in a psych ward. Or maybe it's both—maybe I became crazy trying to fit into the world.

## 59
I don't remember much about my first mental hospital experience—I was in a daze for most of it. There was a lot of group therapy and structured activities and sharing a room with two to three people. Sometimes they would switch my room for no apparent reason. It wasn't a foreign experience to me—I had stayed in foster homes where you sleep in a room with lots of kids. You have to make friends in a situation like that—especially with the ones who want to be your enemy. A psych ward, a foster home, is like a miniature jungle. There is a food chain. Animals do die. Maybe it's more like politics, but anyway, I enjoyed being around the people that were in this first psych ward I'd been to. There was an old guy who sang Elvis songs at bedtime—I thought it was sweet and the nurses did too. There was a guy who was always talking about how he liked to kill cats.

"Why you hate cats so much?" a guy named Ruis asked.

"The reason I hate cats," this guy said, and he went over to Ruis. "I fucking *hate cats* so much..that before I came here, I rounded up stray cats from all around my mom's house—"

"Why your mom have so many stray cats around her house?" Ruis asked. "Does she feed them?"

"Well I guess she must, if she has that many of 'em."

"Yeah I guess she must," Ruis said.

"But anyway," the cat hater said, "if you'll let me speak."

One of the nurses told cat hater it was time for his meds.

"I'll be there in a *minute*."

The nurse walks away.

"So you's rounding up these cats," Ruis says.

"Yes I'm rounding up every single ugly, disgusting, skinny little cat I can find in my mom's yard. And I put them in a bag. And I threw them in the river."

"It's time for your meds now, cat boy."

The nurse had brought his meds to him, along with a cup of water. He swallowed several pills and I wondered what they were. When he was done the nurse checked his mouth and left.

"Also," he said, "I hit a cat in the head with a hammer once because I reached under a house to get it and it scratched me. There is nothing I like less than being scratched by a cat—nothing. You can put ice picks in my motherfucking *dick*, but do, not, let, a, cat, scratch me."

"Language," one of the nurses said.

"Sorry," the cat guy said. "But I'm telling you, you haven't *lived* until you've seen a cat's brains come out of its ears. Forget skydiving, forget Disneyland—get yourself a hammer and a stray cat—*that's* the real key to entertainment on this planet."

And so on and so forth, he continued telling us stories of how he had killed and mutilated hundreds—maybe thousands—of cats. I felt entertained by his stories and was very comfortable being in the same room as him. It's a weird experience when you're locked in a ward full of crazy people and the fact that you're there indicates you're crazy too. It gives you something to think about. And Reader, Dear Reader, while you contemplate whether you belong in the same room as a vicious cat mutilator, please consider my strong opinion that the veil between sanity and insanity is so thin that it's almost not even there.

But I liked the cat guy—and I love cats. But that didn't get in the way of my seeing his point of view.

- - - -

Sometimes while I was in that ward, a high-up doctor, like a supervisor or something, would take us out in a fenced-in yard and we would take our time walking around. Of course they were monitoring our every move and using that information to figure out what meds to put us on and when we should be allowed to leave. I always wanted to have bubbles and they must have provided them for us because I did have them every time we went outside. I blew bubbles in the yard, covered it with so many bubbles you couldn't be there without one hitting you. I would be last in line as we walked in a circle around the fenced-in area and the head guy, the psychiatrist who took us outside, would walk with me and we would talk about the most interesting things—he was much better to talk to than the nurses. He was smarter and creative—like me.

"What are you doing, Davina?"

"What do you mean?"

"What are you doing with your hands?"

"I'm blowing bubbles!"

I dipped the stick back inside the plastic container and blew some for him. One popped right on his nose.

He smiled.

"Why am I in here?" I asked.

"I don't know yet. But sometimes when you've been lied to as a child, then in adulthood it's possible to have less of a solid boundary between what is real and what is not."

"Why do you think I was lied to as a child?"

The psychiatrist walks slower so we have privacy from the other patients walking in a circle.

"If you had a parent or parents who acted like something was ok, when it wasn't, then you can grow up with a fuzzy boundary between reality and imagination because on one level you had things done to you that were not ok, and on another level you have adults—who children implicitly trust—telling you that what they're doing is ok, or simply ignoring what went on—which is another way of saying that it's ok."

"So you think I have a fuzzy line between reality and imagination?"

"Yes, I do."

He said it in the most gentle way, and he walked up ahead to talk to someone else.

I blew bubbles on him as he went, and they covered the entire courtyard. When I went to put the stick back in the bottle, there was no bottle—my left hand was empty. I looked at my right hand and where there should have been a bubble wand, there was nothing. I looked behind me—no bubble stuff. I looked at the nurse who had given me the bubbles and I must have had the strangest look on my face because she had the same look on her face.

I looked around me and bubbles were still falling. I let one break in my hand, and I touched the spot where it broke and the spot was oily.

I broke my place in line and was reaching and diving and running to catch every last bubble. I had always had bubbles on my walks before, and something that psychiatrist said made me think differently—the bubbles weren't real?

When the last one fell against the fence and broke before I could catch it, I went catatonic—couldn't talk, couldn't move. That's the moment I realized that I couldn't trust my mind. Nurses carried me to my bed and all along the way I pointed to things and asked:

"Is that real?—Is that real?"

Every time I asked, they said yes.

"You're not psychotic Davina—everything you asked about is real."

They tucked me in and that was one of the biggest growth moments of my life—the moment I realized just how fucking *malleable* reality is. Reality isn't a fact. It's more like a shared theory.

- - - -

The next day in the yard, head honcho psychiatrist brought me some bubbles.

"Is this real?"

"Blow some and see what happens."

With trepidation, I opened the bottle and pulled out the bubble wand. I looked at my doctor.

He said, "Blow."

And I did, I did blow, and bubbles went all over the yard and instead of marching in their endless circle, all the other crazy people broke their places in line and chased the bubbles. I blew more and more and more and everyone ran and stuck out their tongues and let the bubbles land unpopped on their clothes.

I looked at my doc.

"So..that's how I know that it's real?"

"That's one way, yes—you can check with others, you can gauge their reactions. But nothing is really real, Davina, until it's real up here."

He tapped his head.

I blew bubbles in his face.

He smiled, and told me we'd talk more, and then he turned and left.

Most people do suck—I'm serious, most people are rapists and assholes or at the very least dishonest—and I'm probably one of the ones who suck—but there are a few, like that psychiatrist I met at my first mental hospital, who are so wise and kind and beautiful that they're like spirit guides—some divine being that comes down to Earth in human form to touch your life. He talked to me about figments of my imagination, then made what was just in my mind turn real. My memories of that doctor are so surreal, the things he said so perfectly tuned to what I needed to hear, that sometimes I wonder if he, too, was just a figment of my brain.

## 60
While I was in the hospital I talked on the phone with the kids a few times. My friend Angelina (who took me to the hospital) came to visit. When I was talking to Chris, I heard his mom in the background like:

"Davina's not in there because of me, is she?"

And I was like: *Fuck you bitch that's so selfish.*

The doctors in the mental hospital were trying to get me out in time for some holiday—Easter, it was. I guess they had visions of me reuniting with my family and celebrating the resurrection of Our Lord Jesus or some shit. The fact is I fucking hate Easter.

I remember getting out and going home and not being sure what to do with myself. There were no subsequent therapy sessions or if there were I didn't go. I basically continued in space-cadet mode—I wasn't present at all, not for the kids, not for Chris. All we ate was cereal. I'm sure my kids hated me. Chris would bring home pizza on Fridays and for the kids it was like their celebration of a lifetime. Being in a mental hospital can be a traumatic experience. Most of all that you've been presented with the idea that *you might belong in a mental hospital*. I would sit at my place at the table and pull a single piece of pepperoni off one of the pizzas, put it in my mouth, then chew it exactly one-hundred times—stuff like that.

- - - -

Then we got WebTV. Chris got it for me so I'd have something to do. I didn't see how this was supposed to cure my depression or whatever I had, but I knew that when Chris got home he would ask me if I tried the WebTV, so I turned it on. I discovered a chat room (my first experience with one) and the social interaction hooked me instantly.

I went there every day, every hour..I was chatting with people from all around the world.

I decided I wasn't going back to work—I still had maternity leave from Phillip, time I hadn't used. Chris worked during the day—he got a job as a bus driver. While he was working I would go into TalkCity chat rooms—making friends, having online relationships..I even reunited with this girl that was my friend when Ashley was a baby. I lived for TalkCity. My personality online was not my personality in real life.

I met this guy online. His name was George Taylor. We talked. We flirted. It got sexual. He wanted to meet in real life and I was all for it. I would get offline as soon as Chris got home so he wouldn't know that I was on all day. Eventually I just stayed on, ignoring Chris, ignoring the kids, and soon my WebTV usage became a cause for argument. I don't know if Chris at that point knew I had met someone, or if he was just mad that I was paying more attention to the WebTV than to him, but we definitely had a problem. I obsessively checked the light on the WebTV to see if I had mail. And around that time I started moving further and further away from Chris.

That was when I started writing. It was the first time I realized I could write poetry—the first time I realized I had writing abilities of any kind. I shared poems with people online and they would sing my praises. I started writing all the time—when I was walking, in my room..I had these weird notebooks filled with poetry and prose. I kept my notebooks with me at all times—I even woke up at 2am to write, and sometimes it was hard to get to sleep because I kept having all these ideas I needed to write down. Here's one I call "grave robber":

> my ugly secrets  
> my dirty past  
> the darkness shrouding me  
> like a grave before im dead  
> i hide and cower shutting my eyes  
> suffocating on my silence  
> you come with a shovel  
> and lamp  
> and you brush away spiders  
> pulling words from my mouth  
> like jewels  
> you pocket my secrets  
> and you dust off my past  
> and you walk away whistling  

They weren't all like that. Some were love poems. And yes, some were love poems to George Taylor. He liked what I had to say. And this is just minds interacting, just words from our brains and our souls. George was in Chicago, I was in Yuba City—it seemed safe enough. But George and I got really intimate (not just sexually, but saying "I love you" and stuff like that). Then instead of just chatting we talked on the phone—like every day.

Chris knew.

Of course Chris knew—every day he'd come home and I'd be curled up all cozy in a reclining chair talking low and sweet into this Chicago man's ear. I wouldn't even finish the call, I'd keep talking to George and I'd tell him Chris got home so I couldn't talk sexy anymore and I'm sure Chris heard me say that—he had to. Chris would do his best with the kids and I know it was selfish of me and *not very motherly* but the truth is I wanted to talk with George more than I wanted to do anything else. Even just in words, he gave me things that Chris couldn't give.

My actual life, the one in front of my eyes, was less and less satisfying. Chris and I argued constantly. And I admitted: it was my fault. Everything had been perfect—we were a happy family raising kids and loving each other and laughing and hugging and kissing and Chris and I even used to have sex every once in a while..and me, only me, no one else, had treated everyone shitty and took it for granted that we would always be together.

- - - -

Slowly I decided I was unhappy and then slowly I had the idea that I could do something about it and then, by the same process, I decided I did not want to not be there anymore. There, around Chris and his family—there, in Yuba City—and this is hard to admit, but maybe there, around my kids.

Unfortunately, I didn't know how to drive. I didn't have a car. I didn't have much money. I felt helpless—and then angry—that I was so dependent on everyone. Mom suggested that I "come out here" for a while, visit, then come back. As much as I hated my mother, this seemed like a good idea. I talked with Chris about it.

"But you're coming back, right?"

"I'm coming back, yes, of course."

"How long is this break going to last?"

"I really don't know, Chris—if I knew I wouldn't be taking it."

I gave him a really hard time. I wouldn't answer his questions. I never told him any specifics about George, even though it was clear to him that I was having a long-distance affair.

"I'm not sure I can take care of the kids," Chris said. "Even with help."

"You won't have all the kids."

"Oh?"

"I'm taking Phillip with me."

"Alright, I agree," he said. "That makes sense. Are you sure you have to do this? I mean..are we over?"

I hugged him, held him tight.

"We're not over. But I can tell, by feeling and listening to my brain, that if I stay here I'm going to end up back in the hospital. You don't want that, right?"

"Davina, I definitely do not want you back in the hospital. I support you. If this is what you have to do, then this is what you have to do."

"It is, baby. It is."

- - - -

So we agreed that I would take Phillip, and Chris would get help taking care of Zach and Ashley. Actually I insisted on taking Phillip because I didn't think that Chris could take care of him, even with help.

I was depressed even more every day I didn't have the grit to tell Ashley and Zach what was about to happen. Finally it got so close to my leave date that I had to.

I made them sit on the couch and I sat on the floor in front of them.

Ashley said, "Mom, you're scaring me."

"Why are you scared, baby girl?"

"Because this seems like big news and big news is usually bad."

I stroked her knee.

"It is big news but it's not bad, ok—you can get through this."

All of their eyes were on me.

"I'm going to take a trip to New York to see my mom. Ok? That's all it is. And we can call and talk on the phone and it'll be just like I'm right here with you."

Ashley said, "How long are you going for?"

I told her I didn't know.

Ashley's face got that puffed-up feeling that children get before they cry. Then she took some deep breaths and said:

"Did you even consider taking us with you?"

"Yes I did! I did. But I thought that because you're so settled in school and you have all your friends that it would be better if you stayed here."

"What about Zach?"

"He's staying here with your father."

"What about Phillip?"

"He's coming with me."

"Why does he get to go?"

"Because he's too young for your father to take care of. He needs his mom right now."

Ashley bit her lip and looked straight ahead. I could see her little torso shaking with fear, hurt, something. She was gripping the edge of the couch cushion and her knuckles were white. I saw her calm herself down—that child was amazing. She pushed down the tears and then stood up and gave me a hug.

She said, "Have a good trip."

And she left the room.

I reached for Zach and pulled him toward me and hugged him tight, so tight. I couldn't believe Ashley's strength. I couldn't imagine where it came from 'cause it sure as hell didn't come from me. I hugged Zach and told him everything was going to be ok—something I certainly didn't believe at the time. And as I hugged him I thought of Ashley. She was obviously sad but the only thing she ever showed me was bravery—and every time I thought of that it made me cry some more.

- - - -

Two days later me and Phillip got on a bus. Chris, Ashley, Zach—they were waving at us through the tinted window of this Greyhound. I immediately regretted my decision. Somehow, on the bus, about to leave my family behind, everything in Yuba City and in our house and in my relationship with Chris seemed perfect. *I* was the one creating all the problems—I had always been the one.

I said, "Wave!" and I made Phillip wave.

The whole family below waved back at him—at us—and for a glimpse I knew that I was loved. But I also felt, right then, the transience of love—from lovers, from parents, from children, from friends. The people you love when you're born are not the people you love when you die. Love is just part of the roles we play. And holding Phillip as his puppeteer, making his hand wave to the three outside, I felt that love was thin, and fleeting—and I felt as empty as I had ever felt my whole life.

My family stayed there until the bus drove away and I peered out the window until I couldn't see them anymore.

I cried all the way to New York.

## 61
Phillip and I were each other's worlds on that trip. Sometimes there was an empty seat so we could spread out—most of the time the seat next to us was filled with some homeless or mentally ill or very old person. I talked to them, heard their stories—you don't know the real America until you've traveled on a Greyhound bus.

I changed Phillip's diaper while he was in my lap. People made sounds, told the bus driver to kick me off, leave me in the middle of Kansas with my nasty shit-smelling baby. I only responded to this once. I didn't get out of my seat. I didn't look at the person I was talking to. I just said:

*"Shut. Your. Mouth."*

That's all I had to say. No more words were necessary. After I said it there was complete silence—all conversations stopped. That bitch two seats behind me knew I was talking to her, and by the way I said those three words she *knew* that I would fiercely protect my baby—even against her insults. That bitch *knew* that if she complained *one more time* about how my baby's shit smelled, that I would come back there, cut the bitch's head off, shit down her throat, put her head back on, slap the piss out of her and ask her how my baby's shit smelled *now*. You don't mess with a mother's babies.

But I learned a lot on that trip from listening to people's stories. I learned that I was not alone—that a lot of people, when they're trying to quit drugs or they run out of money or their parents hate them or everyone in the small town they come from hates them..they get on a bus. They go to a new part of the country and start again.

Yuba City, California to Binghamton, New York is four days on the Greyhound bus. Phillip and I had to change busses three times. We used public restrooms, we ate our meals out of snack machines, we slept in line waiting to get on the next bus—literally laid on the concrete floor with people standing before us and after us and we slept while holding our place in line. We had to be near the front of the line so I could get a window seat—which is the only place I can ride on a Greyhound without going crazy.

Being on a Greyhound is like the frog in the kettle where you turn the heat up slowly. At first you're ok—the seats are kinda small but it seems cool. It's just how I imagine a space shuttle launch (one of the ones that didn't explode). You get to look out the window, there are interesting people to talk to. The air conditioner works. Everything is fine. But by the hour, the air conditioner shuts off, those interesting people turn out to be really annoying people, it's dark but you can't sleep and out the window is blackness and the occasional factory—which at night just looks like a bunch of lights hung in the air and somewhere at the top is a pipe with devil flames burning off the waste into our atmosphere.

My mom wasn't there when we got to the bus station in Binghamton. I had to call her, and she was sleeping, and it was the middle of the day, and I had given her my itinerary days before. After I called her it took *hours* for her to find the bus station. I was sitting against a wall with Phillip in my lap. About a million times this security guard came around and said, "Can't sit against the wall," but I was too tired to obey. He'd come around every fifteen minutes and slightly reinvent his order: "Find a new place to sit." "A bus stop is no place for a baby." "They're gonna be waxing that floor soon, you'll have to move." I didn't even look at him after the first hour or so. It was obvious that no one had waxed this floor in a long, long, time (if ever) and I knew that I could wear that security guard down—he had no idea what I had been through in my life. You can't blame him—he just didn't know! How could he possibly know that one of the skills I had acquired in adulthood was called: *Fuck you. I don't have to do anything you say.*

- - - -

This security guard had more problems to deal with when my mom got there. She came in smoking a cigarette. She was probably smoking in the car, and knowing her mind she viewed this trip into the Greyhound station as one quick enough that there was no reason to leave her cigarette outside.

The security guard was all over her.

"Miss! Miss! You can't smoke that in here!"

Lisa just walked right past him.

When she saw me and Phillip she ran over to us and made a huge display of how much she missed us and:

"Look at my little grandbaby!"

"Miss, your cigarette."

My mom takes Phillip from my hands. She throws him in the air dangerously but does manage to capture him with one arm as he falls down.

"Miss I'm gonna have to ask you to extinguish your cigarette."

She takes it out of her mouth and tries to put it in the security guard's mouth.

In this gravelly voice, she says, "Take a drag. It helps with anxiety."

"I don't have anxiety. I just need you to step outside and finish your cigarette, then you can come back and reconnect with your daughter."

"But purely hypothetically," my mom says, "if I stepped outside with my cigarette, wouldn't that lower your anxiety because you wouldn't have to worry about losing your job?"

"We're going," I say. "Help me up."

"I'm not gonna lose my job over you."

"But doesn't it make you anxious just thinking that you might?"

"Mom. Someone. Help me up."

"What's wrong with you that you can't get up by yourself?"

"Nothing's wrong with me I've just been packed into a bus for three days!"

I get up and take my baby. I head for the glass doors. Mom is still back there arguing with the security guard and they're each threatening to call the police and Lisa is trying to emasculate this guy by pointing out that he's basically useless as a security guard because he has no gun.

"Mom! Let's go."

She leaves the security guard. She never puts out her cigarette for the security guard but when I ask her not to smoke in the car with Phillip she happily agrees.

- - - -

Staying with my mom was harrowing. She couldn't stand the baby touching anything in her house—she had all these low tables with blown glass sculptures and stuff that Phillip could swallow. She was collecting dice—which is something I didn't even know you could collect. But you can. There are a million kinds of old dice made by colonial Americans and slaves and shit. Instead of her putting up the dice where Phillip couldn't reach them, Mom left them where they were and we occasionally had a crisis over which trinkets Phillip had possibly just swallowed. We only lasted two weeks in that house.

I smiled at him and he didn't smile back. I felt like I was a horse being looked over by a potential buyer. I guess he didn't like that I had a little bit of a belly—but come on, dude, I told you I had three children. I told myself that he had seen my avatar so he must at least like my face.

"Hi Chad," I said, extending my arm, hoping to at least get a handshake.

He didn't reach out to meet me.

"Well, shall we go?" he said.

"Sure."

I followed him outside, throwing away my trash, and the counter girl I had been with all evening waved and smiled—but it was a pitying smile, and I wasn't sure exactly who she was pitying but I hoped it was me.

## 66
I followed Chad to his apartment. He drove a brand new Jeep Cherokee—I drove my old Nissan Sentra (who, in terms of miles we had traveled together, was pretty much my best friend at this point). When I went to pools, boys looked at me and wanted to fuck me—when Chad looked at me it was a look of disgust, like: *How dare you suggest that a prince like me fuck a frog like you!* He opened his apartment and we went in.

Just because when you finally see me and discover that I'm not a Victoria's Secret model, that invalidates everything we had—even our friendship? Also, you'd rather jack off to some magazine than fuck a real live pussy? If you don't like the way I look then let's fuck in the dark—but let's do *fuck*. What a waste. I knock on the bedroom door.

"What is it?"

I try the doorknob—it's locked.

"I'm changing clothes," he says—which is bullshit.

I step back from his door wondering how you can go from telling your most intimate secrets to someone over the phone and being sexual in your talk to the point of arousal and orgasm, to being basically the wrong cut of meat to play with in person.

I took a shower, hoping that he was just turned off by how a person smells when they've been sleeping in their car for a few days. I stood under the shower head and watched three liquids—the shower water, my menstrual blood, and some of my tears—all mix together on the shower basin.

I cleaned the shower so that it would be nice for Chad when he took one in the morning.

I found the hall closet and got a huge blanket to wrap up with on the couch. I didn't watch TV because I didn't want to disturb Chad. Same with the internet—I didn't want him to come out here and need the internet and me be on it—so I left the computer alone.

Eventually I laid on my side.

I thought about body dysmorphia—where skinny girls think they're fat. They think they're ugly when they're actually pretty. I think I'm the opposite—I think I'm pretty when in fact I'm too ugly to attract a real-life boyfriend, too ugly for basically my best friend to have sex with (even in the dark), and too ugly to live.

That might sound unreasonable but it's how I felt at the time.

I waited for Chad to get desperate enough for pussy that he would unlock his door and invite me to sleep with him in his bed. I would have given him the sex of his life. But he never came out. He never unlocked his door. And I laid there on his couch telling myself that I was too ugly to live.

I said that to myself over and over.

*Too ugly to live.*

*Too ugly to live.*

*Too ugly to live.*

Thankfully, finally, I fell asleep—and my demons fell asleep with me. I thought I had come to stay with a lover. It turned out I didn't even have a friend. It's shit like that that'll make a person weary in this life.

The next morning I woke up way earlier than Chad—I woke up at 4am. I had visions of opening his door, crawling on top of him, and giving him a good morning fuck, but I figured he hated me so much for what I was that (even if I could get past the locked door) Chad would probably kick me out if I tried some shit like that.

Instead, I wrote on his computer. His internet required a password even though he lived alone—total geek—so I opened up Word and wrote my poetry there. It was kind of sad and all about people you think you know turning on you. It happens to me often—I don't have many friends. I left some suicidal poetry up on Chad Bailey's screen, in plain view, so that he might get worried and actually engage me in discussion, or put his arm around my shoulders, or even give me a hug—I *needed* some kind of contact.

At 5am I cleaned the bathroom. I was scrubbing and disinfecting and had a gloved hand down his toilet bowl when Chad knocked on the doorframe.

"What are you doing?"

"Just cleaning up."

"It doesn't need to be cleaned. I clean my bathroom every week."

"Well, you missed some spots. Like down here?"

I point to the base of the toilet.

"I found your whole universe of pee down here. Don't be offended—all guys miss the rim some of the time."

"I don't ever *miss the rim*."

"Then some of your guests haven't learned the finer points of peeing standing up."

"All my friends know how to pee standing up."

"Guess you only have guy friends, then?"

"All I'm saying is there's a universe of pee down here and I'm cleaning it up."

"I haven't disinfected the shower yet but if you want to go in there and try to get clean with all that orange *muck* that's growing on the walls, go ahead. I'd recommend you get someone in here—a specialist—who can remove potentially dangerous biologicals, before you go back in there. I was actually going to ask you if you had any dust masks."

"*Dust masks?*"

"You know..little thing..goes around your head..semi-porous membrane that keeps you from breathing in fumes or some kinds of household bacteria."

"Ok. Stop cleaning my bathroom now. I'm gonna come back here in five minutes. When I come back, you, the chemicals—all gone. What made you think you could clean my bathroom in the first place?"

"It was dirty," I said. "I was trying to help."

After Chad's shower, he got himself some breakfast—Teddy Grahams in a large bowl with water over it. That's right: water. He held the bowl of Teddy Grahams under the tap in the sink and drowned them with water.

"You know that's not a cereal, right?"

"What isn't?"

"Teddy Grahams. It's a cracker. See how soggy it gets so quickly?"

"I like Teddy Grahams."

"I'm not saying you shouldn't *like* them—I'm just saying they're not cereal."

"They sell them in the cereal aisle," Chad says.

Well *la dee da*, what can you say to that?

"What's with the water?"

"I have kidney stones so I have to stay away from calcium."

"That's a myth, you know."

"What's a myth?"

"That a low-calcium diet will prevent the formation of kidney stones."

"Have you ever had a kidney stone?" Chad asks.

"No."

"Well they hurt like hell—some people say even worse than childbirth."

"I don't believe that for a second."

"Neither do I. I mean pushing a coconut out your vag has to be worse than kidney stones—it *has* to be. But kidney stones hurt a whole lot, too, and if I can avoid passing another one, I'll eat cereal with water and never drink another glass of milk for the rest of my life."

"Don't you think in general that's kind of a squeamish way to live life?"

"How is that squeamish?"

"Well, you're avoiding milk, which you apparently like, just to avoid some pain now and then."

"Tell me again how that's squeamish?"

"It's squeamish," I say, "because you're passing up one of the good things in life—milk—just so you never get another kidney stone—which I remind you is a total myth. That's squeamish. You've got to get your hands dirty in all the ecstasy—and the agony—of life. We talked about this on the phone. Why are you so different now that I'm here."

He finishes chewing his bite of water-soaked Teddy Graham fragments.

"I've got to to go work, Davina. Television's yours, kitchen is yours, pantry is in there. Anything in the fridge, obviously the computer. Use whatever you want but please don't go in my room. And don't clean—this is how I like it. If I wanted it deep cleaned I'd hire a fucking maid. You're my friend—you don't have to clean my apartment, ok? I do love you, Davina—I guess I was taken off guard when you actually showed up."

"But we talked about me coming here."

"I know. I'm sorry. It surprised me anyway."

"Do you still like me?"

"Yes—*yes*. Of course I do. I'll see you after work and we'll talk about this more, ok?"

"Ok. Can I have a hug?"

Chad comes to me and gives me a decent hug. Then he goes to the door.

"Call me if you need anything."

I nod, sadly, knowing this is going to be one of the most boring days in my life.

Chad leaves. I lie down on the couch and look at the spackle pattern on the ceiling. Chad's door is open. It takes me about fifteen minutes to get up the courage to go in there.

He's got another computer, with the internet password protected there, too. He prob'ly just forgot to give it to me 'cause he's used to living alone. Or possibly he's a small-time pusher of child porno and he doesn't want to freak out his girlfriends.

I lied in his bed. I masturbated. I masturbated to the image of someone who didn't even *like* me—what power that gives him over me. Shit, I deserve some kind of pleasure. So I gave it to myself and found myself looking around his apartment, checking in little boxes and in closets and under his bed. Under his bed were zillions of shoe boxes, each held closed with a rubber band. I painstakingly removed every box from underneath that bed and opened every one, recording their original positions in my mind so I could put them back the same way.

It was just a bunch of junk: expired lottery tickets, fuckin' *baseball* cards—what a loser. This wasn't the guy I talked to on the phone. The guy I talked to—*my* Chad—was a poet and a philosopher, not some cubicle dweller who volunteered to have his soul sucked out daily, inch by inch, drop by drop.

There were other boxes down there. One contained—I shit you not—the pop tabs from maybe a thousand cans of Coke. Another box held accessories for a phone that was years old—a phone that nobody was ever going to use again. One had the pieces to the game Mouse Trap, without the board to construct the trap on. One was filled to overflowing with empty ChapStick containers—all the same brand and type (ChapStick Medicated). I was starting to think Chad had some sort of mental disorder—maybe OCD. Then I came upon a box that interested me:

In this box, which was a red Nike shoe box with a big-ass *schwiiiiiing!* on the side of it, were hundreds of pictures of Chad Bailey with different girls. It was never the same girl twice. Some were selfies taken during sex—some were just him and a girl posed next to a duck pond. I looked at these pictures for a long time—in fact I tried, with each girl, to figure out what it was that was special to Chad. There were all kinds of girls in those pictures—some skinny, some chubby—there were brown-haired ones, blond ones, black girls, mixed, white, Asians. I struggled to guess why I wasn't among them. I picked out the one that I thought was the prettiest of the bunch and wrote, "FUCK YOU," over their faces. I put that picture on the top of the others. Then I closed that box and replaced all the boxes under his bed.

I went through Chad's medicine cabinet but there was nothing there that interested me.

Then I went through each cabinet in the kitchen, from the bottom to the top, from the left to the right. I looked behind dishes and boxes of detergent, canned foods, about a hundred boxes of off-brand macaroni and cheese, probably double that amount of ramen noodle packages which were all shrimp flavor, and various other foodstuffs like an unopened bottle of beef bullion cubes, and more. Much more, because in the highest cabinet on the rightmost side of Chad Bailey's kitchen is where he kept his prescription drugs.

And I mean he had a lot of them—like twenty bottles.

I never knew what they were—I didn't look at the labels. I just knew they must be something good because they were up high in a sort of secret place. I brought the bottles down to Chad's kitchen table. I poured myself a big glass of water. Then, one by one, I opened every bottle and took every pill that Chad's cabinet had to offer. I swallowed and swallowed and swallowed.

After I ate all those pills, I called George (who had lived with me and Phillip for six months in Binghamton). His secretary picked up and told me George was in a meeting but would I like to leave a message?

I said, "Yes, I would. Please tell George that this is Davina AND I AM GOING TO KILL MYSELF!!"

Then I hung up.

There was silence on the line, then Chris said:

He said, "Goodbye." And he hung up the phone.

I double-checked that I had taken every pill in the apartment, then I locked myself in Chad's bedroom. I put myself down on his bed—hopefully for the last time. I felt the real me lift outside of myself, like I was leaving my body. It was extremely peaceful.

In fact, that's the most peaceful I've ever felt in my whole life—it was wonderful—it was beautiful—and I felt a smile on my face.

I was so happy to be dying.

## 67
The next thing I remember is people banging on the door to the apartment. I could barely hear them—they were quiet and far away from me. People yelling..distant, mute. I smiled to myself—nothing matters. I didn't care what was going on out there. Then loud voices—Chad let them into the apartment and the EMTs broke down the bedroom door and there were like five people in the room and they were like:

"What did you take, Davina? Davina, what did you take?"

I mouthed the word, "Everything."

"It's out here," Chad said. "It's all over the table!"

One of the EMTs went with Chad into the kitchen.

He said, "Whoah."

When he came back into the room I told that particular EMT to:

"Take me to the hospital."

"Oh yeah," he said. "With all that shit you took, you *earned* your trip to the ER. No doubt about that. Why'd you take all those pills? You want to kill yourself?"

"No," I said blissfully.

"You just wanted the pain to end?"

I smiled and nodded at that man. He knows! He knows!

"Well better be careful next time 'cause there's no 'undo' button on death."

I smiled and nodded at him happily.

"Take her to Munroe Regional and I'll radio ahead the meds she took. Do you take any other medicine?"

I shook my head.

"Have you ever been admitted to a psych ward before?"

"I think so."

"Well think hard. It's important."

"Yes, I did."

"You did?"

"Yes."

"Did they give you a diagnosis?"

"It was called grammaflumpty."

"Grammaflumpty? That was your diagnosis? I've never heard of that before."

"That's 'cause I'm the only one who has it."

The EMTs laugh.

"What about major depression, bipolar, schizophrenia?"

"Mostly we blew bubbles. At least I did for a while before my doctor showed me they were fake."

"Delusions, hallucinations," my EMT said.

"Do you ever hear voices inside your head?"

"My thoughts are from many different sources," I said.

"Ever hear voices telling you to kill yourself, kill other people?"

"No."

"Ever see little animals running around that no one else can see?"

"Like what?"

"Like little chipmunks, foxes, squirrels—they just appear out of nowhere in places they shouldn't be, like your apartment."

"Uh, no. Are there people who see that?"

"Yes, schizophrenics mostly. You've never seen anything like that?"

"I guess I should consider myself lucky. I've never seen anything like that."

"Ok, well lie back. We're going to get you into an ambulance and you're gonna get some proper care."

Chad Bailey was standing next to the bed through all of this. He didn't touch me, he didn't ask if I was ok. He just stood there like: *Why did you have to do this in my bed, in my apartment? Now that's always going to be the bed where Davina tried to kill herself.*

They put me on a gurney and Chad put some of my clothes in a plastic bag from Walmart. As they carried me through the kitchen, I could see all the empty bottles on this small round table and I had to convince myself of the reality that I had taken all those pills.

They took me away in an ambulance.

Chad didn't ride with me in the back of the ambulance.

He didn't sit beside me and hold my hand and tell me that I was going to be ok.

He didn't even follow me to the hospital.

I assume he went back to work.

- - - -

I was truly scared for the first time in a while. EMTs were taking my blood. They gave me an IV. I looked up at their faces and the bright lights shining down crossed with the fact that I didn't know them, gave them a soft-light halo effect—they were angels for a girl who doesn't believe in angels.

"You can just stop—I'm going to die anyway."

They laughed.

"That's good—you've still got your sense of humor."

"I'm not joking."

I reach for my IV and start pulling on it.

"Davina! Davina. That's keeping you alive!"

"*To what end?*" I said.

The one guy held my hand.

"It might not seem like it, but these feelings are temporary. There will be a day when you are happy to be alive, when you'll be glad you made it through today. Some days are harder than others, especially if you have a mental illness."

"Do you think I do?"

"I'm no psychiatrist, but if I had to guess I'd guess that you do. Did you know that the vast majority of suicides are attempted or completed by people with a major mental illness? So don't feel bad—people try to kill themselves all the time."

"What counts as a major mental illness?"

"Uhh..usually bipolar disorder, major depression, and schizophrenia."

"What makes those three the major ones?"

"I guess..probably because they're the most lethal. For example—I don't know if you know this, but—twenty-five to fifty percent of people with bipolar disorder attempt suicide at least once in their lifetime."

"Sucks for them," I say.

I'm floating in and out of consciousness.

Next thing I know I'm in a bed in the ER, sitting up, and these two eighteen-year-old nurses are mixing up a batch of this charcoal shit that's supposed to stick to all the pills and pill residue in my stomach. Then it goes through my intestines and gets all the partially-digested pills and pill chemicals, preventing it from entering my bloodstream.

One of the nurses says, "It's gonna taste kind of nasty!"

The other hands me a plastic cup filled to the brim with a mixture of the charcoal chemical and water.

I slurp it down—it's not that bad.

The nurses smile.

"Ok," one of them says, "just one more to go!"

I feel like a child being fed dinner by her parents—*Open wide! Here comes the airplane!*

One of the nurses tells me I'm going to have an epic shit when all that charcoal comes through my system. (She doesn't say it in those words.)

The nurses leave. They put a security guard on the door just in case I try to kill myself in the hospital. Soon I'm speaking with a doctor.

- - - -

"So wha'd you take?"

That was the first thing she said—no "Hello, my name is Dr. Narra," no, "How are you feeling?" but just, "What did you take?"

"My slippers."

"You came here for an overdose today, Davina, and I need to know what you took so that we can give you the best care possible."

"Don't you feel this is all too formal?" I say.

"It has to be. A hospital is a machine. We try to make sick people well."

"I wouldn't bother in my case."

"Are you having suicidal feelings now?"

"I don't know—I don't even know what suicidal feelings feel like."

"Well there are two kinds: passive and active. Passive suicidal feelings are where you think, 'My family would be better off without me.' Active suicidal feelings are where you're standing on the ledge of a six-story parking garage and you're about to jump. Do you have any of those kinds of feelings right now?"

"Is the story about the parking garage a real one?"

"Yes, it happened earlier this morning."

"Did he land like a butterfly?"

"No, he was crushed like an egg. Are you a poet, Davina?"

"How did you know I'm a poet? Did Chad tell you?"

"No one told me. But you're answering my questions in a poetic way, so I thought perhaps you were a writer or a poet."

I looked at this doctor *hard*. I considered whether I could trust her. I decided I could.

I said, "Usually people try to interject logic and reason and facts when I just want to say pretty things."

The doctor cocked her head.

"That's true—or I find it to be true of other people I know..and myself to some degree. Let's make a deal: you say pretty things as much as you want to, and when you feel like operating with me in the factual world, you do that. Is that ok?"

I nod.

"Ok, good," she says, and she looks to her clipboard to find her first question. She started at the very beginning—at my childhood. When we got to Jessie I told her that a big dog came into my room at night and lifted up my covers with his paw because he wanted to see my ta-ta and what started out as licking and touching turned into fucking and I was forever at the mercy of this beast who was bigger than me, more powerful than me, who had the ability to control when I ate and when I went hungry, and he was supposed to be a dog I could trust, like a family friend, but he turned into a monster and in the process of playing with me, turned *me* into a monster, too.

My doctor sat there and listened till I stopped talking. Then she said:

"Want to tell me the factual version of that story, too?"

"I can't."

This lady's kindness was blowing me away. If I could keep a trained psychiatrist with me all the time, and have her help me navigate life, I think I could do this *being alive* thing.

So she basically dissected my whole life—but in the kindest way—and then it was night and she left to go home and it was just me and the security guard assigned to keep me safe—from myself. Just having him there put my mind in the mode of finding ways to kill myself with just items from this room. There were plastic bags lining the various trash cans—you would think they would have figured that out by now. I could use my IV needle to make multiple punctures to my neck real fast so I could bleed out through my jugular—that would be hard to stop. Also this room has plastic tubing everywhere—I could just tie off my neck real quick and be done with it.

That was with the first security guard—he sat outside my room and periodically checked in so there would be no time for me to execute any one of those plans. But sometime in the night a new security guard came in and he sat inside the room looking right at me. I couldn't sleep—you could even say I was manic—because when this new guy actually had the courage or the interest to engage with the hospital's only suicidal patient, we started a big ol' conversation about Ocala and how many mosquitos were down here and where we were both originally from and I could tell he could tell I was manic but he didn't say anything even when I apologized:

"Sorry I'm talking so much—so fast."

"It's ok, I can comprehend you."

"You prob'ly get a lot of crazy people in here."

"Well, you know, we're all crazy to a degree and I try to approach new people like I've never met anyone like 'em before—you know, to give 'em a fair chance."

"But I attempted suicide—that's bad, isn't it?"

"I got a nephew who committed suicide."

"Why did he do it?"

"Like what was the *reasoning* behind his decision? It wasn't anything special. I'll probably never know exactly why. But he wasn't treated well by kids he went to school with—that's prob'ly part of it. You know, Davina, I think life just got too hard for him and his *soul* was hurting—you get what I mean? This world will hurt your soul something awful."

"Yeah," I said, wiping a tear from my eye.

"Want some tissues?" he said.

"No, I don't need any. I can use my hand—or the sheet. I don't want to create work for you—I mean all you're supposed to do is keep me from killing myself while I'm here, right? I mean as long as I don't actually *die in* the hospital, you guys are ok, legally..so you don't have to do anything *extra* for me, ok?"

"Like bring you a box of tissues?"

He sets a little square box of tissues in my lap.

I'm crying fully now.

"You must think I'm crazy. Thanks for these."

I pull out a tissue and wipe my eyes.

"Thanks for these. You didn't have to."

"Davina, you're acting like you're the enemy."

"I am the enemy—to myself."

"Well stop that. We all sick in our various ways. We all have strengths and weaknesses. Maybe I'm not a doctor, but I work in this hospital and here we all a team, from the psychiatrist right down to the security guards. We're all here to help *you*."

"Because I'm a fuck up."

"No, because you're sick. And we trying to make you well."

"You wanna hear a joke?"

"Why not."

"Ok, here it is."

"But after this joke, you have to promise me you'll get some rest. I don't want to get in trouble with these nurses."

"I won't, I won't get you in trouble—I promise."

"So let's hear it."

The security guard pulls his chair closer.

I sit up in my bed.

"Ok. So I'm walking across this bridge one day and I saw this guy standing on the edge, about to jump. I ran over and said:

"Chill out, dude! You don't want to kill yourself!"

"Why not?" he said.

I said, "Well, there's so much to live for!"

"Like what?"

"Well," I said, "are you religious or atheist?"

"Religious."

I said, "Me too! Are you Christian or Jewish?"

"Christian," he says.

And I say, "Me too! Are you Catholic or Protestant?"

"Protestant."

"Me too! Are you Episcopalian or Baptist?"

"Baptist," he says.

"Wow!" I say. "Me too! Are you Baptist Church of God or Baptist Church of the Lord?"

"Baptist Church of God."

"Me too! Are you Original Baptist Church of God, or are you Reformed Baptist Church of God?"

"Reformed Baptist Church of God."

"Me too! Are you Reformed Baptist Church of God, reformation of 1879, or Reformed Baptist Church of God, reformation of 1915?"

"Reformed Baptist Church of God, reformation of 1915!"

To which I said, "Die, heretic scum!" and I pushed him off the bridge.

The security guard's face is blank. He gets my joke but won't laugh.

"I could tell you another joke, shorter and less about suicide."

"Davina, I've sat in on a lot of suicidal patients but I've never met one like you."

"Sheth?" (I got Sheth off his name tag.)

"Yes?"

"Are you required to tell the doctor what I say to you?"

"I have to file a report, yes."

"Is my joke going into your report?"

"If I don't put it in, and next week you kill yourself, I could get fired. Then I wouldn't be able to feed my kids and pay for my house and my car and if I can't find another job that pays what I make here I might not be able to send my kids to college and stuff would just spiral from there."

"Ok, I understand. But this may affect my willingness to tell you jokes in the future."

"That's your call, Davina. But for now let's skip the jokes and have some quiet time." Sheth lowers the overhead light but there's plenty of light coming in from the hallway. I can see Sheth's position, and I can make a run for the hallway if he tries to rape me. That's a thing—that's something that happens—guys being attracted to suicidal girls. In fact I think in general men are more attracted to women who want to kill themselves, maim themselves, or destroy themselves in some unimaginable fashion.

Luckily, that's me :)

## 68
I was in the hospital overnight and in the morning they admitted me to a mental hospital. This involved me being put on a stretcher, wheeled out of the ER, and lifted by two big guys into the back of an ambulance. Sheth wasn't there—I never got to say goodbye.

The EMT riding in the back with me was extraordinarily talkative, which I wasn't in the mood for. I let him take my pulse and my blood pressure and temperature but I didn't speak to him—I was zoned-out from having hardly slept in the hospital and I was bummed that I hadn't gotten to say goodbye to Sheth. I don't like when things are suddenly wrenched away from me.

"We're almost there," the EMT said.

He said it like I was supposed to be happy about this fact.

The ambulance stopped.

The two of them helped me down from the ambulance.

It's a huge building, like something from the 1800s.

The three of us, two EMTs and me, walk up all these stairs. There are other people coming in and out. One girl, about five, was leaving the hospital. In one hand she held a threesome of helium balloons—in the other she held a stack of papers that looked like drawings she had made on the inside.

She looked at me, incredibly, and I felt like I knew her, like she was me at a younger age. I felt a little telepathy between us. She was saying: *Keep your spirit, don't let them break you down.* I was saying: *When you get older, men are going to take advantage of you. You keep your spirit, too.*

Inside the hospital, the front desk was completely walled up from desk to ceiling with thick, clear plastic slabs. Prob'ly for safety from the occasional violent patient who doesn't want to be admitted.

I was more than happy to be admitted—as long as they'd let me kill myself once the paperwork was done.

Me, the reception nurse, and one of the EMTs signed a couple pieces of paper and that was the handoff: the EMTs went back to their truck, one of them saying, "Good luck, Davina!" Then the people in the white coats came to me and each held one of my arms as we went through a door into the admittance wing. The door locked behind us, and I knew I was on a one-way trip from here on out.

- - - -

The thing you need to know is: *there is nothing to do inside a psych ward*. I mean there are a few things, like read the shittiest novels ever written—which are on a shelf in the main room, donated by people so thoughtful that they said to themselves: *I'm doing spring cleaning, here's a novel that I bought years ago that was so shitty I couldn't make it past the first ten pages. Oh, here's an idea, I'll donate it to that mental hospital down the road.* Well guess what, bitch, I can't get past the first ten pages either—next time use that shit for kindling.

When you walk into a psych ward, everybody knows you're new, because you're being accompanied by two white coats who are so big they could beat your ass into the ground if they wanted to. Everyone in that motherfucker—patients and doctors—are trying to size up how dangerous you'll be. 'Cause fights do break out in psych wards, and the veterans, who have been there two weeks or in some cases a lot more, they feel like they own the place—and they do. They've built up rapport with the staff, and they just know more of the history of the current set of patients—when you got there, they had been there before. They've adjusted to the routines and rules and permissions—and even though they're on green level (which means they can eat their meals off-ward, in the cafeteria..and they can go outside, while supervised, to play games in the grassy fields behind the hospital)..even though they can lose all their privileges in an instant, some of those green level patients are the ones you've got to worry about. Think about it: why are they still here after being on green level so long? To me it began to mean that it had been a couple of weeks since they fought someone, and they had gradually gained status from red, to yellow, to green, just by putting off the next inevitable bout of violence for a while—but that at any moment they might misinterpret something I said as an insult and fuckin' beat my ass to a bloody pulp.

I can't really say it was the psych ward that made me not want to kill myself. Actually, being there made me want to kill myself more.

They put me in a room and closed the door. It was a single room (for which I was grateful). But they were checking on me every fifteen minutes to make sure I wasn't tearing my sheets into strips, forming a noose, and hanging myself from..what?..there's nothing to hang yourself from in a psych ward. No rod for hanging clothes in the closet. The shower curtains are so weak they fall if you tug on them with your hands.

I stared out the window, which was heavily grated on the outside so no crazy people can escape. It was swampy out there and I was ok with being inside. Crazy as this may sound, I preferred this quiet single room to Chad Bailey's apartment. Part of why I preferred it is that it would be a lot harder to kill myself here than at Chad's apartment. That might sound silly, but I wanted love and a little taking care of from Chad Bailey. All I got from him was unspoken insults and a cabinet of who knows what pharmaceuticals got me into this place. I calmed down and sat at the desk. They had let me keep my notebook.

After a while I had a chill in my stomach—something empty and deathly and the sensation almost made me believe in spirits. I moved to the bed and covered myself with the one cheap-ass blanket they had given me—it had holes in it but as least it was blue.

I liked blue.

I covered my head.

Then I peeked out and saw my room. It was depressing. Everything was cookie-cutter—you just knew every room was exactly the same even though the people in them had every type of disorder there is. I wasn't sure I had a mental disorder? I tried to kill myself—but that doesn't count as a mental disorder, does it? People do it all the time—does that make them *crazy*? When I had my head above the blanket, I was really scared because I was in Florida, where I didn't know anyone except for Chad. There's no one here that knows me or is on my side. When I put my head under the blanket, I pretended I was a kitty pouncing robins and dandelions in a huge yard. I could romp around for hours and my owner was a gentle man who sat on the back porch smoking and listening to techno. He was just like me. And sometimes I'd rub against his leg to get him to pet me. It worked every time.

So in my hospital room at some psych ward in Florida, I stayed under my blue blanket for as long as I could and I thought about being a kitty. I thought about my gentle owner.

I said, "When I'm a kitty I want to eat a moth and lick your mouth and get dust from moth wings on your lips."

I was completely in my fantasy—I no longer believed I was in a mental hospital. I walked like a kitty. I purred like a kitty. When I left my room to go to the bathroom I brushed up against other patients and doctors and nurses like a kitty wanting love.

"Davina, no touching other patients."

"Meow," I said.

You know something about crazy? You can *go* all the way there, but you can never come all the way back.

- - - -

My first night at that psych hospital everybody was in bed and I was lying on my side with my back to the door so that I'd be less disturbed by the nurse who came 'round with a flashlight every fifteen minutes. He shined it right on the back of my head and I could see my silhouette on the wall. I like to cum before bed but when you're on suicide watch in a mental hospital you're never left alone long enough to make that happen. Then I realized that if I was crazier, I probably wouldn't care—I'd just masturbate anyway, disregard the nurse on suicide checks and get my pussy off.

It was dark for a while, then the flashlight creating a silhouette of my head, then the flashlight got bigger and bigger and the night nurse said:

"Davina."

"Yeah?"

"Dr. Dean wants to see you."

I felt the nervousness, the anxiety rising in me. Dr. Dean was the head psychiatrist of the hospital—I had only been here a day and even I knew that. At meals, some patients referred to him as a hero ("Dr. Dean—he saved my life. I wouldn't be here without Dr. Dean."). They said he had six PhDs (psychiatry, mathematics, architecture, painting, photography, and computer programming). It was something like that, anyway—everyone who listed his degrees listed them a little differently. But the six was constant, there was no argument there—Dr. Dean by all accounts had six degrees.

"Can I go in my pajamas?" I asked the night nurse.

"You can wear whatever you want. This is a psych ward, not a fashion show."

I agreed with the night nurse but I knew that every little thing a patient does in a psych ward is monitored and noted. At any given time someone is assigned to watch you and take notes on what you do. And later, your psychiatrist reads those notes. And those notes are a major factor in whether you get to *leave* the hospital or you get to stay a little longer.

So me and this night nurse—me in my pajamas—go to the nurses' station. The night nurse hands me off to a mental health technician named Abby. The night nurse goes back to his suicide checks and Abby takes me out of the secure ward, down a set of stairs, through a hallway of offices. There are name plates which indicate that in the daytime this hall is filled with counselors, psychologists, psychiatrists, physician assistants, and nurses.

Abby and I walk side by side.

She says, "It's nice to get off the unit, right?"

"Totally nice," I say. "I can't stand that guy shining his flashlight on me every fifteen minutes."

"I'll have a talk with him about that. It's not really necessary to use a flashlight."

"Why does he do it, then?"

"Prob'ly a power trip. He hates working night shift so he wants to make *everybody else* on night shift hate their lives—patients and nurses, mental health practitioners, the med nurse. He's just bored so he's fucking around."

"Do all the checks nurses use a flashlight?"

"No. He's the only one who does. It's a holdover thing—used to be you *had* to use a flashlight and not only that but you had to hold the flashlight on the patient long enough to see them breathe at least once—that was to verify the patient hadn't killed themself in their bed."

"How do you kill yourself in your bed?"

"People make ways."

"But how?"

Abby looks at me.

"I can't tell you that, Davina."

"Oh, sorry—I wasn't asking for myself."

"I know, don't worry about it."

"I was just curious."

"I know. It's ok."

Abby smiled. She swiped her security card through a door that led to another stairway. This time we went up the stairs.

"This is just a little shortcut," Abby says. "A mental shortcut. If we went through that hall that was just above us you would see people with illnesses that will keep them here their whole lives. I didn't think you would want to see that."

"Thank you."

"I have a feeling you're not going to be with us long," Abby said. "We're always glad to see you go, but if you ever need to come back, we're happy to see you then, too."

Now we were standing in front of Dr. Dean's office. Door ajar. Light coming from inside.

- - - -

"Is that Davina?"

"Yes, Dr. Dean. I'm sending her in now."

Abby pushes the door open.

Dr. Dean says, "Are you taking her back?"

"Yes, I'll wait for her outside."

"Thank you," Dr. Dean says.

He asks me to close the door, which I do.

He invites me to sit down in either of the chairs facing his desk.

"Which one do I choose?"

"Either one. Don't worry, I'm not taking notes on that."

So I sit down in the chair closest to the wall—it feels like protection.

He has beautiful paintings on his walls—Klimt.

"You like Klimt?" he asks me.

I nod.

Then I notice he had this poem/note thing I had written to Chad and put on the back of his toilet—I guess it was my suicide note. Then I see my notebooks on Dr. Dean's desk. One of those is open.

"Did Chad bring you those?"

"Yes. I asked him to bring you some clothes and if there was a suicide note to bring me that as well. I guess he thought your notebooks fit into the same picture so he brought those too."

"Can I have them back?"

"I think I'm going to hold onto them for a little while."

"I don't believe he even brought those!"

"Well, actually, I've learned a lot about you from reading them."

"I don't want you to learn about me—I want my notebooks back. Those are *my* notebooks. Those are *my* property."

"Abby. Can we get something for Davina's anxiety?"

"No," I said. "No. I don't need anything for anxiety. The only thing that's making me anxious is you reading my notebooks."

"I just felt like this conversation might go more smoothly if you could calm down."

"I'm calm—see?—I'm calm."

"Can you stay that way?"

"Yes."

"Abby never mind on that med."

Dr. Dean turned the open notebook to face me.

"What is this a picture of?"

"It's not a picture of anything. It's just junk from my mind. It doesn't mean anything, so if you're trying to psychoanalyze it, please don't."

"If you were someone else and you looked at this picture, what would you see?"

"An octopus."

"Really? I see a man with eight arms."

"Well a man with eight arms..*is* an octopus," I say.

"Do you draw from your dreams?"

"Daydreams, yeah."

"Why, do you not remember your dreams from the night?"

"No, I remember them. I just find my daydreams are more interesting. Why are you seeing me this late at night?"

"Does it make you uncomfortable to be in here alone, just you and me? We can ask Abby to come in and observe if you'd feel more comfortable."

"No, that's ok. You don't seem like the rapist type."

"Have you had experience with rape?"

"I—don't know. For me that's like asking a fish if she's had experience with water. Have I been raped? Yeah, I was raped by the father of my first child. If you want the details, read my memoir. But it's not really *about technical* rape—by-the-book, according-to-Hoyle rape—it's about respect, it's about power, it's about love. It's about men feeling *entitled* to women's bodies, whether it's to kiss them, fuck them, tell them what to do, how to act in society, how to be 'assertive.' What if I don't want to be assertive? What if I want to be soft-spoken and passive? Can I be soft-spoken and passive and still be part of the club?"

Dr. Dean was quiet, looking at me, evidently listening.

"I like to yell, too, and I like to fuck shit up."

"I can see that."

"Why, because I tried to kill myself?"

"No, because of the scars on your arms."

"You would cut yourself, too, if you had lived my life."

"I don't doubt that."

Then he brought out another of my notebooks and opened it to a place he had bookmarked. He reads a poem I wrote out loud to me—and he has a terrible reading voice. Then he asks me to tell him what it means.

"Have you read a lot of poetry?" I ask.

"Yes, quite a lot."

"Have you ever written a poem?"

"I don't think so."

"Well we're not exactly talking on the same frequency, are we?"

"I don't get what you mean."

"You've never written a poem—*in your life*—and you want me to explain what my poem *means*? Have you ever heard of the Beatles? Any of their songs make sense to you? What's your favorite Beatles song?"

"You're going to think this is funny but it's *Maxwell's Silver Hammer*."

"I don't think that's funny at all," I say, and I lunge across the desk and grab my notebooks.

Dr. Dean says, "Code green!" to Nurse Abby and a few seconds later while I'm standing in a corner of Dr. Dean's office with all three of my notebooks, using them as a defensive weapon, the PA system announces that there is a "Code green. Second floor. Dr. Dean's office." The message repeats itself until four big-muscled mental health techs come into Dr. Dean's office while I'm threatening anyone who comes near with a swap of my notebooks. Right behind the techs, I see Abby is preparing the shot—which is formally known as The Shot in mental hospitals—it's either Benadryl or Haldol and its dose is enough to turn you into the behavioral equivalent of Teddy Ruxpin for about three days.

The mental health techs lay me over Dr. Dean's desk like I'm about to get a spanking.

Strains of memories of Jessie looking at my butt when I was eight years old.

The techs—four strong guys—holding me down.

Memories of Jessie holding me by the hips and fucking his cock between my ass cheeks.

Someone pulling down my PJ pants in Dr. Dean's office and Abby administering the shot.

Then I was swirling down, swirling down a well with moss covering the walls. Abby was my friend—but she gave me the shot anyway.

I remember yelling at Dr. Dean about his Klimt paintings—calling him a pussy monger and accusing him of getting off on female patients in backless hospital gowns. I told him I was certain he was going to anally rape me once the Benadryl kicked in and I recommended that he use a stronger sedative "because *this* bitch ain't goin' silently into the night."

But down I went, into that bottomless well, and at the top I could see Dr. Dean and Nurse Abby and the mental health techs who had held me down. Even though I yelled at them, they said nice things to me like, "You'll feel better after a little rest," and, "This is just something to calm you down."

That's the last thing I remember for days.

## 69
When I woke up, all I could think about was getting my notebooks. I went to the front desk.

"Where are my notebooks?"

A nurse said, "What?"

"Dr. Dean has my notebooks. He's been studying them to determine what kind of degenerate I am, and I think by now he's had enough time to study them, so can I have my notebooks back PLEASE!!"

"Davina, are you feeling anxious? Do you want a PRN?"

(PRN is short for *pro re nata*, which is Latin and roughly translates to *as needed*. You're assigned a doctor at a mental hospital. The two of you decide together what your medicines are—with the doctor making most of the decisions, of course. But you always get a PRN—some medicine that you can request from the med nurse—and this is usually a sedative or an anti-anxiety pill of the weakest variety possible. In my opinion the only reason they give you a PRN is so that you—a patient in an environment you have very little control of—have something you can control. You find the med nurse, you get your PRN, a tiny portion or your illusion of control is restored, and it chills you out a bit.)

"Davina? Davina? Do you want your PRN?"

"*No* I don't want my PRN—I want my fucking notebooks!"

"Which notebooks, dear?"

"Dr. Dean has them. They're my *private* notebooks—I don't even know how he got ahold of them!"

"You probably brought them with you."

"I seriously doubt that. I was fucking half-conscious. I took a million bottles of pills."

The nurse behind the desk says, "Yes, I know that—it's in your file."

She said it like a human crossed with a chipmunk.

I stared her down. I was standing, she was sitting—I had the upper hand.

"Chipmunk bitch," I said, and turned to walk away.

Another nurse, this one a guy, said, "Talking like that ain't gonna get you outta here *aaaannnyy sooner*."

I went back to the chipmunk bitch.

"Have you seen your teeth in a mirror," I say.

She closes her lips.

"You're like a little chipmunk with a certificate from ITT Tech or some vanity educational program. What was it? Backwater Nurses 'R' Us? How long did it take you to get your certificate? Six weeks? You think you can learn how to deal with *me* in six weeks?!"

I was laughing, and laughing, and laughing in this nurse's face.

"You want the shot again, Davina?" said the male nurse.

"No, I'm all vaccinated up for this year. Thank you though."

"Ok, well why don't you take some time in your room and think things through a little bit. When you're on the unit I need to be able to trust you to be safe. Remember the unit rules we read every morning?"

"No, I don't. Maybe you forgot, but I've been in a coma the last few days."

"Well one of our rules is *no violence*. That means verbal violence as well as physical violence. You have to remember: harsh words can hurt people's feelings. You don't want others to use harsh words with you, do you?"

"I don't have any feelings anymore."

"I'm sure you have some somewhere."

"No, I don't. I used to be a kitty and back then I had feelings. You don't need to write this down," I said to the woman nurse right in front of me. "Like, you don't have to say, 'Patient thinks she used to be a cat.' It's a *metaphor—*it's a *metaphor*! Doesn't anyone think in metaphors? I bet I could present a plausible alternate perspective on every single note you've taken about me, you psycho bitch."

"Davina," the male nurse says. "Go to your room."
