I stared that male nurse down something terrible. I wanted *him* to know..that *I* knew..that I could take him. I don't care how big and strong you are. I don't care how many times a day you go to the gym. You *never, ever* go toe to toe with a crazy person.

"Davina, please. Out of respect for the other patients on the unit, will you please go to your room and chill out?"

"Yes," I said.

The male nurse said, "Thank you."

I turn and go, and as I go, I say, "But watch out for that bitch with the chipmunk teeth. It's none of my business if she's giving you head in the employee bathroom—but proceed with caution. Those teeth were made for stripping the bark off trees, not giving head like a pure, non-hybrid human being. So if you insist on getting head from a bitch with chipmunk teeth, just take precautions. File those fuckers back or make that bitch wear a mouth guard."

I looked back at the nurses' station. The male nurse was holding the chipmunk bitch around the shoulders and the woman was wiping her face with a tissue. That fucking bitch was crying. Damn. I wish she wasn't because it made me feel like a piece of shit.

- - - -

I stayed in my room for hours. It was a single room so I was able to cry freely. I was still on fifteen minute suicide checks and the nurse on check duty opened the door a little to make sure I was still alive and I guess he heard I was crying so he came in and pulled my desk chair over to the bed.

"What's wrong, Davina?"

"When do I get out of here?"

"When your doctor says you can go."

I wanted to bring up the notebook issue again but every time I did, it just made the staff think I was crazier than I even am. Like if you're at home and you lose something, people just think, "Oh, she lost something." But if you're in a mental institution and you lose something, about twenty people start making notes on what that might mean about your mental status. So I definitely didn't mention the notebooks.

I said, "When do you think that will be, when my doctor makes his decision?"

"Well I don't know, but usually it's a result of you showing that you can be safe on the unit."

"What do you mean, safe?"

"That you're not a danger to yourself or others—that's what we mean by 'safe.' "

From that moment on I vowed to be safe. You may have fantasies about being in a mental institution, but in reality it sucks. I made myself do whatever I thought would make those doctors and nurses and mental health technicians think that there was absolutely no reason for me to be there. I went to every meal, I took every medicine offered—I even went to morning group (which was difficult since it started at the frightening hour of 8am).

I even went to *evening* group—which almost *no one* goes to. But every time I did any of these participatory things, mental health techs made notes, nurses made notes, and each day at noon those people would present their notes to my doctor—they did this every day, Monday through Friday, and eventually Dr. Dean mentioned the idea of me leaving.

"Really?" I said.

"Yes, really. I'm thinking Monday or Tuesday."

"So I have to stay here over the weekend."

"I know weekends here are hard, but I'd like you to be observed on this new medication for a few more days."

I exhaled.

"Monday or Tuesday..wow!"

"It's good to see a smile on your face, Davina."

At that moment it struck me that the reason people are happier after spending time in a mental hospital is just that being in a mental hospital sucks so bad that the simple act of leaving one *makes you happy*. I did not share this insight with Dr. Dean.

"There is one problem, though, Davina."

"What's that?"

"The problem is we don't know who to release you to. It says here that you have no family in Florida?"

"Right."

"Where is your closest family?"

"California."

"What are you doing all the way over here?"

"I came to see a friend. Yep. I drove all across the country to see my friend."

"So I am thinking, still, how do we release you?"

"Can't you release me..like..on my own recognizance or whatever?"

"No. We have to release you to a family member or friend or to another hospital."

"Release me to Chad."

"Chad is your friend in Ocala?"

"Yes."

"Can Chad keep a close eye on you?"

"Oh yeah, definitely."

"Does he work?"

"Yes."

"Then how can he keep an eye on you? This is my problem, Davina—you came here with a suicide attempt and I need to make sure you will be safe once you leave."

"I won't kill myself again—I mean I won't try. I promise."

"I appreciate your promise but killing yourself or not killing yourself is not a matter of will. Do you know that the lion's share of suicides are committed by people with a major mental illness?"

"Which ones are considered major?"

"Depression, manic depression, and schizophrenia are the major ones. That is not to put down other very serious, life-changing or debilitating illnesses. It is just that those three are the ones that most likely result in suicide. And I am talking over ninety percent of suicides are coming from these three disorders."

"And you think I have one of those?"

"Maybe. Maybe depression. I am prescribing you Prozac, which is very safe and highly tested. Take only one per day."

"What happens if I take more?"

"If you take more and you have major depression, nothing at all will happen. You might feel uncomfortable, but taking a bottle of Prozac will not kill you. You might throw up. But, if you are a manic depressive—if you have bipolar disorder—then taking just a few more than directed could shift you into a manic state."

"What is that?"

"It is a very dangerous state where you would do things that you had no memory of afterwards, things like spending a lot of money or quitting your job for no reason. Manics are very sexual—but like, sexual on overdrive—you will have sex with people you would not normally have sex with. Manics are also psychotic—they have delusions and hallucinations..false beliefs and seeing or hearing things that others do not see. Not in every case—this is only sometimes true."

"So which one do I have?"

"The disorders?"

"Yeah."

"I don't know. I would need to observe you for much longer to make a formal diagnosis."

"But just..gut feeling..what do I have?"

"I think you have major depression. That is my best guess for now."

"Can you release me to Chad? This place is driving me crazy."

"You're right, there. Being here drives me crazy, too."

"But you get to leave at the end of the day."

"And you have to stay all day and all night. I do understand, Davina, and I will work on getting you released to Chad Bailey as soon as possible—probably next week."

I thanked him profusely—did everything but jump across the desk and grab my notebooks—and then I left the office, walking in the sanest way possible.

- - - -

Over the weekend, my mom called. This is the entire, verbatim record of our conversation:

"Hello..Mom?"

"Davina, George tells me you're in an insane asylum."

At this point I hear my mom snort a line of meth.

"It's not an *insane asylum*—it's a *mental health unit*."

"Damn, this is good stuff."

"Is someone there with you? Did you call me while you're snorting meth?"

"What's with the twenty questions? What matters is that I called."

"I'm glad you called."

"Well don't let 'em give you any of that pharmaceutical crap. It's all mind control! They've been putting fluoride in the water since the birth of Jesus. It's all to get some CEOs rich—do you know how much it costs them to make one pill? A penny. *One* penny. And they charge fifty dollars a pill to a man in a wheelchair about to jump off the World Trade Center."

"Ok, Mom. I gotta go."

"Watch the injections, too—it's all alien implants and the doctors are in on it. They want to track every human on this planet. It's called the New World Order—do you believe me?"

"Yes, I believe you."

And then I just hung up the phone. I figured she'd go on with her rant, finally realize we got disconnected, and then she'd forget me altogether and go back to snorting meth with whoever her boyfriend happened to be at the moment.

- - - -

I left the hospital three days later. I remember standing at the nurses' station demanding my notebooks back. They either didn't know where they were or were covering for Dr. Dean.

Chad was by my side.

"It's not worth it, Davina—let's just get out of here."

"But there's stuff in those notebooks I don't want to lose."

"Call it a loss, D. I don't like being here."

"Try being here for a week."

A nurse pulled Chad aside and talked to him like a baby about how he couldn't keep pills in the house anymore. Pain pills, sleeping pills, Klonopin, other benzodiazepines—basically anything interesting.

A side effect of me having called George from Chicago and also Chris is that all three of them were talking now, passing along updates of *how I was doing* and the like. I like to keep my worlds separate, so this was a big problem for me. For someone who's putting my life into a memoir, I'm a pretty private person.

So while that nurse talked to Chad, I was led to my room. There, like a million nurses and mental health workers and several doctors I never even met while I was at the hospital nonetheless advised me on my situation and even a drug counselor was brought in and with him he had a few papers stapled together containing all the AA meetings in the Ocala, Florida area. He handed it to me.

"But I'm not an alcoholic."

He handed me another stack of papers—all the Narcotics Anonymous meetings in the area.

I handed the papers back to him.

"I don't have a drug problem, either! Who are you?"

"I'm Dr. Lee."

"But I mean who are you, *to me*, in the context of this hospital?"

"I'm a psychiatrist with a focus on substance abuse issues."

"I don't *have* substance abuse issues. Believe me, if I wanted to get high, I wouldn't be eating an entire medicine cabinet of pills. I was trying to kill myself—don't you understand?"

"I have to sign off on your release from this hospital."

"Fine then. Give me your pen. Let's circle some of these meetings."

I circled meetings at random, the pen running off the page and onto my skin. Then I drew some kitties and some dandelions for them to pounce.

Dr. Lee took his pen back. He stood and went to the door.

"I've seen a lot of people come in and out of this hospital, Davina. And in my opinion it's the ones who work a program that stay out of the hospital."

He left.

"MY PROBLEM ISN'T DRUGS, DR. LEE—IT'S THE BURDEN OF HAVING TO DEAL WITH SIMPLETONS LIKE YOU ON A DAILY BASIS."

Dr. Dean comes right into my room.

"Aww, shit, how much of that did you hear?"

"All of it."

He sits in the chair facing the bed.

"How do you feel?"

"Good, except for being stuck in this place."

"That sounds very healthy."

"Does it?"

"Oh yes. If you wanted to stay here forever, I'd be worried."

We both laugh.

"Thanks for taking care of me, you know? I enjoyed our talks."

"Me too."

"Can I have my notebooks back? There's some stuff in there I need."

"Sure. I'll bring them by before you go."

I smiled.

Dr. Dean asked me a bunch of reasonable questions about how I was feeling, the patterns of my thoughts, did I have any delusions or hallucinations, and the key questions: did I feel like hurting myself or others? Answer "yes" to either one of those and you don't get to leave the hospital. Answer "no" to both and they just might give your freedom back.

I answered both honestly, with "no"s. I wanted to get back into the real world, the open..with people to meet and things to do and—yes—even all kinds of mistakes I could make. Even if my life was *defined* by mistakes, I wanted to be out there making them—because that would mean I was living my life.

Dr. Dean left gently. I found Chad, who was still being lectured by some nurse on how to take care of me. I grabbed his hand and pulled him away from that conversation. At the elevator, the charge nurse unlocked the door for us and I was filled with relief as the elevator doors closed—I was never going back to a place like that again.

- - - -

Chad and I were silent all the way to his apartment. I was *so* embarrassed because we had all these intimate moments in chat rooms and on the telephone—but we were basically strangers in person.

I got a few books that I had taken up to Chad's apartment, brought them down, put them in my trunk. Chad came with me back down to the parking lot. I was going for the driver's seat but he came up and hugged me—a consolation prize for a fat, brown girl that could have blown his mind.

We loosened our embrace and Chad took a half step back.

"Well," he said, "did you have a good time?"

"Yes," I said. "Florida's great."

I got in that car and drove out of that parking lot so fast. Rule number one: *Fuck real-life relationships with people you meet on the internet. If you have to be friends with them, be friends with them online.* Rule number two: *Don't ever try to kill yourself. You'll end up in a mental hospital. Mental hospitals suck. Nooooothing like what you see in the movies.* And rule number three: *Damn, I can't think of rule number three—and Dr. Dean still has my notebooks! That asshole'll probably write a book on them, called "The Real Mind of a Psychopath." Then he'll get famous and rich and I'll be unknown and poor. Fucker'll prob'ly have his own TV show—damn.*

## 70
I drove. I drove and drove and drove. 'Cause I'm American, and that's what Americans do. We drive to work. We drive to play. We drive to clear our heads.

Now don't get the idea that I'm some typical American redneck asshole who says the Pledge of Allegiance or cries when some bitch starlet sings the national anthem at a baseball game.

But I like to drive, ok? And I consider that an American thing to do. Driving is freedom, it's not looking back, it's embracing possibilities of what could be ahead. And it's going *fast—real, real* fast.

Looking back, though, I can see that my head wasn't very clear. I had just gotten out of a mental hospital—my second one. And believe it or not, when a psychiatrist tells you you have major depression or maybe bipolar, it doesn't necessarily lodge in your mind that you're mentally ill. People with a broken arm get a cast—you can instantly see what's wrong with them. But mental illness isn't like that: there's no bandage, no crutches..there isn't a shirt you wear that says, "I'm mentally ill." People around you know you're sick because you tried to kill yourself and when you get out of the hospital maybe you tell your family the diagnosis your doctor gave you. Then everybody *ooooohs* and *aaaaaahs* because "major depression" and "bipolar disorder" sound like some pretty nasty shit.

But then everyone forgets—even *you* forget—because going around living your life while constantly reminding yourself that you have major depression and tried to kill yourself..*twice*..is not that fun.

Then your medicine runs out and you can't get more because the emergency insurance they signed you up for at the hospital expires and you don't have the money to pay for insurance or pills.

So now you're off your medicine and you start acting crazy again—although you don't know it. Everyone around you sees the depression or the mania or whatever you have, but you're the last to know—one of the hardest things about mental illness is getting the patient to believe that they actually *have* it.

I'm a slow learner, I guess. I was two hospitals and two suicide attempts into this lovely journey of self-discovery and still, a half hour outside Ocala and I forgot all about mental illness. Chad was the problem—he didn't like how I *looked*? On the phone and in chat we were like *lovers*—we were so close. Then he makes me sleep on the *couch*? Anyone would kill themself in that situation. But *I* wasn't the problem—*Jessie* was the problem. Maybe if I hadn't been abused as a child then I would have *had* a childhood. Then I would have grown up as a real adult who can handle things when they go wrong without *losing her shit*.

So I just told you that taking all of Chad's pills in Ocala was my second suicide attempt—but even that isn't true. It was my third. Suicide for normal people is a taboo topic. And every time they hear about a suicide, they remember it because to them it's a big deal. But when you're as depressed as I've been, *suicide is a way of life*. I could forget a suicide like I forget my car keys.

- - - -

I always forget my first one.

It was when I was still a kid.

Before I ran away.

Living in my mom's apartment.

I took a whole bottle of Aspirin which didn't do anything but make me sick and I went to hide at the park that I later lived at when I was homeless. I don't even remember what triggered it but Lisa and Ron were looking for me and I was hiding in the park bathroom with some guy I'd seen around and he was bracing the door shut while Ron and Lisa were calling my name and trying to get in:

"Davina! Baby! Come back! We called an ambulance!!"

They were banging on the door.

And this guy who I'd seen around was yelling back:

"There's no one here by that name!"

And in the next breath he was turning and looking at me—strange little suicidal girl cowering in the corner—he was asking me if I want to fuck, as if he could somehow brace the door closed and fuck me at the same time and I just thought how even as crazy as girls were about sex guys were crazier—maybe just crazier in general. To ask a suicidal girl with Aspirin vomit on her shirt if she wanted to fuck in the park bathroom. Lisa and Ron were about to bust in at any moment—there's no way we could fuck. But I could see in his eyes that he was excited by all this chaos and turned on by it.

"Baby take your shorts off."

"I don't even know your name."

"Take them panties off, too. Have you ever been fucked by a schizoaffective?"

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"It's when you have schizophrenia and bipolar disorder at the same time. Believe me, if you've never been fucked by a schizoaffective, you're missing out."

I laughed.

"Sounds amazing," I say.

"It is," this guy reassures me.

"No, I'm sure—what could be better than that?"

"You have a diagnosis, right? You get disability? I've got sixty dollars worth of food stamps I'll give you for forty cash."

"I don't *have* any cash. I don't *have* a diagnosis. I'm just a person. I think I'm defective."

This guy I barely knew turned around to me, releasing his hold on the bathroom door, and as Lisa and Ron rushed in to get me, everyone was yelling and talking at once but I looked at that dude and read his lips and what he said was:

"Don't let them ever tell you you're defective."

He was signing a big circle with both of his hands.

"The whole world," he said, "is crazy—not you."

Right, so technically, if you count that first one with the Aspirin then I have three suicide attempts—all failed. Smh. I'm even a failure at killing myself—no wonder I have low self-esteem.

- - - -

Flash forward. Driving north from Ocala and away from the Chad Bailey experiment. I drive on. I forget sleep. Somewhere in South Carolina I stop for gas and I put about twelve packages of Ho Hos on the counter.

"Pump three," I say.

The woman behind the counter is about twenty-three but the wrinkles in her face say two decades more. She is smoking a Kamel.

"Pump three," I say.

I'm staring at her cigarette as if it's the loveliest thing in the world.

This woman looks out at my car—a busted-up Sentra with New York plates that looks like it's about to die. It's 3am—no one else is around. She looks behind the counter and pulls out a pack of Kamels and throws them plus a lighter on top of my pile of Ho Hos.

"I can't afford those."

"They're free."

"Ok. Thanks. My gas is on pump three."

"I know that. Pump three. You've said it three times."

"Well, I'd like to pay for it."

I hold out some cash.

"We're no longer accepting money as a form of payment."

"Since when?"

"Since about four o'clock this afternoon when I decided it."

"Won't your boss be mad?"

"I expect him to—I expect him to fire me."

"I bet he will."

"Yeah, it's a shit job with shit hours and a shit wage. We're all good here."

She waves her hand over the Ho Hos, cigarettes, and lighter, just like a casino dealer saying: *No more bets.*

"I'll get you a bag," she says.

And I say, "I'll be right back."

I go in the trunk of my car. I don't have my notebooks anymore so I tear out the last page of the first novel I see—which happens to be *The Catcher in the Rye*. One side of the page contains the last paragraph of the story—the other side is blank.

After a few minutes I'm back in the store.

"I want you to have this," I say.

I hand her the piece of paper.

"Did you write this?"

"Yes. It's just for you!"

"Like you wrote this now out there just for me?"

"Yes!!"

"This is truly awesome, girl. People have paid with lint, buttons—I even got a belt! But you are my first customer to pay with a poem! I'll never forget this."

We're both smiling.

"Good luck with your boss," I say.

"Good luck with your trip!" she says.

I take my bag out to the car. As I'm taking the cellophane off my free pack of Kamels, I look through the window of the gas station and I see the checker girl reading my poem. She smiles. She looks at the other side and smiles again. Once I get my cigarette lit and a couple of the Ho Hos unwrapped for easy road-eating, I give a couple honks to the checker girl who got me that much farther down the road.

As I drove my Sentra on that free tank of gas, I wonder why she decided to stop accepting money as payment. In her case, she would probably just get fired, but I was thinking larger: what if we all stopped accepting money for payment and everybody just worked together and gave what they have to give. A poem for a tank of gas—is it worth it? I'm not saying my poems are great poems, but I think that a great poem would be worth many, many tanks of gas.

You may think that's crazy, but remember..I'm a writer. And writers are crazy—especially the poets—you didn't know that?

That checker girl wasn't the only one who gave me help. I mean, I must have been going the way the wind blows on that trip—the universe popped up here and there in the form of people giving me just enough gas money to get to the next gas station, where I begged again. But within minutes or hours I was back on the highway smoking Kamels and talking to myself as a child. I didn't need Lisa or Jessie anymore—I was my own fierce mother who would take good care of me, no matter what. I was giving birth to newer and better me's—me's that weren't trapped in the past, me's that were powerful and loved.

I wasn't even stuck when I was stuck—that's how much momentum I had. I was so excited that I stopped following the map and every time the road split I would *feel* which branch would take me closer to my destination. This tactic ended me up in New Jersey, out of gas, starving, with no money. I coasted into a 7-Eleven right up to the pump. I searched the car for any change I might have dropped or dollar bills that might be stuck under the seats. There was nothing there. This style of traveling was weighing on me—I could literally feel the juju draining out of me. I was going from invincible road child to unsteady, unstable, irresponsible mother on the run. I closed all the car doors and went inside the 7-Eleven.

- - - -

Everything was bright, luminous—even candy bars had their own mini LEDs, suggesting that anyone who went to the trouble to custom light a candy bar or a pack of gum must have been selling good shit.

I was super thirsty as well as being hungry. I walked up and down every aisle, gazing at all the food and the drinks inside glass refrigerators. I didn't just want that food—I longed for it. Most of your life you have a few bucks to spend on a Coke and some Doritos—then someday you're penniless and you look at those same drinks, those same snacks, and they're not for you because you don't have any of that green and silver stuff to trade for it.

Then someone was yelling.

They were yelling at me.

I turned around.

The guy behind the counter was all, "Hey. Hey. Are you gonna get gas?"

I shrugged. My super-power self was deflating. When I thought realistically about my current situation I almost cried.

And some guy with a harsh accent was yelling at me:

"If you not getting gas, then move you car! I got customers in and out, in and out, and you can't just keep your car there while you stare at food. You're in the way of paying customers, see? You are affecting my profitability. That has consequences. I make less money. Then my wife, my kids, they will not get to go to Sesame Place next year. They will be very sad with me and I will be very angry with you."

"What's Sesame Place?"

"Have you heard of the Muppets?"

"Yeah."

"Sesame Place is like Disneyland with Muppets. Now, are you getting gas or what? For your ancient Nissan Sentra that is about to fall apart."

"That car's gotten me all the way across this country. Twice."

"That's true, that's true—they are wonderful cars. You need some gas before you go or should I let you browse the candy bars and gum?"

"I need gas."

"How much you want to get?"

"I don't have any money."

"What are you going to do?"

"I don't know."

"Where did you come from? Why are you out of gas?"

"I'm just driving. I'm kind of homeless right now."

The guy leans across the counter.

"What do you think I am?"

"What do I think you are?"

"For work. What do you think I do?"

"You work in a gas station?"

"No."

"You *own* a gas station?"

"No. I am a doctor. A physician. What you would call an MD. That is what I did in my old country, I see children and families and mothers of babies and fathers who work hard to take care of their people, you know? I help people who are sick get well. That is what I used to do. Then I bring my family here for political reasons. I think, ok, we will move to America, things will be good—I will practice as a doctor to support my wife and kids."

"So what happened?"

"My credentials are no good—no good in America. I am a doctor—I save people's lives—but not in America! In America, I can't even work at a McDonald's until I get my GED—a piece of paper that says I can read and write on a high school level. A doctor..who can't get a job working at Taco Bell."

"What are you going to do?"

"What I *am* doing, young lady, is going back to school to be a doctor here. I will not be stopped, because in my heart I *am* a doctor. Some people do it because it is a secure job that pays a decent salary—which is the worst reason to become a doctor. The only good reason to do anything is that you love doing it."

This guy reaches below the counter and hands me a paper bag.

"Fill it with whatever you want."

It's not a small bag either. I walk around the store—I got drinks, donuts, chips..and a lot of other stuff.

I go to the counter. I'm about to thank this guy when he hands me a fifty dollar bill and tells me to fill up my tank.

"You're so nice. Thank you."

He gave me his address and phone number so that once I could afford it, I could pay him back. But I never did—I lost his details.

He even gave me directions. He came out from behind the counter and showed me where I was headed on my map.

When I was well oriented, I asked him why he was helping me.

"It was something you said."

He looked right in my eyes.

"You said you are homeless right now in your car."

"Yeah."

"I have been there. And I have been in worse places. *The world is cruel but we don't have to be.* What good does it do for me to leave you stuck at my gas station? You might think I'm helping you—but really I'm helping myself."

" 'The world is cruel but we don't have to be.' I like that."

"I didn't make it up. I am paraphrasing a much greater thinker than I."

"Who is it?"

"Temple Grandin. Check her out sometime. I think you two might have some things in common."

"Well, thanks again for everything."

"You're welcome."

"By the way, do you own this or you just work here?"

The man laughed.

"I *own* this motherfucker!"

*Insert something here about there being kind, generous people in the world. Then gently imply that these people regularly treated Davina better than her own parents.*

## 71
I drove for two hours before my poor little Nissan Sentra froze up—she just stopped running. We coasted and I kept trying to start the car—she wouldn't even turn over.

I called my sister and my sister told her husband. They own a garage and they brought this huge flatbed truck from several hours away just to help me. The three of us drove back to their place. I didn't even know how to say thank you for making a six-hour round trip to save my ass—so I didn't say anything. I was embarrassed sitting with them in the cab—I was filthy, dirty—I was a mess. Once we got to my sister's house, we unloaded my car from the flatbed. When I opened the trunk of my car it was windy and all these release papers from my mental health facility flew out—just all over, landing on their gravel driveway, one got stuck on my sister's face. I thought the whole situation was hilarious—epic—absurd—and I was laughing like the Joker in Batman.

That was the end of my Nissan. When she froze up on the highway, she never drove again. Jamie's husband sold it for parts. I got like four-hundred dollars. Jamie looked at that situation and thought that since she had given me the car in the first place, that that money should go to her. I looked at the situation and thought that since Jamie and her husband had checking accounts and saving accounts and multiple streams of income, that that money was mine. So I kept it.

That was the first time I panhandled across America. I started with less than one-hundred dollars in cash. I went from California to Florida to Pennsylvania. Then I stopped for a few months. In this country, however, driving is the ultimate pacifier, and I've done a lot of driving since—sometimes in luxury, sometimes in poverty. But I always let strangers pay for my gas—citing reasons of nostalgia. Yes, over my life, bummin' it across this country has gone for me from a necessity, to a habit, to a hobby, to an art.

I stayed with Jamie and her husband for a few months, but by two weeks in it was clear we weren't gelling. My sister was up my ass with a microscope. I'd fix myself something to eat that contained (for example) sour cream *and* cheese.

Jamie would be like, "That's a lot of dairy."

*What do you say to that?*

"Is that the last of that cheese? Because I was planning on making something with that tomorrow."

(This is after she clearly told me than nothing in the refrigerator is off limits.)

"There's plenty left. I just cut a couple of slices. There's almost a whole block of it left."

Jamie would keep looking at the contents of my plate *while I was eating*—as if she was trying to make a point.

With the addition of me, Jamie and her husband's house went one person over its lawful capacity, even though it was just three adults who should have been able to get along if it weren't for me and my sister's childhood baggage—which each of us were obviously still carrying. It stressed us all out to the point that we were always having meaningless fights—stupid stuff like whose job it was to take out the trash and what spices should go into a pot of stew. No one could do anything there without someone looking over that person's shoulder. Maybe it's the particular experiences I had in childhood that Jamie did not have..maybe it's something I learned being homeless..but when someone's making me food, I never, *ever* complain about the way they're going about doing it. That's food that Jamie provided for me—and even though she is the bitchiest, most devilish sister in the entire world, I'm not gonna make words over a pinch of cumin. Nor will I protest if Jamie reveals her true identity and stirs the soup with a cloven motherfucking *hoof*—that's her business. If she cooks, I'll eat it.

- - - -

So I called Chris—you bet I did—every night I tried to work it out with him: We should get back together. It would be better for our kids. It would be better for me, better for Chris, and it might save my relationship with my sister. I suggested we live in Pennsylvania mainly just because it felt new to me and California felt like *going back* where Pennsylvania felt like going forward—and I *desperately*, desperately needed to go forward.

I told myself I had convinced Chris to move east because for two Californians, the northeast would have novelty to us. The way the houses looked, the way that stores and gas stations were arranged, even. But when I dug deep down—and I didn't say this to Chris—the best reason for leaving California—for me—was leaving Jessie. Jessie was a California dude—that or New Mexico—there was no way I was just going to bump into him in Susquehanna, PA.

I started working down the street from Jamie's house at the Pump N Pantry—this gas station/convenience store thing. One thing I loved about the three months I stayed with my sister and her husband is that there were no kids around—and I hate myself for saying that I liked it that way, but I did. Chris and I talked on the phone several times a day. Our plans, which seemed so solid in California, had fallen apart now that we were apart from each other. *Absence make the heart grow..whatever.* Chris flip-flopped on whether he was coming to Susquehanna or not.

"I don't even know where Susquehanna *is*," he'd say.

"It's right next to Binghamton," I'd say.

"I don't know where Binghamton is, either."

"This isn't really about Susquehanna, is it?"

"What do *you* think it's about?"

"I think it's about you not being sure you even want to be with me. You don't, do you."

"*Of course* I do. Baby—"

"No, Chris, no. This isn't an 'of course' kind of question. You either really do, because you really like me, or you really don't, and you want to move on with your life. So which is it?"

A long-distance run of silence on the line.

I hung up.

He didn't call back.

The answer was decided in my mind—if it takes you more than a *second* to decide if you love me—or to say that you do—that is one second too long.

Oh I knew he wanted to come out there with me—my intuition told me that. But he wouldn't say it. You can't just sit silent on your end of the line—you got to say it, bruh. You got to say it.

- - - -

So I'm staying with my sister and working down the road at the Pump N Pantry. Everything is going fine until my sister—that bitch, that devil, that beast of a woman—decides it would be in everyone's best interest if I spent a few nights at the local mental ward.

Her expert reasoning in this case was that I was wearing a rubber band around my wrist and I kept snapping myself, leaving welts. *Oh wow, what a danger to myself and others!!*

One day, Jamie just wouldn't let me go to work. She funneled me into her car and we got on the highway. I took my seatbelt off and opened the door, threatening to jump.

Jamie swerves onto the shoulder.

"You could have killed me by swerving," I say.

"I need you to find another place to live," she says.

"I'm getting Chris to come out here and as soon as he does, we'll find another place."

"So you and Chris, now, *plus* the babies—"

"They aren't really babies anymore."

"Would you shut the door so I can get back on the road?"

I shut the door.

I made a loud *crack* on my wrist with the rubber band.

Jamie walked in with me and made sure I checked myself into the ward. It was called Marian and all I could think about while I was filling out the forms was Marion, from *Raiders of the Lost Ark*, where she owns that bar in like fucking Nepal and drinks everyone under the table.

That could be me—I could do that job.

To be honest, I don't know how I let myself get talked into going to the hospital. I've been in much worse frames of mind than I was then, living with Jamie and her husband and working at the Pump N Pantry. Maybe my sister really thought I was a danger to myself. I don't know. Maybe she thought, since I tried suicide once, I would try it again—and she didn't want me to do it in her house. Or maybe it was the classic move of someone sane defending themselves against the doubts on their own sanity by putting down a person who is labeled "crazy."

And, yes, I was leaving welts but I wasn't bleeding myself out or anything like that. I suppose my sister was worried about me. I had just gotten out of a hospital not long before—maybe she was genuinely trying to help me.

That's not what it felt like, though.

- - - -

It felt like filling out forms under fluorescent lighting with a fat nurse looking at me sympathetically from behind a protective desk—she, the mentally well; me, the mentally ill. And my little sister Jamie standing behind me like my guardian—this used to be the little girl that if *I* ordered cheesecake Oreo ice cream, *she* would order cheesecake Oreo ice cream. Somewhere in there, in the routine of years, Jamie had stopped looking up to me as her cool, worldly big sister and started looking at me mostly as a skanky, used up, suicidal piece of shit. You know how it is when you get with someone: everything's exciting and magical and the first time you have sex with them you have the added excitement of having sex with a stranger—they could kill you (they might be a mass murderer), they might just cum too soon on my belly like every other useless guy out there..or..*or*..they might be special and give my G-spot the rock 'n' roll marathon rollercoaster, intimate, looking each other in the eyes kind of shit that it deserved. But that's not the case with me and Jamie—to me, she's boring..to her, I'm a loser. We're way past the honeymoon now.

I stayed in that hospital for exactly seventy-two hours—the maximum amount of time they can keep you if you don't show suicidal or homicidal tendencies. My sister was so proud and upbeat when she came to pick me up—as if *she* had done something of value in this situation. It was like I was dirty clothes and she dropped them off for someone else to wash and then when she picked them up and smelled them and held them in her arms, *she* was proud of all that work she never did.

You know, it's a lot harder and a lot more meaningful to have a deep relationship with someone who has mental illness than it is to leave *everything* to the professionals, sweeping us into dust piles and waiting for the weekly maid to mop away all the dark and stinking corners where you store your discomfort at dealing with the mentally ill.

There are people I would like to say that to in person, but I'll never get the chance. Or I'm too afraid to—that's most likely the truth.

- - - -

It was awkward when I went back to work. Jamie had worked at that Pump N Pantry before and was a big boss and she had gone in there and told everyone I worked with that I was in a mental hospital. When I first went back to work after my seventy-two-hour stint, everyone patted me on the back and said everything would be ok and they encouraged me.

My first day back, I put on a really dark lipstick and kissed a stranger (one of the customers). I was acting like a slut for no reason I was aware of. This Pump N Pantry was right off the exit so there were tons of strangers—most people that came in were total strangers, but we had a few regulars. One time a guy came in. He was a regular—and he was totally fucking cute. He said something about my Baphomet necklace—I don't know exactly what he said but it was a standard compliment used by guys to get down a girl's pants. He came in once a week and he always stood in the line for my register no matter if it had the shortest line or the longest line. One time when he got up to the front, he said:

"I wanna kiss you so bad."

"Oh really you want to kiss me so bad?"

"Davina, you're so fucking hot. Is it time for your break soon?"

(I smoked at the time.)

"Meet me around the side of the store," I said.

Next thing I know I'm outside—on the side of the store so everybody I work with won't see me—and me and this guy were making out so hard against the wall. People were walking by and we kept moving closer and closer to the back of the store. He was feeling my boobs and I had a flash of the wolf at my childhood window in Louisiana..some type of lightning that just makes your hair stand on end. I had it worked out in my my mind that once we got behind the store, Jessie would be there, and Lisa would be there, and all the old men who had brought me to their cars in church parking lots and put their hand under my young skirts—they would all be there—and with every intimate touch they had destroyed any chance that I would grow up to be anything but a sexual freak.

I'm back at the Pump N Pantry, making out with the cutest guy. Something must have been on his conscience like he had a wife or a girlfriend or maybe he just had a tight schedule to keep, 'cause all suddenly he was like:

"I gotta go I gotta go."

Maybe he could sense my complicated past and that's what scared him away. Whatever it was, it was too bad. My pussy was throbbing and I would have let him go all the way.

When I went back inside the Pump N Pantry, this sixteen-year-old coworker of mine was like, "What were you doing?"—pointing me out to all the girls who worked there. She sang: "You don't have lipstick on anymore" This girl's name was Trina and she teased me relentlessly about making out with that guy. It totally worked, too—I was so embarrassed. Trina had everyone saying, "We know what kind of girl you are."

I guess they knew more than I did. What kind of girl was I—a girl who liked making out and getting kissed by cute guys? Yep, that's me. A girl who likes to be whipped and spanked and choked into orgasm? Yes, that's me too. I like the way I live. I like the way I am. I like the way I cum. If you got a problem with that, then you can fuck right off.

## 72
I talked to Chris for three months about moving out to Susquehanna with me and do you know it took that reticent motherfucker three whole months to say, "Yes." Chris and I had ten years together and I wanted to work it out. I felt bad about convincing him to move out here, away from everything and everyone he knew. He was mad at me for a long time (and understandably so). He said it was ok (me leaving him and the kids, twice, to search for love and peace without him)—and once in Susquehanna he forgave me, again, very formally. While we lived in Susquehanna, Chris and I were still togetherish..but in rare moments of honesty I knew that he and I would never last and my real motivation was to get the kids back before Chris and I finally called it quits.

Chris sold everything we didn't need and got our possessions down to what would fit in the tiniest U-Haul that U-Haul will let you haul—the kind that you can pull behind your car.

Chris was ready to move our kids and our things when I had been at my sister's house for three months. I needed a break from my sister's micromanagement of every aspect of my motherfucking *life*. Even though Jamie yelled at me for a whole day about what a waste of money it was and how it made absolutely no sense, I ignored her opinions and got on the Greyhound. As soon as I was around all the schizophrenics and poor people and black people who still ride the Greyhound even though these days the bus is more expensive than flying, my blood temperature dropped. My stress went away. Give me poor black schizophrenics any day—even though she denies it, there is no doubt in my mind that Jamie has bipolar, and (as you probably have met some) bipolar people are the hardest people on the *planet* to have relationships with. I laid back in my seat and even though every trip on the Greyhound is more epic than any journey Frodo ever went on, let's just skip ahead and say that four days later, the lack of any family voices in my head had re-wired my entire psyche and when I stepped off the bus in Yuba City I was the civilian equivalent of the greatest Zen master who ever lived.

I slapped my ID down and passed it through the tiny window of a motel office. Years of the film of memory played itself instantly at the association of staying in California motels.

I was watching my mom suck dick while I breastfed Ashley.

Losing my virginity to the guy who taught me to put mayo on *both* sides of the bread.

Answering the door to Red Dog—thinking he wanted to kill my mother but hoping he'd fuck me first.

"How many nights?" says the guy behind the window of this Yuba City motel that looks like every other Yuba City motel ever built.

*How many nights?* I thought. *How many nights have I stayed in Yuba City motels? Thousands? Thousands of nights of Lisa sucking cocks in parking lots and Ron dealing drugs and—who knows—maybe he opened up his asshole for gayboys in gay bars to help out our cause.*

"How many nights, baby girl?"

"Uh..one."

"How many adults?"

"One, but I'm having visitors later."

"Register visitors with the front desk."

He stamps a card, photocopies my ID, and gives me my key. The guy looks like Deckard's boss from *Blade Runner*—like he's been living and eating and working in that office for so many years that he's become a seamless part of it.

I went to the room and laid down.

When there was a knock on the door, it was Chris, Ashley, Zachariah, Phillip. They all rushed into the room and the five of us were one big hug. To my self-hating surprise, everyone was glad to see me. Kneeling in that motel room, hugging my kids, Chris and I wrapping them in our arms, I had no idea why I ever left. These kids needed a mom—and that was me. Even though they still seemed to love me, I was beating myself up with all these messages about being a horrible mom and I thought how horrible the world is—especially when you have kids, because you don't want them to suffer, but every generation suffers more than the previous one, not less. I looked outside at the U-Haul, hooked to Chris's car. It was real—the whole family was going on a big adventure, west to east, just like the adventures we went on in my family when I was young—except me and Chris weren't drug dealers running from the law. We were just a family—a plain and simple little fucked-up family trying to make it better than our parents.

We ordered pizza. Actually, we didn't just order pizza. We let everyone pick their own ingredients and we got five medium pizzas. We let the kids sleep in the bed with us—a big human pile on. The TV had pay-per-view and I had the remote control in my hand and I looked at Chris—our kids spread out all over us and each other, stuffing their favorite-ingredient pizzas into their young faces—and I said:

"Movie?"

"Why not?" he smiled. "Fuck it."

So we pay-per-viewed *Shrek* and the kids laughed all the way through but no one laughed harder than me and I could see Chris smiling when I laughed at the stupidest parts but really I wasn't even laughing at the movie—I was just looking around at the miracle that five people could occupy a Yuba City motel room without anyone shooting smack.

After the kids fell asleep Chris and I splurged and got *Zoolander*.

- - - -

In the morning, my legs were sore from Ashley's bony little body pressing on me the whole night but I didn't care.

Ashley and I rode the Greyhound to Pennsylvania. She was fourteen. We talked the entire way—well, she talked, I listened. I knew my daughter was smart—probably smarter than me—but it wasn't till that trip that I saw so deeply into her..into her *brilliance*, really. I hid a tear somewhere in Kansas that fell for Ashley—my probably brilliant daughter—and I said a prayer to the Universe, to the Everything, to *Whatever*, that she would not be touched by the mental illness and the drug addiction that often goes with those kinds of smarts. Nevertheless, as I looked at Ashley on that trip, my face was the face of a proud mother—I believe, for those sweet days, I was a kind of sun and she was a kind of flower—and in that, I received the most precious gift, which was to see my daughter bloom.

Chris and the boys drove to PA at the same time, with the U-Haul in tow. They took different roads and got there before us.

I had already rented a place in Susquehanna. It was in a house from the 1800s that someone had separated into three apartments. The whole house was super old with uneven floors. The bottom floor used to be a store. Our house was shown in all the tourist propaganda as the quintessential Susquehanna house—though most of Susquehanna and its buildings are equally old. Still, it was funny to see pictures of *our* house—in its original capacity as a general store—featured in historical books about the town.

We lived there, the five of us, for a happy week. Then Chris started driving routes again and as soon as she graduated high school, Ash moved in with her friend Chelsea. Chelsea also lived in Susquehanna, so Ashley was close—for a while. Six months later she moved back to California. I was trying to keep my whole family in one place—I know, I know, very old fashioned.

- - - -

Chris was always on the road, so he was only home one weekend out of the month. I stopped working—Chris was making good money. I spent most of my time taking care of the kids and cleaning house—especially before the weekend that Chris got home..I wanted things to be nice for him. Sexually, things weren't so hot between us, but I always made time to give him a blowjob the day he got home. After he came, he said, "Thank you," which basically made me feel like a stranger. He'd get off the bed, zip his jeans, and leave the room. I'd swallow his cum as if that had become our only form of intimacy—not me sucking him off, but the swallowing of the cum itself. Somehow, after ten years, that was the only thing that made me feel close to him.

But that's what I am: a stupid fucking cumslut.

And I couldn't help it, but when Chris was home I felt those feelings again—both kinds: remembering what we had and how we met in the mosh pit and how compatible we were on one hand, and on the other hand, I felt—well—suicidal. The suicidal feelings I had were of the *quiet desperation* type. I was afraid to ask for help—probably because I was afraid of *getting* help. Help would mean more time at the hospital, which I didn't want to do—I'd be away from my kids. So I just sucked it up—which I bet a lot of people do. To the quiet, suicidal ones out there, you have my respect—that's a hard road.

But don't kill yourself yet—I have loads more stories to tell you! I admit, the next one's kind of sad—ok, it's really sad—but I often find, when I'm feeling low, some comfort in the stories of others who are on the edge—or those who have crossed it. Don't you?

- - - -

Here's my story:

Before me and Chris and the kids moved into the house Jamie found us, I met this guy who would come into the Pump N Pantry where I worked. He came in and flirted with me and I went on a few dates with him. I thought he was *nice* and *cute*. His name was Gary. This was before me and Chris decided to work stuff out, and before Chris and the kids moved to Susquehanna. Me and Gary were dating and having sex. Then Chris and the kids came and I didn't see Gary for a while.

Chris and I moved into the house Jamie found for us.

It was a happy time but I did wonder whatever happened to Gary.

Then Gary started calling me at the house. Chris was never home so there was no problem with Gary coming over sometimes—even staying over to fuck me in me and Chris's bed. Gary would come and take me and the kids to the creek to see nature. I started having feelings for Gary—we became an item.

I had to tell Chris about it—it wasn't right to keep a secret. It turned into a big fight between me and Chris on the phone and it turned into an even bigger fight the next weekend when Chris came home from his routes and parked the cab in front of the house that *he* was paying for where *I* was not only fucking but being in a relationship with another guy who was now living there and fucking me all over Chris's furniture.

Chris comes into the house.

I tell the kids to go outside and play in the back yard.

Gary stands up from where he'd been sitting at the dining room table and extends his hand to Chris.

"I'm Gary—" he starts.

"I know who you are,"

"Well no need to be aggressive."

"I'm not *being* aggressive. When I'm being aggressive you'll know it because you'll have my boot in your mouth."

"Chris," I say. "Please. We can all be friends."

"Is that what you want?" Chris asks.

"Yes. I want everything to be cool and loverly and friends. Can you two agree to that? 'Cause I got children in this house I'm trying to raise and the last thing they need to see is you all getting territorial."

"Yeah, cool," Chris says. "But I ain't payin' full rent and water, electric, gas, while you two are having a fuckfest and I'm not even here!"

"Do you want to move the rest of your stuff out?"

"No, Davina! I wanted to leave some stuff here and do my routes so that you and the kids have a place to live, *food to eat*—why do you think I'm doing this, spending four weeks a month on the road and only getting to see my kids two days a month? Ok, so you got bored—you weren't having enough sex so you found this guy—"

"Gary."

"Ok, Gary..or whatever the fuck it is, you wanna see if you can support a family, be a *real* man? 'Cause you don't even look old enough to shave."

"Come on, please. You two would like each other if you got to know each other. Let me make dinner and while I'm doing that you two go talk it out."

I reached into the refrigerator and grabbed a couple cans of beer. I gave one to each of the guys.

"Knock on the window when you need another beer. Talk it out—no hitting. Let's all face the facts and adjust to *living with* the facts of our situation, right here, right now, ok? Now go. I don't want you around while I'm making dinner."

"You ok, baby?"

"I'm fine. I just want y'all *out* of the kitchen while I'm cooking!"

They got the point and left. While I was making a macaroni and cheese with ground beef and bacon, I kept an eye on the men in the front and the kids in the back. The men worked out an arrangement where Chris could keep his stuff there but he would not stop by the house to visit. They didn't share the exact monetary details of this agreement with me—typical boys—but I didn't have to move and my babies didn't have to move and I got to be with Gary so I was fine with it, regardless of the details.

- - - -

Gary moved into the house with me and the kids and for a while Chris kept his stuff there and paid part of the rent. After a while, though, Chris was paying for everything, all the rent and all the bills. Gary went through jobs—his income was inconsistent.

We only made it three months with that configuration. Me and Gary broke up. I couldn't live there anymore because I didn't have a job and I couldn't pay the rent. I couldn't find anywhere to work. I didn't have a car. My sister helped me move all my stuff out of that house and into her garage. Gary and me were still seeing each other even though we were breaking up—we used each other for sex, for conversational inspiration, and mostly, for friendship. He helped me look for a place to live. He drove. We looked in nearby towns. We found one in Hallstead that had a *For Rent* sign in front. We got out to take a look.

The door was open but the screen door was closed. I could see a woman's legs with boots and knee pads. We rang the doorbell and this short woman with her hair in a ponytail came to the door.

"Come on in!" she said.

Me and Chris followed her into the front room.

"I'm repainting everything. That's going to be orange, this'll be blue. New floor in the kitchen and I'll let you pick out the vinyl, if you move in. There's a second floor, two bedrooms. Do you smoke?"

"Not regularly."

"Well if you do I suggest doing it outside. The last people who lived here smoked inside, and now—can you smell that?"

"No."

"No."

"That's 'cause you're smelling with your optimistic nose. You need a place, you want it to work out, but you're no longer assessing things realistically. Take a deep breath and see what you smell."

We did.

"Smell smoke now?"

"I guess."

"A little."

"Well it drives me crazy, the smell of cigarette smoke. But if you rent it, it's yours. You can do anything you want. Let's go see the upstairs."

By the time we came back down I was asking for an application and filling in names and dates and places I had lived before.

"So it's the two of you together?"

"It's not me," Gary said. "I'm just the driver."

"It's me and my three kids."

"So..is his name going to be on the lease?"

Gary and I looked at each other and said "No."

"That's fine, I just want the right names on the lease."

"It's..just me," I said, and handed over the application.

"So you're..Davina? Am I saying that right?"

"Yes."

"Well nice to meet you, Davina. I'm Annie."

We shook hands.

"So you wanna make this place your home?"

I nodded like a five year old. "I'd love to!" I said. "Do you need to check my references or anything?"

"Nah. What's a reference going to tell me? I use my intuition. I can feel that you're right for this house, Davina—and I can feel it's right for you. Follow me and we'll look out back."

That's how I met the spritely young landlord who is Annie—she is my landlord to this day.

- - - -

"I like you, Davina."

She said that on the first day I met her. That was her credit check. Then she looked in my eyes—that was her background check. I swear to god, she's the only landlord I ever met who didn't act like they were doing me a fucking favor. She gave me her word that I could move in as soon as she finished the kitchen floor. And Annie's word was true—in a few days she called me and said she left the key inside the butt of a ceramic frog who lived on the front porch. Gary and Jamie were my move-in team. The kids helped—Zachariah "helped" with each piece of large furniture by at least putting a hand on it. It felt good to do something with my whore-bitch sister—we only carried one piece of furniture together (a tall bookshelf), but she was at one end and I was at the other and that technically counts as us doing something together—right? When you have a beast/whore 666 KKK cocksucking hive queen of a motherfucking sister—a real-life bipolar type 1 cumslut—you never know what to expect. I mean one second she's helping you move a bookshelf and sixteen seconds later she's in the kitchen examining one of my knives, running her finger along the sharp edge, then pointing it at her breast like she's about to kill herself.

"Jamie," I say.

"What?" she says, all peaceful.

"Could you..hold off on the *hari-kari* until we get all the furniture in?"

She says, "Wouldn't it be beautiful if I christened your kitchen on the first day you lived here?"

"No," I say.

Fucking psychopath. And yet, it sounds exactly like something I would say. At this point in the day, my mantra becomes, "My family is fucked, my family is fucked" I make that into a little song and sing it over and over until it fades away. Seeing Jamie's suicidal craziness *so close to me* and *in my peaceful house* scares the shit out of me because I know that all you have to do is scratch the surface and she and I are the same fucking thing.

But we all got through it. I bought everyone pizza and we sat on the hardwood floor in the dining room. (Assembling the table was a job for another day.) Everybody was full on pizza and Gary and Jamie left. I put the boys to bed on mattresses with no sheets and I slept downstairs in my reclining chair.

- - - -

Chris ended up marrying a woman he met online—they'd been talking for a while before he ever came out to Susquehanna to patch things up with me. He never mentioned her until after I was in my new house and he had moved his stuff out of the house we shared in Susquehanna. I don't blame him for not telling me. It's possible that I could have reacted violently to that information. And I don't blame him if he never told her about me, either.

- - - -

I laid back in my recliner until all the kid noises had stopped. I cleaned out my pipe and packed it with green leaves. I smoked a bowl, then set the pipe aside. I wriggled my body until I was fully comfortable in my chair. I stayed awake a couple hours—in my pot trance I thought about all the little moments in my life that had to happen to lead up to this one. I had gotten such a face of pity from that woman who was looking for a nanny. *How can you take care of my baby if you have to take care of your own?* That lady made me so mad. But so many years later I finally know why: she was right—I was wrong. She knew a little part of me better than I knew it myself. Some pregnant *kid* is not going to be that woman's nanny. That lady knew it—I didn't. It made my face burn.

But now it was different. I had my boys under one roof. Ashley was in Cali doing her thing. We were safe from abusive men and lying women. From here on out we were gonna do things my way—we were gonna be safe, healthy, and happy. I don't know if it was the pot or the uncontainable *joy* I felt at being in a living situation I finally had some control over, but I cried. I let it come out and it came out in streams and then I made a yelping sound like a dog who was lost and spent months trying to get back to her home..until she finally sees the house she's from and her owner standing in the door. It was that yelp, that moment of joy, that me and my imaginary dog felt that first night in Hallstead.

## 73
I mean moving into this house in Hallstead, I was frankly ecstatic. It was only the second time I ever lived anywhere by myself as an adult—the other being Binghamton for a while. I was happy because in Susquehanna it had all been hillbillies. I'm not saying Hallstead is a bunch of *intellectuals*, but here I can at least carry on a conversation with my neighbors. I found a job with CareGivers America—this home health service where you go to elderly or sick people's houses and give them basic care and company. It was me, Phillip, and Zach. Even without Chris and Ashley, the three of us were a little family. There was peace for once. And especially before I got my first CareGivers paycheck, we were real Spartans. We wiped our asses with newspaper that I stole from my next-door neighbor's driveway. We used (and cleaned) as few dishes as possible to lower the water bill. I stopped short of having the boys and I take showers together to save water—but, believe me, the thought did cross my mind! To me, everything about life was wonderful back then—fresh. It was a new beginning—exactly what I needed. You know how a person or other animal has to leave the community for a while to grow into an adult—I believe in that. Your family, your culture, is always constricting you like some killer vine that grows on trees in the Amazon. When I moved in to this house where I now sit in an upstairs room, texting you on a T9 phone, I remember I had started writing my own memoir—with your encouragement—on an old-ass laptop with cracks covering the screen from a time when I dropped it. That stopped my memoir adventure. I remember so clearly how awesome everything was in the beginning. I didn't make much money but the money I made we spent well. I never bought new cars—me and the kids always bought cheap-ass beater cars and ran them until they didn't run anymore. Then we bought another for one-thousand, two-thousand—sometimes less. And we ran that one into the ground like the last one.

A trivia fact, so that next time you watch Jeopardy you won't lose: this area (Hallstead, PA and all over northeast Pennsylvania) is the only area in the world that has blue stone—aside from someplace in China—so "blue stone" is a common name around here..The Blue Stone Brewery, Blue Stone Tanning Salon, The Blue Stone Diner. Everything around here is Blue Stone something.

Hallstead is one giant quarry. Most of the land is owned by excavation companies—they extract this blue stone from the ground and it ends up as countertops in rich people's kitchens. Our house might go someday, if Annie decides to sell. I try not to think about it—as much as possible I'm living for today.

- - - -

I still saw Gary—he was still around. I went to check the mail and I saw him across the street and he waved me over and as soon as I was in reach, he started kissing me.

"You're too hot to be single," he said between kisses.

"Well you're the one who broke up with me!" I said.

But I kissed him again. I didn't even *want* to be coupled, not with him or anyone. I was happy to be single, to be free, to have a job, to be doing what I thought I should be doing with my life.

Gary continued to crush on me—and I crushed on him too. One time I heard a clicking on my bedroom window. I opened the window and looked down. It was Gary. He was standing on the sidewalk, throwing rocks at my window.

I said, "Hey what's up?"

"Is it cool inside of there?" he asked.

"Yeah."

I invited him in but he wouldn't come in. He never came inside this house except when we were viewing the place to rent it. I never bugged him about it—maybe he was shy. But we maintained a waving relationship—I saw him around town all the time and we'd wave at each other.

Shortly after that Gary had a heart attack and went into a coma. While it was still just a heart attack situation, before Gary was in his coma, they operated on his heart. After that he was in recovery and ok and talking to his family and me and some of his other friends—then he lapsed into a coma.

I went to see him almost every night when I got out of work. I'd stay until eleven when visiting hours were over. Some of Gary's family was there too and I could feel by the way they looked at me that they didn't want me there—but I was the last person Gary had loved and his family knew that I cared about him. They were as polite to me as they had to be.

As for me, I was devastated. Gary was a guy who didn't need any more bad luck. I felt awful for him and this awful was like a deep black cave in South America that only the natives could go into safely. But Gary had gone in with his headlamp and his hiking boots and right as he was yelling back to the top, "It's amazzzziiiiing!" his voice was cut short because some animal demon had eaten him and was carrying him down to hell.

I went to Gary's room at the hospital every night. I sat there next to him and talked to him and held his hand and sang him songs—I'd stroke his beard and look at him and hope that he was finally happy because the whole time I knew him, he was a very unhappy and peaceless person.

And there was a reason for that. Gary had a daughter that his best friend killed and after that happened, Gary was always very cold and distant to everyone. I think even before his daughter's murder, he was never a cheerful person. I hoped for him that in his coma he finally found some peace—and maybe some happiness if he was with his daughter.

- - - -

Here's how the murder happened. Gary's best friend started dating Gary's ex-girlfriend (his baby's mom). Gary always said that he noticed abuse to his daughter. He reported it but nobody ever listened to him. One time Gary found his daughter's hip was out of her socket (this sometimes happens when adult men abuse children). He found bruises. His daughter came home with a broken arm. Then one day she didn't come home at all.

There were eyewitnesses—you bet there were. See, this fucker who killed Gary's daughter (formerly Gary's best friend, now his forever ex-friend)..he didn't do this in a darkened basement or an empty trailer. Rumor was that he'd take her from the playground and go to this guy's mom's house. His mom would be watching *Family Feud* and he would kick his mother out—tell her to go to the upstairs bedroom and shut the door. That's where Gary's best friend raped Gary's daughter and covered her mouth so no one would hear the screams. He used to pick up the girl's head and slam it into the floor. This guy's mom used to sneak back downstairs so she could watch Steve Harvey make tame jokes for the benefit of a clueless, middle-class audience. Gary's friend's mom would laugh at all the jokes, even the ones where the survey said something sexual—like *condom* or *feminine itch*.

By the way, most of these details come from Gary's friend's mom. She didn't say shit at the trial but if you brought over a crack pipe and some nice-sized rocks she would tell you everything. Old people love crack.

So one day Gary's friend took Gary's daughter to his mom's house, and he fucked this little girl the way no one should be fucked.

Then he carried her to the park—taking his time, not in any hurry. Of course I wasn't there when this happened—I didn't even meet Gary till two years after his daughter got murdered. But the park where Gary's daughter..it's horrible to say it but the place where Gary's daughter was killed..that's a park where I often took my boys. And every time I went there after hearing the story of Gary's little girl, I had sort of an alternate reality experience where I was both in the present, watching Zach and Phillip play on the merry-go-round and other toys, and in the sand—and I was also playing through the gory details of the story of that girl's death, as I had collected from people around town who said they were there when it happened or at least claimed to *know someone* who was there or who knew or was related to a Hallstead police officer, judge, bailiff, lawyer..I mean if their aunt Bea was lesbian lovers with the court fucking *stenographer*, they thought they were an expert on that little girl's death. We all did. This was one of the legends of Hallstead. And like any great legend, it starts with a kernel of truth and then everyone who hears it, and everyone who subsequently tells it, spins it out their own way with their own details and it gets more horrific and less truthful, and anyone who wants to dig into the stories we all tell about that little girl, to try to get at the truth..well, that person has to go through a process of digging and sifting more complex and laborious than the process that the enemies of Indiana Jones went through to dig up the Lost Ark of the Covenant—and as that movie shows, no amount of digging will get you to the truth.

But what I saw, like an overlay in a video game, a heads-up display or some type of augmented reality, is my two boys, in reality, playing in the sand. Then in augmented-reality mode, my mind replaying the best version of the story as I could determine it: Gary's friend carries this little girl to the merry-go-round and sets her down, then goes to the edge of the park, winded. He lies down in the wood chips.

"Hey big shot!" I yell at the man.

Phillip and Zach look at me, trying to figure out who I'm yelling at, because there's no one there—it's just the three of us in the park.

But in my mind I'm watching the murder.

The guy says nothing—he's still lying in the wood chips, catching his breath. He's likely been high on meth for five or six days.

I look at the girl he has deposited on the merry-go-round. Her hip is out of its socket, her arm is broke. Her whole face is one big giant bruise. And she isn't wearing any pants. Her panties are on backwards—I suppose since she couldn't put them on herself, someone had helped her.

I take my phone out and I almost call 911—then I remember I'm not there..she's already been murdered..I would have to make a time-traveling 911 call to do any good. I almost dial the number anyway, thinking that I should *do something*—but I'm years late. I play it out in my mind, my impossible 911 call that could have saved that girl's life.

I dial 9-1-1.

They say, "What's your emergency?"

"Uh..this rapist child-molesting motherfucker just dropped off this girl at the park and I'm not squeamish but looking at this girl makes me want to throw up."

"Is she still alive?"

"Yeah, she's still alive but I wouldn't want to say for how long."

"Is the person who dropped her off still there?"

"He's lying in the fucking wood chips, lady!"

Gary's friend sits up and looks at me.

"Who are you talking to?" he asks me.

"My mother," I say.

"Oh, 'cause I thought you were talking about me."

"Not at all, man—get your rest."

The operator asks me who I'm talking to.

"I'm talking to *the guy*, the one I told you about who's prob'ly raping this girl."

Then I hear the operator order cops and ambulance to the scene.

Then she's asking me if I can do anything to help the little girl.

"Um..not really. I've got two kids myself I'm watching."

"You're doing the right thing," she assures me. "Don't let your kids out of your sight. I'm sending police and medics your way."

"Ok, thanks."

I stay on the line.

I look at Gary's ex-best friend. He's lying still with one arm over his eyes to cover the sunlight.

Then the child on the merry-go-round starts to cry. And it's breaking my heart looking at that broken little girl wailing and wailing as only someone whose entire life has been destroyed can do. I would run over and grab her, take her, rescue her, run her to the hospital and turn her over to doctors and therapists who would fix her whole life—I would do that, but this is all in my mind. There's no broken little girl lying screaming on the merry-go-round—she's been dead for years. I just like to come here and re-enact her torture and last moments—it comforts me the same way some people are comforted by going to church.

My boys come up to me and each one grabs an arm.

"What's wrong, Mom?"

The little girl's screaming goes up an octave.

I've got tears streaming down my face.

"Mom, it's ok. What are you sad about?"

I pull my boys close to me. Zach takes the phone from my hand and holds it to his ear. He listens for a while and says:

"Um, we called you by mistake. No. There's no little girl. No. There's no killer here either. No. You don't understand. Mom, we have to go. They're sending the police."

"THEY *SHOULD* SEND THE POLICE TO PUT THAT MOTHERFUCKER IN JAIL!!"

Zach says, "That's my mom. No. She's not yelling at anyone. No. We don't need psychiatric services!"

Zach hangs up.

"Mom we gotta go before they take you to a mental hospital."

But all I can see is that little girl lying on the merry-go-round—where she'll die—and I can see the man lying in the wood chips. I want to kill him myself—it would be the only sufficient justice.

With what that motherfucker put that girl through, she'd have probably had PTSD for the rest of her natural fucking life. No amount of counseling would have ever brought her peace. She would have probably grown up hating herself twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week—like me.

Every instinct in my body tells me to go get her, carry her to safety, but there's nowhere I could take her where that guy couldn't outrun me. I make the decision to hold onto Phillip and Zach until the police come. And although I stick by that decision to this day, I'll always feel partly responsible for not doing something to stop what happened next.

The rapist gets up from the wood chips.

He goes to the kid on the merry-go-round and all the way there he yells at the tiny person he's about to murder:

"SHUT UP!! I MEAN IT!! YOU'RE DESTROYING MY HIGH!! WHEN I GET HIGH I DON'T LIKE TO HAVE MY HIGH *DISTURBED* BY A SCREAMING FUCKING *CHILD!!*"

It's so weird I feel like I'm in a dream.

He's to her now—he's at the merry-go-round.

Everything he's doing is making the child scream louder.

I imagine—even though he never did this—Jessie, on our Sunday afternoon naps, holding his hand over my mouth so I can't scream while he takes me from behind, pushing his penis all the way inside me and filling me up to my stomach and making it so that I cannot scream, with his tight hand and his tight fingers covering my mouth. I can taste his fingernails. But I can never scream. I can never speak—I can never speak to anyone about this. And that is true long after Jessie takes his hand from my mouth and the taste of his fingernails goes away.

He pushes the screaming girl down gently so she's lying on the merry-go-round and puts his hand over her mouth.

Of course it's just Zach and Phil and me in the park. I'm staring at the merry-go-round and scaring my boys to death.

The killer's fingers run up her face and with two fingers on one side and two fingers on the other side of her nose, he squeezes her nostrils shut and she has no way to breathe—he suffocates her. A two-year-old child and her last seconds of consciousness featured the face she had come to fear most—the face that meant she was about to get raped (before she ever had a chance to learn what that word even meant)..and the other thing she saw right before she died was the big blue sky.

At this point, in reality, I'm looking up at the sky and crying:

"No!! No.."

And grabbing my boys' hands.

Then I look back at the merry-go-round but it's all blurry through my tears.

I hope that sky was what she was looking at when she died—and I hope she grasped, while the devil was choking her to death, that a full life consists of both terrible and beautiful things.

- - - -

Then the police would arrive, in real time, present day time, and Zachariah would try to convince them that I was fine. Of course they knew the routine.

The cop puts his arm around me.

"Davina, why don't you go home and get some rest?"

"If I was here," I sniffle.

I stand up and shrug the cop's arm off me.

"If I was here I would have killed that motherfucker."

"I know you would have."

"You know *people were here*!!"

"We don't know that, Davina. She was found in an empty park. There's no guarantee that anyone saw it. And if they did see it, there may have been no way for them to safely save her life."

I'm looking at this cop. His name is Officer Patrick. We know each other well.

"Someone was here."

"How do you know?"

"Because this park is never empty. And you don't always get a chance to *safely* save a life, do you Patrick?"

"No you don't."

"You know those videos you see of mothers whose baby is stuck underneath a car and the woman suddenly has superhuman strength and she can pick up a VW Bug and get her kid? That's how I would have been if I was here. I would have knocked that motherfucker off the merry-go-round and bashed his skull in with my motherfucking *hands*. I'm not talking about bash his *face* in—I'm talking I woulda smashed that motherfucker's skull in a thousand places."

"Then you'd be in jail."

"I'd rather *be* in jail."

"Why don't you try to forgive him?"

"The day I forgive my *stepfather* is the day I'll forgive a man who rapes and kills a two year old. That was his *best friend's* daughter!"

"You ever hear that saying?—it goes: *Not forgiving someone is like drinking poison and expecting the other person to die*?"

I roll my eyes.

"Sounds like some AA bullshit."

Patrick laughs.

"Do you want a ride home? I could give you and the boys a lift so you didn't have to walk."

"No, we better walk. Walking is good for your brain. I saw this thing on the internet, they had brain scans of people before and then after a fifteen-minute walk.."

I go on for a while telling Officer Patrick the difference between these brain scans and I see him looking at me, then my boys, and I know we're on a countdown—there's only a certain number of times I can call 911 while imagining the end of Gary's daughter before Officer Patrick is going to take my kids from me and put me a psych ward.

I grab my boys' hands and we start to go.

"Goodbye Officer Patrick."

"Davina! You still have my card?"
