"I couldn't honestly say. Business cards are so small and sometimes I throw them out thinking they're trash."

"You can call me at the station any day, just ask for Patrick. I mean feel free to. If you ever have anything you want to talk about."

I stop walking and turn around.

"I didn't mean to call 911."

"It's ok."

"I mean I know that's like a crime in itself and you should lock me up every time I do it."

Officer Patrick touched his fingers to his head even though he wasn't wearing a hat.

"It's ok," he says.

"I'm sorry," I say.

- - - -

Gary's ex-friend, the rapist and murderer of Gary's two-year-old daughter, was convicted for what I call *rape and murder of a goddamn baby* but the lawyers had longer and more slippery terms for this. The guy went to jail for like three months. He's a minor celebrity around here because he has a band that's a big deal in Hallstead. He's a big fish in a small pond and you can't keep famous people in jail—we need them to entertain us. He's also a tattoo artist and tons and tons of people know him. Jails are for losers and homeless people—you can't keep popular people in jail!

One time after that, I went to a show and his band was one of the bands playing. During a lull in the songs someone yelled, "Baby killer!" It's common knowledge in this town that he killed that little girl. He should have served more than three months but I guess living in a small town where everybody knows everybody, and everybody knows you raped and killed your best friend's *two-year-old* daughter..having that reputation is punishment enough. Every place you go, everyone you see, every girl you try to fuck..they all know that you like to get your dick wet with two year olds..after you've beaten their head in so it looks like a giant blueberry (of course), broken a couple of bones (snap! snap!), and dislocated their pelvis (naturally!—I mean you haven't really *fucked* a kid until you've dislocated their pelvis!). Every girl you take back to your bedroom knows that at some point in your past, you used a two-year-old's tiny cunt as a masturbation sleeve. Then you took her life. And you know what? This bitch you brought back to your room? She fucks you anyway.

- - - -

So Gary's two-year-old daughter gets raped and killed by his own best friend. Gary turns cold and distant from everyone. Then he has a heart attack and goes into a coma.

He was in that coma seven days and I was there with him all seven of those days. I was with him more than his family.

And finally they decided Gary wasn't going to wake up, so they turned off his life support. Everybody went—it was a big deal to the whole town, not just Gary's family. There were so many people there, we couldn't all fit in the room at the same time. People were going in and saying their goodbyes—this lasted all day. I left the hospital to pick up Brett (Gary's eighteen-year-old son who didn't have a car). I was driving so fast from the hospital to Gary's house to pick up Brett and twice as fast on the way back to the hospital—I wanted to be there when they pulled the plug. When Brett and I got there, it was family only in Gary's room but one of Gary's aunts saw me and knew that I had been his last love, and she pulled me into the room and pushed me up close to Gary's bed.

That's the saddest thing in the world—watching someone you love, die. You don't know—he could be totally aware inside that coma and screaming in his mind for them not to pull the plug. Or maybe he left us a week ago, when he had his heart attack. I had one of his hands clasped inside mine and I looked across Gary's bed to see his mother and father. They were both crying.

"My loss is minuscule compared to yours." I whispered this to Gary's parents.

They said, "We know you loved him."

"He loved you, too." Gary's mom said that, and she broke down crying.

I realized that what I said to Gary's parents, I could have also said to him. My loss—the loss of a lover and a friend—was minuscule compared to his loss—the loss of every year he might have lived and everything he might have done with each of those imaginary days that we always tack onto the history of one who dies young.

His family was praying as the doctor flipped one matte-black switch on the column of machines that was keeping Gary alive. No one moved and everyone's ears and eyes were fixed on Gary and his heart monitor—waiting for what we had all seen on TV, that moment when the vital signs monitor next to the hospital bed goes from beeping the patient's heartbeat and showing an animated graph of its contractions..to showing a flat line..and instead of its comforting *beep..beep..beep* that tells you your friend, lover, brother, father, mother, *child* that you are still alive, the beeps stop and all you are left with is a single tone, unwavering, that means there will be no more talking or kissing or loving..that as soon as the heart stops pumping, the brain will start dying—and in a few short minutes, all that glorious electrical activity that makes us so interesting to each other..will be gone.

## 74
I did have a couple of jobs in Susquehanna before moving to Hallstead. In Susquehanna I started with a company called CareGivers America and I continued working for them through the move to Hallstead. Before that I was working at a bar/restaurant as a waitress.

I worked at CareGivers America for about three years. I liked that job—I'm good at taking care of people. At CGA, you're a caregiver, a home health aid. They send you randomly to different homes. Sometimes you don't stay more than a few days till someone else more permanent comes. Sometimes you stay for a few weeks or a few months. The last client I had was a family, and they were perfect for me: a husband and wife with two kids. The lady's mom had Alzheimer's and she needed round-the-clock care. Her name was Estelle.

I fed Estelle, bathed her, dressed her, changed her clothes, took her to the bathroom.

When I first got there, I just kept an eye on Estelle. Her daughter Madie would yell at her kids to take all of Estelle's clothes out of the drawers and put them in the wash—Madie seemed to think that her mother would live longer if there were fewer germs on her clothing.

I talked with her about this.

"Estelle has Alzheimer's."

"Don't you think I know that? I don't need advice from *some nurse* to tell me my mother has Alzheimer's."

"It's a brain disease."

"I know *that*."

"Washing her clothes every day isn't going to help."

I touched Madie's hands and she cried with me.

"I just wish there was something I could *do*. Isn't there something more we can *do*?"

"No," I said. "She's going to die from this."

Madie wailed.

"What we can do is make this process easier for her."

(Sobbing.) "How do we do that?"

"Well what we do is you let me take care of the details like washing her and feeding her, and you—when you feel like it—spend time with your mother talking or watching TV or whatever you two used to do together, within limits."

"She doesn't talk to me anymore."

"Is there a favorite show you used to watch together?"

Madie nods. She wipes her nose.

"Family Feud," she says to me.

"That's perfect!" I say. "So once a day you two watch your show together. I'll stay out of your way and keep the kids with me so it's just you-and-your-Mom time."

She hugs me.

"We're so glad to have you here, Davina. You've already changed the feeling in our house. You know how when you walk into a house where someone's dying and there's this *smell*. It's not even the smell of the dying person's body—it's the smell of *death*, of death itself. Before you came, this house smelled like that—it doesn't anymore."

"Madie, I'm glad things have changed since I've been here but your mother is going to die..and she's going to die soon, and there's nothing that can change that."

"I know."

Madie hugs me and rubs her hands all over my back.

"I know she's going to die. You know what really makes me sad?"

"What?"

"That I'll never be able to tell her goodbye. It's hard to know if she understands anything right now. She's had Alzheimer's so long I don't even know what conversation was the last one she understood."

I hug Madie again.

"Let's just start with you two watching *Family Feud* together and we'll go from there, ok?"

"Ok."

"You can do this Madie. There is nothing that can happen that you can't deal with. You own this day, alright—you can handle whatever it brings."

"Ok, you're right."

"What time does *Family Feud* come on?"

"I don't know. I'll check the TV guide. Thank you."

I wanted to tell her not to thank me, that this was just my job, but I had worked there long enough and come to know the family well enough that if I had said that, it would have been bullshit. So I said:

"You're welcome, Madie. Everything's going to be ok."

And even that was half bullshit, but it was the unavoidable kind.

- - - -

Estelle deteriorated quickly.

I basically took care of her like she was a baby. She couldn't get out of bed. I changed her diapers. I sponge bathed her. She started taking some pain medicine intravenously and I maintained the IV line into the back of her hand and injected medicine throughout the day. I got her dressed each morning, brushed her hair, brushed her teeth. I fed her like a baby, bringing spoonfuls of applesauce and other soft foods directly to her mouth. I made sure she took all of her pills. I kept her company—which mostly consisted of me giving her encouraging words and her saying nothing—though sometimes Estelle would shout out perfectly nonsensical and classically poetic phrases like:

"If you sleep like a princess you'll do battle like a princess hark!"

Or: "You *never* had your religion! Hark like a season of the one who wraps blood around your head."

I admit I was scared by some of what she said. Like when crazy people say stuff that gets stuck inside your head—they might not even know what they're talking about but your brain twists it around and adds in little pieces and soon enough it sounds like the stuff that prophets from the *Old Testament* say. Finally I figured out that she was using short words to take the place of longer words or phrases—like "hark" meant "Joan of Arc." She used hundreds of these substitutions and I learned as many as I could. After that, our communication improved significantly.

I was with her every hour of the day except when she was sleeping. What did I do for her? Whatever a human being needs.

I worked with Estelle and Madie and Madie's kids Monday through Friday eight to five every week. That family loved me—it was perfect. I would go over to hang out and go swimming even when I wasn't working. But when I was there, even when to CareGivers America I was off the clock, I was always on the clock with Estelle. I loved that woman. Loved her like she was my own grandmother.

- - - -

Now let me tell you about their dog.

Me and Madie's kids would watch TV when Estelle was all taken care of in her room. Me and these two kids would lie on our stomachs in the living room and watch the Cartoon Network. Madie was locked in her bedroom drinking gin. *How do you know this?* you might ask. Trust me—I know the smell of gin.

So I'm lying there watching cartoons with the girls—like on our tummies with our necks propped up on pillows and arms. I had on my scrubs and all of a sudden I felt hot breath pushing up my butt crack! I was not only surprised, but instantly fucking excited and turned *on*! We watched cartoons extra long that day—I didn't want to stand up until that doberman's spit had dried.

From then on, every time I saw that dog she would come up to me and smell my crotch. Madie even said something about it once:

"That dog *reeeeeaally* likes you."

"Yeah, isn't that funny?! You never know who a dog will latch onto. Boy, though, she's a beauty, isn't she?"

"Yes she is," is all Madie said.

What you really want to do in these situations is scream:

"I WASH MY VAGINA EVERY DAY IT'S NOT *MY* FAULT YOUR HORNY-ASS DOG SNIFFS ME EVERY TIME WE MEET!!"

"Sadie. Get off me."

Then Sadie would go in the corner and give me these highly-personalized looks of embarrassment and scorn. The dog's feelings were hurt because I wouldn't let us bring our love out into the open. I was so scared Madie would find out that I had let her dog lick my cunt through a pair of green scrubs one time.

That was the sensible me, the me that judged her own actions, who knew that it was *beastiality* to let that doberman lick my cunt through my scrubs.

But there was another me, a me who needed intimacy like an addict needs a fix—they'll do anything to get out of the hole of withdrawal, and they'll do anything to get out of the hole of normalcy that follows. I was like that addict.

Sadie and I had our own secret relationship in that house. When others were around, I shooed Sadie off my crotch, and she'd leave me alone right away. But in the afternoons, when Estelle was all taken care of and I had given her her pain med, when Madie checked on me and the kids watching TV in the living room and she went off to her room and locked the door, once I could smell the juniper berries from Madie's gin, and once Madie's two girls were engrossed by the TV, I'd get up and tell them I was going to check on their grandmother—but that's not what I'd do.

I'd find Sadie—I'd find that nasty dog—and I'd lead her to the laundry room. I must have had some fantasy left over from childhood about letting those puppies lick my tiny cunt but as soon as Sadie entered the room her paws were all over my scrubs and we were just like two people whose love is still fresh, ripping each other's clothes off as soon as they're inside the door to their apartment—they never make it to the bed, they just fuck each other right there on the floor.

Sadie was like that with me.

I'll never forget our first time in the laundry room. I pulled my pants down and let her lick me and I came super fast. She was so strong that the force of her licking me pushed me into a corner—and I felt out of control, and dizzy, and bad.

I am a bad girl.

I do bad things.

I just accept that parts of me will always be secrets, because nobody is ready to handle them.

I always came with Sadie. Because you see, dogs are not squeamish about eating pussy. With people they're always afraid they'll wear out the clitoris and they hold back with their mouth and teeth like it's a tongue-only affair. A dog will eat that shit out like it was *crème brûlée*. It's one big crazy world with everyone trying to get off. Men fucking sheep, boys fucking cats, brothers and sisters fucking each other, parents fucking children. I mean, if you asked me if I had ever let a boy dog fuck my pussy, the answer would be no. But not 'cause I don't *want* to—just 'cause the opportunity hasn't presented itself.

Sadie licked my pussy many times. We eventually moved our liaisons to a spare bedroom. I'd get on the bed, then Sadie would get on the bed and I just couldn't get my pants off fast enough. I could go on, but most of the great sex I've had is the product of my imagination. And some of my very best orgasms were with that doberman at Estelle and Madie's house.

If you had asked me, back then, if I felt bad about playing with Madie's dog in that way, I would have blushed—I'd've been as embarrassed as hell.

But I've lived a few years since then, had a few more experiences. And what I'd say now is that life is hard, and life is lonely. - - - -

Eventually, for all of us, that day must come to die.

Estelle's room was packed with family from the door to her dying bed. I was as much her family as any of these people I had never once seen visit her in the two years I took care of Estelle—so I knelt beside her and held her hand. She didn't talk anymore—all her coded expressions which only I understood were locked in Estelle's brain, never again to be revealed by her as the object of my decoding.

The kids were across Estelle's bed from me and they were holding the rails, their eyes moving side to side but not a pinch of sadness on their faces. They didn't understand death—not the way grown-ups do, and I was happy for them that they knew by the number of people in the room that something big was happening but they didn't quite understand what.

When you've been charged to take care of someone in the last years and months of their life..when you feed them every bite of food they eat, when every sip of water that person drinks, they drink out of a cup you are holding and a straw you have cut to be just the perfect length..you get close to that person almost like a mother and a child—the only difference is that when someone is being born, it's the younger one who needs the care of the older one..and when someone is dying, it's the older one who needs the care of the younger one.

It is a privilege to be present at someone's death just as it is a privilege to be present at someone's birth.

Of course my mind thought of Gary and that sent my emotions into the stars, rolling around Orion's belt. We had sent space transmissions there, and got an answer—but no one paid any attention. Estelle was dying in her bed and not once had I seen Madie come into her mother's room. They never hugged, they never talked—that's what I was there for—to do things that daughters should do for their mothers. I wiped that lady's butt three times a day and what did Madie do? On dying day she stood just outside the doorway to her mother's room. Never got a step closer. It was like she was making a point that she wasn't technically in the room when her mom died.

I didn't judge though. I didn't know what Madie's relationship had been like with her mom but if it was anything like my relationship with Lisa..well, I can tell you this: there is no amount of money that would make it worth it to me to wipe that woman's butt.

So we held hands, and we held Estelle's hands, and people started crying. First I did, then the kids did, then, seeing the children, everyone in the room cried.

I could feel Estelle's heart shift into low gear and I squeezed her hand to make it go faster but it didn't work. She shifted down, then down, and then I lost the pulse.

Right then her eyes opened like she had seen a surprise.

That's the last movement I saw of her. There was no doctor so I stood up, placed Estelle's hand beside her, and I closed her eyes with my fingers.

- - - -

I left the room. Let someone else pull the sheet over her head—if they had the decency to do so. I couldn't get Estelle's last look out of my mind. Had she seen something you can only see after you're dead? Or did that old lady feel that her heart had stopped and that had shocked the hell out of her.

I took care of that lady for two years. I loved her. I sat on the back patio, alone, on one of the pool chairs, and I bawled. Wiped my eyes and nose on my scrubs and didn't give a fuck if I looked professional.

I was kneeling there.

I was holding her hand.

I felt the last pump of her heart.

I looked in that woman's face and saw her take her last breath—she inhaled, then exhaled, and that was it.

And unlike anyone else in that house, I was getting paid to do this. If I was getting paid to watch my own mother while she died, I'd prob'ly slash her neck with a steak knife. Estelle was a mother who never yelled at me (she couldn't), who loved to see me each morning when I arrived (because if didn't feed her, her daughter Madie certainly wasn't going to), etc., etc. It's like in upper-class America they'd rather farm out everything to a contractor. I was paid to help Estelle die because it was easier for Madie to pay me to do it than to do it herself.

It was the same thing back in California: a brown woman pushing a stroller with a white baby in it. I guess that's one thing good about being poor: you can't afford to pay other people to play with your kids, and you're forced to play with them yourself.

I used to think I would go to my mother's funeral—now I'm sure that I won't. Maybe Estelle and Madie had those kinds of differences between them—crimes that can never be fixed.

As I sat on the pool furniture behind Estelle's house, I had a picture in my mind of that woman taking her last breath..and the look of surprise on her face when her eyes opened. Some images, they burn into your mind forever—that was one of those for me.

- - - -

The whole thing was too much—it was the kind of job where they pay for your head, but (if you're like me), you end up throwing your heart in for free. Now that Estelle was dead, CareGivers America would assign me to another house with another dying person in it. I couldn't do it again. The very next day I went to the CGA office and put in my two weeks' notice. My supervisor read my resignation on the spot.

"We all die, Davina."

"I know."

"You have a big heart—probably too big."

"Too big for what?"

"For this life. You know, sometimes the solution is to *care less*."

"I can't. I can't do that."

"I respect you for that, Davina. In fact, more than respect you for it, I can say I even love you a bit for feeling that way. Before I worked here, I ran a homeless shelter in Allentown. We took volunteers to work the desk, heat up food, give people jackets and hats and gloves and everything. Most of the volunteers worked out fine, but there was one I'll never forget. Your typical volunteer comes in, heats up a couple pots of soup, hands out jackets, takes a census of who's there and when the volunteer finishes their shift they feel like they've something good, they leave, and that's it. They *care* the right amount for that job. So then I get this guy, one winter, he calls me and says he wants to volunteer. He wants to work nights which is good for me 'cause it's hell to fill that 10pm to 6am time slot. But he does it. He picks up extra shifts, he works five nights in a row—this guy is like some crazy godsend. But after a while he gets frustrated because, as he put it, 'We're not really helping people.' 'Sure we are,' I say. 'There's twenty people here who didn't freeze to death tonight.' He explained to me that his reason for volunteering was to get people out of homelessness—that was the problem he was trying to solve. I explained to him that our job was to keep as many people as possible from freezing to death while they slept tonight. We're not finding people jobs. We're not building people houses. We're just addressing the very real problem of people dying tonight in the cold. That volunteer quit being a volunteer, right then, right as we were speaking. He cared too much about the people we were helping to be part of a short-term solution that in no way addressed the root causes of homelessness."

"Interesting story."

My supervisor opened one of her desk drawers and pulled out a checkbook.

"This won't be a payroll check, is that ok?"

"Ok for what?"

"For your last two weeks of work."

"I don't think I can go back to that house."

"I don't expect you to."

"I don't understand."

"I'm paying you for your last two weeks of work, Davina."

"Am I going to a new house?"

"No. You're taking a break from death and dying. Do something fun! You've done an excellent job with us, especially with Estelle—I know that must have been hard."

"It was."

She's holding out my check—I take it.

"Thank you."

"You are very welcome, my sweet Davina. If you're ever looking for a job, think of us!"

I sat in my car outside CareGivers America, looking at the check my boss had written for me. It wasn't two weeks' pay—it was a month's pay. At first I thought it was an accident and I should take it back inside for a correction. Then I realized there's no way it was an accident. The truth was the hardest thing for me to accept: everyone in this situation was happy with me. There weren't going to be any bad surprises—everything was in the open. I mean yes I had let Madie's doberman lick my pussy but that didn't detract from the work I had done for her family and for ones before.

I guess it was this really weird feeling of: *I didn't do anything wrong.*

I felt so happy that no one was mad at me that I sat crying on my steering wheel for about an hour.

Then I went to the bank to cash my motherfucking check.

## 75
That check didn't last long, though, and Chris paid for everything for a few months—he's so generous. Also it didn't hurt that I got food stamps.

I kind of got lost for a couple months—I needed to take a break from everything. Phillip was always getting in trouble at school. It was upsetting: to me, to the school, to Phillip. He always had problems with any kind of authority, but around this time it got a lot worse. He started smashing his fingers in his desk. He pulled the strings on his hoodie till his face was blue. He banged his head on the wall while his teachers were speaking. He was sent to the principal's office. They sent him to one of the school counselors—that relationship ended when Phillip emerged from one of their sessions early, with a chunk of the counselor's long black hair gripped tightly in his fist. That counselor wouldn't see Phillip again. They sent him to another of the school's counselors—this time a man in his sixties named Mr. Acedorian who was completely bald. After he met with Phillip, he met with me. He told me Phillip needed medication. I believed him—I did what he said. He told me to take Phillip to a mental health clinic that he specified, so I took Phillip to the the mental health clinic.

What we got from the clinic was nothing of what I expected. We waited for *three hours* before an evaluator could see us. It was a depressing waiting room to sit in—everyone there had obvious mental and physical disabilities. It didn't seem like a place where me or Phillip belonged. It was depressing to see people like that, who could hardly live their lives due to some disability that had nothing to do with their spirit and everything to do with the hand that genetics had dealt them. I know now that what unnerved me then about being in that place was that that clinic was *exactly* where we needed to be—we were that poor, we were that sick.

I went back with Phillip to see the evaluator. She wasn't a doctor, she wasn't a scientist, she wasn't a nurse or a physician's aid or a licensed nurse practitioner. She was just a lady making two times minimum wage to fill out forms on a computer. She didn't show any interest in Phillip or me as people—she barely made eye contact with either of us. She asked me a few questions—didn't even speak to Phillip—then she printed out a list of prescription medications for Phillip to take.

"Door nine, window four and we'll have you all taken care of."

"What?" I said.

The woman looked at me like I was a fucking idiot. And yet, *she* was the one wearing artificial nails so long she could hardly type our answers into her online form when we were answering her questions.

"Door nine. Window *four*. Take your prescriptions with you."

"When do we get to see a doctor?"

"Ma'am. I know you're confused—"

"I'm not confused."

"Many people at this stage of the process have questions that really need to be asked of a doctor, not me—"

"What are you?"

"I'm Phillip's social worker."

"I never asked for a social worker—I asked for a *doctor*."

"And you're going to get your doctor if you follow my instructions. First you go to door nine."

She was speaking to me like I was mentally impaired.

"Then you go to window four."

"What happens then?"

"Then you'll meet your doctor."

I got up. Phillip followed.

The social worker said, "You see how easy things go when you follow instructions?"

I thought of making a smart remark about her artificial nails, but didn't—the bitch was below me.

Door nine, window four was easy enough to find.

"Everybody, have your scripts ready, *in your hand,* and unmarked and un-torn. A blemished script is a bad script. Do you understand me?" Then this announcer—who was real, right there in the room—repeated her message *verbatim*: "Everybody, have your scripts ready, *in your hand*.." Etcetera, etcetera.

I looked at her face and she seemed a good candidate for some kind of alien-human hybrid. No one with a human soul could stand there and repeat that all day.

I asked Phillip how he was feeling and he said he was ok. I promised we'd be out of there soon. I was proud of him for staying calm at that clinic—the place disturbed me so much it made me want to start snapping motherfuckers' necks.

- - - -

When we got to the front of the line, the clerk at window four said:

"Are those your prescriptions?"

"Yes."

"Well, hand them to me."

I put the papers in a slot below the service window—there was no way I could "hand" this woman anything.

She checked that my scripts were in suitable condition and then took two steps over to a small Japanese man who was playing video poker on a handheld device.

"Dr. Fujimoto?"

He held the back of his hand up to the clerk.

She stopped.

When the doctor finished his turn in video poker, he took the scripts and set them on the table. He signed them all without looking at a single one. He stacked them up, handed them to the clerk, and went back to his game. The clerk slid the prescriptions back to me.

"You should have no trouble filling these anywhere," she said.

And Phillip and I left that crazy clinic.

We drove through Burger King as our reward for submitting ourselves to that tiny slice of bureaucratic insanity. We drove through, then ate in the car in their parking lot. We talked—a lot. I asked him if he knew why he was doing the violent and disturbing things he did. He didn't know. A huge part of me—which remained silent—wondered if this was normal kid stuff, that needed no medication to "fix" it, that he would grow out of with time.

But I trusted the system, and I filled those prescriptions, and I put Phillip on them.

- - - -

Then I researched the medications they had him on and decided it was not a good idea for him to be on them—they were relatively new medicines with no track record long enough for anyone to know what the long-term side effects might be.

I took Phillip off all the pharmaceuticals.

We tried homeopathic cures I found online that are supposed to stabilize your mood. Chris wasn't even around but he paid for everything Phillip needed—anything I wanted to put him on. We tried pills and drops that dissolve in your food, aromatherapy, massage, acupuncture, salts that you put in your bath..none of that stuff worked.

We eventually went the therapist route—which is what my gut told me to do in the beginning. We got Phil all kinds of therapy—and he seemed to like going. We settled on a therapist, and that therapist led to a psychiatrist. Between the therapist and the psychiatrist, Phil was diagnosed with Asperger's, ODD, and ADHD.

Asperger's (in case you don't know) is high-functioning autism.

ODD means if anyone tells you to do something, you'll do the opposite. ODD stands for Oppositional Defiant Disorder. A better definition is that if you have ODD, you disobey and defy authority figures—which, honestly, I'm proud that Phillip does. I've always questioned authority and in this country anyone who doesn't is stupider than I care to discuss.

Phillip also got diagnosed with ADHD—I'm pretty sure you all know what that is by now.

- - - -

I got a job at Family Dollar. I loved that job *ridiculously*—especially after CareGivers America. CGA was so intensive responsibility-wise—people's lives are in your hands, where at Family Dollar all you had to do was stock shelves and ring cash registers. I didn't care that it was menial labor—I'm not proud. I was singing and dancing and joking with my customers and this guy came through the line almost *upset* with me that I was so happy working there.

"Do you really love your job that much?"

It was like my happiness was a personal affront to him.

"I do, I do" I sang.

"You realize," he said, "that you are a *cash register* at a fucking Family Dollar?"

"That's not exactly true," I sang, in a Glinda the Good Witch voice. "I'm a *cashier*, not a cash register—the cash register is right here."

"People in your class aren't supposed to be so *happy*."

"Maybe *you'd* be happier if you switched from diet root beer to tequila. Have you read the ingredients in this stuff? It contains aspartame—that turns into formaldehyde in your stomach."

"No it doesn't."

"Fucks up your DNA."

"Where did you read that?"

"Hey, I'm just trying to keep you from getting cancer, dude—it's *your* life."

"*I'm* the customer here. The customer is always right. You're not paid to lecture me on the products I buy. Do you know how much I make in a year?"

"How much *what* you make in a year? Heated carbon dioxide? A fuckload is what I'm guessing."

"I almost want to see your manager."

"Well, here's the thing about that. If I'm a cute lil gecko with those funky eyes on the side of its head—you know the lizards that kids like to keep as pets, the ones that change color to blend in as an evolutionary defense mechanism—"

"Yes I know what a gecko is."

"Yeah well if I'm a gecko, my boss is a fucking megalodon *shark* and she'll bite you in half for giving me shit about being *happy* while I work. That bitch'll probably file a lawsuit against you for impeding commerce. So. Your choice."

I pick up the register phone.

"Do you want to talk to her?"

"Maybe you could just ring me up."

I hang up the phone.

"Are you still going with the formaldehyde root beer?"

"I'm not *changing my purchases* based on what you say."

I point outside the store.

"Three blocks that way?—up Speedway?—is a liquor store," I tell him. "Generic tequila, no aspartame, for less than you spent on gas today."

"Do you recommend this to all your customers?"

"Only the uptight ones."

I hold eye contact with this white-collar terminal piece of flesh.

Then I scan his three huge generic-brand bottles of diet root beer and tell him the price.

"I'm having a party," he says.

"Looks like a wild one."

"It will be. It will be wild."

"I'm sure it will be. What's the occasion?"

"Me getting fired. Hahahahaha."

He takes his bags. Near the automatic doors he turns around and yells:

"Next time don't be so *fucking* happy!"

"Yeah well check out that megalodon shark—get some personality tips, maybe you don't get fired next time."

Then he goes.

My store manager Suzy comes over.

"You want me to file an incident report?"

"No need," I say. "I can handle him."

- - - -

Soon after that they made *me* a store manager and after that my job sucked—I didn't like the responsibility. Before I was a store manager I was making out with the other stock girls, finding hidden spots in the stock warehouse and feeling each other up and down, kissing—just making each other feel good. Then they made me store manager and it was my job to tell girls not to make out in the stockroom. Before I was store manager, the other checker girls were my friends. Getting promoted destroyed all that for me. Instead of doing a quantifiable job for my employer, I was now paid a dollar fifty extra an hour to rat out my friends.

I often tried to get freaky with Suzy when we were in the upstairs office, but she never would. Instead, she looked in the direction of the cameras that were watching us. I still flirted with her—I'd come up behind her in a hallway and reach down her Family Dollar uniform-beige pants and touch her over her panties. One time she turned around and kissed me for like three seconds, but that's as far as she ever went. I thought about her licking my pussy in the office of the Family Dollar for the rest of that day at work, and as soon as the boys were asleep that night, I got off on every aspect of Suzy that I knew of: her seniority at work that meant she could have me fired at any moment, her tight ponytails and pigtails and buns she wore to work (the simple idea of Suzy undoing her hair could make me cum)..her impeccable scent..her alertness and her seriousness (what an honor it would be to make her laugh—what an honor still to make her cum). I think that was it: I wanted to take the virginity of her seriousness—I wanted to take her tightness and her discipline and be the one she trusted to make that stiff ball of stress that was Suzy's pussy, for once, let go and cum and squirt all over my hands and face so that *she* knew..that *I* knew..that she hit the ceiling.

This fantasy was running through my mind from the minute I met her on my first day at Family Dollar. When I got promoted to store manager it got even worse. So many times when she was talking I had to ask her to repeat herself. My reality wasn't really with Family Dollar and the work we did there. Every stock request I made, every damaged/void form I signed..through all of that I obsessed about Suzy.

It's sick.

I know it's sick.

You don't have to tell me.

But *damn* I wanted to be one of the first people to play with that little girl's pussy. She was sixteen. I was thirty-three. I guess you can draw your own conclusions from that.

Once or twice a week I would get called out of work to get Phillip from school—I would have to miss days at Family dollar. They were accommodating when I was still a cashier, but as a store manager I couldn't skip days. Suzy and her boss called me into the office. They talked a long time but I didn't hear a word—I was looking at Suzy up and down, and even her feet were clad in white Keds..so subtle, but so cute. I wanted a dick just so I could fuck that one girl with it. Wanted to spread those reluctant little legs and fuck her so good that I was the first person besides herself to make her cum.

Then my focus came back to the upstairs office at this dismal American corporation called Family Dollar, where in all likelihood I had just gotten fired.

Suzy and her boss were staring at me. Everyone was silent.

"So, can I just take my things and go?"

"Suzy will escort you out."

"Good. This is actually better for me since I have a family to take care of and you're in the business of..selling things to families for a dollar."

Suzy escorted me downstairs and took my locker and office keys. We went to the front of the store. I walked backwards through the sliding doors and made a peace sign with my fingers, kissed it, and held it up to her. Suzy walked back under the fluorescent lights without even a wave and I realized what a boring person she was—who fit perfectly into this boring life.

The sliding doors closed.

"I'M STILL GONNA COME SHOP HERE, FUCKERS!!"

I yelled that as loud as I could, banging on the plastic window pane of the exit door, but nobody—not Suzy, not Suzy's boss, not any shoppers in the store, not any customers in the parking lot—heard me.

It's me standing in a parking lot full of empty cars, yelling at the top of my lungs, yelling for help, yelling for attention, maybe just yelling for company—but there's no one here. A hundred empty cars parked in front of a Family Dollar and not a single person just to share a word with. I would say, "Story of my life," but it's not just mine—it's every person in this goddamn country's.

## 76
I *thought* they fired me but apparently I must have majorly dissociated during that meeting with Suzy and the general manager because when I talked with Suzy later it became clear to me that they thought I was *quitting*! Oh well.

Me quitting was good timing anyway because of all the things that were happening with Phillip. Some lady from one of his special schools asked if I had Phillip on disability.

"No, I don't."

"Well it would be a good idea if you did."

"Really, why?"

"It'll open up new options for his health insurance. And it would help you out financially."

I applied and Phillip was approved and that brought us a little bit of extra money. We also got food stamps and some monthly cash that was basically welfare—that's what they used to call it anyway.

I tried to focus on doing whatever I could do with Phillip. I didn't go back to work for a while—and I didn't feel bad about being on welfare or not working..I've always been a hard worker. I stayed home and tried to keep Phillip from hurting himself or others. He has a big mouth, but he's never hurt anyone physically. At school, his teachers and the principal and some of the students thought he was dangerous but he's really not—he just has a lot of pent-up rage from legitimate societal ills and half of it is prob'ly because I'm such a fuck-off parent. If I raised the devil from a baby, he would have turned out better than Phillip did.

- - - -

Also, right about then, something happened to my back. I tried to break the boys up from fighting and my back was the loser. I was having major amounts of pain all the time. I spent all day in bed—I was too sore to get up and I didn't want to do anything, it was so bad. My doctor gave me pain pills sometimes. I would get a bottle of Vicodin and trade it for weed. I never much cared for painkillers.

As you may have noticed with bad things that happen to me, when it rains, it pours.

My back pain couldn't be one of those temporary, fixes itself in a few weeks kind of pain. No. My doctor told me I had to have back surgery, that I had degenerative bone disease that would likely plague me the rest of my life.

I remember the surgery date—it was August 23rd, 2011. I was in the hospital for three days. They cut open my spine and found crumbling pieces of discs pressing into my nerves—and a bulging disc—all that was pressing up against my nerves and causing me a freakish amount of pain. I couldn't even lie down for a while because fragments of discs were floating around my spine. And the great parting news from my surgeon as I left the hospital: I could expect this to happen in the future maybe. This was scary news, but you just gotta go on with your life. You know that quote, "Be kind, for everyone you meet is fighting a hard battle?" Usually I think of myself as the one who needs to be kind, but during that back surgery time, I thought of myself as the one who was fighting the hard battle.

What that surgeon did, did help me. I had Phillip and Zach move my mattress downstairs because I wasn't allowed to climb stairs for four weeks (doctor's orders). But after the recovery period, I could stand up—a few days later I could walk. It still aches to this day and I can't stand up for long periods of time, but I can do stuff like walk to town.

It was so bad leading up to it, my back was already stressed, it was already aching. I knew enough to know that something was wrong with my sciatic nerve. I was going to school online at the time and my back was already bad from working on the computer all day and night. It was hard to get comfortable—which may sound like a small problem but it becomes a big one when you're dealing with it 24/7, every night you try to sleep but can't get into a position where nothing's hurting so bad it prevents you from sleeping. You can't stand, you can't sit in any position for very long before something in your back starts to hurt again.

I'll tell you what fucked up my back—but you have to promise not to tell my boys—I would hate for them to think they hurt their mom.

One day I was downstairs with the boys. I had been upstairs in my room but I heard Zach and Phillip arguing and I went downstairs and saw them roughhousing—I was trying to break them up so I got between them. Then Zach was pushing me and pushing me against Phillip and I felt and heard a popping sound like a cracking bubble.

I literally crawled up the stairs and got in my bed and cried. The kids came upstairs and Zach said:

"I'm sorry, Mom. I didn't mean to hurt you."

"Oh you didn't hurt me," I said. "I've just been stressed lately. Go back down and play."

Phillip hugged my face and that had me crying all over again.

Phillip said, "I'm sorry."

"It's nothing you did, ok!? What would make me happy is if you give your mom some alone time. You can go downstairs and play but play *gently*—do that for me?"

My pain got so bad I couldn't function.

Sometimes I would lie on the floor wailing and crying and the kids didn't know what to do. I'm sure it frightened them—having their mother incapacitated for so long. All I did is sit around crying in pain, all day every day. I couldn't lie down..I was so tired..the only position I could be in was sitting up in this one office chair.

When you're that fucked up on pain or anything debilitating, it's hard to be your own health advocate—it's not like you have all that bunny rabbit energy to make calls and set up appointments and research what doctors are in your area that specialize in the right fields *and* take your insurance.

But at least I *had* insurance and—slowly, slowly—I kept trying various therapies (physical therapy, chiropractic) and then finally my PCP X-rayed my back and I got the surgery I needed. After that, compared to the pain I had before, I was in heaven. When I went to physical therapy after my surgery they did electrostimulation on my back, as usual, but I had been there enough times by then that the nurse gave me the machine and let me control the voltage myself. There were probes all over my back. By degrees, I turned the machine up, and up, and up. I felt shocks all over my back, little twitches, but hardly any pain. Like I said, I was in heaven.

## 77
The kids would go to school, I would stay home, clean house, cook dinner for the kids. We had a daily cycle—for a while it was really nice.

My sister Jamie and I spent a lot of time together for a few months—we saw each other almost every day—and then we'd slip out of it and wouldn't talk or see each other for like half a year. Christmases would come and go and we wouldn't even talk. I'd sit there with my phone on Christmas day, debating the pros and cons of calling my little sister and wishing her a merry Christmas. Normal people don't do this. They just make the call, say some quick, half-sincere bullshit and then everyone feels they did their familial duty. I think it's truly sick, the way most people expound a lifetime of niceties to their family, their friends, without ever in their whole, limited existence as a human being, really getting deep with someone, getting into a real relationship, you know—an *intimate* relationship. It's a fucked-up choice but I think I'll take me and Jamie's way: each of us in our own houses, each of us sitting with our phone in our hand, and each of us not calling the other one on Christmas. At least our way is honest.

You've heard me call my sister a demon—a devil—and she really was. I guess because of her bipolar she could be nice to you for as long as one of her cycles lasted and then—with absolutely no Earthly warning—she would turn into the devil. By which I mean that she would reach behind her head (where the zipper starts) and she would slowly unzip her Jamie suit all the way over her head, down her neck, and she would unzip everything about her that was the super-nice, super-socialized, super-friendly and loving Jamie..and out would come the stinking, writhing, maggoty devil—just the way they taught him in Sunday school. That's why I call Jamie the devil—'cause that's half of what she was to me.

- - - -

Just consider the way she treated Phillip:

During the time I was at Family Dollar, Phillip would say he was going to burn down the school or blow up the school and he would get in trouble. Jamie would pick him up for me because I was a store manager by then so I couldn't leave on a whim. Somebody had to pick him up from school—as he was suspended—so Jamie went.

Next thing I know my sister's calling me from home while she was watching Phillip. She was bawling, saying, "I can't do this anymore!"

"What can't you do?"

"Watch him! I don't know how you raised him or what kinds of things *you exposed him to*—but I can't do this anymore."

"What exactly do you think I *exposed* him to?"

"Oh, Davina, cut the crap. I know you had a *special* relationship with Jessie—"

"What the fuck does Jessie have to do with anything?"

"Well, I think he made you a more sexual person and you've obviously passed that on to Phillip."

"What exactly did Phillip do?"

"You wanna know?"

"Yes."

"I can hardly repeat it."

"It's that bad..that you're having trouble telling your own sister..over the phone.."

"Yes."

"Well what was it?"

I leaned back in one of the manager's chairs, overlooking the Family Dollar. The phone had an option to broadcast this call over the store's PA system but I decided not to use it.

"Well," Jamie says. "I caught him in the living room saying sexual things to the other little kids."

"What was he saying?"

"I *don't know exactly*. I wasn't going to stick around for the details but when I hear 'cock' and 'ass' in the same sentence I pretty much know where that's going."

"So what if he said 'cock' and 'ass' in the same sentence? Kids are sexual. They pick up on that stuff from adults and they play with it..Jamie?..they try it on for size. You still there?"

"I'm still here, Davina, listening to my older sister tell me I should allow her son to molest my girls and kids from the neighborhood."

"Wait wait wait. He *molested* them?"

"How am I supposed to know? They all had their pants down and Phillip was pretending to be their *psychiatrist*."

I laughed. Oh boy did I laugh.

"Jamie, just tell them to play a different game—one where their pants stay on. Kids do that kind of thing."

"Maybe *your* kids do."

"Look, I'll be there in less than two hours. Thank you for taking Phillip today. I know for you he's a handful."

"He's a handful for anyone."

"Alright, look, I'll see you in a while."

"There's more," Jamie said.

I waited.

Jamie used her *very serious adult voice*.

"I called she school."

"And?"

"I said that Phillip tried to molest my daughters."

"You said that?"

"Yeah."

"To who?"

"I don't know. I think the principal."

"Why did you say that?"

"I don't know! I also said he was a sexual predator."

"Jamie, he's *eight years old*!"

"I know but I watched this show on Investigation Discovery called *Rapist Kids* and they had a kid on there who raped his own grandmother. That kid was only six so I figured Phillip might be a rapist, too..and I figured it could mess up our family even more if Phillip raped one of my girls."

"Our family's so fucked up I think that would hardly make a dent."

Jamie laughed.

She said, "I'm sorry."

"Why oh why did you call the school?"

"I know I know I'm so sorry."

We talked for a lot longer than that but the basic thrust of things was that Jamie always thought that Phillip was a bad kid and she didn't want him to hang out with her kids, especially her daughters..and since Jamie also babysat kids from the neighborhood, she thought it was best that Phillip didn't come to her house at all.

I was thinking: *What if you invite me over, do I have to find someone to watch Phillip during that time?* I mean these are the type of conundrums you find yourself dealing with when your sister is a psychotic devil-controlled bipolar bitch.

I was so nice to her, though—we talked on the phone right up to when I was going to clock out. I don't consider myself a nice person—I'd as soon cut you as say hi to you if you said hi to me on the street—but what can I say? There's a little bit of magic in the world that makes an older sister willing to go to the ends of the Earth if doing so would help or save one of the ones who came after her from her mother's womb.

- - - -

But I oscillate, you know? One minute we might have things patched up between me and my sister or me and my mom but the problem is the two of them are so fucking crazy—even crazier than me. I mean with me you have a crazy person who is growing, who is changing her ways. Year after year I get better—because I work through my shit. The problems I have now won't be the problems I have twenty years from now and they're not the same problems I had twenty years ago. I at least have the *humility* to admit my wrongs. The two of them go out of their way to make things hard for me and that's why I don't talk to them anymore. My mom came over once when I was in bed with my back pain. She brought over hot/cool back pads which were useful—I thanked her for that. Then she asks:

"Can I do some laundry while I'm here?"

She wasn't asking if she could help around the house by doing some of *my* laundry—she had brought her *own* laundry and was asking if she could use my washer/dryer to do *her* laundry. The ice packs, the hot and cool medicine—all that was a ruse so she could use my washer/dryer. It would have been one-hundred percent better if she had just dropped the pretense, told me she didn't feel like walking to the laundromat, and asked if she could use my machines.

But she's my mother—and how can I say no to my mother?

"Yeah, mom, do whatever you want!" I said.

She was halfway down the stairs so I had to yell. She didn't care what I said—I was stuck in my bed, I couldn't get out without someone's help. I heard every little thing she did: I heard when she turned the knobs on the washer. I heard the washer start. I heard her going through my DVDs—this activity wouldn't have been loud enough to identify if she hadn't dropped some of the DVDs on the floor and talked aloud to herself while she tried to put them back on the shelf in the order I had them. The woman was sincerely just walking the fuck around the first floor of my house picking things up and dropping them..talking to herself all the way. And the way she talked to herself..it was like if a kindergarten teacher had dementia and still thought she was teaching a bunch of five year olds:

"Now let's see..Davina has a chicken roaster..or a roaster of some kind..Davina if there's one thing I was forced to say about you—gun to my head—it's that you have basically no domestic skills..like why would I want to come to your house for thanksgiving?..I'd rather go to Old Country Buffet..I mean your daughter is 30 years old and can't make a decent gravy..I'm sorry to you, Davina, I must have skipped that when you were growing up..thinks the whole world revolves around her just because she has a little back pain..I know pain..and *that's* not pain..*that's* a woman who's learned laziness..what from her many fathers..and I admit, girl, that some were better than others..not my fault, not my fault..you make your bed and lie in it..and you know what, D?..sometimes that bed's gonna have lice, sometimes it's gonna have bedbugs and scabies and yes, maybe you're going to have to go to school with lice comin' out your head and marks of scabies running up and down your arms..people like *us* weren't *meant* to live the luxury life..YOU *KNOW* THE GAME IS FIXED, DON'T YOU!!??..we *ain't* the motherfuckin' Waltons..and we ain't never gonna *be* the Waltons."

All the while she's opening every pantry door and commenting on the fact that I don't have enough *spoons* for fuck's sake.

"Mom, I don't *need* any more spoons. I have enough for the people that live here."

"But what if you have company??" she shouts.

"Not having a lot of company over right now, Mom."

"We used to have company over all the time."

"Who? Drug dealers? Louie, to take my virginity?"

"*Did* he take your virginity?"

"*Yes*, Mom—you know he did."

"Well..did you *enjoy* it?"

I hear her moving dishes around in the sink.

"Mom! *What* are you doing?"

"I'm just doing some dishes—this place is disgusting."

"It prob'ly looks better than where you're living."

"No, Davina, my place does not have gnawed chicken bones on the kitchen floor."

"Leave it alone! I *SWEAR* TO FUCKING GOD!! If you don't leave it the fuck alone I'm gonna call the cops!"

"And they're going to kick me out when I came over to help my decrepit baby with her housework?"

"You came over to do your laundry. Own it."

"I'm sorry baby but I gotta draw the line at chewed-upon chicken legs on the kitchen floor."

"If you touch that chicken bone I'll come down there and kill you!"

"That's a death threat. I'm calling the police" she sang.

"Mom."

"What?"

"If you don't *shut up* I'M GOING TO KILL MYSELF!!"

"Go ahead. People are always threatening to kill themselves..but they never do. Sounds like my baby needs some hospital time."

Then a long silence. Then I said:

"What are you doing?"

"Dialing 911. For you. Yes, my daughter is suicidal. Can you come pick her up?"

"Hang up the phone, Mom."

"I don't know. Davina, do you have a specific *plan* of how you're going to kill yourself? Or is it more of a suicidal *ideation*. This operator wants to know. Has she made previous attempts? Yes, two."

"Mom. Hang up the fucking phone."

After a long while I hear the handset placed into its cradle.

"You know what the problem with your suicides is, Davina?"

"What."

"You never complete. See, it's bad enough to have to say that I have a daughter who attempted suicide..twice. It would just be easier—on me—if I could say: I had a daughter who killed herself and then *wham!*—that's the end of the conversation. But no, you stick around and the rest of us never know if you're enjoying our company or if you'd literally *rather be dead*. You see the problem there?"

I don't answer. I'm shaking. I'm shaking uncontrollably.

"Mom?" I say.

"What is it, Davina?"

"I think you're going to have to find a new place to do laundry."

I was so proud of myself for saying it. I didn't yell—I just asked for what I needed. This was a growing point for me. My mom eventually responded:

"I will, dear—right after this load."

When she was done drying, she came upstairs.

I presented to be asleep—covers *all* the way over my head.

She left something on the bed, near my feet.

When she had gotten her laundry and was all the way out of the house, I looked to the end of the bed. It was the hot/cool pain patches she had brought over—the only really selfless thing she did for me that day.

- - - -

Chris called the next morning.

I answered the phone:

"Hello?"

"Davina. *What* is going on in that house?"

"What do you mean?"

"I mean..your *mom* called me and said the kids are in danger and she's going to call child services! Are you there? Davina? Are the kids in danger?"

"No! They're not in danger at all!"

"Lisa says there's no laundry soap?"

"There *was* laundry soap but Lisa came over and used it all!"

"Why is she doing laundry at your house?"

"I don't know! She's spying on me!"

"Don't you think that might be a slight exaggeration?"

"No, actually, I don't. While she was here she looked at every DVD in the movie cabinet—I mean she was *taking DVDs off the shelf* to look at them. I don't know if she was checking the ratings to see what was appropriate to kids or what but then while her laundry is running she goes through the *kitchen*."

"What was she looking for in the kitchen?"

"I don't know—she's prob'ly looking for a reason to call CPS on my ass!"

"Ok well calm down. Nobody's gonna call Child Protective Services."

"She might. She called 911 while she was over here!"

"About what?"

"She thought I was a homicidal suicidal threat!"

"Are you?"

"No, baby. No. The only thing wrong with me right now is my back."

"I'm sorry your back's still giving you problems."

"Thank you. Chris I'm telling you sometimes I just lie in my bed and cry, it hurts so bad."

"I'm so sorry."

"Thanks."

"Look, Lisa said I should come pick up the kids."

"It's your choice, but there's no reason for you to do that. The house might be a little messy because I'm stuck in bed all the time. But everyone's getting fed, everyone has clean clothes, the electric is still on..everything's fine. Do you believe me?"

"Of *course* I believe you. I know your mom lies. I think this is just her way of telling me you need a break and to come over there and take care of the kids. Which I will do if necessary."

"No, Chris, it's totally not necessary. We're doing fine."

"Ok, baby. You call me if you need me."

"I will, Chris. I will."

Chris and I may have fizzled, chemistry-wise, and we may hate each other's families significantly more than is usual, but Chris and I got each other's back when it comes to the children. My mom can lie all she wants to try to manipulate the situation, but she's last on a long list of things that I would ever let destroy my family.

- - - -

And my sister. My sister. I always tried to have a relationship with my sister—I always thought that I could be helpful towards my sister. But I didn't approve of how she raised her kids—that's strike number one. And I'm not talking about normal disciplining—Jamie was mentally and physically abusive to them. I would talk to my mom and brother about it sometimes..but what were they gonna do?

Jamie lies about everything. One time she told us all she had cervical cancer and didn't have long to live. Everyone was distraught. Only my mom knew Jamie well enough not to believe her. Mom told me not to get too worried about it. When I asked Jamie if she really had cervical cancer, she blew up at me, just about yelled me out of her house, then threatened to show me the doctor's records and X-rays. I told her I believed her just to shut her up.

Another time Jamie said she was diagnosed with bipolar.

"Then get medicine—they know how to treat bipolar now."

"But I think I can make it a little longer without meds."

"That doesn't make any sense, Jamie. If medicine will make you feel better—"

"It's not that I feel all that bad."

"Ok."

"But I just feel..the *slightest* mood swings..and I'm worried about my girls."

"Do you want me to take them for a while?"

"I'm not sure that would be such a good idea."

"I'm trying to help you and you're insulting *me*?"

"How am I insulting you?"

"You implied that I wouldn't be able to take care of your girls."

"It's not you," she says. "It's *Phillip*."

"Fuck you Jamie."

"Well fuck you, too. I tell you I have a life-threatening *illness* and you can't even help your own sister."

"I'm *trying* to help!"

"Yeah by trying to get me to take medicine."

"What did your doctor say to do?"

"To take medicine."

"Well there it is! It's a known illness and if you have it, you take medicine to cope."

But that was never good enough for Jamie. She didn't want to solve the problem—she didn't want to get better. She wanted the attention she'd get for being sick. She thrived on tormenting the rest of us with the mystery of whether or not she was dangerously ill.

She always wanted to be the center of attention.

When she didn't have a terminal illness to hang over our heads, she started cutting.

When we stopped being worried about her cutting, Jamie upped the ante—she made suicide attempts.

She made sure that she was always the center of attention with all this bullshit. She loved it. She really did. And she did the same thing in her marriage—she constantly fucked other guys (and girls) and she and her husband were always fighting over this. I can't imagine being married to her, but even as her sister, Jamie had always been a very difficult person to try to have a relationship with.

- - - -

I remember once when Jamie and I weren't talking because of Jamie telling Chris that I was a bad mom, Jamie came by the house with her girls in a side-by-side stroller—they were babies at the time. As we made small talk some emotion like a splinter was growing inside me and I finally figured out what it was. It was that Jamie hadn't just been out for a walk with her kids..she hadn't just *happened* upon my street and pushed her stroller down it..she didn't just happen to come by my house. No. She came here on purpose to show me the new double-wide stroller she had purchased. She came here to show me how *well behaved* her children were, even as babies. And what she was doing worked! Without saying a thing, my little sister Jamie—who used to adore me *as a god*—now that she was an adult, was making me feel bad for being a subpar mother, for my kids being fucked up psychologically, for me not having the money to buy a top-of-the-line stroller..I always just had the *regular* stroller that fit *one* kid—what was wrong with that? So what if only one baby fit in the stroller and the other ones had to walk along beside?

This is the kind of bitch my sister was. And I don't care if you have cervical cancer, bipolar, or just a factitious disorder..that shit is not cool. Insult me on some other subject, please—but please don't call me a bad mother.

After Jamie and I talked a while on the sidewalk, when the conversation could have been over, I started it up again by asking her not to bring her kids to my house in the double-wide stroller anymore.

"Why not?" she said.

"Because it hurts my heart..when my own sister comes by to parade her kids around in front of me and remind me that *my* life is somehow less than yours because *you* make more money than me and that fact alone gives you this feeling of superiority that is *sweating* through your pores. I mean, be honest—isn't that why you're here?"

To my genuine surprise, Jamie started crying. She wiped her face with her sleeve and looked at me from the gaps between her fingers.

"Yes!! You're right! I brought the girls because I knew it would be hard for you. I'm so, so sorry."

"Don't be sorry for *this*," I said.

And Jamie was choked up, bent over at the middle, clutching her sides with cramps because she cried so hard. She managed to lift her face to look at me.

"What *should* I be sorry for?"

"For telling Chris that I'm a bad mom. For *calling the school* and telling them that Phillip is a child molester!! Do you know how much trouble that caused me..and is still causing Phillip? You *ruined* his life at school."

"I'm sorry."

"Now I've got Child and Family Services calling my house. I've got social workers calling my house. I've got the principal of the motherfuckin' *school* calling my house, asking for god knows what!!—some kind of *permission slip from a psychiatrist!?* that says Phillip is safe to go back to school. You *know* Phillip. You *know* he's not a child molester."

"I'm so sorry, Davina. Forgive me."

"Cleaning up after your messes is a full-time job! I don't have time for this shit! Do you *want* things to go bad for me?"

"No!"

Jamie comes toward me like we're gonna hug.

"Stop right there," I say.

"I just want to hug you, Davina."

"Are you really bipolar?"

Jamie shrugs. She shakes her head. Tears are running all down her face.

"I don't know!" she wails.

"Well keep seeing that doctor."

Jamie nods huge nods over and over. When she figures out that we're not going to be hugging, she turns the stroller around and goes back up the hill from whence she came.

Somewhere up the street she just starts cussing at no one—maybe herself? It's a wild string of language that goes something like:

"—motherfuckin' stupid motherfucker Jamie is a stupid, stupid, *stupid* motherfucker I'd never try to hurt Davina she's a momma too we gots to *stick* together like in the *ghetto* where your life is a flash in the *pan*, motherfucker, motherfuckers get *shot* but I'm rollin' with Fifty and we *always* got our backs rollin' up in the club NO! we never step *foot* in the club with a half-steppin' nigga that's how you *learn* to be the original gangsta of Hallstead y'all better step aside because I just ruined my sister's *life* that's what I *do* without even trying to honeysuckle a blind rat that's what it's *like* when you a *mom* for the first time, *see*..niggas don't even *know*—"

That's the gist of what she said. I can't recall it word for word because it came from the mouth of a crazy person. But that's pretty much what my little sister was shouting as she went up the street.

## 78
Well things come and things go and for a while me and my sister went our separate ways. I didn't want her advice on how to raise kids and she must have had her reasons not to call me either. But one night I went to the bar with Calum and before we even got a drink Jamie and her husband walk in the door.

"Well look who that is!"

"Hey, sister!"

We introduced the boys.

That first night I saw Jamie at Harmony Inn we all sat in a row at the bar and I mostly talked with Calum and Jamie mostly chatted with her husband. But James and I were sitting side by side and at one point we turned to face each other.

"So how's Phillip?" Jamie asked.

"He's fine."

I didn't really want to get into it, especially with her.

"Come on, don't be mad."

"You just really put me in a bad position is all."

"You mean with the school?"

"Yes! With the school! They think he's a child molester now! He has special handprints they put on his desk with tape to teach him how to keep his hands to himself!! Can you imagine what that's doing to his self-worth? All the other kids don't have *handprints* on their desk! And when he goes outside, he's not allowed to touch girls—only boys—I haven't figured out exactly how to tell them but I think Phillip's gay."

"Really?"

"Yeah."

"Well that's kind of cool."

"Yeah, I guess it is."

I smile and sip my beer.

"So how are your kids?"

I had to ask, but I also wish I hadn't asked, because Jamie takes this as an invitation not to tell me a little about her kids and let the conversation return to its normal flow. No, to Jamie this is me permanently passing the conch shell in her direction, and she's gonna talk for so long and in such detail about her kids that by the time she's done the conch won't even be a shell anymore—it'll be a thousand individual fragments too small to even make jewelry from. She talks about the girls most of all. Even though they can hardly walk and neither of them has spoken their first word, Jamie has them enrolled in the mentally gifted program at school.

"But..wait..what are they..like..in an accelerated pre-school?"

"They call it Premie-K," Jamie says.

She is so fuckin' proud of herself that I want to clock her with my bar mug. *Premie-K?!* You've got to be kidding.

"So what types of things do they do at *Premie-K*?" I ask.

Jamie says, "Are you making fun of me?"

"No, I'm sorry. Yes, I was. Sorry, Jamie. What do they do at Premie-K? Fuckin'..Mensa puzzles or something?"

"Fuck you," Jamie says, and she turns back to her husband.

I hear Jamie complaining that I'm being a bitch and I'm impossible to deal with and everything's a joke to me and soon after that they leave. Me and Calum hang out the rest of the night and everything's cool.

- - - -

But, in a few days' time, Jamie calls the house. I'm surprised when she invites me to the Harmony Inn, just the two of us.

As soon as we order drinks Jamie tells me there's something I should know. But she won't tell me.

"You're pregnant?" I suggest.

"No."

"You're becoming a vegetarian?"

"No."

"Vegan?"

"No."

"Raw foodist?"

"No. It's nothing to do with food."

"Just tell me, James. Is it bad?"

"You..might think it's bad, yes."

I grab her arm.

"Well fucking tell me. Jamie, if it's something that affects me, *you have to tell me*. So what is it?"

I squeeze her arm.

"I gotta go," she says.

"What the fuck?"

"I gotta check on the kids."

"No you fucking *don't*."

I do an indian rug burn on her arm.

"Ow—fuck!"

She pays for her beer and heads for the door.

"Bitch," I say.

"Cunt."

"Whore."

- - - -

A few days later, she calls me at the house.

"Meet me at Harmony?" she says.

"Jamie, I'm not Buddha, ok?—I don't have all the patience in the motherfucking world."

"Am I testing your patience?"

"No. *Before*, you were testing my patience. *Now*, you're on my last motherfucking nerve. Why don't you tell me over the phone?"

"I think this has to be in person."

"Ok, I'm trusting you but if you don't tell me this time I'mma kick your ass."

"That's fair."

"Harmony in ten?" I say.

And she says, "Harmony in ten."

Jamie and I get a table this time. We both drink beer.

"So what it this big life-changing secret you have to tell me?"

"Ok, this is..something that's kind of scary..for you it might be scary..but..it's something that I want to tell you before you found out any other way."

I took a solemn vow that I wouldn't say another word until Jamie told me whatever it was she had to say.

Jamie took another sip of beer, swallowed it, and finally said:

"Jessie moved to town."

I waited for her to say more. She didn't say shit.

"*Jessie?*"

"Yes."

"Moved to *what* town?"

"This town. Hallstead. But don't worry, he lives with me and he almost never goes out—he just sits on the porch."

In the history of internal flip-outs, mine that I had in Harmony Inn right at that moment when Jamie told me Jessie was in Hallstead—*in her house* no less—that had to make the top ten internal flip-outs of all time. My whole weak scaffolding of psychological ok-ness shook in that moment and I didn't know if I could stand being broken again. If fact I knew I couldn't. Just the *thought* of Jessie tore me down an inch that could take *weeks* to repair.

Now nothing was safe. What if Jessie wanted a drink? He could come right in here, any minute, and I would have to face him. I could run into him on the street, in the grocery store..my whole world, my whole Jessie-less life I had been building here for *years*..was flatly ruined.

- - - -

I was watching Jamie speak, but there was no sound. I couldn't make it real that my stepdad, Jessie—the man who had ruined my entire life—was here, three-thousand miles away, in this tiny shit town in Pennsylvania.

"*How?*—How is he here?"

"I invited him."

I must have been shaking my head at that psychotic bitch.

"I invited him to live here..look, Davina..he was having a hard time in California and I know he's just your stepdad but that's *my* dad, you know, he's my dad and I want to know him before he dies and I want my *kids* to know him."

I said, "You do realize he has a disease that causes him to touch little children, right?"

Jamie looked away at some safe spot on the floor.

"You have so many victims under your roof—"

"Davina, I have to ask you a question."

"What."

"What really happened between you and Jessie?"

"I've told you before."

"I know," Jamie says. "But we both know mental illness runs in the family."

"You think I'm mentally ill?"

"I think we both are. I just want to hear, without the lens of bipolar or depression or PTSD or whatever you have—"

"*You're* the one with bipolar."

"Maybe I am," Jamie says. "Maybe I am. But could you look honestly at yourself and tell me just the facts."

"As opposed to how I feel about the facts?"

"Right."

"Well, ok. From the time I was eight to the time I was thirteen, your father molested and tormented me, sexually. That's five years of my growing-up life where I was being touched by an adult who controlled my life, before I should have ever been experiencing sexual things—you know? I should have been climbing trees and chasing grasshoppers but instead I was lying in bed naked with Jessie cumming on my face, cumming on my belly, cumming all over my pussy—"

"Did he ever rape you?"

"Like did he ever actually stick his penis into my vagina?—"

"Or your ass. Or your mouth."

"He never stuck it in my vagina. He never stuck it in my ass. He did make me suck his dick *many times* so I guess that's oral rape but no, he never stuck it in my vagina, so no, he never officially "raped" me. But it felt like what *rape*, as a concept, was designed to be: someone taking over your space, someone forcing you to do things with your body that you didn't want to do..that's rape, right?"

Jamie was silent, looking at me doubtful.

"Didn't he ever do anything like that to you?"

"No," she says.

"Do you know why? *Think*, Jamie—it's obvious."

"Maybe it's 'cause you're prettier than me, Davina."

"Maybe you just don't remember."

"What's that supposed to mean?"

"It means, Jamie, that he knew you from the time you were born. He could have molested you when you were too young to remember."

"'Vina, just 'cause you *claim* to be sexually molested by Jessie doesn't mean he molested me, it doesn't mean he molested you, it doesn't mean anything. Prob'ly all it means is you smoked too much meth and your brain doesn't work anymore. You know sometimes bipolar people make up things in their fucked-up brains that have *no* basis in truth—they're just delusions—your brain's little fairy tales."

"You think I made it all up?"

"Yeah. I do."

"Go ask him, then. Tell him not to lie. Ask him what he remembers about *his* side of the story."

"Ok I will."

"Or you could just *believe* what *I'm telling you!!* You don't believe me, do you?"

"I'm sorry, no. I think you wanted attention as a child and since then you've done too many drugs and the truth is mixed up inside your brain."

"I never did that many drugs."

"Well it sounds like it."

"I didn't. I did *some* drugs—here and there—*nothing* like what Mom did."

"Do you think Mom did drugs when she was pregnant with us?"

"Uh, yeah, yes—I'm sure she did."

"Do you think she did meth?"

"Probably, Jamie, probably. But don't go getting mad at Lisa for shooting meth while we were still inside her. I'm sure that's affected us, but if you're talking about mental illness—bipolar, depression, schizophrenia—it's genetic. Nobody has any control over it, except by choosing not to have kids with someone else who has it. But don't worry, mental illness isn't caused by drugs."

"How do you know all this stuff?"

"I do research."

"Well, Davina, I'm gonna talk to Dad."

"Please do. I'd love to hear his infinite denials."

"I'm sorry, I just want to hear his side of the story."

"Go ahead."

"You know Dad's an alcoholic, right?"

"Almost everyone I know is an alcoholic."

"Well Dad's the real kind—he's gonna die soon."

"Of what?"

"Cirrhosis of the liver."

"No shit."

"Yeah, it sucks."

I thought of Jessie lying in a hospital bed taking his last breath. I had pity for him even though he fucked up my life.

- - - -

Jamie said, "You should come over sometime—I'll make sure Jessie's not there."

I stare at my beer.

"Do you know *how much stuff* you're bringing up inside me just by you talking about him? That motherfucker *came on my ass cheeks* when I was eight years old."

The bartender looks over at me.

"Want another one?"

"No, we're just about done here."

"So anyway since he's dying," my sister said, "I see us on one side and him on the other. Especially if he molested you."

"Oh he did. The only question is if he'll ever admit it to *you*."

"Well..still..you're invited over any time."

I turned to my sister.

"Normally I'd do the polite thing and say, '*Sure!* I'll be over sometime,' catch up with you and your husband and your perfect kids—and now that we've added Jessie to the mix it's a regular family reunion. Then Jessie drags your twins and maybe even Phillip into a back room they start out under the blankets playing a tickle game and the next thing you know your dad is raping his own grandchildren—gotta get in a few more victims before his liver kills him!"

Jamie gets up and walks out of the bar.

"You gotta pay for that!" says the bartender.

"I got it," I say.

"Thanks," the bartender says. "If the drawer's short I have to pay for it out of my tips."

"Heh. Well I'll pay for her, even if she is a Satan-worshipping death fuck."

"Sound like she's your sister, too?"

"Yeah, she's both."

"I got a sister like that, too—well, in my case a brother."

"Is he single?"

"Yeah!"

"Why don't we get these two together?"

"Dope! Is your sister single?"

"No, she's married—but she likes to cheat."

The bartender laughs.

"What's next, more of the same?"

"No I'm switching it up."

"Do you want to hear what we have on tap?"

"I'm switching to shots."

"Ok! Madame! Excellent choice!"

- - - -

I'm not going to tell you which shots I drank or how many—none of that matters.
