What matters is that over the next few days Jamie would call me and I would ask her if she asked Jessie the deadly question and she would say no, she wasn't ready yet and I would say well call me after you and him have had the talk. Then Jamie would use all this language that insinuated that she was on the same side as me—that it was me and her on one side and Jessie on the other. Jamie always reminded me that her dad would be dying soon—I thought she was offering that as an olive branch, like: *I'm sorry my dad fucked up your life but here's your consolation prize: a set of steak knives and a dead stepfather!*

I wanted to be like, "There *is* no consolation, bitch—leave me alone!"

Jamie called me at least three separate times on three separate occasions to invite me to dinner. She never mentioned if Jessie would be there but I assumed he would. I told her no. I didn't want to be a part of anything that was happening at my sister's house.

- - - -

As I drank my shots, I wondered: had Jessie been living at Jamie's house the day she accused Phillip of being a child molester? Did Jessie *show* Phillip what it meant to be one? Did Phillip *practice* on one of Jamie's little girls? These are the horrible things you fear as a mother.

"'Nother shot, madame?"

"You know it."

## 79

After that, I started seeing Jessie everywhere. It was like when you come across an unusual word in a book you're reading, then all of a sudden everyone you talk to is using that word. Jessie became that word for me. Every time I went into town I'd see him. I took alternate routes through the neighborhood streets just so I wouldn't catch him coming or going from Jamie's house. When I went to Harmony Inn, I went in through the back, right through the kitchen (I knew people who worked there) and I scoped out the whole place through the food delivery window—I was making *damn* sure that Jessie wasn't there before I went inside.

I identified his motorcycle by process of elimination. Which motorcycle did I see everywhere I saw Jessie? This made it easier to avoid him: see the cycle, walk the other way. But I couldn't always walk the other way—sometimes I was in town to get groceries, or I had Phillip with me. In the grocery store we were like *SEAL Team Six*. I told Phillip that Jessie was Osama bin Laden and we had to make sure he didn't see us or he would lock us in his basement and do horrible things to us.

"Like what kinds of horrible things?"

"They're *so* horrible, Phillip, that I'm afraid to tell you."

Phillip started to cry.

I knelt to hug him.

"I'm sorry, baby. Forget it. He's not Osama bin Laden, ok?"

"But who is he?"

"He's my stepdad. And he's really not that nice of a guy. Just stay close to me? Don't go running off?"

Phillip sat on the front of our cart as we went down the aisles. I guess that was his way of staying close to me.

I stayed one aisle behind Jessie—following him as he shopped. When I was sure he was in the next aisle I did my shopping on that aisle he had just left, praying he wouldn't forget something and come back. I won't say I was completely *comfortable* shopping this way, but we needed the food. In fact, with every time I had to wait at the end of an aisle for Jessie to finish up so I could go, I felt my madness boiling. I've felt crazy before—I mean, Jesus, if you've ever tried to kill yourself, you should be able to self-apply that label *if you want*—but even when I wanted to *die*, I did not feel the madness I felt peeking around the baking aisle watching my tormentor casually picking out spices and flour and candles and sprinkles. He was so at ease—like he had a clear conscience about me and he could just go *stay with my sister!!* No problem!! Everyone else in the family thought he was *such a nice guy!!* Yeah, if you're not an eight-year-old girl who had to lean over her bed to be spanked by Jessie and your own mother watching, her pinkie finger curled around her chin with that one long nail parked between her lips, smirking at the fact that my ass cheeks and labia were being whipped with a belt for no reason except that Jesse enjoyed doing it..but if you didn't know about all that, then yeah, sure, Jessie was a perfectly nice guy.

I'm peeking around the corner of the baking aisle.

I'm blushing. I'm hot. I remember Jesse whipping me.

I wasn't wearing any panties.

And I know Jessie's cock got rock hard when he saw my pussy lips get redder and redder as he spanked me with that leather belt.

This is what I'm thinking in the grocery store while Jessie takes his time over what sprinkles to put on his grandbabies' cupcakes. Not *my* grandbabies—ya catch me?—it's the fact that they're all over at Jamie's having innocent little fun (on my back) and I'm stuck over here with no support, no mother who loves me, no father who is around. I don't mean to bitch but it really is harder when you're raising a family on your own. It's not the extra work—it's the being alone.

In the check out I looked through the giant windows on the front of Rob's Country Market. Jessie, sitting on his bike, idling. My heart dropped into my stomach. He was waiting there for me. Waiting for me to bring Phillip and my groceries out to the car, and then he would follow me home.

*Jamie already told him your address.*

*He already knows where you live.*

*And late at night, he parks his bike a block away..then he creeps down the street in biker leathers, that switchblade always at his side.*

*He would kill the boys and rape me, for old times' sake.*

*I'd beg him to kill me. I wouldn't care if he raped me as long as he killed me afterward. I always thought that was something meaningful he and I could do together.*

- - - -

"Miss?"

I looked around. In Rob's Country Market, paying for groceries. Jessie still on his bike, waiting for something.

"Would you like paper or plastic?"

She says it with a smile, she's in her sixties, and I see absolutely no reason that asking that question should make her smile.

"Paper or plastic?"

"Oh, fuck it," I say. "Let's kill some nature."

"Excuse me?"

"Plastic, plastic—they can't be recycled, they kill the environment, little baby ducks get stuck in them and suffocate."

The lady has a blank look on her face.

"Little baby ducklings get stuck in them and die?" she asks.

"Yes," I say, "they do. Most major cities in the world have banned plastic bags for this reason..don't you watch the Discovery Channel?"

"Ronald won't let me get cable."

"Why not?"

The woman leans in close to me. Her name tag says, "Ida."

Ida confides in me.

"It's those late-night infomercials," she says.

"Your husband doesn't want you watching infomercials?"

"No, no," she wags her finger. "It's the *intimate* ones."

"*Oh!*" I say.

"Now you get it now," Ida says.

I look out to the parking lot—Jessie's still there.

"Ida. Have you ever heard that Prince song that's like: *Boy you better try to have fun no matter what you do?*"

"I haven't heard that."

"Do you have internet?"

"Ronald has it on his desk."

"Give me a piece of receipt paper."

Ida rolls off some paper.

"And a pen..please."

I write down a couple of website names, fold up the receipt paper, and give her that and the pen.

"Tonight, when Ronald goes to sleep, you go to his desk and get on his computer and type in those addresses."

"But won't I get hacked?"

"No. You won't get hacked. No one cares about what's on your computer except the CIA."

"Won't the CIA be hacking me then?"

"Look: just forget about the CIA. They're not gonna care about what you're looking at."

"And what exactly am I going to be looking at?"

"Unfold that paper," I tell her.

She does. She unfolds it, she reads it, her face turns bright red.

"Promise me you'll go there."

She smiles.

"I promise," she says—and she almost lets out a giggle.

"You might want to lay out a towel. I would hate for you to mess up Ronald's chair."

Ida and I giggled together until I looked out the window and Jessie's bike was gone. I mean it's Rob's Country Market in Hallstead—it's not like the place is teeming with customers. Me and Phillip must have stood there talking with her for twenty minutes.

- - - -

On the drive home I was constantly checking the rearview mirror to see if Jessie was following me. I ran a stop sign on our street and didn't know it till Phillip pointed it out to me. He thought all of this was hilariously funny.

"Phil. Listen to me. That man we were hiding from in the grocery store..remember what he looks like?"

"Yeah."

"Well if I'm not around and you see him, you yell as loud as you can. You yell, 'Child molester! Child molester!' You find a safe adult and you stay next to them. Don't let him take you for a ride on his motorcycle, understand?"

"Why not?"

We're parked at home. I turn around and look Phillip right in the eye.

"He's a dangerous man. He hurt me very badly when I was a child. I don't want him around you—he can't be trusted."

"He hurt you when you were a baby?"

"When I was a girl, yes."

"Well that's not fair. When we get inside I'm going to get He-Man and Skeletor to go back in time to protect you."

"Thank you. That would be very nice."

I get Phillip inside, then bring in the groceries. Every time I go outside I check everywhere Jessie could be. He could be in the car. He could be under the car. Behind my neighbor's fence. Behind any number of trees. I even thought of him posting up on his bike a block away, spying on me with binoculars. Ok, yeah, maybe that's paranoid, but after all I'd been through with Jessie—anything was possible.

As I was putting away the food, Phillip came running into the kitchen, flying He-Man and Skeletor all over me with his hands. I was one inch away from yelling at my sweet kid's playing when Phillip said:

"I sent them back in time, Mom, I sent them back in time—now Jessie can never hurt you anymore, because for every minute he goes back, I'll go back two. I love you."

Phillip hugged my legs. I kneeled down and hugged that boy so tight. Hugged him on his head. Hugged him on his back. There may be a million, there may be a thousand, or there might only be a hundred moments that tax you as a parent..but for every streak of hard parenting moments, there is that one..there is that one moment when your Asperger/ODD/ADHD child blesses your past self with time-traveling action figures. He says for every minute Jessie goes back to hurt me, these comic book heroes are going back *two*..just to save my ass when I needed saving.

I grab Phillip by his lovely head and ask him what he wants for dinner.

"Macaroni and cheese with beef, jalapeños and pepper jack cheese."

"You got it! Now go play and I'll call you when it's ready. Don't go outside!"

When Zach got home from school I told him the new rules: come right home from school, stay inside, don't accept motorcycle rides from strangers, and scream 'CHILD MOLESTER!!' if anyone comes near you."

I admit, I was on pins and needles. I was ready to crack. I had no reason to think that Jessie would touch my boys—or any boys. But what I said to myself is this: Why take a chance?

- - - -

When Jamie and me got together, she would always bring these little messages from Jessie, along the lines of:

"Jessie told me to tell you he's sorry." "Jessie says if he could take it all back, he would." "Jessie really wants you to accept his apology—he says it's killing him not to be able to see you in person and he thinks if you two meet you'll see how much he's changed."

I would look at Jamie wherever we were when she delivered one of Jessie's apologies. On the outside I was quiet. But on the inside I was screaming: "YOU RUINED MY LIFE!!" Jessie, damn, you foolish man, you ruined my life.

Jamie was over my house one day and she had us both sit down in the living room. I sat in my favorite reclining chair and she sat on the couch.

"So what's going on?" I said.

"Well, I'm going to tell you. I decided to talk to Jessie."

"What did you have to talk about with that asshole?"

"I asked him. I asked him straight out if it was true that he had a sexual relationship with you when you were younger."

"I bet that motherfucker lied."

"I don't think so," says my sister. And then she begins to tell me things that only me or Jessie would know. Things that happened in bathrooms. Things that happened in the dark. Things that happened to me under the covers, in my bed, when I was supposed to be sleeping.

I told her to stop.

For a moment Jamie and I just looked at each other across my living room. I was wondering what she was wondering—did she wonder why he had touched me and never her? I wondered if she felt left out for never receiving the only kind of love her father had to give. It sounds absurd, but: Was she jealous?

Finally Jamie broke the silence.

"He said yes—he did molest you. He said you were such a hot little girl and Lisa wasn't putting out for him and he couldn't stand not to touch you. He was crying when he told me this—like deep down—like man tears, Davina..*man* tears—"

"Ok. Fuck. He was crying *man* tears—I get it!"

"He was saying he felt neglected by Lisa—"

"So he *abused* me??!!"

Jamie came and sat on the arm of my big reclining chair.

"I believe you now," she said.

She tilted my head so it was leaning on her thigh and brushed my hair with her fingers.

"It's us against him from here on out," my sister said. "It's us against him."

I knew that wasn't true because Jessie was still living in her house and she couldn't be *totally* against him while that was going on, but it was a nice thing to say..especially from my younger sister who really *was* on my side when we were kids..and especially since it's so rare that people say nice things to me. We sat in my recliner with Jamie running her fingers through my hair—that was the first time in forever I felt truly loved by someone other than my kids.

## 80
Jessie was a tricky one. I knew he had the Harley. But then I saw him leaving the Harmony Inn and driving off in a white Bronco—probably his child-molesting vehicle. It's hard to rape a kid on the back of a Harley—but in the back of an old, beaten-down white Bronco? *Disco.*

Now that I knew both of his vehicles, he was easier to track. He never left Hallstead. Basically the only places he went were the bar and his apartment—guess Jamie kicked him out since she figured out Jessie really was a child molester. You can laugh if you want to, but I followed Jessie everywhere he went for days bordering on weeks. In my mind, something needed to be done—kill 'im or catch 'im. I'd start off casing the Harmony Inn—that's where Jessie got his first drink of the day. I'd stay outside parked on the other side of the street and when I saw him come out and get in the white Bronco, I would follow him home and sit outside his apartment building. I'd sit there for hours thinking about the searches he might be doing on the internet:

"father/daughter rape"

"twelve year old tied, choked, suffocated"

"church deacons in underage child sex ring"

Eventually Jessie would exit his apartment, get in the Bronco, and go. I followed him to his usual places, which were school playgrounds where Jessie would get out of the Bronco and go up to the fence to talk to little kids. These fences were too tall to climb but I imagined Jessie was priming the kids, winning their trust so that when he saw them later where there was no fence between them, they might be willing to get into his truck.

I got to know the specific kids he was targeting. They were on average about ten years old, had black hair, and looked a lot like me. The day I noticed he was purely stalking Davina lookalikes was the day I first thought that I might be in real danger. That was the day I started looking for a gun.

- - - -

Look, on the playgrounds and at the bus stops and at the daycare centers Jessie stopped at, I was right behind, asking those kids what that man in the white Bronco had said to them. He offered them drugs, offered them beer, said he had a cool apartment if they ever wanted some coke or meth or h. He only spoke to pretty girls—their boyfriends weren't invited. Some of them, he straight-up propositioned with sex, offering to be their teacher, explaining that without ever touching them he would take their little girl vagina and turn it into a big girl vagina. I told these girls to go straight home, describe the man in the white Bronco, and have their parents call the police.

Honestly, I didn't know if I was buying a gun for my protection, or to protect those little girls, or if one day when my stepfather left his apartment, I was just going to end his life. Bang. End my stress of having debilitating flashbacks every time I see the motherfucker. End the possibility of him abusing any more kids. Just *end*. End end end.

I asked people to help me find a gun. I was ready to spend my life in prison to punish that motherfucker for what he did. Nobody that I asked took me seriously—which was infuriating. No one that I knew had been through something in their life that truly made them want to kill somebody. But I was going to buy a gun whether they liked it or not.

I was going to kill him! For revenge—to make him pay for what he did to me. And for the future—to protect all the eight to twelve year olds that might turn him on in the future. When I told this to my friends, they looked at me like I was crazy. Nobody would help me. I mean, shit—I'm in goddamn fucking America and nobody will help me find a gun!

I'm leaving out the names of all the people who refused to get me an illegal weapon, since listing them here would no doubt put them on some government hit list, but I will say that I asked some people in my small group of friends that I hang out with. I even asked my sister.

But everyone said no. Weak, weak, bitch-ass faggots.

One of my guy friends once asked me about how I feel about rapists. He said:

"I guess we as people generally fantasize about forcing rapists to have empathy for their victims?"

And I said, "Not sure about that, bro. I feel like I don't care if a rapist feels empathy..but more *hurt*."

"Ok. You want the rapist to *hurt*..as *payment* for what they did?"

"Yeah," I said. *Now you got it, my brother.*

- - - -

When I asked one of my unnamed friends to help me get a gun, he said:

"Why do you need a gun, Davina?"

"Because I'm angry at this guy."

"So this guy you're angry with, what type of guy is he?"

I stopped and thought about it.

Then I said: "He's the type of guy who'd fuck a small dog just to hear it whimper."

"Daaaamn, that sounds like a cold motherfucker."

"He *is* a cold motherfucker."

"Right, that's what I said—he a cold motherfucker."

"I know that's what you said—I was just repeating you!"

"Well that's funny 'cause *I* was just repeating *you*!"

"Alright, so we've established that he's a cold motherfucker. Can you get me a gun or not?"

"Whoah, whoah, let's not jump into *that* topic before we're *absolutely sure*. How do you know this guy?"

"He's my fucking stepfather and abused me as a little kid and I need a gun so can you get me one or not?"

"He's your stepfather?"

I nod.

"You can't kill your family."

This is one of my guy friends. He's completely stoned. This is one of the only times in my life I remember thinking: *I wish this person I am talking to* wasn't *stoned.*

He's going on about the circle of life and that crazy weird shot in The Lion King where "the camera moves back while they're zooming in on the focus and it creates this weird effect that's like the entire universe self-destructing itself but in slow motion and for the benefit of all humanity."

And I'm like: "What does this have do with you getting me a gun?"

"You can't just kill your family, D."

"Why the fuck do *you* care? I never should have mentioned who it was."

"Honestly, I'm glad you did. And as your friend I feel obligated to ask you when the last time was you smoked some weed."

"I have no idea."

"Well that's a problem, D."

"Why is that a problem."

"Because I took an oath, this morning—a solemn oath—not to let myself in range of anyone who wasn't high at a distance of ten meters. No, ten feet. I can't remember now if it was feet or inches but I think you should smoke some pot with me."

So, I did. I smoked pot with this unnamed friend and it was good company and chill and took my mind off killing Jessie. But you have to understand, weed is my jam. I've smoked one or two bowls of pot every single day my entire life since the seventh grade. So no pacifist friend's smooth coercions and a little bit of weed is going to make me forget my primary mission.

"Thanks, dude."

I got up.

"You leavin'?"

"Yeah I gotta go take care of something."

"Be chill girl—you can't un-shoot someone."

"Oh, no—it's not that. I just remembered I left some clothes in the dryer."

- - - -

Cut to: me walking into a gun shop. High as *fuck*. Nathan's Guns, owned and operated since 1904. Run by law enforcement and law enforcement types. There's three such types here now: two behind the counter, the other a cop in uniform, Glock at his side. Behind the counter is hung a huge Confederate flag.

"Y'all catchin' many negras?"

Silence. All three of them looking straight at me.

The cop says, "*What* did you say, young lady?"

"Just admiring the flag, is all. Old Glory, that's what she's called, right?"

"Is there something we can help you with?"

"Yeah. I'm here to buy a gun."

I look around at the walls, each one displaying firearms.

"I mean, this is a gun shop, is it not?"

"That it is, that it is," says hillbilly number one.

"What exactly are you looking for?" asks hillbilly number two.

"I need something small..that'll fit my hands."

Hillbilly number one says, "Well step on up to the counter and let's take a look at those hands."

I go to them and lay my hands on the glass counter—palms upturned, self-harm scarring and tattoos visible all the way up my arms.

"You do them yourself?"

I nod.

"They hurt?"

I say, "Don't you like the pain, though?"

Hillbilly number one is who I'm talking to. He unbuttons his shirt. I could see the top of a flagpole with his shirt unbuttoned, but when he spreads open his shirt I can see the whole thing: a bald eagle, as big as his chest..holding a rod with an equally giant Confederate flag on it.

"Nice," I say.

There are years of history and thousands of facets of race relations in this picture.

I can't go into them now, but if you can, go find a non-white person in America and ask them if racism is still alive and well in 2016.

The cop moves over so he's right beside me. He picks up my hand via the wrist and turns it over and back.

The hillbillies and the cop are all looking each other, presumably reading each other's minds, because eventually they all say in unison:

"The Baby Glock."

"Yep, that's what I thought," says hillbilly one, going into the back of the store.

The cop asks me about my self-harm scars.

"Oh, those? Those are just outlines for future tattoos."

"Have you ever been taken in on a 5150?"

"I don't even know what that is."

"You've never heard of a 5150?"

"No, what is that, some new kind of ultra fast bullet that only kills bad guys?"

"Young lady are you high?"

"Uh, that. No, I'm not. I just got a ride down here from my friend and she smokes pot so every time I'm around her I'm like: *Linda, you've got to quit this shit! They've done studies that show pot actually* shrinks *your brain!* But, Officer..Brecht, did you know that scientists also found that smoking pot *increases the neural connectivity of your brain*. So it's sort of like..smaller but faster..you know? Anyway, if you can smell the pot on me it's like..ask Linda!"

Hillbilly number one comes out from the back and places a box in front of me.

He says, "Open it."

I do. And every tale I'm telling these men is only hiding what I really think when I look at that gun: *This is the gun that will kill Jessie. This is the gun I will use to end my tormentor's life.*

"Hold her," the hillbilly tells me.

I hold the Baby Glock.

"See how it fits her hand?"

They all go *oooh* and *aaah* over how well this weapon fits my hand.

"Can I pull the trigger?"

"Sure! Check her out! She ain't loaded!"

I hold her with both hands, swivel so I'm facing away from everyone, and I was in this deep head state where I believed that if I pulled that trigger I would end child abuse *everywhere*—all across the world.

I pulled the Baby Glock's trigger and I thought of one red hole right in the center of Jessie's forehead.

"I'll take her," I say.

I went back to the counter, placed the gun in her box, and smiled at all three gentlemen—I even smiled at the cop.

"So," I said.

"There's a three-day waiting period," says the cop.

"I know the Pennsylvania law on guns and there is no waiting period."

"If I search you right now, am I gonna find pot on you, Davina?"

"How do you know my name?"

"It's a small town."

"No shit." I look as straight at him as possible—defiance.

"Davina, there's self-harm scars on your arms. As far as I know you might constitute a danger to yourself or others. Are you feeling suicidal?"

"No, I am not feeling suicidal."

It took all my effort not to scream.

"Why do you want this gun?"

"None of your business."

"I'm making it my business because I think you might be a threat to yourself or others."

"I'm not! You're making shit up!"

"Now you sound like you might be out of control. I'm thinking I might need to call an ambulance, get you placed under that seventy-two-hour hold."

"I am not suicidal. I know the laws—there's no waiting period in Pennsylvania—so don't try to bullshit me."

"If I run your name am I gonna find 5150s in this state or any other state?"

I push the Baby Glock box farther away from me on the counter. You sign papers in most states when you're admitted to a mental hospital that says you agree not to own a gun for the next five or 10 years.

I back away from the counter, slow steps to the exit. And as I walk, I say:

"They say there's a waiting period—she says that's not good she needs something now. What do you need a gun for now, they ask. She says you don't understand. This is the kind of guy who..well, once, when I was a kid, him and his friend were talking about how low they would go to fuck a child. You know, how young the child could be and the man would still fuck the kid. You wanna know what they said? My father says three, his brother says eight—so I need something *now*, you understand? Not after a waiting period and a background check—*now*."

## 81
When I left Nathan's Guns, that cop followed me out—in fact, he followed me all the way to my *home*. I guess they do that when you make thinly veiled death threats in a place you buy guns. I kept checking out the window and that motherfucker kept sitting there, idling in his cruiser, probably looking at pre-pubescent girls on some Russian website, jacking off all over the steering wheel of the state-owned car—or perhaps he was fondly reminiscing on all the little black boys he had shot between the eyes, making their ignorant welfare mommas weep for a lifetime over those useless nigger sons. Plant a couple of crack rocks on the kid's body, tell your supervisor the hoodlum had reached into the kanga pocket of his hoodie and the cop thought he had a gun—simple!—even when it turns out that all the kid had in there was a pack of Combos. American cops are deathly afraid of black kids who eat Combos—trust me, they've done studies. It's hard for even me to get on the side of these hardcore cop-killer assassin motherfuckers, but when you look at the pigs' behavior, I mean, they're more than asking for it. So this cop stayed in front of my house for about two hours. I wished I had a gun—I would have gone out there in my robe and slippers and as soon as he rolled his window down I would have shot that pig in the face. I hate fucking cops.

- - - -

The next time I saw Jamie I told her the story of how this *sow* of a cop had followed me home and waited in front of the house.

"That's fucked up," she says. "But wait till I tell you about *this*."

"What? Is it bad? Should we smoke first?"

"Uhh.."

"Is it bad news, Jamie? 'Cause if it's bad news we smoke first."

"I guess you could consider it bad news. Yeah."

So I packed a bowl in my favorite pipe and Jamie and I got stoned.

"Are you high?" I asked. "Or do we need another bowl to handle this news you're bringing me?"

"Umm..no..one bowl will be fine."

"Then tell me the news. Don't sugarcoat that shit either. I'm an adult—I can handle whatever you throw at me."

"Well..Lisa..and Jessie.."

"Jamie girl, tell me the news or I'm gonna decapitate you and use your head to go bowling at the Rock 'n' Bowl."

"You're a really aggressive person—you know that right?"

"Jamie, I mean, if you want to know what the situation is: right before you called, I was about to get into the bath—because I'm done..I'm done for the day. Then you call and say you have some important news and would it be alright if you came over. What am I gonna say—you're my sister—the answer's always gonna be yes. So now you're here. We smoked a bowl. But when you open your mouth to tell me, all I hear is *well*s and *um*s and *ahhh*s. Just tell me the shit."

"Ok," Jamie says. "Jessie and Lisa are dating. Sort of pseudo-dating. I don't know if they're having sex—"

"That is doubtful," I said, "since Jessie likes to have sex with little girls. But continue. Please. I'm sorry I interrupted you."

"I think that's right," Jamie says. "I don't think they're having sex."

"Then what do they do on their dates?"

"Don't laugh but they go to the Rock 'n' Bowl."

"I'm laughing internally. What else do they do?"

"Sometimes they shoot pool. And she buys him clothes."

"*What??*"

"Yeah. Weird, right?"

"Is she his fucking *mom*??"

"I don't know, Davina, but I thought you would want to know. I'm sorry it took so long for me to get it out and I'm sorry about the um*s* and well*s*—"

"Don't worry about it. I was just giving you a hard time. I'm glad you told me."

We're both quiet for a minute. Then Jamie says:

"Why do you think he molested you and not me?"

"Because you're his real daughter," I say.

"Oh," Jamie says. "I never thought of that."

"Yeah, well, I've had a lot of time to think."

- - - -

Jamie left and while the boys were playing video games I was in the kitchen slow-drinking tequila out of a tall glass and literally pulling chunks of hair out of my head—it was something I could control.

I thought about Jessie and Lisa going out on the town. Even though bowling and shirt shopping is a lame date, my point is that they were going out and having fun together—having a grand old time—and here's me, so alone, so hurt, in so much emotional pain *because of stuff they did*! They forgot about me, they did. I was their fucking *child*. They used me for a Band-Aid on their *gaping* fucking wounds and then they just forgot that I existed. *Davina?—who fucking cares about that bitch.*

Looking back, even though I had a lot to be mad about, I am aware that around that time I came a little unhinged. I put blankets over all the windows, I was so sure Jessie was outside the house—lurking, staring at me, laughing at the fact that Lisa still loved him even though he literally came all over my childhood.

If you haven't guessed by now, that dog I hallucinated outside my window when I was a little girl in Louisiana..that's Jessie. It represents the demon who wants to kill the little girl in me. And that demon almost did, he almost made me a girl without a name, a girl who had lost her spark decades ago and would be an old lady in her dying bed someday with pieces of her soul chewed through.

And our poodle who had puppies—the ones I used to set on the bed and lick me when I was such a little girl that I couldn't yet orgasm—those represent my idea of sex at the time. When Jessie wanted pleasure and Mom was out of the house, Jessie took an innocent little animal (me) and made me suck his dick. That's one of the first lessons I learned about *how to pleasure myself*—do you see? Jessie was the one with *power* over me—he made me suck his dick. I used those poodle puppies in the same way Jessie used me: to give me pleasure. Of course those little dogs didn't mind licking my vagina one bit, and no psychological harm came to them—that's the difference.

And then there were the puppies who died on my way to the 7-Eleven, who got run over by trucks, who died in a horrible way. Sunny, cheerful, unaware. They would have followed me to the ends of the Earth since they had imprinted me as *mother*. And they did follow me—they followed me to their senseless, unnoticed deaths on a four-lane street of vehicles who only heard a *thunk* and never ever knew that they had killed something beautiful.

Those three puppies that died and the one I carried home screaming at my own mother..if I was guessing, I'd say that those are my kids, who even though I do the very best I can for them, they fall through the cracks. I never get the education plan I want for Phillip—I mean the school structure seems to be set up to dash my plans of getting people who can teach my kid who—god forbid—has special needs.

I was supposed to take care of those four puppies.

One survived.

The other three went *splat!* and were killed for no reason and the only way to think about their deaths is to put in the *splat!* and say their bones and their stomachs and their eyes were crushed, were flattened, by tires and road—which are crazy, angry, fast, indiscriminate things that leave streaks of blood and pelts and guts strewn naked for everyone to see. I saw those puppies' insides as I crossed that street on the way back to where we were living. It's horrifying, really—every living thing should have a funeral where gawkers aren't staring at your insides. Tell me we at least deserve that..that level of dignity.

Sometimes, though, I think the analogy goes another way. Maybe those puppies are my mom's kids.

And maybe that puppy I carried back across that busy street and to safety, to home—maybe that puppy is the part of me that burns bright, who still has her spark, who I would do anything to save.

So you see: maybe, possibly, that puppy is me.

## 82

Ashley moved back to Hallstead.

It was a big deal: we had to arrange where everyone was living and Ashley is not exactly a *step lightly* kind of person. I wasn't drinking much alcohol before she moved in, but I started out drinking with her and soon enough I was drinking alone more than I had been before Ashley moved in. Oddly, the stress that I was drinking to was mostly the stress of Ashley and I living under one roof.

She got a job at Dunkin' Donuts.

It was Ashley's birthday, her first year back in Hallstead, before she got pregnant. I had an appointment at a clinic across the street from the Dunkin' Donuts where Ashley worked. I kept seeing Jessie around town. I would go to the store and see him and everyone at home would notice that something was wrong with me.

They'd ask me, "What's the matter?"

Sometimes I couldn't even answer them, I was so withdrawn. Seeing Jessie wasn't like seeing any other man to me—it was a shock to my system, like PTSD. When I saw him I was no longer a responsible adult capable of running my life—I was that little kid, about eight, having her panties pushed down and not understanding what was happening to me.

I didn't feel safe walking to the store. I didn't want to run into him face to face. I played out scenarios in my mind: what I would say, or if I would start punching him. I was scared to go outside—really stressed over everything. Like if I dropped a pen or pressed the wrong buttons on the remote, it could devastate me for an entire day.

Somewhere inside, I knew things had gone too far for me mentally, so I made an appointment to refill my meds. The entire walk into town, my stress built and built on the thought of seeing Jessie. I looked into the windows of the Dunkin' Donuts and Ashley was showing someone how to place the donuts in a certain pattern for display. Ashley: totally cool and confident. They'll prob'ly promote her to manager in a week. Me, in my appointment: I couldn't stop staring at the ceiling. A doctor was asking me questions. I started breaking down. It snowballed. I was crying and saying:

"I don't know what to do with myself."

"Do you think you're going to hurt yourself?"

"Yes," I said.

I was bawling.

"Is there anyone we can call? So you don't have to be alone?"

"My daughter is across the street."

"That's good."

"No! It's not good! I don't want my daughter to see me like this—*embarrassing*! And it's her birthday. And I didn't get her anything but this stupid card that I hand made with *junk* from my room. See? *She* has it together. *I'm* the crazy mom who would be *homeless* without our disability. This is *Ashley's special day* and I don't want to ruin that."

- - - -

I wasn't even aware they had called Ashley before Ashley came into the doctor's office and pulled up a chair so she could sit close to me. She held my hand and looked at me and asked if there was anything she could do. This made me cry even more. I didn't grow up learning how to love—I've had to learn that later. Just knowing Ashley was there by my side—on *her* birthday!—it stretched my heart strings almost to the point of breaking.

"Heart strings are real," I blurted out, and Ashley and the doctor stopped talking. "They're part of the structure of your heart and they can break when you get too sad. And if too many of them break, you die. So you see, you really can die of a broken heart. Elephants, too, I believe, are the other species that can die of a broken heart. Can you imagine a tiny elephant in its mother's womb, who will be the baby that breaks that mother's heart?"

But Ashley and the doctor were back to talking business, so no one heard that last little part about the elephant. Just you and me, Dear Reader, are privy to that part.

"That was Binghamton," the doctor told Ashley. "They have an open bed."

"Can you two please tell me what you're talking about?"

The doctor said, "We're talking about you going to the mental ward in Binghamton."

Ashley said we didn't have a ride because Aaron (my pot dealer and Ashley's new boyfriend) was at work and he had the car.

They called an ambulance which took me to the Binghamton General ER. I zoned out. At some point I saw that Ashley was with me in the ambulance talking (to me, I guess) but I wasn't in my body enough to respond to what she said. I mean you have to understand psychosis, paralysis, catatonia, for any of this to make sense—and once you understand it, it *still* doesn't make sense. But in certain types of psychosis, ordinary things can take on special significance to the psychotic person. Like lights—that's one a lot of people get. You start believing that lights have some special meaning beyond just lighting up the room—you believe that they carry special meaning *for you*, and if you don't pay attention to them, you might miss some of that meaning. It makes no logical sense—that's why they call it psychosis :)

- - - -

I couldn't stop looking up, even when they took me to the psych ward on the third floor of Binghamton General to be evaluated.

They put me in a room to wait for the doctor and the light panels had clouds and sky on them and I thought that was so goddamn amazing—they knew patients would be lying in bed looking at the ceiling so they painted clouds and sky on them so we had something to look at. I also felt it justified me looking upward—now, there really was something up there for me to look at.

They gave me pills to calm my heart rate and lower my blood pressure.

They asked me so many questions.

It was kind of scary in there because there were a lot of patients and it was crowded. I felt like maybe I needed to stay there but also I didn't really want to stay there. Ultimately it wasn't my choice—the hospital decided they didn't want me. This was a relief to me—in my mind it meant that Binghamton's psych ward had people lots crazier than me—honestly it has given me confidence every time I'm released from a psych hospital. You look at the patients who are staying—those are the people who are not ready to go back into the regular world. And then there's you..and somehow the psychiatrists and neurologists and nurses and physician's assistants and the fucking *janitors* have all gotten together and said: *Yep, she's ready to go. Let her loose in the world of the officially sane.*

But all of us who have come and go from psych wards—doctors and patients alike—we know a little secret: those who have never had counseling, who never thought of suicide, who've never spent the night in a psych ward..those people are at the middle of the bell curve. They are not *alive* in the truest sense of the word. There's a friend of mine who says, "You've never lived till you're bipolar." Now I don't know what I have, if it's bipolar or straight depression or PTSD or general psychosis or what, but I believe that statement that my friend says. Its kernel rings true.

- - - -

When they kicked me out of Binghamton they just told Ashley to keep an eye on me and to get me in to see my doctor soon. The doctor who took care of me at the clinic on Ashley's birthday turned out to be my regular doctor there. When I talked with nurses at that clinic they whispered to me that I had gotten "the best doctor" there—and it was true. That doctor took such good care of me. She referred me to a therapist and he turned out to be the best person I've talked to in that capacity. I wasn't afraid to cry in front of him—he would just quietly sit there and let me cry.

That therapist was really really nice and kind and a good listener and had lots of really *bullseye* advice and suggestions and for once in my life I was talking to an adult who about a lot of things was wiser than me.

So you know what I did?

I stopped going.

*Great life choice, Davina—just stellar.*

Now check out this irony: part of why I told him I can't see him anymore is that I can't leave the house. I left him a nervous breakdown message explaining that going outside makes me fearful and I basically can't deal. He left me a message saying we could talk over the phone. I texted him and told him that was no good—the telephone makes me anxious. Why? The suggestion of some disembodied consciousness floating into my ear irks me on some soul level. It's not right, I claim—*it's not natural*—the telephone should never have been invented. My counselor texts me back and says I can text him instead of calling. So I can't see him in his office because I can't leave the house. I can't call him for sessions because I get worked up on the phone. And even though the guy is offering to counsel me over *text message*, really *that's* too imposing to me as well and after he left his nice message suggesting this last option, I never texted him back. It's like I'm drowning..right next to a ship..and they throw me a rope..and instead of letting them pull me up, what do I do?

I cut the rope.

I've always hated myself for stopping seeing that guy.

Since then, when I've gone to see other therapists, I don't know what I'm doing there. It seems pointless. I can see beyond their simple tricks and it's clear to me—maybe to them, too—that they can't help me.

Maintenance therapy is no good for me.

I always stop taking my meds.

I never even recognize when I'm crying more than normal or when my thoughts are out of control.

I guess I'm the kind of person that just waits until everything is completely and unfunctionally insane and *then* I go to the hospital.

## 83
One time me and Phillip were walking to the grocery store and Jessie was there on Main Street, on his motorcycle. We walked past him as he pulled out from in front of a bar—it wasn't the Harmony Inn but a darker, creepier place that was more like a private club for bikers. Jessie drove away, then made a U-turn and came back. When he passed us he went real slow. He tipped his hat at me and nodded and smiled. It looked to me like he was pleased as fuck to be tormenting me again in a random town 3,000 miles away from California.

- - - -

I didn't even know I had stopped walking until Phillip squeezed my hand. I was getting honest with myself about Jessie in a way I never had before. The way I always tell it, Jessie abused me from the time I was eight until the time I was thirteen. That's what I absolutely, perfectly remember. But I will always have suspicions that it happened before that, before my dad took me to the indian reservation.

I do have some specific memories from that very young age.

One that I think I mentioned was of Jessie wanting to play a tickle game when I was in the bath at four or five years old.

One of my earliest memories is of me and Lisa and Jessie in a studio apartment. Mom put me in front of the TV and put a movie on—it was the *Trilogy of Terror*, one of maybe five VHS tapes we owned—and while I was supposed to be watching this movie, Lisa and Jessie were fucking like crazy on the bed behind me. I must have been four or five. This one scary part of the movie comes on so I turn my face from the TV and I see my mom's face which I could not distinguish from pleasure or pain. She and Jessie were fucking doggy style on the bed—Jessie was just *plowing* into her, like every thrust he made she had to resist being pushed off the bed on top of me. I remember that my mother was wearing a see-through nighty, red, with flowers.

When Jessie was done *destroying* my mother's asshole, he would come down off the bed and sit beside me. Mom laid in bed shooting meth. Every time something scary happened in the *Trilogy of Terror*, Jessie would grab onto me and pretend *he* was the scared kid and *I* was the adult. He may have touched me inappropriately then—well, in all probability he *did* touch me inappropriately then. But whatever he did, if anything, I blocked from my memory long ago. All I remember is when I watched *Trilogy of Terror* with Jessie it was a lot scarier than when I watched it on my own.

- - - -

I was reliving these childhood memories when Phillip squeezed my hand and said, "Mom, let's go." It shocked me out of my head (which—trust me—is a grade-A house of horrors) and I was standing again on the sidewalk on Main Street, holding Phillip's hand.

Jessie was nowhere in sight.

That day at the grocery store I was sure I'd see him. I was peeking around aisles and running the cart fast through safe areas, always focused on the next checkpoint—an intersection of aisles—where my blood ran fast and hot in anticipation of my tormentor.

When you touch a kid in a way that violates them, that shit is with them *for the rest of their life*. You know that saying: *What doesn't kill you makes you stronger.* Well, in my experience, that's not exactly true. Some stuff that doesn't kill you drives you to hate the very life you're living, makes you never trust adult men—and women even—far past the age where you are one of them. The Joker says that whatever doesn't kill you simply makes you stranger. But mostly in my experience the things that didn't kill me made me *sicker*. Being molested by my stepdad didn't make me stronger—it made me sick, it made me paranoid, it made me weak. It makes me jump when someone touches me, it makes me completely undateable because I carry a truckload of baggage that nobody has time for. Yeah, I grow, and in some ways I grow through that shit. But it's kind of like a bruised piece of fruit—I can learn to live with them, but the bruises never go away.

We didn't see Jessie in the grocery *that day*, but we were bound to run into him eventually because Hallstead is a very small place.

- - - -

Skip ahead three weeks. I went for groceries. I push our basket through the double doors and you know how they always have those Redbox automatic video rental things right as you come into the store? Well, Jessie was standing at the Redbox picking out a movie and beside him was a grocery cart that would have been completely empty if there hadn't been a girl—three, maybe four years old—sitting in the basket.

This spurs all kinds of fantasies for me like: *who is Jessie living with?* and: *Is that her little girl?* and: *Shouldn't he be listed in the sex offenders database?* I looked at that girl in her pretty little dress, patterned with sunshine logos and I looked at her face, so wonderful in its youth, but withdrawn. She was looking at the floor, afraid to make eye contact—and I wondered if Jessie had made her that way. I wondered what kind of outlook this child would have throughout her life, and most of all I wondered what kind of horrible things Jessie was doing to her.

I ditched my cart and went back through the sliding doors. Jessie didn't see me. I couldn't shop—I just couldn't do something so *trivial* while I knew that girl was suffering under the same hands and cock and *breath* and beard that I had suffered under. I was alone. I ran home crying and thinking of every time I could remember that Jessie had touched me in a way he shouldn't have. It was too much to even remember. When I got to the creek I sat on the ledge and let my legs swing—back and forth, back and forth, like rocking a baby. I watched the water pass under and the clouds pass over and I thought two things: One, I reminded myself that I had never molested my kids. I know I already told you that, but that was an accomplishment for me. I was raised to think sexual abuse was normal—and I am an extremely fucked-up person, sexually, because of it. I can't say I was never *tempted*—to treat my children as my puppies or to violate them during a bath. But the point is, I *didn't*. I let that stop with me. None of my kids' lives will be fucked up by sexual abuse—they might be fucked up by other things, but not by that.

The second thing I thought, sitting on the edge of that bridge, is that I had to save that little girl from Jessie's cart. I had to find her mother or get the police to take the girl away on the basis that Jessie was a sex offender. Surely when he was arrested for the things he did to me, they put him on some kind of list. I came back down from the ledge and started walking toward home. I needed to get on the internet and look up the number for the cops. I needed to find the sex offenders database and see if my stepdad was in there. By the time I was over the bridge and back on regular streets I was *running*—I might not be able to save that girl *completely*, but I was going to save her as much as I could.

I got to my house, sat down in front of the computer, and called my friend Beth.

- - - -

"Davina!!"

"Hey, Beth. I need your help with something."

I had met Beth at Dunkin' Donuts. She worked there and we had talked when I went into see Ashley and we had traded numbers.

"Are you ok?"

"Yes. Well no. I mean *I'm* ok, but someone else might not be."

"You sound out of breath."

"Yeah well I ran all the way home. I called you as soon as I got here."

"Was someone chasing you?"

"No. Beth, listen. You know how my stepdad is back in town?—I mean like he moved here, several months ago?"

"Is his name—"

"Jessie. Yeah. Well I don't know if Ashley told you this, but he molests little girls."

"What?! Eww, yeah, I even shook his hand the first time he came in to see Ashley. I have child molester germs now!"

"This is serious, Beth. Ok? He touched me when I was a child and I'm still fucked up about it."

"Maybe you should see a counselor."

"This isn't about me. I've been to counseling. Many times. And it was completely unacceptable."

"Have you tried yoga?"

"Beth, this isn't about me! Today I saw Jessie at the Redbox at Rob's Country Market! He had a little girl in his cart. I don't know if that's like his girlfriend's kid or if he picked her up from a playground or what."

"Did you ask him?"

"Did I ask him what?"

"Whose girl it was."

"No! Fuck no. I don't want to talk to him. He *ruined* my fucking childhood. I thought I was safe when I moved here, but now it's like a big conspiracy between my sister and my mom to move him here and get him set up so he can fuck with children again!"

"You need to call the police."

"He already served his time for molesting me."

"But what about that girl?"

"That's what I'm *saying*."

"You know what you need to do?"

"What?"

"See if he's in the sex offender database. Call Children and Youth."

"Hold up. I can look it up right here."

I went to the Children and Youth website. I queried Jessie by his full name. When his mugshot came up I almost took a shit in my pants—I have strong associations to that face.

"Beth?"

"Yeah."

"He's in there. He's on the sex offender's list."

"Call Children and Youth."

"Yeah.."

"I'm serious, Davina. Call Children and Youth and tell them you saw a registered sex offender with a little girl in his shopping cart."

"Ok."

"Promise you'll make the call?"

"Ok. Yes. I will. Do you know the things *he did to me*??!!"

"Tell me later. I'll call you tonight when I get off work."

"Ok."

"You gonna be ok?"

"Maybe."

"You have to promise me you're not going to kill yourself."

"Ok, Beth. Bye."

"Promise me."

"Ok! I promise that if I'm going to kill myself I'll do it at the Dunkin' Donuts."

"Alright, good enough. See you girl."

- - - -

I called Children and Youth.

"Children and Youth Services. This is Nikki speaking."

"Hi Nikki I'd like to report a registered sex offender who I just saw in Rob's Country Market with a little girl in his cart at the Redbox renting a movie. It's my stepdad and he molested me when I was younger. He has a thing for cumming on girls' asses—at least that's what did to me. He might be into different stuff now, like child and baby rape. I don't *know*, I'm just saying he might have escalated."

"Most rapists do."

"They do?"

"Yeah, most violent offenders start out as peephole artists, they like to steal a certain kind of object from the kind of person they're interested in. Like they'll steal a toddler's first pair of underwear. They might touch themselves with it when they masturbate or just keep it in a secret place—it makes them feel connected to the child. But ramping behaviors occur—just holding that person's underwear isn't enough. So their behavior escalates to friendly conversations with the child to convincing the child to let the offender heavy pet the child, touching them, trying to stimulate the child as a means to slowly developing a relationship—"

"Stop. Please stop. I know all this. You're punching buttons all over my childhood. I'm a fragile person right now and I can't keep going over and over this shit *because. it drives me. fucking. crazy.*"

"Ok. Davina. Are you a danger to yourself or others right now?"

"I'm not a danger to myself but I'll be a danger to Jessie if I ever see him hurt another kid."

"I'm going to forget I heard that last part—and I won't put it in my notes. You did the right thing, Davina, by calling us. You're not going to go all vigilante on me, are you?"

I laughed. I wiped away my tears.

"You mean like when Bruce Willis cuts that guy's dick off with the samurai sword in *Pulp Fiction*?"

Nikki was exceedingly calm.

"Exactly. No samurai swords. Do you own a gun?"

"No. But I do own some swords."

"Just keep them on the wall, ok? Let me handle Jessie—? What's his full name?"

I told her.

I heard a few clicks of her keyboard.

"Well, Davina, your offender is in our database. So that's a good thing."

"Ok, he's in the database. But what do we do *next*? This guy is living with a girlfriend *who has kids*. Can't we get him out of there or tell the mom that he has sex abuse charges in the past?"

"How do you know that he's living with a girlfriend who has kids?"

"Because my sister told me. She's his real daughter, so of course they're buddy buddy and he tells her *everything*. I'm only his stepchild so of course *I* was fair game—I mean we're not blood relatives so *what the fuck does* he *care*??"

"Davina, listen to me. I'm very sorry for what happened to you—"

"Did anything like that ever happen to *you*," I asked.

"Yes. It did. That's why I volunteer at Children and Youth."

"You're *volunteering*? Geesh, I'm sorry for taking up your time with my frivolous concerns. A lot of people have had it a lot worse than me. I know that, ok—I know that I'm just a drop in the bucket and my life was smooth sailing compared to others..maybe yours."

And then I started crying.

"Davina, it's ok. What you've been through is hard. You don't have to compare your story to others' to discount yours or to feel that yours is worse. Know what I mean?"

"Yes."

"Now I need you to tell me everything you know about this guy."

"Well, you have his picture."

"Is there anything else you can tell me about him?—his car, his address."

"Oh yeah, I did notice that the address you have on the offender database is wrong."

So I told her the real address of Jessie's girlfriend, I described the van he sometimes drove and I told her about his motorcycle.

Then Nikki told me thanks for calling and that someone would get back to me.

- - - -

I was revved the fuck up over this whole morning so I smoked a bowl in my comfy chair. The pot cut through some of my anxiety but my mind wouldn't let me relax: it was showing me claymation versions of my stepdad straight *fucking* that little girl from his shopping cart. His claymation cock stuffed itself into that three-year-old's pussy—and when she screamed, her red claymation lips formed a large, quivering, "O."

When Jessie came, it was white translucent clay that shot out of his penis.

When the little girl's vagina bled, it was bright red clay that stained the sheets and seeped through to the mattress. I won't say they were ruined, but those three items—the mattress, the sheets, and the little girl, were forever changed.

My phone rang—it was Children and youth.

"Davina?"

"Yes."

"Hi. This is Micah. I understand you talked with Nikki before."

"Yes. I'd prefer to speak to Nikki again..if that's possible."

"Well, Nikki's on another call. Would you mind speaking with me."

"No, that's fine," I said.

But it really wasn't fine. I trusted Nikki. I didn't know Micah from Adam.

"Ok, here's the deal, Davina: as long as he's registered as a sex offender, he's technically not doing anything wrong. He's required by law to update us if he moves or changes his phone number, but right now, legally, he has his ducks in a row—"

"Just shut up, ok? Just shut up. This guy is a *child molester*. He's living in a house with several young kids. What if his girlfriend doesn't know about the sex offender registry. I mean, you know, maybe she doesn't *check the internet* every time she meets a new guy."

"Well she should."

"Micah. Help me. What can I do next here?"

"Nothing. As far as we know, this guy isn't breaking any laws."

"What about you could go over there and tell his girlfriend that this guy likes to fuck children."

"We only do that in extremely rare cases, when the offender has been witnessed with a cut, bruised, or damaged child—or if someone witnesses the abuse. Did the girl you see have any apparent injuries."

"No, she did not."

"Then there's not much I can do for you, Davina. You did the right thing to call us, and if you ever get any more information about this guy or any of his victims, don't hesitate to call."

I'm *one of his victims, asshole*, I thought.

But I just hung up.

- - - -

I directly dialed the police.

I told them about me and Jessie as a child and him trying to burn down our house and how he would get Lisa to jerk him off while I mowed the lawn and I even told them about my dog hallucination—which probably wasn't a good idea—then I got into the recent situation at Rob's Country Market where Jessie was holding that little girl—I put hard emphasis on the fact that she was three or four years old—and I said I thought Jessie would rape or molest his girlfriend's kids if somebody didn't stop him.

"You want us to stop him?"

"Yes."

"For what?"

"For being on the sex offender list and being alone with a three year old."

"Was he beating her at the time you saw the two of them at the Redbox?"

"No, he wasn't."

"Was he treating the child inappropriately in a sexual manner?"

"Not at the time when I saw them."

"Well it sounds like he hasn't committed a crime. Unless you could show me pictures—or even better, video—of him abusing that child, then there's nothing we can do."

"Couldn't you go there and examine the children?"

"Not without probable cause. Jessie, he's paid his time for whatever crimes he committed in the past. He's in compliance with the sex offender list, so he's legal. Just because he wasn't responsible with little kids in the past doesn't mean we can lock him up forever. He's a free man. Now if he *does* abuse one of them kids you say he's living with, and somebody calls it in, then that's a different situation. Then we raid the house and take his ass to jail, if necessary."

"So basically we've got to wait until he ruins some other girl's life before we do anything."

A sigh on the other end of the line.

"That's right. We can't arrest a man before he commits the crime."

"So you think it's ok..for you and me..and everybody else who *knows* this man is a child molester..and who *knows* that he's living in a house with children who either are or will be inside the target age that this man likes to have sex with or at least have sexual contact with..you think it's *right* for you and me to stand by waiting for him to actually start fucking with these kids' lives before we do anything?"

"I didn't say it was right. I said it was the law. I'm just doin' my job, lady."

## 84
Lisa's older sister died and I guess that got her thinking about the fact that she would someday die. She called me and told me that her sister died and asked me if I wanted to have lunch at Olive Garden. Like she was afraid *she* would die before she told me a few things that were tugging on her grody, defunct little heart.

So that bitch took me to Olive Garden. She picks me up in this really badass fully loaded all-black Jeep. My mom always was a real cool cat. I get in and it's beige leather, recently polished, fuckin' heads-up display that shows your land speed, RPMs, external temperature. Today it happens to be minus ten or some crazy shit. I'm looking all around her Jeep—I can't help it. The dashboard is so high tech I feel like a fish looking at a typewriter. Goddamn. We grow up in the Foothill Motel and now *this* is what my mother drives? I know right away she's clean because you can't afford nice shit when you're using. But that's how she grew up. Her clothes, her jewelry—high-end boutique shit—stuff *I've* never been able to afford, that's for sure. She had all that when she was a kid—her family was well off enough that she went to Catholic school her entire school career—if you can believe that. Everything about her—her pantsuit, her earrings, her bracelets, her shoes, and this dope-ass Jeep—it's all super nice and I'm insanely jealous of it. Of course I don't let on—my main emotion is shame. Shame that my mom is somehow easily easily living like this while I can hardly pay the heating bill in sub-zero temperatures. She's blasting internet radio in this Jeep that looks like something Jay Z would drive and back home my kids are covered with ten blankets each so they don't freeze to death. I honestly think I never have known this woman—I look her up and down, taking in the short fur coat, manicured nails, Manhattan hair cut—I swear, the only thing that bitch was lacking was a cigarette holder, and I was sure she was on the lookout for one. She reminded me of Cruella de Vil—I thought at any moment she was gonna pull out a Dalmatian puppy and break its neck just to show me who was boss.

I forced her to say the first word—I was silent all the way to Olive Garden, looking out at the snow and counting the number of years I had been in Hallstead. Lisa understood the game I was playing—hell, I probably learned it from her—and she also didn't say a single word all the way to the restaurant. That's *if* you can call Olive Garden a restaurant. Total waste of money. Small portion sizes. The only way to enjoy a meal there is to drink about two bottles of wine before you order your appetizer. But Mom didn't order wine, so I didn't either. After Mom ordered a bowl of their *stupid* bottomless salad she said:

"So your aunt Marcy died."

"Yes, you told me over the phone."

"I guess I thought it would be best to tell you in person."

"Then why didn't you?"

"Oh, Davina, I never have had the strength for your questions. I feel like I'm on the witness stand in some court when we talk. Your tone with me is always so..interrogative. I feel like we're in a court of law."

"Well, Mom, that's what happens when you treat your kids like shit."

"I never treated you like shit."

I slammed both my palms on the table.

"You looked the other way!" I say.

"Is this about Jessie again?"

"Mom, the whole motherfucking *thing* is about Jessie, and Louie, and you shooting meth, and that Icelandic dude you let fuck me for drugs when I was like eight months pregnant!"

"I don't remember any Icelandic dude."

"Yeah, it's much easier to remember someone's name when they've got their *cock up your ass*!"

People from the table next to us were listening. Honestly, they deserved some entertainment that would beat watching reruns of *Family Feud* all night.

My mom stared at her paper placemat, sort of nodded, then scratched one of her teeth with a fingernail, then she looks up at the ceiling and her face kind of winces, then she shakes her head decisively. She says to the placemat:

"I don't remember any Icelandic dude."

She's not even talking to me—she's just consoling herself.

Our waiter comes over and my mom is attempting to say the name of the dish she wants but she can't see the letters on the menu. It's not like she was trying to say some Italian phrase, either—when she finally reads it and says it, it comes out, "Beef Ravioli." I look at my mom like she's stupid—which, I'm sorry, in some areas she is. She can't read the phrase "Beef Ravioli" off a menu, yet she can drive thirty miles from Hallstead to here—in two feet of snow—without crashing that beautiful Jeep.

"Do you have your glasses with you?"

"They're in the car."

"Why don't you *wear* them?"

"Frivolity and details, my dear Davina."

She reaches out to touch my hand but I pull back.

The waiter asks me what I'd like to eat.

"I'd like penne pasta with chicken, lots of sauce, and a glass of wine."

"May I show you our wine list? It is very expansive."

"Nah, just get me the cheapest Cab' you got."

"You would like me to call you a cab?"

"I want a *Cabernet*."

I point to the general location on the menu.

"Cabernet. Cheapest glass you got."

"Ok very good so I have one Beef Ravioli, a penne pasta with chicken, lots of sauce, and one glass of the cheapest Cabernet we have. Will that be all for you ladies?"

"Actually, I'll take a glass of that Cabernet, too," my Mom says.

I look at her—*hard*.

"Better make it a bottle," she says.

"No," I say to both of them. "No bottle, no glass for her, no glass for me—just, don't bring us any alcohol."

"Very well, I will have your dinners out shortly."

The guy leaves.

"So what did you take us all the way out here to talk about?"

"I wanted to tell you your aunt Marcy died."

"You already told me."

"Well.." my Mom says, and she's back to staring at her placemat.

"How did she die?"

My mom looks up.

"She died in a car accident."

"Who else was in the car?"

"It was just her."

"Well, was anyone else injured?"

"No. Just her," my mom says quietly.

"Was it near the house? Highway? What?"

"You're making me feel like a courthouse, Davina, with all your questions."

"Alright. I won't say anything else till you finish your story."

I sit back in my chair, folding my arms across my chest. This should be good.

- - - -

"It was last night," my mom begins. "It was Friday. And you know your uncle Peter—Marcy's husband, who works at the GM plant up there. Well Peter took on an extra shift for one of his buddies. Took a night shift. When he came home the next morning he presses the button to open the garage. Marcy's car was still there—which was a surprise since you know Marcy volunteers for that literacy program for little homeless kids. Peter drives his truck all the way into the garage and then he sees a hose coming out of Marcy's window. And Marcy is in her car, pure white—I mean Peter told me it was the whitest color white that he had ever seen—stark white. Marcy's eyes were open. Peter said the expression on her face was the most pleasant look he'd seen on that girl's face since the day they got married. Pete said even though he was sorry for her sadness—which he admitted he was a major cause of—that he liked seeing her face like that and he was glad she died peacefully. I mean—who wouldn't agree?—carbon monoxide poisoning is a lot more dignified than a shotgun to the forehead."

Lisa waited for a while and then she said:

"Go ahead. I'm done. You can speak now."

I lean forward.

"You told me she died in a *car accident*."

"She did. She was in her car, she had an accident. I try to shield you from the rougher parts of life, Davina. You're my baby. You'll always be my baby."

"If you haven't noticed I've got three kids of my own now and all of them deserve to know the truth about how aunt Marcy died. A *car accident* is when you bump into someone in a parking lot. A *car accident* is when an eighteen wheeler falls off of an icy bridge and crushes a car on the highway below, but miraculously the pregnant woman in the driver's seat—and her three-month-old baby—*survive*. A car accident is *not* when aunt Marcy sticks one end of a garden hose in the front seat with her after she stuck the other end into her exhaust pipe—that's suicide. And if we're gonna make any progress in this family, we've got to stop lying to each other. Sue me if I'm not sad about Marcy killing herself but *I never got to know them*."

"I didn't know you were interested."

"That's the parents' job. Little kids aren't going to come up to you and say, 'Please can you take us to go meet uncle Peter and aunt Marcy.' *You* have tell them, as an adult."

"Well I'm sorry if I've disappointed—"

She's cut short by the return of our server. He delivers salad, salad bowls, bread sticks, and tall plastic cups of ice water to our silent table and even when he asks us if we need anything else, neither my mom or I say a thing.

My mom continues with her raspy voice.

"I'm sorry if I've disappointed you. I wanted to do something nice for you and I remembered that this was your favorite restaurant when you were a kid—"

"No it wasn't."

"Sure it was—we used to take you there all the time and it was your favorite place to go."

"Mom, no it wasn't my favorite place to go and you took us there like *once*. And I hated it that one time and I still hate it today—I've hated it all my life."

"Well I'm sorry if I didn't know you hate the Olive Garden so much—we could have gone somewhere else."

"But you never thought to *ask me*."

"What??"

"You took me out to dinner but you never thought to ask me where *I* might like to go. It's like there's only one person in this relationship: you. Don't you ever notice that? It's like you're pulling the strings and I'm just your little puppet."

"Well I don't know what *that* means."

"Ever since I was a little kid, it wasn't like I was your child—it was like I was your *pet*."

"Davina I don't understand a word you're saying. I've always loved you. Both your father and me."

"Which father?"

"Jessie, baby. We did the best we could for you."

I shake my head.

"You're gonna sit here and lie and tell me you and Jessie *both* did your best for me—that fucking rapist—"

"Keep your voice down, Davina."

"Why? Rape rape rape."

"Your father never raped you."

"No. He did everything but."

"Davina, baby, things happen in life. You've gotta move on. The things that happened to me as a child are more than I can tell you in this restaurant."

"Why not?"

I take the salad bowl that we're both supposed to be feeding off and I empty it in the middle of the table.

Lisa says in a low demonic voice: "Davina. Why did you do that?"

I stare that bitch down.

- - - -

"Why did *you* bring me to fucking *Olive Garden* to talk?"

"I thought you would like it."

"It's not *private*, Mom. Look around us: there's a table, there's a table, there's a table—everyone can hear every word we're saying. We'd be better off talking in a parking lot."

"Well," she says, "obviously I'm a piece of *shit* for taking you out to get some food, but I do have some things I want to say."

"About what."

I make a face like if she says the wrong thing I'm about to pull out a samurai sword and cut the bitch's head off.

"About Jessie."

I push back my chair and stand up.

"Where are you going?"

"Somewhere I don't ever have to hear that name again."

Everyone in the dining room is looking at us.

"I'm trying to apologize to you, Davina. Please sit down. I won't mention his name until we get our food."

"Are you promising me this?"

"Yes, I'm sorry. Please sit down with me."

I sit down.

The waiter comes by. He doesn't have our food. He looks distressed.

"Um..some of the other tables have mentioned some loudness coming from this table so if you want to dine here I'm gonna have to ask you keep your voices down and your language moderate."

"Look, square boy, we'll keep it down but don't try to legislate the contents of our speech." I said this. "Shoo. *Shoo.* Get our food and bring it out to us—that's *your* job, ok?—so do your job and we'll tip you real nice and you tell those other tables to *mind their fucking business*..or we *will* have trouble. Understand?"

The waiter rolls his eyes at me—but he leaves.

Mom takes my hands.

She says, "Me and Jessie just wanted to say we're sorry for raising you up so haphazardly."

I look at my mom for like twenty seconds before I reply.

"You said. You wouldn't mention his name. Until our food came out."

"See?" she says. "Every time we talk it's like you're a lawyer and I'm on the stand."

"You sealed it up that way a long time ago, Mom."

"What is that supposed to mean, I 'sealed it up'?"

"You *knew*. You knew what he was fucking doing to me and *you didn't stop it*. That makes you complicit in what he did and don't forget: you were *there* some of the time. You two did it together."

"What did we do together?"

"You were in the room when Jessie told me to lean over my bed. You were *there in the room* when Jessie pulled my dress up and pulled my panties down. You were there in that room when he spanked my ass *red!!*"

"That was twenty years ago, Davina. You ain't a kid no more. You're an adult—like me, like Jessie."

"So you admit you were in that room."

"Sure. *Sure.* I was in the room sometimes when Jessie spanked you."

Now I wasn't a courtroom lawyer—I was a little kid again on the verge of crying right smack dab in the middle of this fucking Olive Garden.

"Why didn't you stop it?"

And here, my mother's face cycles through a number of expressions and colors like a next-generation mood ring. Finally she says:

"I was scared to."

First I was too mad to say what I wanted to say, then I felt for her, this old woman who carried around an apology for two decades in some festery pocket of her soul.

"How much of it did you know about?" I said.

"I don't really know—I can't know, can I?"

"He used to lick my pussy."

Lisa made a covering motion where her hands covered her head, like it was raining *rocks*—not raindrops—but something too heavy to handle.

"I'm sorry, Davina."

"He used to cum all over my ass during Sunday nap time. He would sneak over to me and get his dick off between my ass cheeks."

Mom tightened the roof made of arms and hands over her head and looked down at her plate.

"I know," she said.

"Did you know him and one of his biker friends tried to rape me one time? Did you know that every time you left me alone at the house with him, he made me suck his dick?"

"No! *No!* I mean *yes!*—I knew about some of it. Please don't say any more."

I wanted to send her every detail, make her break down and cry in this chintzy, wannabe restaurant.

"Are you aware that you ruined my childhood?"

Lisa nods.

"Do you know that I'm always gonna be fucked up from all that?"

Lisa raises her head and nods at me.

"I'm never going to live a normal life."

And she says, "Some of that is up to you."

Which is true, definitely true, but I wasn't done humiliating her.

"I hear you and Jessie are hanging out? Is that true? That you're buying him clothes? Going to dinner? Do you know how that makes me feel? You and Jessie are like *friends now* or what? Jamie says the reason Jessie started abusing me is that you wouldn't put out!? Is that true? I bet you liked that little triangle: Jessie pays the rent, Jessie pays for drugs, you look the other way while Jessie is molesting your eight-year-old daughter—so *he's* getting what *he* wants (kinky sex with your fucking *daughter* who you don't give a shit what happens to her), and *you're* getting what *you* want, which is to lie in bed all day and shoot crystal. What do *I* get—fucked-up memories for *a lifetime*."

- - - -

The waiter brought us our food. It was disgusting—I make better at home.

Mom said she had no idea where I got the information that she and Jessie were going on dates.

"Jamie *told* me."

"Well..you know Jamie can't always be trusted to tell the truth."

"You mean because she has bipolar disorder?"

"Right," my mom says.

"I trust her a lot more than I trust you, Mom."

"Just because Jamie says me and Jessie are going on dates doesn't mean we're going on dates. Jamie has a mental disorder—you can't trust people with bipolar."

"Mom, it's not just Jamie—other people have told me about it. Hallstead is a small town. You can't keep secrets here."

"Well, all I can say is that Jamie is lying."

"What about the other people who've seen you, though, Mom?"

"They must be lying, too."

"Do you realize how incredibly unlikely that is?"

"Think what you want but I AM NOT SEEING JESSE!!"

The whole restaurant is looking at us again. Our waiter is on his way over either to kick us out or give us our second warning. I stand up and go for the door. The waiter stops me by the bar.

"I'm going to have to ask you and your guest to leave."

"Why? We're paying customers. We have a right to talk about whatever we want."

"Your language is disturbing the other customers."

"You think Grandpa Eddie and great aunt Sue have never heard the word *fuck* before?"

"I'm sorry," he says, "but this is a *family* restaurant."

"Oh yeah? Then why does your bar carry Johnnie Walker *Black*??"

I get right out of that Olive Garden and start walking home. The snow beside the highway is two feet deep—I can't even see the railing because the snow that was plowed off the highway is a six-foot hill on both sides of the road. From the Olive Garden to my house is about thirty miles—there's no way to walk it but I can't think straight and I'm just happy to be anywhere than sitting across a table from my mom. I'm going to enjoy every moment of quiet, my boots crunching with each step, every sound muffled by the snow instead of my mom's crone voice permanently insisting that things that happened in the past shouldn't affect me anymore.

- - - -

A couple minutes later Lisa pulls up in her boss Jeep and the window nearest me opens.

"Get in, Davina, you can't walk this."

"I've walked a lot worse than this before."
