She keeps pace with me.

"Davina, let me at least drive you home."

"I''m not getting in that car with you."

"Why not?"

"Because you look like a villain out of some Disney movie."

"What difference does it make?"

"IT MAKES A LOT OF DIFFERENCE!!"

I stop walking. She stops driving.

"WHY THE FUCK DID YOU TAKE ME TO THE OLIVE GARDEN??!!"

"Well, it's just a place to sit..while we talk things over."

"Yeah, in a place where everyone around us can hear every word we say."

"No one was listening to us, Davina—you're paranoid!—nobody in that restaurant gives a scratchy old *fuck* about what happened to you as a child."

"*'Scratchy old fuck?'* I've never heard that one before. You might want to get checked for Alzheimer's."

"Davina, would you please come here. I'm trying to give you something."

She's leaning over the passenger seat reaching out with her arm trying to give me a thin, white envelope. I step over a mound of snow to take it.

She says, "Buy the kids stuff."

The window starts rolling up—Cruella de Vil—fur coat, black Jeep. The customizations make her stand out like a drug dealer.

I open the envelope—it's two-hundred dollars in cash.

I wanted to yell, "Fuck you! I don't want your fucking money!" And then I was going to throw that envelope back through the window of her millionaire car, but by the time I look up, I can't even see the Jeep.

I mutter, "Merry Christmas."

- - - -

Then I continue the long walk home. People slow down to give me rides but I just hold my arm way up and extend that middle finger.

"FUCK. YOU!!"

I ain't hitchhiking. Do I look like I'm hitchhiking?? If I was hitchhiking, I would have my thumb up. There's a way we do things.

So I kept crunching through the snow.

I was extremely mad at myself for breaking my no-Lisa policy. Just looking at her makes me feel inadequate—she doesn't even have to open her mouth. Anticipating a meeting with her gives me anxiety that can only fixed with benzos.

And with Lisa's family, it's never been like we have our differences but we get along. It's always been like *fuck you*. But being related doesn't give you a card to be cruel to each other.

This walk wasn't calming me down. I didn't have gloves, wasn't wearing a jacket, so I was freezing and my knuckles hurt from the cold.

I imagined Lisa going back to my house, letting herself in, warming herself by the fuel oil that *I* paid for, maybe helping herself to some of my liquor. Insanely, I convinced myself that she and Jessie would be waiting for me in my own house when I got home.

I imagined the details of the mass murder that would ensue.

I would end up on *Deadly Women* as a series of cheesy reenactments performed by actors who were too sucky to get a real job.

They would interview my friends.

They would interview my neighbors.

And everyone, friends, neighbors, family, police—they'd all say the same thing about Hallstead that everyone says at the beginning of every single episode of every true crime show ever made:

"We had no idea something like this could happen here."

"Hallstead is such a safe town. Everybody knows everybody. No one even locks their doors."

The rookie police officer who gets tasked with the case will inform us that there has never been a mass murder this horrific in the history of the town.

*Hallstead is just a bunch of good, hardworking Americans.*

Then they'll cut to some eighty-four-year-old whitebread gramma who will basically say that Hallstead is mostly conservative whites and this crime must have been committed by a black drifter or a liberal. She'll ask the camera operator who she voted for in the last Presidential election and frown when some twenty-year-old film school grad gives the wrong answer. Gramma slowly steps back and closes her door, her face drenched in fear and suspicion.

I must have spent an hour thinking about stabbing my mother to death.

I kept thinking about that two-hundred dollars and how I was going to smear period blood all over it, piss on it, shit on it, and mail it to that bitch.

Two-hundred fucking bucks. I gradually understood this amount to be the highest amount she could give to me that would mean nothing to her to lose, but that she thought would mean everything to me.

I was incensed that bitch was right. It was going to be hard for me to let go of two-hundred dollars by mailing it to her covered in period blood just to make a point. I couldn't afford to make that point and that fucking bitch knew it. I wasn't lying to myself about how that money was going to be spent, either. Those C-notes weren't going to buy presents for the grandkids—they were going to buy weed. If Lisa wanted to buy her grandkids presents, then she should do the work to maintain a relationship with us—that was the part that was missing.

I don't know how many miles I made it—maybe two, maybe four—but somewhere between Olive Garden and Hallstead it occurred to me that the two-hundred bucks weren't really meant to buy Christmas presents for my kids—it was intended as payment from Lisa to me in exchange for her looking the other way—for passively pimping me out—my entire childhood.

- - - -

I raised my hands in the air like a kachina and turned in circles and circles and circles and circles and yes I am an anti-theist but I was praying to some form of god—call it the sky—to help me escape the fate of becoming my mother, my father, or anyone who came before me.

My prayer was this: *Just leave me alone..just leave me alone forever..*

I wanted to stop all the patterns and the tendencies and the genetic inheritances and live my life like I was born fresh—a blank slate—a new person come into this savage world at least to raise my own three children with everything..everything I could possibly know of love.

## 85
Many cars passed and many cars tried to pick me up—it was that kind of weather where if you're outside for ten minutes you're supposed to die. I just kept my hands in my pockets and ignored everyone who slowed their vehicles.

They were trying to save me—I didn't want to be saved.

I wanted to die.

Mothers aren't allowed to die, though—you've got a built-in job and that's to take care of your kids. You can't just selfishly let yourself freeze to death because you, yourself, don't want to go on.

So when this Lincoln Town Car pulls up with tinted windows, smelling like pot, I was like: *This is interesting.* I figured it was some low-level celebrity who couldn't afford a limo and I was at the limit of how much cold I could take without doing serious injury to my exposed bits of skin and my muscles were quivering and I could feel individual organs inside my body freezing up. That's what it seemed like anyway.

The Lincoln stops next to me. Somebody pushes the passenger door open. I can see it's this white guy in his fifties, lots of facial lines indicating a lifetime of laughing and smiling. He's dressed like a Rastafarian, hair dreaded up, mostly tucked away in a knitted dreadlock bag on top of his head, but stray dreads are coming out all over his neck and shoulders.

"What's goin' on there, my pedestrian sistah?"

"Don't worry about me."

"It's minus-sixteen degrees accordin' to me dashboard instrumentation, my young friend. Let me give you a ride at least inta tha town."

"You don't want to mess with me. I've got family problems."

"Ahh..that's the worst kind, no doubt about it. Let me give ya a lift."

"I can't."

"Why not, little one?"

I stop walking.

"Because I don't trust you, that's why."

"Ah, that be a rightly reason, yes."

I look at him, trying to read his eyes.

"Why do not you trust me, sistah?"

"Because too many people have failed me! You're not a girl so you probably don't understand this, but when you grow up as a girl, everyone sees you in this mold of sexuality—are you with me?"

"Yes I am sister."

"So I've had people in my church touch my pussy when they were acting like they were getting the devils out of me. I've had a boyfriend of mine—who is now the father of my first child—knock me out and fuck me in the ass. Physically punch me in the head until I lose consciousness, then use me like a corpse or a slave like all I am is a doll for him, you know what I'm saying?"

"Yeah. That be evil, in our teaching. Need to be cast out or he can't live in the society. I is sorry—I think I interrupted ya."

"No problem. I was just saying. And my stepdad, from the age of eight..you don't want to hear about this."

"Keep talking, sister—sound like you got some major blockages in your brain and you need to let those fuckers out before they destroy you."

"Anyway my stepdad is a pedophile—that's all you need to know."

"Then I won't be askin' you no more questions, my injured sister, but get in the car so you don't add death by freezing to your list of accomplishments."

I look up and down the road. It's just snow, and me, and this white Rastafarian in his low-rider Lincoln Town Car.

I get in.

He goes nowhere.

I'm staring straight ahead.

"Every second you sit there with your foot off the gas, I feel less and less safe about being in this car with you."

"Tha's jus' fine, but I don't go anywhere until everybody in my car has their seatbelt on."

I fasten my seatbelt.

"Is that good enough?"

"Tha's good enough," he says, and the car squeals back onto the highway, driving ninety miles an hour.

"Uh," I say.

"Talk to me sister."

"There's black ice. You might want to slow down."

"That's what the seatbelt's for," he says, and laughs like someone who never had a bad thing happen to him in his whole entire life.

He reaches for a pair of sunglasses that are on the seat between us.

I instinctively move away from his hand, closer to the door.

He takes his eyes off the road and looks at me. I must look scared, bloodshot eyes, like a mouse versus a chainsaw.

"Just don't touch me, ok!!"

He puts on his sunglasses and looks back to the road.

"You and me are way past that, sistah. Your stepdad fuck with you when you was young, now every man you meet, he's your stepdad in some way. I get that. When I was young I was the only white kid in an all-black neighborhood. All-black school. Every day after school I got beat up. I've never been beat up by white kids, never by Mexicans, never Canadians or Londoners either. Do you think I'm a racist?"

"I don't know. Why would you be?"

"Because, even if you ain't no racist, if the only people ever beat you up is blacks, you're going to think differently about da brothers—does ya hear what I'm saying?"

"Yes."

"Good."

"Is that why you became a Rastafarian?"

"Not exactly. But that's a nice side effect, my brilliant sistah! I'm ridin' with a smart motherfucker."

"Just don't touch me, ok?"

"I won't be touchin' nothin'. Just sit back and enjoy the tunes and let me take you home."

- - - -

He turned up the volume and we were listening to contemporary reggae by rap artists you would recognize. I never knew those guys made reggae, too. They likely make everything, but can only get the media to play certain stuff to Americans.

The guy drove me home, and aside from me telling him where my house was, we didn't say anything.

He stopped in front of my house.

I got out and leaned down so I could see him.

"You can come in if you want."

"That be a righteous invitation, friend, but you look a little shocked and I is afraid I might shock ya some more."

"Well don't you have any advice to give me, like parting wisdom to the fucked-up half-indian girl from the white Rastafarian prophet? Isn't this that scene?"

"I ain't no prophet, but does you got some weed in that house of yours?"

"Yeah."

"Well my advice be to sit back, smoke up, and forget about everything."

"Ok, I give you a B grade as a prophet."

"Only a B grade? Oh tha's right, me forgot you was the brainiac trying to kill herself by crunching through ice on a wicked-deadly vehicular thoroughfare in minus-sixteen degree temperatures. How about this, child of Jah: Simulate amnesia. Get yourself so *you* are like that snowflake—it have no idea where it come from and no idea where it's going—in fact it has *no ideas* at all. It don't know that it making it dangerous for me to drive—every snowflake out there is trying to wreck my car and kill me dead but they have no idea of that part of their function and when we look at them we say: *Beautiful, beautiful!* They don't care about me one bit. Your murderer don't come at you like Freddy Krueger—she comes at you like Judy Garland, and as she singin' that beautiful song to you and takin' you back to your childhood, to the place where a child becomes a queen, your whole world shift from black and white to Technicolor without you even realizin' it—and that's death, sistah—that be the Lord takin' you home. You a thinker?"

"Yeah."

"Don't be."

He laughs.

"What my prophet grade now?"

"Ok, A."

"Just A? Not A plus?"

"If you come inside and smoke with me I'll make it an A plus."

"I would love to but I'm afraid I might do something to hurt you. Build yourself feather armor and a laser weapon made of Starburst and we will meet again."

He reaches across, closes the door, and does a wide turn in the Lincoln Town Car. He drives up my snowy street.

The fucker won't come in because he knows I'm fragile as an egg. It's a catch-22: I'm fucked up from the sickos of my childhood, so only sickos are attracted to me now. Non-sickos are too scrupulous to hang with me—their sense of responsibility precludes them from fucking with the damaged.

I went inside and smoked and watched *Meerkat Manor* until I was a bubble of laughs in my lean-back chair and everything inside my head was delivered in that white Rastafarian's silly put-on accent.

Fucker. We could have at least smoked together.

## 86
For about the next year, I gave my mom a second chance.

I tried hanging out some afternoons at her house—in the winter we played cards (usually as a drinking game)..in the summer we cooked burgers on her back porch. Of course there was always the point when my mom said or did something to make me cry. One time she let *me* cook the burgers and serve *her* drinks and in her eye everything I did was wrong. If I made a mojito, it was wrong.

"Did you know Hemingway made his mojitos with powdered sugar?"

*No, I did not know that Hemingway made his mojitos with powdered sugar in them. I think powdered sugar in a mojito sounds awful.*

"So the real way—the *only* way—to make a mojito is to put powdered sugar in it. We have some powdered sugar inside," my Mom says.

I grab her glass and throw its contents into the yard.

"Make it yourself," I say.

And my mom laughs.

Or if I make a burger it's always too well done for her.

"You can't taste the *flavor*," she says.

"Yeah but my way you don't get *mad cow* disease. You want me to cook you another one?"

"No, just bring me a steak knife so I can get through this. It's hard as a rock."

I'm inside her house picking out a knife and I hear my mom dropping her burger onto her plate, then picking it up and dropping it again. My kids have come up from the yard and they're laughing at everything Lisa says about my overcooked hamburger patty. I'm thinking about grabbing one of her really sharp knives and cutting her throat with it.

Outside, I drop the steak knife on the table next to her plate.

She doesn't say *thank you*—she's a heartless bitch, why would she? She just grabs the knife and makes cutting into my burger look as difficult as an act from the circus.

I don't even eat—I just there opposite my mom, watching her theatrical struggles against a perfectly edible burger. She doesn't even talk to me while she eats—she's totally focused on what *she's* doing, where *her* headspace is. She holds up a piece of the hamburger and examines it like she caught a strange insect. She finally eats it.

Then she looks at her plate—only half the burger has been eaten.

"Are you on chrys, Mom?"

"No, why, does it look like it?"

"You're acting a little strange," I say.

"Well strangeness runs in our family, so be glad for the blessing."

"When was the last time you did chrys?"

"Well baby, this might surprise you, but when I accepted a job with the Corzan Medical Group I had to give it up."

"They piss test you?"

"I can't even *describe* how I feel being asked that question by you—as if the only reason I would stop doing a particular drug is that I had to get clean for a *job*. You know, before you came along, I went to Catholic school—thirteen years—and back then we had real nuns and real priests and real brothers as teachers. It's not like today where a Catholic school is a Catholic school *in name only*—with non-religious teachers teaching *religion* classes, even. So yes, the Corzan Group has regular piss tests and, *fine*, yes, that *is* part of why all I can do anymore is smoke pot for a few days after they test us. And no more meth, yes—I stopped using meth a long time ago. Maybe you're just so used to seeing me fucked up that you don't recognize the other part of me that has a job, has self-control, does work that's useful for other people and *gets paid* so I can support myself, which is more than I can say for you."

"Congratulations. *You're* doing fine and all of your *kids* are fighting for survival."

"That's not exactly, true, Davina—of all my kids, *you* are the only one who refuses to accept the rules of this world."

I just shake my head. You grow up with a crystal meth addict who had us *squatting* in abandoned houses while Ashley was a baby..who gave me meth when I was pregnant so I wouldn't be hungry. And then what? Lisa snaps her fingers and becomes a responsible adult, quits meth, smokes pot once a month, drives a phat-ass Jeep?

Fuck, I hate that woman.

Now she's a model citizen.

"Are you high on pot now?"

"Are *you*?"

"We're not talking about me. We're talking about you."

"I smoked this morning. My plan was that you and I could smoke together when you got here so we could have a nice motherly-daughterly picnic."

"I don't want you high around my kids."

"Davina, you're high around your kids every *day*."

"I hide it from them."

"Uh-huh. Like I hid it from you. You're kidding yourself if you think they don't know you smoke."

"I never smoke when they're in the room."

"They'll get exposed to it eventually."

"Yeah, but not today. That's the whole point of childhood: so you have some safe days where a whole bunch of dangerous *shit* isn't happening to you—so you can have some positive memories in the past."

"*I* don't have many positive memories in *my* past."

My mom, saying this, breaks my heart.

"Do *you*?" she asks.

- - - -

Telling her some real memory from my past would open me up to her meanness and criticisms—I have zero doubt that she will turn it against me, twist my words, compare it to some better experience she had (thereby rendering my special story pointless).

But somewhere in me I know I have the strength to endure it—I can handle anything she says, and handle it with grace. This is two moms talking—or a grandmother and a mom. I'll say my story and know that she cannot hurt me with her reaction.

"Yeah, Mom—I have a positive memory. It was in one of the houses where we lived in Louisiana..or maybe Texas. I think it was the one where there were like these decked-out Ferraris and Lamborghinis in the garage and the police came to take them away."

"That's the house where I slit my wrists."

"Yes, Mom, that was the house where you slit your wrists."

"I'm sorry to you about that, by the way."

She reaches her hands to me across the outdoor table.

I don't make a move. That woman fucked up my whole childhood and if she thinks a couple of friendly barbecues are going to fix that, then all that meth has gone deeper into that woman's head than anyone had previously thought.

I sigh deeply.

"So anyway, in that house with the Porsches where you slit your wrists, there was a fig tree in the back yard."

"I don't remember a fig tree in that house. Didn't Jessie burn them all down?"

"No, Mom. Jessie burned some palm fronds that were in the back of Ron's truck. But it doesn't matter. I'm telling you a story about all of us kids, something we did one night when you and Ron were out."

"Did you all have sex oh god."

"No, this wasn't about sex."

"I'm such a horrible mother," my mom said. "Was it drugs? Tell me if it was drugs. It was drugs, I know it. Did you go into me and Ron's bedroom?"

"No we did not and you need to chill out. You want me to bring you a cold towel?"

"No."

"What about a glass of water?"

"No, no—just tell me your story."

"So one night when you and Ron were out, and there hadn't been food in the house for *weeks*, I climbed that fig tree in the back yard and I tasted one of those figs and it was *so good* that I called the kids out of the house and they climbed a little bit up the tree and I picked figs from the top and handed them down."

I'm silent.

I look at my mom—who does in fact seem to be listening.

"That's *it*?" she says.

"Yes, Lisa—that's it. We ate the most wonderful food—which was right in our back yard. We didn't have to pay for it, we didn't have to beg from you and Ron. And I think it was a special kind of moon that night, half moon, whole moon, shooting star—something like that."

"It's called a full moon—not a whole moon," my mom says.

I say, "Now you tell your story."

"A positive story I remember?"

"Yes! Anything positive from your past."

"Well that kinda narrows it down a bit, don't it?"

I sit back and wait.

- - - -

"I think it says a lot about you, Davina, that your story wasn't one of your children. You've never been the mothering type, all the way back to when you were a kid. You played with stuffed animals that were *animals*—not human dolls—*animals*. I don't even think you *like* children."

"I like 'em more than you."

"Could you not interrupt me?—I'm trying to tell my story. So. I was in the maternity wing with my cousin Caroline, who was in her twenties at the time. Most beautiful woman I've ever seen—swear to god—my entire life. Sometimes Caroline would take me with her to the hospital where she worked. I guess I was somewhere around ten. So this woman was having a baby..just like normal..just like all the other times I went to work with Caroline. She hid me behind the sheets that separated the big room into private spaces for the mothers—you know how they do. So I'm watching this woman give birth, and all I can think of is that this woman is *waaaaay* too skinny to have a baby. And they were going with a vaginal birth. Some sound on one of the monitors went off and instantly instead of five people in the room there were twenty. They had surgeons and specialists and they had all the family go out to the hallway to wait and see what happened to the little baby who was being birthed by practically a little baby herself. I watched the entire thing from my slit in the curtains and everything they did took over an hour. Everyone had blood on their scrubs, blood splattered on their safety goggles. There were so many lights and sounds going off, it was like being at the county fair."

"What happened to the baby?"

"Oh, that baby died."

"What happened to the mother?"

"She died too."

"Why are you telling me this as your positive story that you remember about your life?"

"Because Caroline was in on that delivery and because the delivery had been such a traumatic experience, the doctors let all those nurses take the afternoon off. So Caroline and I went to get ice cream—I even remember what I ate: it was Oreo cookies in cheesecake-flavored ice cream. It was so thick you had to eat it with a spoon. Caroline told me what it was like to be an adult and she even showed me how to kiss—I mean she demonstrated by kissing *me!* But she said kissing cousins is ok and I still believe that to this day. She moved my ice cream aside and kissed me with her tongue—like a long, deep, lover kiss. My vagina got wet. If that baby hadn't killed her mother and herself during that delivery, and there was never any blood on the doctors' safety goggles, then cousin Caroline would have had to work that day and we wouldn't have gone out for ice cream. She never would have taught me how to kiss. And my vagina wouldn't have gotten wet. That was one of the best afternoons of my life..well I guess you said to tell one of the best memories of a *day* in our life..so, I guess, yeah..that was my best day."

I was going to say something but she interrupted me.

"Sometimes I think of you when I think of that mom whose baby killed her that day. When you were pregnant with Ashley, especially—'cause you were so thin. I was so worried that your baby would kill you on the way out."

I stand up.

"Mom, I have to go."

But she continued: "I think of that fetus almost every day: all purple and veiny and not making a sound. We all expected to hear screaming when they took the baby out—but it was silence. Until, of course, the baby's mother figured out what that silence meant. Then the thin little mother spent the last minutes of her own life lamenting her dead child and cursing god for not giving her a healthy baby girl—a few minutes later the mother bled out, all the doctors and nurses left the room, Caroline found me, and away we went."

Mom was full of quaint stories like these—amazing positive little rainbow-colored *gems* she tosses out just to make sure everyone else is as miserable as she is. I come from my mom—and she's very dark—that's just to say that I come from a very dark place.

## 87
After she and I got talking again, Lisa got it in her head that she wanted to take me camping in the finger lakes and do wine tasting. We had never done anything like this before. I was terrified. Thinking of spending a weekend with Mom—the idea physiologically changed me: I had diarrhea, sweating..I couldn't sleep more than four hours a night. After we made our plans official, I was scared every moment of every day right up to (and including) the day we planned to leave. When I washed dishes I couldn't get it out of my head that I'd be spending three whole days with her—I accidentally broke three separate plates and thought it was an omen, but of course it was just because of the stress. My boys asked what was wrong and I told them Mommy was scared.

"Of what?" they asked.

"Of my mother."

I knew I should have sheltered them from my relationship with their grandmother, but I didn't—I felt like telling the truth.

Even as an adult, I am intimidated by my mother. I didn't want to spend a weekend alone with her. One thing my mom's really good at is making me feel bad. I went out of a sense of obligation—and yeah I guess I had a crazy idea that me and Lisa would mend things up, that she would show protectiveness toward me and I would feel love toward her. Of course that didn't happen—she was as unthinking and uncaring toward me as she was in my earliest memories. And I was rude to her—I'll tell you that so you get both sides of the story—but I had to be rude and distant for my own protection. Anyway we drove up to the finger lakes for a long weekend and, truthfully, it wasn't horrible. My mom snores like a motherfucker so I didn't get much sleep. I was very guarded with information about my thoughts and feelings and plans for the future—I just couldn't let her get close to me in that way (close to my heart). And since Lisa seemed to believe speech was a faculty that existed purely to insult people, our dinner conversations were 97% Lisa insulting people and 3% me running to the bathroom to fortify myself with tears. I knew when I returned she would know I had been crying—but at least she didn't see me in the act.

- - - -

Fast-forward to us driving home. I'm driving the black Jeep and Mom is secretively shooting meth into her right arm, stupidly thinking I can't see her.

"You alright over there, Mom?"

"Oh yeah, Davina. I'm doing great."

I couldn't tell if she was speaking ironically, sarcastically, literally, or what. I guess this is the kind of moment you never imagine having with your mom—her getting high on one of the worst substances our species has ever cooked up, you in the driver's seat of a black Jeep that felt like driving a *tank*, keeping your eyes on the road, watching out for cops, making sure your mom doesn't overdose. At least for these few hours, *I'm the sober one—I'm the one taking care of my mom.*

Then she sets a syringe on the dashboard. The plunger is extruded to 4 cc's and it's not full of clear liquid as it would be if she was shooting meth—it's four cubic centimeters of blood.

"Mom!"

"Eh?"

"What is that?"

"Blood."

"Is there meth in it?"

"No."

"Is there coke in it?"

"No."

"Then what's in it?"

"Blood. Just my blood. Don't you ever do that, when you're out of stuff to shoot but you still need that action of shooting yourself up. It's that action, that action of pulling out the plunger and seeing your own blood mix with coke or meth or whatever, *inside* the syringe. It's like you're taking part of you *out* of your body and introducing it to this foreign substance but first they interact in the syringe—*then* they interact in your body. That's the best part of using a needle. It's partially the instant rush—yeah—that's a big part of it. But to me it's like I'm operating on myself—doing something so *intimate* with my body. It's more intimate than sex."

"What are you gonna do with that?"

Lisa picks up the syringe.

"This?"

"Yes."

"Shoot it."

"You're gonna shoot that back into your arm?"

"Yes, isn't that what I just said?"

"You've been over there shooting your own blood this whole time?"

"Well, yeah."

"Why??"

"Because I like to shoot up. Just because I can't use drugs anymore doesn't mean I don't still have the compulsion to stick a needle in my vein."

"You get pleasure out of that?"

"Of course I do," Lisa said. She sounded wistful. "It reminds me of when I could fuck around, back when you and me and Ron and the kids were squatting in mansions in Texas, robbing old-timers for the ATM cards!!!"

Lisa was laughing so hard she came out of her seatbelt. She punched my arm.

"We had some fun times back then, didn't we 'Vina!!?? You can never say you had a *normal* upbringing. Don't you hate *normal* people? They're fuckin' *everywhere*—they breed like *robots* from..what's that movie?..*what's that movie..?*"

I look over at the crazy lady they call my mom. She picks up the syringe and injects 4 cc's of her own blood back into her vein—totally pointless. And yet, after she did it, she slid down in the Jeep's plush bucket seat like she had just been hit with a shot of the purest ice you could get—which back then meant southern California ice, and the only way to get that was from some fat Mexican named Jimmy (or some guy just like him). You couldn't buy it—no—those guys don't work in cash. All they work in is free rent and underage pussy. I look at Mom. She's slid down almost all the way to the floor.

I grab her shoulder.

"Mom, you ok? What was in that syringe?"

She sits up in her seat.

"I told you. Blood. Nothing."

"Are you high?"

"I can't *get* high anymore. Corzan Group tests us once a month. I'm serious, baby, I'm through with that shit. Search me—there's no meth here. I just like to remember what it feels like to run my own body for once. Can't call it liberty if you can't choose your own drugs. We're in a—this country—this is a rat maze made of *shit*. And you know who's sitting on top?"

She paused, but I didn't say anything because I assumed this was the rhetorical rant of someone with an undiagnosed mental condition.

"*Do you know who's on top??!!*"

"I don't know, Mom—who?"

"Five white guys. The guys who control all the money. The guys who keep us all in slavery. Wars are only to control us—those aren't real wars. Those are Playskool fucking kindergarten *pageants*. Do you know how many people died in 9/11?"

"I don't know. Three-thousand or something. Then like a hundred-thousand Iraqis that we wanted to kill anyway."

"Zero."

"What's 'zero'?"

"*Ze-ro.* That's how many people died on 9/11. It was all holograms!! That wasn't an act of terror—it was a *bank robbery*! That's what a bank robbery looks like in 2001."

Lisa picks up her syringe, extracts about 4 cc's of her own blood, and she holds the blood up to the window of her Jeep, to the black trees and the gray sky, contemplating—I don't know what..the beginning of life, amino acids dancing with each other in some hot ocean..maybe her own blood cells and platelets and her own mortality, held there in a tiny cylinder in her hand.

- - - -

I don't know how this scene hits you—it depends on your background. You might be grossed out by the blood and the gritty portrayal of an addict's pointless comfort ritual even after months or years of abstinence from her drug of choice. I'll probably never know what this scene means to you, but I can tell you what it means to me.

These last five minutes of conversation between me and the woman who birthed me were maybe the closest five minutes we ever spent together. They're certainly the closest we ever got to that American ideal of what a family should be. While they were happening, I was having visions of Alistair Cooke hosting *Masterpiece Theatre* with me and Lisa sitting on the floor in front of the fireplace—me smoking pot, her shooting meth—and us both listening to that elegant man and the stories he introduced. The story for tonight would be *Our Town* by Thornton Wilder. By the time the residents of Grover's Corners had resolved their questions about life, mostly either by having babies or hanging themselves..and by the time dead Emily (played by me) went back to Earth to relive the most important day of her life (her twelfth birthday), which she can't handle, but which teaches her that the key to life is to live each moment fully while it's happening..and by the time she asks the Stage Manager if *anyone* is truly aware of the value of their life while they're living it..and by the time the Stage Manager authoritatively responds:

"No, Emily, no one does. The saints and the poets, maybe—they do some."

By that time, Alistair Cooke would have finished his outro and Mom and I would both be passed out on the set by fireplace—which is a real fireplace, by the way. Alistair Cooke would tap us both on the shoulder so gently and the three of us would drink hot chocolate, do drugs, and talk about plays and literature.

Yeah, in my world, watching Lisa shoot her own blood while I drive us back from our finger lakes weekend is most closely related to the *Masterpiece Theatre* scenario that I just described, than anything else I can think of.

## 88
Home to my house in Hallstead. My mom and I get out of the Jeep and stare at each other.

"Aren't you inviting me in?" she says.

"Why," I say.

"To say hi to the boys! I wanna meet your babysitter!"

"I already told you!! I can't *afford* a babysitter. The boys are at Ashley's. *Remember?*—I told you that when we left."

"I don't remember that."

"Well check your transcript 'cause I already told you. We had a whole conversation about it."

"You trust *Ashley* with your boys?"

"Absolutely. Yes. I trust her completely."

"No you do not, Davina, don't even lie."

"I trust her enough to take care of my kids for a few days. I'm sure they're fine."

Lisa closes herself inside the Jeep and lowers her window.

"Call Ashley right away—make sure those boys made it through alright."

"Mom, made it through *what*? Don't worry about my boys."

"We should do this every year," my mom says, all smiley, as if we grew up together in some house with a huge yard and every fall she would rake the leaves into a big pile and I would run and jump in them.

"Well. Maybe," I say.

"You're a good girl, Davina—you turned out better than any of my other kids—or am I just lying 'cause you're standing right here??!! Ha ha ha ha ha!!"

She backs out of my driveway and heads up the hill.

What she said might have phased me in my younger years, but I had developed such a shell around myself from her, I hardly ever listened to what she said. I'm like a character from *Final Fantasy*: I got defense potions, regeneration spells, I've even got *gods* who come down from the heavens and destroy my enemies for me.

As Lisa drove away I just said, "Bitch."

My mom called that afternoon and much of what she said was unintelligible to me, but from a few scattered words that I did hear clearly, I got the idea that she thought that we had a really good time.

And you know what?—good for her if she thought that.

- - - -

Personally, I had been waiting for that trip to be over from the minute it began—I mean before we ever left the driveway. When my mom picked me up she saw my bag and said:

"Is that *it*?"

"Is what *what*?"

"You know we're not coming back here until Tuesday morning."

"Yeah, I know."

"Well do you have enough *clothes*??"

"Mom, I have clothes galore! I know how to pack for a trip."

"Well, if you say so, but don't go asking to use my underwear when you run out."

"Psycho! Mom! I won't be asking to borrow your *underwear*!"

"I just worry, is all."

"Well don't. I have what I need."

"I pray for you sometimes, Davina—that you get your head on straight."

I threw my bag in the back of that woman's Jeep.

I sat in the passenger seat.

"LET'S *GO*, MOM!!"

"Aren't you going to say bye to your boys?"

"They're at Ashley's."

My mom smirked.

"What?" I said.

"They're *your* kids," she says.

"That's right and I've got it covered now can we please *go*!"

Anyway much of our trip consisted of this kind of inane conversation wherein my mother fusses *infinitely* about something I've done wrong (in her mind). And it wasn't just me—she did this with people she didn't even know.

- - - -

The last night of our camping trip, something happened that made me decide that Lisa was not someone I wanted to have a relationship with. She had been bragging about how she had saved enough money to take us both out to a nice restaurant. And when the last day of our trip rolled around, Lisa does in fact take us to a restaurant. It's not a "nice" restaurant—it's a diner.

"Look baby, I'm sorry it's not some rootin'-tootin' forty-dollar-a-plate kind of restaurant but it's the best that I can afford right now. But I got checks comin' in—I'll take you to a real nice restaurant some day."

"It's fine, Mom. I *love* diners."

(Which is true.)

"Oh, Davina, I'm glad you feel that way. I'm buying more than usual—"

"You're buying more meth than usual?"

"What? *No.* That fucked-up drug? I told you I'm clean—this is just stuff from the shopping channel. Meth, god. I'm sorry I ever made you do lines of that shit when you was pregnant and we were walking to California. It's one of the things I hate about myself the most. But I hated to see my daughter so hungry, and I thought: maybe a line or two of crystal is just what this girl needs."

As my mom has been talking, a hostess wanders up to the hostess station. She hears all this my mom is saying about meth, she waits politely for my mom to stop talking, and she says, "Is it just the two of you?"

"Yes," I say.

"Table or booth?"

"Booth, please. The most secluded booth you have."

"Absolutely," she says, and this teenager does lead us to a booth that is actually secluded. It's one of those mondo booths for like eight people but the hostess uses it on us because she can tell that we're in the middle of some fucked-up heart-to-heart discussion.

She says our server will be right with us. As she leaves, I check out her body. And I think of mine. Everything about her is still tight and perky and innocent of the world—I'm droopy and sloppy and my pussy isn't pretty anymore. If I could give one piece of advice to all the young people of the world, it's this: *Fuck early, fuck often.* Sure, there'll be things about sex that you won't discover until your thirties and forties, but ain't nothing like young sex—*trust* me on that one. So get your little text message on, get your little Twitter. Girls get your douche on and boys wash behind that foreskin. And eat your little butts out—everybody wants their ass licked but nobody wants to make the first move.

The hostess is long gone and I realize I am sitting in a booth reserved for eight. I can't remember exactly which state we're in—which is somehow comforting—and as I come back to reality, my mom's voice fades in. She is going off on this complete *diatribe* about Donald Trump. Not just the hair, either: the investments, the wives, the reality show, the two-hundred-million-dollar inheritance from his father—everything.

I kept my mouth shut and acted like I was listening. Really I was glancing around the diner, checking out the people and making up stories to go with them. There were truckers—who I was always comfortable with. There were couples who according to their dress seemed like they were on the exact type of trip as me and Lisa. That morning Lisa and I went on a hike—we looked at lakes and waterfalls. And now we're sitting across from each other at the diner, mother and daughter, with absolutely nothing to say to each other.

- - - -

A guy comes in and he looks like he might be homeless—dirty clothes, a backpack that looked like it had once been purchased at a military surplus store but had seen many miles and a few years since then. The hostess sat him behind me.

My mom made faces—disgusted faces, full of disdain—like *she's so* offended at this homeless guy. She raises her arm to try to get someone's attention. I imagine her trying to make him leave. No one saw her hand, but those looks my mother was leveling at this guy pinched the places in my mind that kept copies of all the dirty, hateful, spiteful, threatening, unloving looks that she had given me since I was a kid right up to this day. You know how some people are expert at masonry or chess or being a pastor or catching snakes or something?—well, my mother's specialty was making people feel bad, by any means necessary. She was throwing faces at this possibly homeless man who was seated behind me and my only hope was that he was reading the menu and didn't happen to see my mom.

I was burning. Those looks were like everything that I ever felt from Lisa—nothing kind. I thought: *Why are* you so *much better than this person who's in the same establishment as you, ordering the same food from the same menu.* She hated poor people, even though we were always only one step away from homelessness ourselves. Even when I was younger she always wanted me to join her in her mockery of crippled people, downcast people, etc.

"Homeless people have to eat, too," I said in a whisper.

"But not here," my mom says. "They should have a trough out back and if they wash enough dishes they get to stick their head in the trough and come up with a banana or an ear of corn or something—maybe even a potato!"

"Could you please quiet down?" I said.

"Why? So that homeless man behind you can eat here in peace. If this wasn't our last night here, I'd lodge a complaint with the owner and find another place to eat."

I was so embarrassed. At the volume Lisa was speaking, the guy behind us could clearly hear her. I turned around and said:

"I'm sorry about my mom."

This guy, once you looked past the dirt..I mean the only way to say it is he looked like Jesus or Kurt Cobain or something—he had some kind of divinity or enlightenment that shows itself in the eyes.

I sat back down and faced my mother.

"What did you tell him?"

"I told him the steak was good here."

"Davina, you've got to stop talking to people of that stature."

I pointed at my mom.

"That man back there *towers* over both of us in stature."

"You are such a *fantastic* dreamer, Davina—I think you'll end up daydreaming your whole life away."

"Well my daydreams are sure as shit better than this place."

"I think you need a career," my mom says.

"Like what?"

"I don't know, like hairdressing or an office assistant. But you can't keep living on disability, you and Phillip and Zach."

"Why not?"

"So you've decided to be a moocher till you die."

"Mom, Phil has Asperger's—"

"We didn't have so many disorders when I was a child. You were told to shut up and do your work. And if you didn't they'd spank you—at my school it was a wooden paddle with a handle and the whole thing was splinters. This wasn't *polished* wood—you'd be working those wood fragments out of your ass for *weeks*. Sometimes the principal lifted girls' skirts up and when he spanked you like that you were bound to get some of that stray wood up your cunt."

"The principal really made you pull up your dress?"

"Yes ma'am—it was like a military inspection."

"Did he ever make girls pull down their panties."

"Davina, we lived in a different world back then. Children didn't have rights. You couldn't divorce your parents in a court of law and you definitely couldn't sue your parents for being bad parents and end up taking home half their assets. Anyway I don't want you to end up like Mr. Homeless over there."

"Stop talking about him."

"You kids just don't have the work ethic we had pounded into us as children."

"Oh yeah, so *your* work ethic is sucking enough dicks to buy meth and not get us kicked out of whatever motel we were living in at the time? How do you think that made *me* feel?"

"Davina, I don't want this to turn into amateur hour for budding psychiatrists, so please shut your mouth and let's wait for our food."

I drank my fucking coffee.

- - - -

My mom went straight back to looking at the homeless man behind me. She made those horrible disgusted faces again and let out exasperated sighs. Then she said at full volume, directed to the homeless guy:

"*Why don't you get a job?!*"

"MOM!! *Shut. Up!* What job have *you* ever had except sucking cocks? Seriously. Reach into the past, imagine yourself at any age, and try to remember a time in your life that didn't consist of swallowing cum for a couple of bucks."

"I don't want to eat at this restaurant—let's go somewhere else."

"We already ordered our food."

"Yeah but I don't think I'll be comfortable eating while I'm looking at someone *from the streets* sharing the same air as us. I doubt I'll be able to swallow with *him* in my sight. Fuck. I mean..get yourself a *j-o-b*, even if it *is* sucking cocks—what's wrong with sucking cocks? Davina, am I to believe that you lived your whole life up to this exact moment and you never sucked a man's cock to get him to do what you wanted him to do?"

"You disgust me," I said.

"Oh, so you're better than me? My own daughter is better than me! You'd rather have dinner with Mr. Homeless than me? Go on over, then—I'll eat by myself. It doesn't matter if you find a stinking, gross parasite of a human being, living off government programs—!"

"Mom! We've been living off government programs *our entire lives*."

"He still stinks, though—you can't deny me that."

- - - -

That night, at that diner, probably wasn't the worst offense of hers I ever saw, but that was what did it for me. She went from being a person I could barely tolerate to being a person that I didn't want to know at all. I decided that night to stop talking to her. She's so full of lies I can't stand it.

That Lisa—of all people—would sit there just pummeling this homeless man, this drifter, with insults that probably only her meth-fucked brain could fully understand..it made me feel sick to my stomach. All the words she shot at him were shot at me, too—everything she said about him reminded me of everything she had always hated about me. I was flooded with memories of all the filthy looks she had given me over a lifetime. And these faces she gave me for whatever I did wrong, always came as bad surprises. She was always there—like she was tracking me, carrying this stack of devil masks—and whenever I fucked something up she picked the most appropriate of these masks and that's what she became. Her faces got so deep inside my body that my bones would nearly break when she looked at me with one of those faces. And when one of those devil masks she wore on her own skin..when one of those was seen with my eyes and understood by my brain, it left in me an indelible mark of fear.

And that fear is such that it will never go away with therapy, it will never go away with medicine, it will never go away with self-harm, drinking, drugs—it will only go away when I die, and my deepest wish is that I didn't pass that evil along to my children as my mother passed it along to me.

## 89
I didn't excommunicate her right away. After the camping trip, Mom did come over a few times to see the kids. One time she brought my nieces over to visit. She wanted the girls to see Ashley. But Ashley came back later than when she said she would and—in true form—she was falling down drunk. How she even got home, I could hardly imagine—she fumbled with her keys, trying to let herself in. After a few minutes of this, I got up and let her in.

She said, "Fucking door shouldn't even be locked in the first place."

Then she fell on her face. She didn't trip on anything—she just used up her last conscious step and fell forward. Fortunately her face landed on my feet or else she might have had a concussion. She needed my help to stand. This whole event was awesome for my mom to see—another example of why I'm a horrible parent. I tried not to look at my mom's face, but in my periphery I could see her disapproval. I felt that Lisa would someday use this event against Ashley or me. My anxiety was off the charts and a little timer in my brain started right when Ashley fell and it was going to keep running until my mom brought up *The Night When Ashley Came Home Falling Down Drunk*. This event didn't end when I helped Ashley upstairs to her bedroom and put out a vomit bucket and a glass of water—no, no, and a resounding *no*. This event would last until my mom said her last word about what a horrible mother I was and what a drunk my daughter was—and waiting for Lisa to say her last word on something like this could literally take *years*.

- - - -

I didn't invite Mom to come over after that—like, ever. Mom kept texting for a while, saying: *I don't know what I did wrong.* She brought up our camping trip: *I took you camping and now you won't talk to me.* My mom *has no clue* why I stopped talking to her..I haven't told her. The confrontation isn't worth it—I don't care enough about my mom to go through that with her. Where she sees a stinky homeless man, I see a person—a whole human person—that the mechanism has failed. To me, a homeless person reflects the evil in the smiling faces and handshaking and baby kissing and finally the deeds of every corrupt politician and so-called "leader" on this planet. When my mom made disdainful faces to that drifter we saw on the last night of our camping trip, that was the day I broke up with my mother. I decided to quietly shut her out of my life.

She's never been good to me. She's never been my *mother*—you know, like caring about your kids and shit. And I've never really cared about her.

*At this point in the interview Davina is sobbing. And I admit to myself that I am no longer a detached interviewer—I care about the subject and how her life will go. Davina continues.*

Am I even going to cry when my mom dies?—am I even going to care? I feel like Lisa's getting everything she deserves as far as her kids are concerned—the last I heard none of her kids were talking to her.

We all remember how she mistreated and neglected us. And even after the camping trip I was stupid enough to let her abuse me via text messaging and the occasional phone call. Lisa said things like: "Don't blame your feelings on the past. Get over it." She also said: "Grow up. Stop acting like a child." Those are things that Lisa says when I try to be brave and explain to her why I no longer invite her to be part of *my* family.

The really *sick* part of this is that Mom never admits to anything bad she did to us in childhood. Anything I bring up, Mom says, "I don't remember that," or, "It's not as bad as you remember." Then I worry that maybe I'm just a complainer—everyone has a hard life, right?—why should she or you or anyone care about mine? All I know is when my mom selectively remembers the trauma that was inflicted on me as a child, it's crazy-making—it literally makes me feel that I belong in a nuthouse. What if it didn't happen the way I remember it? What if my brain is flawed and I was never even abused as a child? I feel half crazy most of the time, but mostly I feel crazy after talking to my mom.

- - - -

It really hurts my feelings that Mom took me to an Olive Garden to say she's sorry about things that have tormented me my whole life..these intensely personal things..and she chooses to talk to me about them in a public place with people talking and laughing and waiters and waitresses coming up to your table to offer you another terrible portion of their bottomless salad and breadsticks gimmick. To me it just shows how truly little my mom cares for me. That was a conversation that should have been had on a bridge standing over a meandering brook—that sort of thing. Instead I've got some random waitress talking in one ear about this glorious Zinfandel that would go wonderfully with my entrée when I don't remember what entrée I ordered..and I've got Lisa talking in my other ear trying to convince me that Jessie never did no wrong.

Mom loves the Olive Garden. This is because some part of her internal compass is broken—she can't tell a bad restaurant from a good one. And in that same compass, another part is broken—and unfortunately for me it's the part that makes you want to keep your kids safe, protect them from harm, never let anything bad happen to them. To her, this trip to the stupidest Italian restaurant I know, was 80% her cramming breadsticks in her mouth and 20% her trying to have a conversation that—if she had treated me better and kept track of her boyfriend's cock—never would have needed to happen in the first place.

The Olive Garden—really? I would rather she pull off on the side of a road to have our little heart to heart. It's like parents who take their children to McDonald's for their birthday—that's got to be one of the most hollow experiences a kid can have. Here's the text from their website:

> *You bring the kids, we'll make the party. Super-fun for the kids, stress-free for you! We've got this party thing down to a science: Happy Meals, cake, decorations, party favors. Talk to your local McDonald's manager to make arrangements.*  

Your parents don't even have to buy a cake—that shit is all included! Mom stands over a table of greedy children and attempts to cut the generic birthday cake with a loosely-held contraption of plastic utensils—Dad sits on the sidelines of the McDonald's PlayPlace drinking gin out of a disposable cup.

That's not just an example—I've endured my share of McDonald's birthday parties. The lucky ones went to Chuck E. Cheese's, but my family was never that lucky.

- - - -

I have always felt, since the earliest times I can remember *anything*, that my mom doesn't like me. And so from an early age I always asked myself the question: *Why should I like her?* I feel that the only reason Lisa wants to hang around with me and talk to me now is she wants someone to take care of her when she gets older. None of us kids like her. I think she's at that age where you start thinking seriously about death—you imagine yourself at hospice in some room where hundreds have died before you, in a bed where you'll be the next to die. But I see my mother's room, and it's empty because no one wants to visit her—especially us kids, because we're the ones she harmed the most. You can't take care of kids when you're a drug addict—you can't even take care of yourself.

And when you grow up hungry because your mom spent it all on meth and there's nothing left for food, *that's* something you'll remember your entire life. *That's* something that'll make you never want to see your mother's face again—even on her death bed.

I know this is cruel, but I want that kind of death for her: a lonely death, where none of us kids and even Jessie isn't there while she lies in some eighty-year-old bed..no one to comfort her except maybe a nurse that Lisa cussed at for the duration of their relationship..but not today, on the day when Lisa feels it—like each one of us can feel it—the day you're going to die. Sincerely I hope that Lisa is looking at some industrial green wallpaper that's peeling away to show what is behind the sickness of that metaphorical color. There is her human sickness, carried down from family to family—and passed on to me—behind that is sheetrock and behind that are bricks and bricks and bricks, and behind that are crumbling stones older than any person on the planet.

My mom will be staring at those ancient crumbling stones and they will be turning to sand in time-lapse, fast motion—*game over*. Behind the wall are oceans she will never see again. Maybe she never really saw them when she was alive.

And the nurse will hear my mom's last exhale, for which there will never be a matching inhale.

And her nurse will note the time of death.

- - - -

You might think I'm cold for spouting off fantasies of my mother's death—if you do, it's probably because you had a better mother, a mother who showed love: who picked you up and swung you around and didn't hang with child molesters..shit like that. As I've told you before, my mother and I never made that bond—I wasn't a child to her..more like a puppy she liked to kick.

She would let me off at the front of a business plaza where everyone was busy designing unmanned drones and guidance systems for nuclear bombs—shit like that—and she sends me in to wander the cubicle farms asking people if they had a *phone* my mom could use to make her super-secret drug deals. Of course none of these people were going to let us use their phone—they just called security and guards drag me off the premises.

"This isn't your fucking playground, kid."

That's what one of the guards said to me when they dumped me in the parking lot. Even though I was a kid then, I still remember his exact words:

*"This isn't your fucking playground, kid."*

Then the other guy said, "What happened to your shoes?"

I gave them the finger without looking back.

"No, seriously—hey, girl! You'll burn your feet on the blacktop. We can give you a ride if you need it."

But I said: *No, fuck them. Fuck them and their whole paramilitary ego.*

So I walked barefoot on burning hot asphalt to where my mom had parked at the *very* back of the parking lot. I walked on painted lines and parking bumpers when I could to keep my feet from blistering but they blistered anyway—that afternoon I was poking holes and squeezing pus out of the larger ones.

I try not to dwell on that stuff but my mom was in such a hurry to get a phone line that she stopped me when I was slipping on my sandals:

"Davina! Go! We don't have time for this!"

So I step out of the car and run to the nearest office building. Once I'm there, my feet cool off. I tell the security guards that I'm Hamilton Hamilton's daughter and I needed to take his pills to him or else his heart will explode. The "Hamilton Hamilton" bit I threw in to see how fucking dumb they were—and these two geniuses failed my little test. Hamilton Hamilton? You really think someone who works here goes by the name of Hamilton Hamilton? I was laughing my ass off when they were out of range.

But then when I got kicked out of the office complex, my mom is nowhere to be seen. At first I thought she left me—which didn't surprise me because it seemed like something she would do. Then I saw her car—waaaaaay the fuck out in the outskirts of the parking lot.

Was she scared to park closer because she was afraid of getting caught by the police? Did she simply prefer parking under the cover of trees instead of out in the open sun? It wasn't this particular difficulty that got me—it was that this sort of thing happened all the time: she'd do what was convenient for her and show little or no concern for me.

She never made me feel like she loved me. Never. I never felt cared about. I never felt protected. I never felt *liked* by her—let alone loved or nurtured.

- - - -

My therapist once suggested that the way my mother treated me is the reason I have felt so alone my entire life. When she first said that, I denied it. I suggested things that *I* had done, assuming all the fault was mine. But by the time my next session came around, I told my therapist I had thought about what she said and that I agreed with her, that my decades and decades of aloneness *had* come from my mom and her treatment of me.

"How does that make you feel?" she asked.

My blood turned black and my fists clenched and my lips tightened so I could hardly speak. It was one of the moments in my life where I could hardly hold onto the image of myself. I could have lost my entire personality right then—become a maniac, hospital ward, permanent street person. I was the product of parents who didn't give a fuck about me.

"Davina, what are you thinking?"

"That my mother never loved me."

"What else are you thinking."

"That my father never loved me."

I was crying, and seething, and embarrassed to tell a stranger these things.

My therapist got me a box of tissues. I was convulsing as I cried—my whole body was crying out some grease, some bile, that had been present in my life since the day it began.

My therapist waited till just the right time to say:

"What feeling are you feeling right now."

I looked that bitch in the face and the words were boiling and burning when they came out.

I said, "I..feel..*anger*."

- - - -

How can you be friends with someone who molested your daughter on a daily basis for years at a time? And drink beers with them and play pool with them—why is that cool? Why doesn't she care about my feelings?

*Davina is crying on the phone at this point in the interview. I ask her if she wants to stop but she insists we continue. We've done so much work on the project that to stop now would be unacceptable. Davina continues.*

About once a day I ask myself if I forgive my mom—but mostly I don't care—if someone asked me if I forgive her, I would say no. I spend so much time wondering about my mom's childhood: if her mom was mean to her and that's why Lisa turned out so selfish and unloving. I want to understand my family history so I can get to a place of less anguish. It's hard enough to go through your life thinking that your mom doesn't love you. It's harder still to think that she did things deliberately to hurt you.

When I think about my mom and I think about Jessie, I am madder at my mom than I am at Jessie because my mom let all that stuff happen. Jessie couldn't help himself, but my mom could have. She could have stopped it instantly—but she didn't do a thing. She just let it happen over and over and over. Sometimes I think that it happened on purpose—that Lisa knew about it and let it happen because she didn't want Jessie to leave.

I think part of it, too, is that my mom was so damn beautiful—she was always the center of attention because of her beauty. When she gave birth to me, all the attention shifted from her..to me. She was jealous of me, and it couldn't have helped that her husband obsessed over me—I mean, that *had* to be difficult.

- - - -

Sometimes I go back and think of all the things that have happened to me until this moment. It's a mishmash, and I think of these things mostly in winter, looking out the frosty window from my recliner. These will be days when we've run out of heating oil and the only things I feel like doing are hiding under covers in my bed or covering myself up all cozy in my favorite chair.

Then I just think.

I think: I haven't had a boyfriend in the eight years I've lived in this house. Gary—the guy who went into a coma and died—was the last relationship I've had. When you love someone, and they die in front of your face, they say it can take two turns of the calendar to fully get over it. Well I must be some kind of a freak because it took me a lot more than two years to get over Gary. I've had some sexual relationships here and there—but then came a dry spell: I've gone the last five years without having sex.

Some days Christopher and I spend the day at the park and we go though the cemetery and we visit Gary's grave.

*What's it like for you at Gary’s grave?*

It's ok. Kind of sad. He seems forgotten.

*When asked how she is feeling at this point in the interview, Davina says she is feeling really sad.*

It's so deep, you know—it goes so far back. That's why it's worse for me about Lisa than it is about Jessie. I feel like there's something really missing in me that will always be missing: to not be loved by your parents..at least one of them..at least your mom.

And even Lisa was handsy with me, as far back as I remember. She one-hundred percent denies handling me inappropriately or touching me when and where she shouldn't have. It makes me crazy, though, when everything I remember, both my parents deny. Imagine growing up with that happening every day: you remember something that happened (you're still a child now), and when you tell the story as you remember it, all the adults crack up laughing and tell you that what you remember never even happened. When you're a child, you trust your parents to protect you—and you trust them not to lie. What happens when your parents lie about something you know to be true is that the boundary your mind maintains between fact and fiction..gets a little blurry. Or maybe it gets a lot blurry. Either way it fucks you for life, because your mismatched brain is always telling you to doubt that things are true. Even when it's something like *the sky is blue*, you have your doubts. Someone is always trying to trick me, or confuse me, or hurt me by lying about what's really gone down.

Sanity and certainty is where most of the world lives—they are confident about the validity of their facts and beliefs. That is not the world I live in. My world—of insanity and delusion—means I have questioned the canon of so-called facts and have come up with alternatives that sometimes *I* don't even believe in.

You might find this idea romantic, but believe me it is not—wanting to kill yourself, or in fact wanting to hurt yourself in any way, a complete lack of self-love and self-esteem, lack of a solid childhood history with parents who loved and cared for me. Etc. Etc. You know the rest.

And if you don't mind, I'd like to ask you a favor, for the betterment of the world. And that favor is this: if you have children, don't ever lie to them—if you do, they will likely turn out psychotic..like me :)

## 90
For a while it was just me Phillip and Zach.

One time over dinner, Phillip stabbed Zach in the leg with a steak knife. (Zach had to have a couple of stitches.)

It was always a constant vigil with Phillip. You watch and you teach and you take away household items that Phillip had ingeniously turned into weapons. I'm proud of Phil—always have been. And I'm impressed by his smarts. From a very young age he was repurposing yard tools into deadly weapons. When the zombie apocalypse comes, Phillip will be one of the last humans left alive.

The Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, by whatever fucked-up, junkyard flowchart some intern was following when she made Phillip's school decision, had Phil going to a school where all the kids were mentally challenged. Phillip didn't fit in there. They had a padded room in each classroom and sometimes students had timeouts in the padded room so they wouldn't hurt themselves or others. That school was below Phillip's mentality. They only put him there because of the physical nature of his outbursts—they had people on staff trained to handle that sort of thing. He stopped going there because I took him off his medication in favor of homeopathic remedies. All the teachers and the principal and even the pathetically-trained school nurse got mad at me and said I wasn't allowed to take Phillip off his medication. I didn't give a fuck *what* they said—we were sticking with homeopathics.

Phillip's school called one day—"He's being bad," they said.

"What's he doing?"

"He's looking out the window."

"So?"

"He's going to the window and then he's coming back to his desk and he's drawing pictures of the kids outside."

"I'm still here," I said. "And I'm still waiting to hear what my son is doing that is, 'bad'—as you say."

"Do you remember the conversation we all had at the school last week, about Phillip going off his medicine?"

"That was less like a conversation and more like a lynching, wouldn't you say?"

Silence on the line.

"Do I need to come up there and get my son?"

"Well it's reading time and Phillip is drawing instead."

"Who gives a shit? Is he disturbing the reading group?"

"It's not about that—"

"I'll take that as a resounding, idiotic 'no' from you, ma'am. So the only one he's bothering is you. What's your name?"

"Davina, we only met last week—how is it that you could forget my name so quickly? Do you use drugs in the home your child lives in? Because we find almost universally that a child who is using drugs doesn't have much chance to succeed whether it be kindergarten, high school, or entering the workforce."

"Ok. You obviously didn't hear me when I asked you a question a minute ago. So..I am going to repeat it to you very slowly so that you might *hear* it: What is your full name you psycho fucking *bitch*? Do you have a fungus or bacteria or maybe a small poisonous snake rooting around the tangle of your brain? Why did you *call* me today to tell me that my son—who is not mentally challenged, by the way, but is likely on the autism spectrum—why did you call me to tell me that my son is *drawing a picture* during all-hallowed reading time?"

"The problem though is that his pictures are anatomically correct."

"Why is that a problem?"

"Well, Miss Casillas, it could disturb the other children."

"Is it disturbing them now?"

"Not yet--"

"So the only person it's bothering is *you*?"

"Right at this moment—"

"Don't borrow trouble," I say. "You're worried about something that hasn't even happened. But by worrying about it, you're putting the radar smack dab in the middle of my kid's drawing. The only way any of those kids are even going to *think* about that picture is if *you* make a big deal out of it."

"In any case I'd like you to come down to the school. Some of the other teachers and I would like to speak to you about the environment Phillip is living in and your unilateral choice to stop his medication."

"Wow: 'unilateral'—I'm gonna have to bring along a dictionary so I understand what you geniuses are saying to me. They must have *doubled* the standard pay for a school teacher to attract so many geniuses like yourself. Do you find it much easier now to live off the twenty-eight thou' a year compared to when you were making fourteen k, scrappin' it, eating off the student lunch trays as the conveyor took them back to the kitchen? I bet you know what it's like to eat corn..out of a can, no microwave, no bowl—just you and your corn and your imaginary bottle of chardonnay and your imaginary friend to eat with. He or she will give you a forehead rub and then eat out your pussy. That's a nice way to go to sleep—having your pussy eaten out. But I can tell that hasn't happened to you *in years*—I can tell by listening to your voice. All you've got to look forward to is your job: a bunch of grade schoolers who hate geography even more than they hate your face. Stings a little, doesn't it, when a class of third graders have made up a special song to sing on those unfortunate days when you wore the same dress two days in a row?"

"Miss Casillas, please—"

"Or that time you wore the same dress *three* days in a row and by the time you taught that class late in the afternoon, they had *taken their lunch hour* to make up a third verse."

"Please, Miss Casillas, please. I will tell you my name if you stop saying those things."

"I know your name, bitch—Miss A. McMurphy. I'm coming up there right now to talk to your gang about how fucked up it is for my son to draw an *anatomically-correct* picture of some kids on a playground while he's actually supposed to be in the reading circle reading some Richard Scarry book that's way below his level. But, Miss A. McMurphy, listen close to what I'm gonna tell you now: if you ever call my house about Phillip doing something 'bad' and it's not capital murder, or if you *ever* discourage his artistic creativity *in whatever forms it comes in*..I will come to your house."

"What does that mean, you'll come to my house?"

"What do you *think* it means, bitch?" (I added that extra "bitch" on there for emphasis.)

"You don't need to come to the school now. I think Phillip's fine."

"Oh, I'm coming to the school, Miss *A.* McMurphy. You better have your posse gathered up 'cause I'll be there in ten minutes. And if you *ever* treat Phillip unfairly because of his autism, I will straighten you *out*. *That's* my little baby wolf—*I'm* the big ol' mama wolf. And what happens when someone messes around with that little baby wolf?"

Silence.

"You can answer."

"Themommawolfgetsmad."

She said it so fast it all blurred together into one word.

"*How* mad does the momma wolf get?"

"Very?"

"She gets *so* mad that for one second the laws of the universe recuse themselves, as do the laws of government and even the laws of *god*—and for just that *one* second that momma wolf is allowed to do *anything* to retrieve her little baby wolf."

I heard the schoolmarm disconnect. Either she was calling the police on me or running to the bathroom to pull off the pee panties she'd been wearing when I slapped a poetic death threat on that bitch. I bet they were soaked through and through—'specially when I told that bitch I'd come to her house.

- - - -

I did her one better: I went to the school. I was tear-assing through backwoods Pennsylvania on all these dirt roads—this was the locals' secret paths that would get you to anywhere you wanted to go.

I marched into the principal's office where a ton of teachers were standing in little sub-groups, doubtless talking about what to do with Phillip.

"Where's my son?"

"He's safe. He's with Miss A. McMurphy."

"Well I just spoke to Miss *A.* McMurphy and I'm not convinced she's giving my child the guidance and care that I expect when I send a child away to school."

"What do you want us to do?"

"I want you to have Miss McMurphy bring my child into this room."

"No problem—Nancy!"

Nancy was on it. Over the PA system came Nancy's garbled voice:

"Miss McMurphy to the main office, Miss McMurphy to the main office."

There was sort of a sing-songy way she said it. Then she added:

"Please bring Phillip Rogan, Please bring Phillip Rogan."

When Phillip came into the principal's office he ran over to me and hugged me tight.

"Did they do anything to you?"

"They took away my pictures."

"Who did?"

Phillip pointed toward Miss A. McMurphy.

I looked at that woman and immediately I could tell she hadn't been fucked in a long time—believe me, I knew the feeling.

"Miss A. McMurphy, I'm just curious: what does the 'A' stand for?"

"Anne."

"What a beautiful name, Miss Anne McMurphy."

"Thank you Miss Casillas."

"You're certainly welcome. Now..Anne..will you do me a favor and bring me all of Phillip's drawings from today?"

"Do you want them in a box, or an envelope, or.."

"An envelope is fine."

Miss Anne leaves the office and scurries her way down the hallway.

Then Grunt speaks up. Grunt is the school principal. Her real name is *Something* Grummand, but I prefer Grunt for her. Mrs. Grunt says:

"Should we interpret this as you taking Phillip out of school?"

"No," I say. "You should interpret this as a mother who knows the importance of encouraging a creative child. When Phillip and I get home we're going to get out a roll of masking tape and we're gonna have ourselves a little party—an *art* party where we hang our children's art on the refrigerator. And when we run out of space on the refrigerator, we'll cover the walls. Anything less is child abuse."

"So if I don't have any of my child's art on the refrigerator, *that's* child abuse?" said the Grunt.

And I said back: "What you do in your own home with *your* kid's artwork is up to you. That's none of my business. What *is* my business is when one of your teachers discourages *my* child from doing a creative thing."

"Miss Casillas, his 'art' had *penises* and *vaginas* in them."

"So?"

"They were *realistic*, not figurative. We can't expose the other students to that kind of work."

"Let me ask you something, Grunt: can you draw a realistic penis?"

I turned and looked at all the teachers in the room.

"Can *any of you* draw a realistic penis? *Can any of you..draw..a realistic..penis!?* Who's the art teacher here?"

"We don't have one. The state took away our budget for a dedicated art teacher."

Miss Anne McMurphy came in with a thick envelope, handed it to me.

"You did all this today?"

Phillip smiles.

I hug him tight.

"So, we're done here? None of y'all is going to play the fool with my child anymore over *what he fucking draws*?"

The Grunt, that mini-Hitler bubble-gum mosquito-bite faggot with peas for eyes and fuckin' under-cooked ramen noodles in place of a brain..she says:

"Really, Miss Casillas, the drawings are not the problem."

"Then why the fuck did you get me down here?"

"Because," the Grunt says, looking around the room to see if her teachers were gonna back her up, "based on Phillip's behavior we can only conclude that you are a neglectful parent towards him."

"Oh yeah?"

I turn to all the teachers.

"How many of you think I'm *neglectful*, as a mother? Has he ever come to school without a packed lunch or money for the cafeteria? Has he ever attacked someone?"

"No, to our knowledge Phillip has never started a fight here. But he has—I don't know how to say this—"

Another teacher pipes in: "He threatens other children verbally."

"You know," I say, "Phillip and I have talked about this, and he's working on making fewer verbal threats, but look in every school in the goddamn country..look at every school in the goddamn *world*..and you will find three or four children making verbal threats. In Phillip's case, sometimes his threats are *counter*-threats—imagine how *you* would feel if every day some *moronic* kid with an IQ below one-hundred came up to you and physically and verbally *taunted* you into a fight by calling you retarded. And it's *your* responsibility too, everyone in this room, to *teach* these kids what autism is since you have autistic students learning here. How many of you even know what autism is?"

One guy raised his hand.

"*Who's neglectful now?* Yeah, you, the *only guy* with his hand up—what is it?"

"It's like that guy in *Top Gun*—Tom Cruise's sidekick."

"You must be the gym teacher."

"That's right: gym and sex ed. How did you know?"

"Don't ask. You're talking about *Rain Man*—which also has Tom Cruise in it, which is probably what got you confused. If Dustin Hoffman's character in *Rain Man* is all you know of autism, then you don't have enough knowledge to teach my son."

The Grunt steps up to the plate now.

She says, "Miss Casillas, the regular admission of psychiatric medication is part of the treatment for almost every child here. We're staffed to handle children who are *on* medication, not off."

"Yeah I respect that, Miss Grunt—"

"Why do you keep calling me *Grunt*?"

"Because I think it suits you."
