"My name is Abigail Brighton and I'd prefer you call me some variation of that. Abby. Miss Brighton. Those are all fine."

"O-k, Abby."

"Thank you. And please listen now: we can no longer welcome Phillip as a student under the circumstances."

"The circumstances of him not taking medicines that were invented less than a year ago whose side effects cannot possibly be known because there hasn't been enough time for clinical trials to take place? The circumstances of Phillip not taking psych meds whose LD50 is as low as *three times* the recommended dosage?"

"What's LD50?" one of the teachers asks.

"LD50," I say, "is the amount at which fifty percent of people who take it..die. Do you all read the label warnings and side effects of all the drugs these kids are on? I'm not about to put my kid on medicines where some of the side effects are that you are *unable to reproduce* or that it *punches holes in your liver*—"

"Well I think you're exaggerating."

"I'm not! I'm not. Look this shit up online and see how really dangerous these medicines are."

"You've been dramatic ever since you and Phillip came to us, and you are being dramatic now," says the principal. "Davina, unfortunately, we can no longer accept Phillip as a student of this academy. And I'm going to have to ask you to leave school grounds and not return."

"Listen—Grunt—you might be able to get rid of me but you can't get rid of my son."

"I thought I just did."

"Well, you didn't. Read through your own student contract—check out some of the *fine* print—your own contract says you can't terminate a student in the middle of an academic session."

The principal said, "I'll have to look at that again..refamiliarize myself with the specific language."

"You do that," I said, and I walked out of that room with Phillip beside me.

- - - -

I like all my children the same, but part of the uniqueness of my bond with Phillip is that more than my other two he *does* need special help, he *does* need more of a constant partnership with me than the other two. I love *all* my kids—don't get me wrong. But I've just spent more time with Phillip because he *does* have a disability, he *does* have special needs.

In a moment of self-doubt (and out of a willingness to try anything that might help my son's life go easier), I put Phillip on all the medicines they suggested. That didn't last long. Phillip gained tons of weight that he could never take off even after we stopped the medications. And his so-called behavioral problems didn't go away no matter what prescriptions they supplied.

I believe—and I'm setting aside as much of my natural paranoia as possible when saying this—but I believe they just wanted him and every other kid in that *school* on medicine because they want zombie students because they're easier to deal with than real life, playful, curious, smart kids.

I became adamant about that aspect of Phillip's treatment, noticing how many kids were on medication—I mean *all* of Phillip's friends were on some kind of medication. And the weight problem—it wasn't just Phillip who gained weight on these meds—all his friends put on pounds with those medications that they've never been able to lose. This is a national scandal—maybe you've heard of it?

After Phillip went to that special school for almost a full year, they let him go back to his normal school, Blue Ridge High. School administrators—including the Blue Ridge principal—met with me to talk down to me and insult my intelligence with respect to Phillip not being on medication. I argued my case like a fire-crackin' lawyer—no piece of misinformation they threw out lay unchallenged. I stated my case and made it clear that none of them knew more than me about Asperger's syndrome. I concluded by informing these dolts that Asperger's is not even something you *treat* with medication.

"*Maybe* his ADHD justifies medication—I'll look into it next time we see Phillip's doctors. But for now, why don't *you* worry about the teaching and *I'll* worry about the medication."

There was a long silence during which it sunk in with the faculty of Blue Ridge High School that *I* was calling the shots on this one.

The principal said, "Well Davina, if you think your experience raising three kids somehow trumps the centuries of collective experience that our staff has accrued—if you're unable to consider that *we* might have some valuable insight that would help Phillip have a better experience here or a better experience in life--then go ahead. You've obviously made up your mind to choose the harder path for your disabled son. And we can't stop you—legally there is no reason why Phillip cannot be re-admitted to this school."

I said directly to that woman:

"*My* kid deserves the same attention as any kid here. And if I find out that Phillip is neglected, made fun of, or in any way treated unfairly by this school I will bring a court down on this establishment faster than you can say, 'My cunt is on fire!!' I'll have you people behind bars."

"Thank you for that admonishment, Davina. You have become quite the litigator, despite your own lack of education. It is unfortunate that you don't spend more of that well of energy raising up your kids right."

I could have pummeled that bitch's head right in, make it look like a cross between a chili cheese dog and Mr. Potato Head.

But I didn't. I left her head alone. I had accomplished my mission—get Phillip into Blue Ridge High—and in my opinion there's no honor in punishing someone who just gave you exactly what you wanted.

- - - -

There's a lesson in this. I'm a movie person if you didn't figure that out by now—I have a large collection. And there's this quote from David Mamet that I love. Two guys are fighting in a jiu-jitsu class. One guy has the other guy in a headlock against a padded wall. The teacher says, "Good, good. You *know* the escape." The guy in the headlock does some crazy move where he walks up the wall and now *he's* on top, *he's* the one with the upper hand. Both men are on the floor, sweating, trying to make the other one pass out. The teacher ends the fight and says to the loser, "You just got tired. What's the lesson?" The student doesn't know. The teacher tells him, "*Don't get tired*—let the other guy get tired."

I always think of that when I'm dealing with the peon administrator of a school or a gas station or some shithole corporation. I have infinite patience for those sorts of people because there's a voice inside my head that says, "You got this, D. You've been through *hell*—what did this bitch do with her life but sit in her office on a swiveling chair doing paperwork." That's what my voice says. She says, "You can just wait this bitch out. 'Cause I will not be blown off by you and I am willing to sleep *all night in your parking lot* just so I'm the first person you see when you close the door to your yellow Mercedes-Benz *Kompressor*. First you think it's funny that I slept all night in the cold just to admit my son into your school. Then you feel sad for me that I'm poor and my kid *has* to go to this relatively shitty school—you know goddamn right that *your* kids would never have to go to this school. And finally you succumb—because, while you don't know my history of being homeless and hungry, of living with a mom who shoots meth, of *years* of incestual sexual abuse by my stepdad—you finally succumb because all those precious experiences melded together are the reason why my eyes glow gray..like a wolf..and you can tell you're dealing with a wild animal, essentially, not a normal person who is going to take piddly shit off you. The scariest thing these administrative/managerial types have ever done is try a new flavor of ice cream. Try sleeping on the streets for one night with no money—that shit'll change your life.

Phillip said, "Mom?"

"Yes?"

I looked so proudly on him.

"It's time to go. You're staring."

I took Phillip's hand.

"Just staring at the world, Phillip, as if it were *in my hand* and I could rotate it any way I want—that's how the Earth was created, I must believe. It was painted by aliens with *beautiful* locks of hair and they dipped their whole heads into parts of the universe that had more colors than ours and they spun their locks around to create the entire world—and that's why, Phillip—that's why this world is so messy and so beautiful at the same time."

"Mom, we need to go."

And I heard him—I heard Phillip say that—and I was aware that the principal and most of the teachers who were in the meeting about Phillip's admission to this school..those teachers were still there, and Phillip and I were right in the middle of the waiting area of the school's main office. I just had to say one thing to my boy, so that he might learn it decades before I did.

"Phillip. You know those people who sell those giant rugs when we go to Allentown? On the side of the road?"

"Yeah!"

"Do you remember—you were so young I don't know if you'll remember this—but do you remember when you climbed in between those metal bars that held up the rugs and you got to see the back of the tapestry?"

Phillip nods.

"What did you see?"

"A whole bunch of strings."

"Exactly. The front of the tapestry is a beauuutiful picture—but behind it, it's just a bunch of strings."

"So are you saying to look at the beautiful side of life and not let the ugly side stop you?"

"Oh no! I'm saying that *both* sides are beautiful—they have to be, because they're one and the same! I truly believe..one side is sanity and the other side is insanity. You can't have one without the other!"

"Why are you telling me this?"

"No reason—it just popped into my head! Think of it as an analogy you might be able to use in the future. Maybe on the street corner of an alien world."

"I will. But we gotta go. This crowd—"

"Is it making you nervous?"

"Yeah it is!"

"I'm sorry we stayed here too long—I'm so sorry baby."

"Don't call me baby."

"I'm sorry, Phillip. Let's go."

- - - -

We waded through the crowd of teachers. Some of them scowled or made disapproving looks. I was smiling all the way, holding my baby's hand, knowing this beating by the faculty would only be the first of many. But I also knew that most of those people were experiential lightweights—I could go round for round with them and yes, some of the blood left in the ring would be mine..but that school would be the boxer with one less tooth, lying face down in our mutual blood puddle, and I would be the one victorious.

My victory would be a very small thing to many, but to me and Phil it meant the world. He would be going to a school with no metal detectors and he would be placed in a classroom with other smart children who are there to learn, who don't bring brass knuckles and switchblades to school. That is what I hoped for, at least.

Me and Phillip got in the car and I hugged him and told him this school was going to be *so* much better and how excited I was for him and how proud I was of him and how much I loved him and always would. I'd give an arm if it would make *one day* of Phil's life better—and the same goes for my other two. I might be a person who gets tired sometimes, but one day I'll never be tired is the day somebody fucks with my kids or my kids' education—that day I'll have all the energy in the world.

"Do you wanna do special lunch day?" I asked Phil.

"Well—only if we have enough money."

"Don't worry about the money, please? I'm taking care of the money and we have enough for special lunch day. Pick any place you want."

"*Any* place?"

"Within the continental United States."

"Ok..I pick..Noble Roman's! Is that too far?"

"It's within the continental US so it's not too far."

"Thanks, Mom."

He hugged my arm.

"You're welcome."

I almost gave him the speech about my willingness to give an arm for him to have one day where no one hurt him or misunderstood him or got impatient with him because he was doing his thing in his own quite particular way.

But I didn't go into all that. I just kept my quiet—which often is the best way to keep the peace—and I drove us to the nearest Noble Roman's, in Allentown. Some Noble Roman's project old movies that you can watch and listen to while you eat. We were the only customers there. Phil picked the seats which had the best view of the movie, in his eyes. Fortunately, the movie was *La Dolce Vita*—a movie we both like. Unfortunately, there were chairs blocking the subtitles and neither me or Phil can read Italian. And, ultimately, fortunately, we had seen this movie so many times together that we started shouting out the lines we knew—in Italian. The owner was so impressed with us—his only customers—that he sent his hostess and his one and only chef down to our table with more pizzas than we ordered or could possibly eat. Even the owner came down for the end of the movie.

"You wanna watch again?" the owner asked when the movie was over.

I asked Phillip: "What do you want to do?"

"Go home," he said.

I thanked the owner for his generosity as he was packing our numerous extra pizzas using fresh boxes and everything. He treated us like the best customers he ever had.

As Phillip and I walked to the car, we saw the owner and his two employees come out the front door. The chef locked it. The hostess went to her car—the chef went to his. The owner unlocked the door of his modest Honda.

"Are you closing early today?"

It was the middle of the afternoon.

"We are closing for good."

"When?"

"Right now. You are our last customer."

"What?? You're shitting me."

"No shit. I am telling you no shit. There's a Pizza Hut, Domino's, Taco Bell, Burger King, Arby's..all within a mile of here. People don't have time to stop and watch a movie while they eat. They want fast-food drive-thru window convenience and they're on their way—sprqeeeeeee!—back on the roads, driving to their death."

"Well thanks for having your final meal with us."

Phillip threw out a thumbs up.

"You're the best, man."

"Thank you. And thank you—I couldn't have asked for a better last customer."

The owner of that now closed, glorious pizza place got in his car and left even before Phillip and I drove off.

Phillip cried and I put my hand on his leg. When Phillip cries it's either the complete arrangement with him breaking furniture, throwing iPads at the wall and breaking them, cussing at me in combinations of words that 1) I have never heard before and 2) eventually make me cry. Or you get the small arrangement, like when he's mourning Noble Roman's (just one location) closing.

It was sad for me too, so that very night we made our own pizzas and watched a VHS recording of *La Dolce Vita*.

To this day, I have as association between Fellini films and thin crust pizza. I wouldn't be surprised if Phillip had the same.

## 91
The administrators of Blue Ridge High School insisted that Phillip at least go to therapy because "his teachers weren't always able to be at Phillip's beck and call"—they were barely able to put forth the effort required to teach their "normal" children. The principal of Blue Ridge High School told me that—do you believe that shit? I almost firebombed the school when I heard that one. I had my finger on the button—I swear to fucking god. To my surprise and gratefulness, they worked with the county to get a full-time therapist who went to school with Phillip every single day, every minute, every hour this therapist was by my baby's side. If Phillip felt he was about to have an emotional outburst, the therapist would help—either they'd leave the classroom and yell it out together, or they'd take a quiet walk and the therapist would just *listen*. I know that was so good for Phillip because sometimes the choices Phillip makes when he communicates are very hard to know how to respond to. I think someone who was with him every day—someone Phillip grew to feel comfort with—was the perfect thing for him right at that age. Your mom is your mom—but there are some things you can't tell your mom. I was glad he had someone other than me to talk to. And any parent out there will understand this: you *love* your kid, but you also need a *break* from your kid. I felt guilty about leaving Ashley with other people when I went out, but by the time Phillip came along I was perfectly comfortable leaving Phil in the care of trusted adults.

We even had a mobile therapist that came to the house twice a week. They always sent really young people—for those young therapists we were just another stepping stone in their career so none of them stayed very long. It was disruptive always inviting someone new into your house. My house was a protective place and I hate having new people over—I only like family and really good friends to come over. I don't like state people types coming in and seeing all my movies and the weird trippy drawing I do with my menstrual blood. I mean they'd probably just assume it was red watercolor but my point is you just know. Someone who would be willing to work for the government..I'm sorry but they can't be trusted. Phillip and I begrudgingly did this in-home therapy thing. We had three therapists in a row—two were women, the last was a man.

This last therapist was with us for two years. He became a fixture in the family. He was really good with Phillip—better than me in some ways (and I'm ok with that). This therapist actually got Phillip out of the house—away from the TV and the cable and the video games—and into the community. Sometimes I went with them and sometimes it was just the guys. I remember Jackson (that was the therapist's name) take me and Phillip to breakfast and lunch and out to farms (Jackson called this "animal therapy") and the first time I went, I discovered it wasn't just for Phillip's benefit—to stand up on the bottom rung of wooden fences and lean over and pet a cow on its head and ears and its damp little nose..even to smell that necessity of life we call manure, enlivened me. Somedays I feel that all the people of the world need—that all we are lacking—are just different experiences. I just think: what if every now and then city folks came to the country and country folks went to the city—wouldn't we understand each other more?

So I'd think and the guys would talk about what was happening in school and Jackson would give Phillip suggestions of what to do in a hard situation—it was Jackson's spin on dialectical behavior therapy (which I had learned some of in my psych ward days).

Then the three of us would talk together about how life was going for Phillip at the house. Sometimes those talks were hard—Phillip and I sharing with Jackson the details of our fights and revealing feelings that we hadn't even shared with each other. Some days we left the farm in tears, Phillip and I hugging tightly and never wanting to let go. Some days we laughed ourselves all the way to Jackson's car and then laughed some more on the drive home.

Therapy is one of those things that everybody needs—but not everyone *knows* they need. And it gets worse: the people who need it most are the least likely to go. It's kind of like getting an oil change on your car: it's necessary maintenance that you need to keep your machine running at its highest levels. No one would make fun of you or pass you over when filling a job just because you regularly change the oil on your ride. But tell people you or your kid is in therapy, they assume you're an unfit mother and your son is the next Unabomber. I'm like—dude—if the Unabomber had gotten some counseling, maybe there wouldn't ever have *been* a Unabomber.

- - - -

Then Phillip took a knife to school.

He forgot he had it in his pocket. He just put on the same pants that he had worn the day before. Somewhere in first period he realized that he had a knife in his pocket and he asked permission to go to the main office. Once he was there, he turned it in because he thought it was the right thing to do.

And those school officials, they praised him—they praised him! *Yes, Phillip, you did the right thing! Thank you for bringing this to us! Anyone can make a mistake but you corrected yours—and willingly! What a boy!!* They kept telling him that it was the right thing to do and that he was a good boy. They sent him back to class and Phillip finished out the day.

But when the principal found out, it was a different story. She called me at home and told me that Phillip brought a knife to school and that Phillip *claimed* it was an accident—

"If Phillip claimed it was an accident, then it was," I said.

"Yes but we have to treat all our students the same," she said.

There was a musical quality to how she said this that let me know just how frickin' much she was enjoying this call.

I said, "Let me tell you what, Miss AllPowerfulPrincipalOzBehindAFakeAssCurtain, why don't you cut the crap and jump straight to the part where you punish my son for *an honest mistake* that didn't *hurt anyone*, that he *self-reported* to your staff the moment he realized he had it on him. What are we talking about, here? Suspension? Expulsion?"

I wanted to call her by name to add to the humiliation factor but by god that bitch's name escaped me.

"I'm not going to suspend your son," this bitch said. "I'm not going to expel him either."

"Good—" I started to say. I was going to thank her but that fucking bitch interrupted me. (That's her right, of course—she's "The Principal." I'm nobody—just a civilian—just the *parent* of the kid in question!!)

"I'm sending Phillip to PATH."

"Where and what the fuck is a PATH?"

"It's a continuation school. It's a special school for people like Phillip—"

"I know what a continuation school is. You don't have to explain it to me."

(A continuation school, if you don't know, is a school for thugs and punks and gangsta wannabes. Everyone at a continuation school is a drug addict—just about. You see a lot of ankle bracelets because half these kids are on their third or fourth drug offense. The only places they're allowed to go are school or home—and church of course. We can't deny deadly fuckin' menace-to-society drug dealers their right to praise fucking Jesus Christ every goddamn Sunday morning. If any of them *do* go to church it's probably just to sell speed to the ministers.)

When Phil got home, I hugged him hugely.

"You did the right thing, Phillip, you did the very most right thing that you could do in that situation and I want you to know how *proud* I am of you."

I was gripping his shoulders.

"You told the truth and—Phillip, look at me—telling the truth is sometimes a hard thing to do. Sometimes it's almost impossible to do. But—"

I looked in his eyes.

"—it is *always* the right thing to do. What you did today impressed me *so much*. I'm not even sure that I would have been as brave as you were in such a hard situation. I want you to listen to this: what you did today was right, it was honorable, it was brave. When you think about this, whenever you think back to this day and what you did, I want you to remember these words: right, honorable, brave*.*"

"They're going to send me to a continuation school, aren't they?"

"Who told you that?"

"Nobody. That's always what happens when someone brings a knife to Blue Ridge."

- - - -

We were both lost in thoughts of what might happen to Phillip at the continuation school. Phillip is gay—and though no doubt there were other gay kids at PATH, they likely had protectors they did things for in exchange for never getting beat up. I had smoked pot with Phil but never let him drink alcohol. There was sure to be alcohol at PATH, along with every other drug known to humanity. I was scared he'd do the drugs I'd tried—I was horrified he'd try the drugs I hadn't. Before my time it was ludes, then came shooting heroin and coke, then came crystal meth: snorted, smoked, injected. To someone like me, crystal meth is the best drug and the worst drug I've tried (except weed, of course—most of us don't even consider that a drug). But crystal meth: it feels *so* good, *so* fast—it knocks your sexual responses off the charts. It's an extremely sexual drug and that's part of why I like it. What's popular now is pills—prescription pills. People who need them for pain sell or trade their codeine for money or blow, cigarettes, meth, coke—there's a whole economy that starts with prescribed pain medication and food stamps sold for cash so people with no jobs can do whatever drugs they want.

Anyway I didn't want Phillip to try that stuff—that was my main concern about him going to the continuation school. I mean, I like drugs—if you've read this far you know that. I smoke weed every day and it calms me the fuck down, so I don't act even *more* like a bitch to everyone I meet than I would if I wasn't smoking pot. Basically I grew up with the drugs of the 80s and 90s—those drugs I *understand*, those drugs I know how to start and stop. Now kids do Molly, thinking they're going to get the ecstasy experience their parents had..but most Molly doesn't contain a single drop of MDMA—it's just a mishmash of synthetics that approximate the effects of ecstasy and is cheaper to make (and more dangerous to use).

Cheaper still is krokodil, a home-cooked alternative to heroin that literally eats the flesh off your skeleton. Google "krokodil" and check out the images if you want to seriously gross yourself out. Keep a barf bag near—there's nothing cool about looking at a man who's had the skin and muscle melted off half his face and you can see his exposed cheekbone, jawbone with no muscle, no cheek skin (as in: *no cheek—it's missing!*), bare dry teeth, no gums, an eye without a socket—I mean, any sensible government who realized its citizens were doing this drug would instantly start handing out heroin for free.

Anyway they made my baby go to that continuation school with kids who were fuckin' insane maniacal drug addicts who shot up under their desks and smoked meth openly in the courtyard. Teachers didn't do anything—they were afraid they'd get shot.

- - - -

Phillip was the youngest student there.

It wasn't fair.

Every morning you had to get searched before you went inside the school and before every class you got searched again before you could enter the classroom. It was military style, with armed police on campus at all times. They used wand-style metal detectors, walk-through metal detectors, and they used the pat down. Kids are smart about what they want to be smart about, though—they might not choose to study for a math test, but for sure they'll find time to invent ways of getting a meth pipe through school security.

Every night Phillip came home from PATH, I'd make him dinner and he'd tell me all the new drug terms he learned in school that day. I'd make macaroni and cheese with real cheese and hotdogs, with minced garlic—a favorite ingredient in our house. I'd plate the food and put it on the table while Phillip quizzed me on the new vocabulary words:

"Mom, do you know what a 'skeezer' is?"

"No, what is it."

"It's a prostitute who smokes crack."

"Oh! Isn't the English language wonderful! A specific term for everything! In my day we just called her a crack whore."

"What do you mean 'in my day'—you're not old!"

"Old enough to not know what a skeezer is."

"Do you know what 'buck' means?"

"What does it mean?"

Phillip said matter-of-factly: "It means to shoot someone in the head."

"Oh, glorious. Why don't you tell me a nice one next time."

"Ok. I think I have a nice one. How about: 'strawberry.' "

"I don't know..strawberry..like cherry?..a girl who's never been fucked before..or maybe..a girl who's never been fucked in the ass..I give up. Tell me."

"It's a female who trades sex for crack or cash."

"Phillip don't ever say 'female.' It's disrespectful."

"Why is it disrespectful?"

"Because it indicates that the girl or woman you're talking about provides no value except the value provided by her body."

"I don't understand what that means."

"It means all she's good for is sex."

"Do we have time for one more?"

"Are they giving like vocabulary lessons in the courtyard led by dealers and pimps??"

"Pretty much."

"Ok, one more and then the vocabulary lesson is over."

"Hmmm..I'll find you a good one."

I could see Phillip querying the databases in his mind.

"Ok, here's one you might not know: 'thoroughbred.' "

"You're right. I don't know that one."

"It's a dealer that only sells pure narcotics."

"Yeah, I used to know people like that in Cali. See in New York, Pennsylvania, New Jersey, only the rich people sell pure—by the time that good coke reaches the parks, the 'Squares,' you're basically snorting baby powder, baking soda, whatever. It'll trip you up but not 'cause you're high—because sucking baking soda up your nose gives you a headache—instantly. Don't ever waste your money on New York cocaine unless you're in a penthouse thirty floors high."

"And California?"

"California's cool—rich people get the same drugs as the poor people. In fact, California rich people and California poor people hang out and do drugs *together*. It's much more democratic."

"What about the midwest? What about the southwest?"

"Phillip, I'm not an almanac of drug statistics for the whole effing country. Look online, the CIA or FBI prob'ly keeps that kind of data."

I saw Phillip's brain cataloging what we just said and I knew that after dinner he'd be on the internet *memorizing* every drug fact there was to know. The second day of school he knew more about drugs than all the courtyard dealers at PATH combined.

I told Phillip I'd rather talk about any topic than drugs.

I watched him eat his macaroni and cheese with hotdog slices and I knew I couldn't hold his hand and walk him to school every day.

(Metaphorically.)

I couldn't be there in the courtyard with him.

I saw when he got up from dinner he went straight for his computer and I knew if I checked his browser history in the morning it would be empty.

I thought about the conversation me and my son just had and I felt like the worst mother in the world.

Zach was sitting quietly eating slowly—that's his way. He never said anything about me and Phillip's drug conversation. Zack is my smartest kid, and—as I learned from watching him—often times the smartest person in the room is the one who doesn't say a thing.

- - - -

It took me five days but I finally got someone from the board of education to talk with me on the phone. I didn't catch his name—it was Mr. Peabody McFuckerton or something like that.

I got right into the guts of the thing, thinking that was best:

"The only thing my kid is learning about at PATH is drugs!"

"If you don't like the classes he's taking, I suggest you and your son visit Phillip's guidance counselor and disenroll him from the drug education class, if you don't want him learning about the pitfalls and perils of using street drugs."

I said, "Good use of the word, 'disenroll,' by the way. You're obviously a smart man."

He said, "Thank you."

"You're welcome. I'm a bit of a writer myself. I write poetry."

"Oh how excellent—I'd love to read some of yours sometime."

We laughed and smiled and "Oh yes, next time we're both at Phillip's school"'d each other—even though we knew damn well neither of us was going to take *ten seconds* of our precious lives to read the other's shitty poetry. Mine wasn't shitty, but I was sure his would be—and he probably thought the same of me.

"So. Mr. Peabody McShitterson, the drugs my son is learning over there *aren't* from any drug education class—"

"Then enroll him in the drug class—I hear it's quite excellent."

"They don't *have* a drug education class. I've got the class listings in my hand, in a brochure that I got from the school. It was right next to a stack of books they were giving away for free. Do you know what the title of the book was? *How to Survive and Thrive in Prison*. Do you see what's wrong with this picture?"

"With all due respect, Mrs. Casillas, half of our students don't graduate, and half of those will spend a protracted period of their lives behind bars. You might find the title of that book offensive, but given the statistics we feel that educating children for a life spent in prison is one of our foremost duties. Schools have always educated their students to prepare them for their future careers—we're doing the same thing here."

"Oh, no no no no no, Mr. PickleDick. You've got it all wrong."

"How am I wrong?"

"First of all, you're wrong that the sole purpose of *education* is to prepare for a job. An *education*—and god knows *I* never got one of these—but an *education* is learning about the *extraordinary* world we live in, and learning about the *extraordinary* people we can become if we're given encouragement and information that helps us learn how to then go into the world and teach ourselves whatever we want to know, and do whatever makes us happy—within a profession or without. Planting a book like that *How to Survive and Thrive in Prison* on a table in a school is *encouraging* students to fuck up and go to jail."

"Well, Davina—"

"Giving that book to students says: *It's ok for you to go to jail—in fact, it's expected.* Is that the message you want to send to students?"

"Davina, of course it's not—"

"Then throw away that stack of books and I want Phillip back at Blue Ridge by tomorrow."

Mr. DickFace laughed.

"Look, even if I thought Blue Ridge was the right environment for your son, there's the small problem of Phillip having brought a knife to school when he was in attendance there—that sort of thing is unacceptable at Blue Ridge High."

"Ok, let's talk unacceptable, if you want to talk unacceptable. You know what's unacceptable to *me*?—you guys putting my son in a fucking *gladiator academy*. Phillip is sensitive—he has Asperger's syndrome *and* he's an accelerated learner. He needs to be put in a mentally gifted program—not some continuation school where fucking thirteen-year-old mothers are breastfeeding their kid in one arm and shooting up meth in the other."

"I think it's not wise to exaggerate."

"When's the last time you took a visit to PATH."

"It's been a while."

"Well when you have a spare moment between votes to raise the school board salary, take a little gander over PATH and ask yourself: *Would I want* my *kid going to this school?* Hello? Mr. Peabody McFuckNugget—are you there?"

"Yes Davina I'm *here*. I'd appreciate it if you would call me by my proper name."

He told me his name again but it rolled off my brain as if my brain had been thoroughly treated with Scotchgard right before this conversation. Honestly, fuckheads like this, politicians, bureaucrats, congress..eventually they all get caught stealing money, tampering with the voting systems, killing hundreds of thousands of people in other countries just because *we make money off the war (and by* we *I mean* they*)*. This school board motherfucker was just another politician to me, just another leech getting paid more each year than I'll make my entire life. And what does he do that's useful?—*Nothing.*

"Mr. Fuckhead," I asked, "how are we gonna get this done? I need a yes..today. I need you..to check whatever box..will get Phillip back in Blue Ridge. Until I get a yes, I'm going to be calling you every day—including weekends—and we're gonna talk about what it is that is stopping my son from attending Blue Ridge."

He made a short laugh.

Then there was a long pause.

Then Mr. FuckBurrito said, "I just don't see any reason to change Phillip's school."

"What about the fact that he's an accelerated learner?"

"Davina, I'm looking at Phillip's file right now and I don't see anything about your son that would lead me to believe he's an accelerated learner..of any kind."

"Did you ever test his IQ?"

"No."

"*No!?* How can you make a statement about his smarts without giving him an IQ test??!! *Mother*fucker."

I hung up.

- - - -

I thought about calling that asshole right back and asking him how many dicks he had to suck to get his seat on the school board. But I didn't make that call.

And I'm kind of glad I didn't. Three days later Phillip was back at Blue Ridge under one stipulation which was that if he brought another weapon to school he would be expelled. I checked his pockets every morning—it was my new religion to make sure no stray knives had wandered into Phillip's pockets from the previous day's play.

Phillip and I managed to keep him weapon free for the rest of his time at Blue Ridge.

And that's the story of how we got Phillip into, and out of, then back again into the school I thought was right for him.

## 92
That was not the end of Phillip's problems with school (or my problems with Phillip for that matter).

Phillip's personal therapist got a better job—I still don't know if it was the money or that he just had enough of my crazy family—but it was probably the money. After that we didn't want another therapist—our last one had been so good there was not much chance of getting someone better. Plus it's the work involved in getting to know a new person, to trust them in your house and trust them with your children not to do something or say something that will fuck them up forever. That happened to me—you can bet your sweet goddamn ass that wasn't going to happen to my children. I did kind of a half-assed search for a behavioral health therapist in our area. I never did find one that I thought would be an actual *help* to Phil, and he and I just naturally adjusted to living without a therapist. It's been a year and a half since we had a therapist dedicated to Phil—and I'm sorry to tell you just *one more way* in which I'm a bad mother—but once we both got used to not having one, I stopped looking for one.

I wrongly thought that Phil was getting better.

I started getting phone calls from the school.

I got more and more calls from the school that I was getting one every day.

Phillip was having outbursts at school again, yelling and flipping desks over and shit. Some kind of incident in the cafeteria where Phillip throws his lunch tray over that spit guard they have and all his food was in the lunch lady's hair and streaming down her face. When the principal asked him why he did it, he said it wasn't real food and it was illegal for a school to serve poison to its students and then (he told me this later) he had to *educate* the principal on what genetically modified food is.

"She didn't even know what Monsanto was," Phillip told me later. "She didn't even know what GMO stands for."

Phillip was incredulous. He stood up from our dinner table and described the process of genetic modification of food and how, "It's not what people think."—that it's not just breeding different strains of corn together, etc.

"Phillip."

"Yeah?"

"I need you to sit down and eat your dinner. And I need you to calm down—you seem overly excited to me and I want you to *slow it down* before you get upset."

He sat down. He took a bite of food. Then he said:

"I'm already upset."

He chops pieces off his mashed potatoes and unloads them from his plate onto the dining table. His cuts and chops and throws get more violent with each one he does.

"I mean how do you know this potato isn't genetically modified? This is how they're going to control us, Mom. First it's fluoride in the water—Hitler used that as *mein kampf*, Mom!—they've got modified humans spreading the plague in El Salvador!!"

"How do you know that?"

"YouTube, Mom."

I felt like this was some sort of directive to be less old.

"How can you *not* be upset about this stuff, Mom?"

"Baby, I am. I am upset."

"You don't look upset to me."

"Phillip, that's because I'm older than you and I've seen more horrible things than you have and I don't believe there is anything I can do about the world's problems."

"You're wrong, Mom—anyone can change the world. You just have to want it and you just have to try."

"You may be right. There are certainly examples of people who have seemingly changed the world all by themselves. If that's what you want to do, I'll be your biggest fan. I believe you can do anything you want."

"But *you've* decided not to try to change the world," Phillip says.

"No, I just decided at some point in my life that I would live in the small world."

"What's the small world?"

"This house."

"What can you do *here* to change the world?"

"I made *you* dinner! That way *you* can be the revolutionary in the family and I'll cook so you can focus on change in the big world. How's that sound?"

"Deal," Phillip said.

He ate his dinner and went to play computer games. We didn't say another word that night.

- - - -

After Phillip's desk-throwing event, the school put more restrictions on him, like we have to do anything they say, regardless of whether it made sense or not. That bitch principal told the security guards to search my son twice, so he's getting extra scrutiny while all the other kids who only have to be searched once stand in a crowd laughing at my son. They made him sign up to see this therapist at school—who was of course terrible. Phillip told me she made him curl up in the fetal position while she sat on the floor and held him. At far as I knew she had no training. There were no degrees hanging on her wall, no framed certificates. The only thing she had was a pair of Hello Kitty panties hammered with a single nail through the sheetrock.

When I was in her office to discuss the fetal-position exercise she had been doing with my son, I stared at her, then at the Hello Kitty panties, then at her, then at the Hello Kitty panties—I did this back and forth a few times and then she goes:

"Oh! Do you like my Hello Kitty panties?"

"I didn't say I like them."

"Those are the panties I lost my virginity in so I like to keep 'em around for special occasions."

She saw the look of horror on my face.

"I mean not special occasions—" She slaps herself in the forehead. "—but more like nostalgia, you know what I mean?"

"No, I *don't* know what you mean."

"Wanna hear the story about how I lost my virginity?"

"No."

But lo and behold, this bitch told me anyway. It happened in upstate New York, where she was born and lived until the age of twelve. She went to this party—a hotel party with a bunch of people from the middle school and high school in that area. This guy Buck was supposed to be there—and so was the twelve-year-old version of Phillip's therapist. This little girl let it be known to her friends that she wanted to lose her virginity that night and she wanted it to be with Buck. So Little Miss Virgin is getting herself what she thinks is the proper level of drunk for her vagina and her psyche to be able to endure the event.

So..what? Buck shows up and Phillip's therapist's friends tell him that this twelve-year-old girl has like this *super, super* crush on him and she wants him to take her virginity—tonight.

Buck finds the girl and takes her into the bedroom. The future therapist locks the door. Buck is immediately on top of her and pulling her pigtails and the people outside are screaming her name and banging on the door yelling:

"Vir-*gin*! Vir-*gin*!"

"Two minutes later I was wiping cum off my pubes, sitting on the toilet in that hotel bathroom, no longer a virgin."

That therapist sits back in her chair like I'm supposed to be impressed by that shit.

I lean forward.

"Try getting fucked in a weekly motel by some limp-dick Mexican drug dealer who just got you high on meth. And imagine your mom standing outside the door counting the cash she got for letting that middle-aged Mexican fuck her oldest daughter for the first time. This guy had children, a wife—everything. Guess he got to cross that off his bucket list—fucked a virgin with a limp dick—*check!* You were twelve, though—*damn*—beat me by a year. I was thirteen when I lost it to Louie so I have to give you points on that one. I'll tell you one thing, though, if I was a middle-aged Mexican and I was fucking my friend's daughter and my dick didn't get *completely hard..and* if I didn't cum in that little thirteen-year-old bitch's cunt..I wouldn't feel like I had got the full experience. You know what I mean, little miss Kitty?"

She didn't have to answer—I could see on her face that she knew what I meant. Butch had banged that Hello Kitty pussy *hard* and it was her *pain* that made that motherfucker cum. He was excavatin' her cunt like one of those tunnel-drillin' machines, just a stiff dick in a piece of bubble gum. Butch had his way with that tight little white girl pussy and she tried to get up but Butch put his hands on her shoulders and fucked *good* until he was grabbing her neck and her tight Hello Kitty muscle could feel that bulldog of a cock flexing and flexing, pumping his cum into her stick of bubble gum, and then he gave her a few more thrusts for good measure and took his hands off Hello Kitty's neck. Then the cum was dripping out of her and she ran to the bathroom to let it drip into the toilet and Butch zipped up his pants and went out of the bedroom to be congratulated by the rest of the hotel party for taking Hello Kitty's virginity and taking it right, not like some limp-dick Mexican with kids and a wife. His name was Butch, and he had a stiff cock, and he was the first person to ever cum in that virgin cunt and he filled it with cum *like a man is supposed to* and he left Miss Hello Kitty squirming, embarrassed, sitting on the toilet in the bathroom, light off, forgotten, cum dripping from her no-longer virgin cunt. But in the main room, the victor was revered: His name was Butch, and Hello Kitty would never forget him.

"His name was Buck," the therapist said.

"Whatever."

- - - -

I got up from that therapist's office and went down to Principal McFuckface's office and I sat myself right down.

"Don't let that stick-thin little dimwit of a lightbulb fuck my son."

"I guess you saw the—"

"That your fifteen-year-old 'therapist' has her virginity panties hammered to the wall in her *office*??"

"Well Davina, it's none of your business what that woman has on her walls but I *have* asked her several times to take them down because they send the wrong message."

"I don't want my kid seeing that woman."

"Miss Casillas, you don't have a lot of pull here after Phillip's last chair-throwing incident."

"She's holding him in the fetal position asking him about prenatal memories and shit. What kind of a *therapist* is this? And tell me she's older than fifteen."

"She's in her twenties. She has a master's degree from Temple University. Phillip is going to keep seeing her if he wants to stay in this school."

"Aren't there *rules* about faculty touching students?"

"Yes there are, for faculty and staff. I'll review the rules at our next staff meeting."

"I'll tell you what," I said. "Why don't you just make a flyer and stuff one of those in everybody's mailbox—be a little easier for you."

I got up, went to the door, then turned to the principal.

"And you tell that little firecracker of a therapist down there that if she gets pregnant by Phillip..*she's* paying for the abortion."

- - - -

This was every day of my life for a while. The phone rang and it was always the school telling me the latest thing Phillip had done to cause chaos in their lives.

I dreaded answering the phone—every time I thought it was going to be the last time—Blue Ridge was going to expel my kid and he'd be back to the continuation school, practically going to school in a co-ed prison.

But they never called to expel him, and gradually I adjusted to spending hour-long sessions with Phillip's nurse, therapist, teachers. Sometimes I had to chew them out because *Phillip* was right in the situation. After one of those long phone calls I always gave myself permission to smoke. I think I surprised myself by taking these calls with the school and actually feeling ok when the call was over. Before, I was the powerless little girl—but those calls with the staff of Blue Ridge were part of what solidified me in my role as a mother.

I am powerful.

I am a powerful mother.

I am a capable mother—I got this!

*I got this—yeah :)* But that—as always—was only part of the story. Those everyday calls with some of the cast of Blue Ridge High School—I mean yes I got better at them but they were extremely stressful. And after an hours-long talk with Phil's psychotic therapist, Chris would call me to talk about when Phillip was going away to live with his dad in northern California. I felt like I was losing my mind—it was too much change for me, to be away from Phillip (who will always be my baby), even as stressful as it was to parent him. And when I thought of Chris taking care of Phillip while Chris worked a trucker's job, on a trucker's schedule, I was scared.

In fact, would you mind if we stopped?—I don't think I can answer any more questions tonight. Thinking about Phillip leaving and whether or not I'll be able to keep this house on only my Social Security—or the thought of not seeing Phillip every day and cooking us all dinner and me and Phil watching at least one movie together every night—I'm feeling very untethered and adrift and a little unstable I won't lie.

## 93
I'm having a hard night.

I feel like I'm buckling under the stress of Phillip today—I'm sorry to say that about my son but the truth is he can be very difficult. I fucking hate myself for saying that but I couldn't think of another way to say it. It's hard without help and I've been doing it alone for so long I just get mentally and emotionally exhausted sometimes.

He's having a bad night, threatening to leave, and he just broke his phone so he's upset about that now. Threw the motherfucker from the living room where we were talking all the way into the kitchen where it smashed against the wall. He wasn't throwing it *at* anyone so I guess that's a saving grace. But he's gonna need a replacement phone—no way the one he smashed could be fixed.

I want to go to sleep and be unconscious and have all these emotions straighten themselves out for me like in *Beauty and the Beast* where all the dirty dishes clean themselves up and put themselves away—all while singing you a lovely song. I don't think I can rest anytime soon though—earlier, when I was washing the dishes (with no help from Disney), Phillip said he wanted to stab me. It really hurt my feelings. I mean I know he doesn't mean it—he just has a hard time expressing his frustration. After I washed dishes, Phillip and I watched *The X-Files*. That's when it got to me. I held my hand over half my face so Phillip wouldn't see me crying.

Phillip starts punching the couch and saying, "Stupid, stupid, stupid," and calling himself names I'd never want anyone to call my son—especially if it was him doing the name calling. You love your kids from the moment of birth until the moment you die, and all I could think about while Phil and I watched *The X-Files* was: *Where did I go wrong with this precious child of mine.*

I watched Phillip go through the phases of calming down: he walked to the kitchen, to the site of the phone crash, picked up the pieces, put them in a sandwich bag, then put the bag on the kitchen counter, then threw the bag away. He came back to the living room, sat on the couch. He was stroking the raised pattern on the arm of the couch—feeling textures is one of the calming techniques that Phillip found for himself when he was just a baby. I'd be changing his diaper and his arms would be spread wide so he could feel the carpet texture with his fingers and hands.

I knew by the look on Phillip's face that he was super sad. He kept getting into new positions on the couch—I suppose looking for the one most comfortable. But he couldn't get comfortable so he got up, went back to the kitchen, got the cell phone bag out of the garbage, dumped the contents onto the kitchen table and after failing to single-handedly repair and reconnect the circuit board and tiny pieces that made up the phone, he shouted:

"IMPOSSIBLE!!"

He swiped the pieces all over the floor.

Then he started banging his fists on the dining room table.

I stood up.

"Phillip. Phil! STOP BANGING!!"

He did—he stopped.

"Come in here and finish this episode with me and then go to bed. You need to *calm* yourself down. Please try one of the breathing exercises Jackson taught us."

Phillip was sobbing now.

"*Which one?—I don't know how to choose!*"

"Try the one where you count your breaths from one to five, then back down from five to one—that one always seems to help you the most. And Phillip, don't worry about your phone. You and I will go to the Verizon store tomorrow and you can pick out a new phone."

"*Really?*"

"Really."

"But this phone doesn't have insurance on it so it's going to be *more expensive*."

"I will pay for it. You pick, I pay. We have some extra money."

That calmed him down and I saw Phil doing breathing exercises through the end of our *X-Files* episode. He didn't give me a hug and he didn't say thank you—but I wasn't expecting them. Just seeing my son counting his breaths and settling down on the couch in a comfortable position was enough to make this mother happy.

I wasn't doing nearly as well as Phillip seemed to be doing. I didn't want to cry in front of him—I wanted to be the motionless rock that he could lean on, if only in his mind. But behind my faux smile and the pretense that I was actually watching, listening to, and comprehending the *X-Files* episode, I was holding back an emotional torrent. The stress from the day hurt my heart so deeply but right then I just stuffed it all DOWN DOWN DOWN.

I think I'm going crazy—is anybody out there?

When I get to my bed tonight, I can't tell if I'm going to bawl or laugh like a maniac.

The night was ok—we've definitely had worse. I went to bed after *X-Files* and Phillip did too. We talked before that, just about his dilemmas and he apologized a lot and I scolded him some. It ended ok I think.

- - - -

The next morning I'm drinking my coffee, sitting down in the comfy chair, still half-awake and half-asleep, when I get a call from—*you know who!*—Blue Ridge High School, Principal's office. The principal speaks:

"Miss. Casillas, Phillip is *refusing* to go to therapy."

"Where is he?"

"He's right here."

"Put him on, please."

"Mom, that counselor—"

"Did she fuck you?"

"No."

"Did she touch your penis?"

"No, Mom! She's just too stupid to talk to."

"Most everyone you meet in your entire life is going to be too stupid to talk to."

"So what do I do?"

"Phillip, I need you to help me out on this one. I need you to go to counseling when they ask you to. You don't have to say a single word to that lady. Just sit there and live in your head. But *go* to counseling. If not, you're going back to the continuation school. Please, please, baby, go down the hall and sit in that therapist's office for the rest of the period..ok?"

He agreed.

But a minute after I got off that call, another came in.

It's some number I don't recognize.

I pick it up anyway.

"Mom, it's Phillip."

"Is everything ok? Where are you calling from?"

"One of my friend's cell."

"What is it? Are you ok?"

"Yeah, I'm generally as good as the last time you talked to me."

I laughed.

"I just wanted you to know that I overheard Principal McLaren—"

"Oh, is that what that bitch's name is?"

"Principal McLaren just talked with Children and Youth and they're coming over to the house."

"When? Today?"

"I don't know but they're going to be checking up on how well you're taking care of me."

"How well do you think I take care of you?"

"I think great."

"Well that's all that matters to me. Thanks for the warning. Now go give that therapist the hour of her lifetime."

"Ok Mom, I love you."

Then the call was dead.

Even though "I love you"s are common from Phillip—he says them to me once per day—when I get one it makes me as happy as if he only told me once a year, as if it was a total surprise—his words are that sweet to my ears.

- - - -

Phillip Phillip Phillip. I had to jump through so many hoops with Phillip—cross so many t's, dot so many i's—all to keep him in a system which never gave him a lick of help. But in this bullshit society you aren't allowed to *not* be in the system. What a fucking piece of shit—like you *have* to be doing something for the state or you're not even recognized as human, as a thing with rights, as a *person* with needs..and gifts to give.

I can't wait to fall off this grid. I hate all these lists: polls, the census, FBI, CIA, no-fly lists, a politician who laughs on CNN when he admits they kill people based on the telephone calls they make. Yeah, stuff like that makes me spiritually claustrophobic. They'll kill you if you're a revolutionary (someone who helps the people)—but they'll also kill you if you're friends with a revolutionary.

Everyone has to be accounted for..and all the money must be accounted for..and everyone has to work a job that pays taxes—unless you're giving the allotted amount of your minimum wage, you are of no use to society ("high society").

This kind of shit is a huge trigger for my recklessness—I'm kinda scared even thinking about this kind of stuff. I just..I don't give a fuck about laws and cops and systems and it leads to disorderly conduct out there and in here.

I'm defiant. That's part of why I called Chris: 'cause I don't want Phillip to suffer from my actions whatever they may be. He should be away from here so he doesn't get taken away by Children and Youth because he's living with an unfit mother. Phillip and I talk a lot about his future. We came to the conclusion together that he would be better off having a job than going to high school. But here's the catch: Phillip can't drop out until he gets a full-time job..but how can he get a job while he has full-time school hours?

I'm telling ya, you go outside the rules—they'll kill you. They don't shoot people in the back of the head anymore. They just put a single drop of liquid in your Burger King Coke. You come down with a cancer that no doctor or hospital has ever heard of. Then seven days later you're dead.

- - - -

Zach was always quiet for his growing-up years. He did get in trouble a couple times—just minor stuff at school—nothing I felt the need to get upset or punish him over. Stuff like pushing a known bully down a flight of stairs in response to threats from said bully. Then Zach went down those stairs and kicked this asshole in the chest—that one kick was worth two weeks of that asshole lying in a hospital for broken ribs, internal bleeding, concussion (Zach might have done more than kicked him in the side—that asshole ended up with stitches in his eyelids and also in his lips). The whole school hated that bully and everyone—including the principal and every student witness—lied and said Zachariah had nothing to do with it..it was just an accidental fall down the stairs.

Zach failed ninth grade twice—he just didn't want to participate in class and not once did he ever turn in a single homework assignment. When they tested him, he tested higher than any other kid in his grade. Zach is very intelligent, very well read—really too smart for his own good. He's in his head a lot—I suspect the worlds he visits are more beautiful and complex than this crazy Earth most of our minds are stuck to.

On his last school day of the second ninth grade, he came to me. I was sitting on the back porch with a Ball jar and a pitcher filled with homemade mojitos, reclining in a beach chair.

"Did school end early?" I asked.

"No but I decided it was over for me."

"So, we doing ninth grade for a third time?"

"If you're asking me if I flunked, then yes, I did. If you're asking me what I'm going to do next, I think I'll drop out of school and stay with you and practice my guitar and play video games."

"Zach, that sounds fine to me."

"You're not mad?"

"Not at all. I mean this country has crippled itself beyond measure for not understanding that smart kids are special needs too. We *throw away geniuses* in this country because corporations don't want to hire really smart people who will rock the boat—they want *zombies*—sorry, this must be a good mojito because I am *waaaay* off topic. Zach, just do what you want, ok? Make yourself happy and then everyone you know will be happy."

"Ok, Mom—thanks."

That summer we went to the school and he filled out the papers required to drop out rather than go to ninth grade a third time.

He hung around the house for months—close to a year—being quiet, being his unobtrusive self, playing his guitar with headphones, playing video games in the living room. I'd be sitting comfy in my reclining chair watching dog-on-girl bestiality—obviously with my earbuds in. Zach and I would sit in mutual silence and give each other the peace of omitting all small talk..in fact we omitted *all* talk and just sat there doing our things.

Zach didn't get a job for over a year. He got snobby and grumpy towards me and Phillip—then when Ashley moved in it was even more stressful. Zach finally got a job with his big sister's sometimes boyfriend Aaron at a pizza place called Armetta's—it was in town so Zach walked to work. Sometimes Zach would walk over to the Dunkin' Donuts to see his big sister Ashley who within a few days of working there had been promoted twice and was now managing the entire store.

Being over at the Dunkin' Donuts so often, one day on a usual visit to see his sister, Zach instead saw something which is just about the best thing a straight boy with eyes can see: working behind the counter was the twenty-one-year-old version of a girl Zach had liked since elementary school. Zach. Kelly. "It's ok, just get your ass behind the counter for the rest of the day. And you—bro!—you've *got a job* and *it ain't here*. Stop fucking my employees!"

Of course I wasn't there to see this. This is just the story as pieced together from my kids. I was overjoyed for Zachariah, you know—if you find that kind of love once in a century you're billionaire fortunate.

Zach and Kelly synchronized their break times. Where they went, I don't know, but they found places. Now they're living together, practically married, so they don't have to fuck in the alley behind the pizza shop anymore.

## 94
Before Zach dropped out, he started bringing friends over and they said how awesome I was. I'm sure the main reason I was awesome to them was that I let them smoke pot in the house. They always shared with me if they had some, so it was a good deal—plus I had minor crushes on some of Zach's friends.

They started asking me if they could have parties at the house. They would hang out and smoke weed and have band practice with drum sets and guitars and big giant speakers. A DJ from next door would come over with mix boards and smoke machines and more speakers and he'd play dance music and dubstep and we'd move all the couches back and everyone was dancing in the living room. They were parties just for us. We had strobe lights and shit. So many of the kids' birthday parties were at our house with their favorite bands. I liked having all the kids there and letting them play their music—sometimes it was a party, sometimes it was more like band practice. The neighbors got upset every time I had people over.

The mom from next door comes to our house.

I open the door.

"You couldn't even wait till the weekend, could you, Davina."

"Suck my left nut," I say, and I slam the door in that bitch's face.

She could never call the cops on account of her being a major tweaker. You could smell it on her and if the cops got a whiff they'd search her house and she would go to prison for a long, long time.

But, yeah, I was always letting people crash on the couch—I picked up a couple homeless people, a couple travelers—I let them stay there and shower, gave 'em something to eat. The kids never looked down on me for doing this. As young as they were, they knew you never turn your back on someone who's hungry or homeless. That could be you someday, carrying everything you own in a backpack—or maybe you've already been there.

The best thing about Zach's friends coming over, or having homeless people stay, was that I had people to take care of (more than usual). I learned something new about myself recently—from one of those internet quizzes. Don't laugh—I'm sure many of you think that anyone who bases their life on a quiz from the internet must be *loco*. But I don't base my whole life off it—I use it as a guide.

Anyway my quiz results were something like: *As a mother, your love and strength are more powerful than the jaws of a hyena.*

I'm sure it's more powerful in the original Mandarin.

But it made sense at that time in my life. I was a mother, literally, to three—and I was a mother to many more when you count Zach's friends and the occasional clan of hardcore drifters—the ones who ride trains all up and down the east coast, begging or using their parents' money, refusing to work or even bathe, saying *fuck you* to the whole entire system.

I was kind to those drifters.

I was even kind to their dogs.

And though it took that internet quiz to start me thinking of myself this way, once I took that quiz I started hearing what everyone in the house was calling me—from my own two boys to the DJ to the people who played in bands: anyone who wanted my attention always called me..*Mom!*

- - - -

Ashley called me collect one day. She said:

"I'm in California, my boyfriend just broke up with me, and I don't have anyplace to go."

"Do you have a job?"

"No."

"Do you have any money?"

"Not really."

"Are you calling to ask me if you can come home?"

Ashley's voice was strained, so tense she sounded like she might break. She said her words with a distinct tone of self-hatred when she informed me over what was obviously a payphone that she could only be *my* child and I could only be *her* mother—as if describing some sort of deadly embrace that was playing itself out over generations of our common genetic/emotional material. I wanted to hug that hatred out of every pore in her body—but of course it doesn't work that way.

Ashley—broken and worn out—said, "Can I come home, Mom?"

"Of course!" I said. "Come here, my baby, and rest—then you can plan your next move."

"I don't think I have any more moves in me."

"Do you have enough money for the Greyhound?"

"No."

Ashley is crying at the other end.

"Ashley baby."

She's sniffling.

"Ashley, baby. You can handle this. Find a Western Union and I'll wire you the money for the bus."

"You would do that for me?"

She's in hysterics.

"Baby girl, my baby baby girl, do you think if you were in trouble I would stand by and do nothing?"

Sniffling: "No."

"That's right. You did the right thing—you called for help. Some people wouldn't even get that far."

"They wouldn't?"

"No, they'd let their pride get in the way. You're a smart woman. What I hear you doing in this phone call is *survive*."

"Really?"

"Yes, Ashley—look at how brave you are right now."

"I am?"

Sniffle.

"I'm brave?"

"Ashley, you're one of the bravest people I know. You grew up well—"

"No I didn't."

"Well you grew up better than me, let's just leave it at at that. Where are you?"

"At the airport."

"I'm not gonna *even* ask what you're doing at the airport."

"I was gonna buy a ticket to Sydney."

"Ashley, why?"

"Because no one knows me there and I won't know anyone."

"Baby. Baby. *Please* don't do that. You'll end up on another continent with no money. Use whatever you have to buy a ticket to Binghamton. Do you need more money than you have?"

"No. I'm fine. I have his credit cards."

"Good. Now tell me when we get off the phone what you're going to do."

"I don't know," she says tearfully.

"The first thing you're going to do is find a bathroom and splash some cold water on your face. That'll help you calm yourself. Once you do that, take a cab to the Greyhound station and buy a one-way ticket to Binghamton. You'll have a lot of time on the bus to think about what just happened and what you want to happen next. Ok baby?"

"Ok Mom."

You know, I could have thought right then that Ashley coming to live here would at least be hard on us all, and at most would throw my once-peaceful house into utter, unsurvivable chaos. But what am I going to do—let my own daughter be homeless? That won't happen in a thousand years.

Really, I was just so excited that all my kids would be with me under one roof and it never occurred to me that anything significant would change.

The next few days I was filled with apprehension and butterflies. I washed the house, vacuumed, picked up every piece of dirt. Phillip got upset since he could sense Zachariah's mood and the only reason Zachariah got into a mood is that *I* was in a mood.

"IT'S NOTHING, OK?? YOUR SISTER ASHLEY IS COMING HERE TO LIVE. THIS IS WHAT FAMILIES DO FOR EACH OTHER!! SOMETIMES WE LIVE IN SINGLE HOUSES, FAR, FAR AWAY. AND SOMETIMES WE CONTRACT WITH EACH OTHER INTO LIVING IN ONE HOUSE, WHICH IS WHAT WE'RE DOING NOW."

I had planned a much longer speech but by the time I had finished my opening paragraph, both boys were looking at me like I was a crazy mom—which is true, all moms are crazy—they both were standing on the stairs taking in my oration but then they just shook their heads and each one went in his own direction, Zach up the stairs and Phillip into the living room to play games. He put on his headphones to drown out the sound of my vacuum. My sons' complete lack of verbal response convinced me that their mom was obviously out of her mind.

- - - -

I cleaned for three days and with each day my hope and anxiety filled, and filled, and filled my well until it was overflowing. If I saw a mosquito in the house, I stalked it with techniques employed by the ancient Japanese warriors. (Girls and women reading this: oh yes, women can be ninjas.) I used some thick rubber bands, a pair of chopsticks that came with our last take-out order, and some small shards of glass from the street. It would be too difficult to describe the resulting weapon in full, but rest assured it was easy to use, one-hundred percent accurate, and completely humane—these little bugs never knew what hit them.

Anyway it's safe to say I was in full hysterics mode when Ashley arrived. I left Zach and Phil at the house, crossed my fingers and my toes, and hoped that nothing bad would happen while I was gone.

I drove to Binghamton, New York where the closest Greyhound station was. When I got there Ashley was sitting on the concrete leaning on her bag. I pulled up beside her and popped the trunk. She throws in this oversized olive-drab duffel bag.

When she gets into the passenger seat I look at her and say:

"You look like you just got your ass handed to you. And you smell like a boys' locker room."

She hugs me.

"You know goddamn well what it's like to live on the Greyhound for three days," she said, and I could hear her smile: "*You're* a Greyhound veteran."

"I wish it weren't the case," I said. "Sit next to any psychos?"

"None as crazy as you. I did ride a stretch of *five hours* with a schizophrenic sitting next to me."

"Oh yeah—I know *that* drill."

"White lady, real tall, nasty curly hair, carries all her personal belongings in a suitcase and says racial things to everyone around her."

"Why?"

"Because that is the exact same woman who sat down next to me in North Dakota—or maybe New Hampshire—all I know is she got off at Cincinnati and never got back on. I saw her through a shop window—she was browsing Tupperware containers. I thought: *Maybe someone should bang on the window real hard to get her attention. Or the bus driver could do a head count and discover someone was missing.* But none of those things happened. I saw that she had her pillowcase and it did seem to contain everything she required in order to function in life—except possibly one more piece of Tupperware. Plus, I'm not her *mom*—for all I know, that schizophrenic bitch *wanted* to get off in Cincinnati."

"Yeah," I said. "Maybe Cincinnati is her home."

"Only if you give me one."

We smoked together on the way back to Hallstead.

"Maybe old girl with the pillowcase doesn't have a home, you know? When we're older maybe you and I can do that, as a team, and when I die you can stop the bus and just roll me out onto the side of the road and within hours I'll be decimated by ants."

"I don't want to think about that," Ashley said. "That's one topic that's off limits: your death. We don't talk about that, ok?"

"Ok," I said. "I'm sorry to worry you."

"I'm not worried—just..don't talk about it. Do you think it was the same woman we saw on the Greyhound?"

"I wouldn't be surprised. She probably gets a check and spends it on one of those ultra Greyhound passes that let you go anywhere in the country for a whole month."

"How much are those?" Ashley asked.

"I don't know..maybe three-hundred..maybe five hundred."

"We should do that when we get older, you and me, just bus it out to Colorado, take some pics. Make it to Utah—have you ever been to Utah?" she asks me.

"I drove through it, as a kid."

Ashley leans on my shoulder and holds onto my arm with both of hers. It was dark—I could see the stars. For a moment Ashley was asleep and I engaged in some ridiculous fantasy where Ashley is my perfect daughter and I'm her perfect mom—but actually this wasn't far from the truth.
