When we got back to the house Ashley was definitely awake.

The house was full of people. Some were my friends, some were Ashley's friends, some were both. Everyone was drinking and smoking and Phillip's system was playing reggae and rap from artists I had never even heard of—it was wonderful.

Ashley was like, "What the fuck?"

"Welcome home baby."

"Is this for me?"

"It's for you."

"Jesus Christ, Mom, I didn't even know you'd be happy to see me."

I look sternly. "If you ever say that again I'll slap you."

Ashley cried. She straight-up cried in my car.

"I'm sorry! I would never slap you."

"No, I know. It's just *this*..I expected to sneak quietly in the back door and go without dinner and sleep on the floor."

I had to dry her eyes off.

"Do you think I would ever let my only daughter and *the child who made me a mother* just *sneak in the back door* and go without food..or a place to sleep? Ashley, you're *so* precious to me. You're irreplaceable."

"I can see that now."

"Now go have fun. I'll have Phillip get your bag."

*You didn't think I was going to be happy to see you? What did I do wrong, honestly—god? Anything out there that exists—the universe, nature, mountains, the California sea—what did I do wrong that my child didn't think I would be happy to see her?*

No answer. Typical.

Maybe it's not me—maybe it's the world, the way Ashley's brain was programmed—just like all of us—to see ourselves as small and weak and unable to make a difference. You know I can lament the whole world in a single second? I can feel all the pain and all the humiliation and all the violence perpetrated on every single person—all in this one second—and it penetrates my heart with some kind of chemical weapon from the military that can kill everything in the universe with a single drop of something that's like *the opposite of blood*. I feel that in one second and then I raise my head, and blink, and I'm back again, just a normal person feeling normal emotions.

We partied hard that night. The house was full of people—people spilled out into the yard. That might have been the most people we ever had at this house. I was a) happy, b) excited, c) hopeful for our new life together whatever it may be. We all drank so much that night—we drank rum and vodka and tequila and beer, ignoring all the rules about which ones make you *never sicker* and which ones you should *never fear* when you drink them in certain orders. Me and Ashley did so many shots together—we all drank so much to celebrate her arrival that I didn't notice anything unusual about her drinking—I didn't even know Ashley was an alcoholic till a couple weeks after she got here.

I didn't think that Ashley living with us would be anything but perfect added to perfect. Me and the boys had a good life—now I'd have all my kids together again. I thought of my magic bubbles in the Florida mental hospital. That was one of the metaphors I was using to see my daughter's return—something like Glinda the Good Witch floating down from the sky in a pink dress with a sparkly wand, except my daughter would be wearing all black and have straps and lace, Madonna style, and there'd prob'ly be holes showing her nipples and a hole in the crotch where you could eat out her pussy.

I honestly thought nothing would change—and if it did, it would be for the best.

Maybe that was a dumb fantasy—but in my experience this world is so rotten that sometimes your dumb fantasies are the only way you make it through.

## 95
I make it sound like Ashley came to us as a hydrogen bomb—arriving in the deep of night, stealthily carried in a black duffle bag, its contents undetectable by Greyhound's bomb-sniffing dogs. With devil horns and that hot little body. As though within a week she had destroyed any peace, any logic, any house routines I had established over the years.

It wasn't that way at all. But I will say this:

It is interesting to stand looking over an ant hill—every ant on a mission, deliveries going in, deliveries going out..somewhere deep in the pile a happy queen, fat, docile, the supreme beneficiary of this little world. Everything ordered—and therefore everything happy. But then you decide your Jolly Rancher isn't jolly enough and you spit it onto the ant pile.

This changes everything. Ants that were supposed to zig are now zagging. Those going in are now going out. And that scent thing—where they rub noses to exchange information on where to go?—Totally fucked. All new activity is around the Jolly Rancher—it eclipses all the other tiny bits of food the ants were aware of. All their attention is focused on this new piece of candy.

I guess I was jealous. Ok, I'll say it: I *was* jealous.

Ashley had been in California this whole time, showing off her bod to hot men on beaches. She was wearing California clothes when I picked her up—just brands you can only get in California, stuff like that. But you know she didn't pick those clothes out by chance. It's the same thing when a guy who moved to Allentown when he's thirteen is still proudly wearing his NYC cap even though he hasn't been to NYC in years. Quite simply, it's a statement that *I come from better stuff, cooler stuff, and I'm a big fish in a small pond who has plans—big plans—to blow this popsicle stand (as soon I save the necessary money—which will be impossible to do in this fuckwad town)..but yeah, I'm a New Yorker and I'll be one for life.* But that's ok. I get the desire to do that. I'm a California girl—*will be* for *life*—I just don't wear it on my sleeve.

- - - -

I'll give Ashley credit, though—when she came to live with us, she was respectful, unlike when Zach moved in last year. Ashley went with the flow—when Zach and Kelly moved in to help me with the rent, after Ashley and Phillip moved out, they took it upon themselves to rearrange anything that would make their lives easier. Ashley might have touched a few things—that was ok, I mean she lived here too, now—she can make the place hers a little. I want everyone to be comfortable. When Kelly and Zach came, though, it was like: they rearranged the *furniture* in the living room. And the real kicker: Kelly rearranged my movies. You don't do that. I bought that cabinet special. I collected those movies over a period of ten years. I had them arranged exactly like I wanted them and last year, when I needed her and Zach to move in for financial reasons, Kelly is casing my movie cabinet like some sort of predatory cat. And I'm behind her, in my reclining chair, and I'm like: *I hope that bitch doesn't have plans to rearrange* my movies*.* But that's exactly what that bitch did. She never asked me, she never even threw a look my way. I was like one of those NASA rockets that blows up mid-air with like six scientists inside it—that's how fucking pissed I was. And Kelly just kept standing there, like she owned my house *and everything in it*, and she arranged and rearranged my entire VHS and DVD collection according to some alien law. I looked at it and I couldn't even figure out what her order was—it wasn't by subject, it wasn't any type of alphabetic. I felt violated.

"I had those in a special order," I said.

Kelly said, "But this way you can actually *find* things."

"I could find things just fine before."

Kelly didn't answer.

She rearranged the pots and pans in the kitchen—and also my spice racks.

"Kelly, I can't find the crushed red pepper!"

She yells back from the living room:

"Cabinet above the stove, on the right side, there's a whole Lazy Susan with all the spicy ones."

"They're *all* spicy."

"The hot ones, Davina. If you really can't find it, I'll come show you."

"*Please*," I said in the most put-off way possible. "And the next time you decide to rearrange my whole entire *house* why don't you give me a tour—I would think that would be the minimum of courtesy."

Kelly comes up right behind me, reaches into the *center* of the Lazy Susan (where I couldn't have possibly seen *anything* without removing several spices first) and she gets the crushed red pepper and hands it to me.

"There was no more room so I put it in the middle."

I started to ask my son's wife, since red pepper is something I use almost every day, if it could go on the outside and switch places with some less-used spice.

But I didn't..I didn't. She's my fucking daughter-in-law and I'm her fucking mother-in-law and I'm supposed to be the mature one between us so I zipped my mouth and put the crushed red pepper back where Kelly wanted it.

Note to my young readers: How do I say this? Basically: age just makes you taller, and fatter, and less able to remember things. Pretty much, if you grow up and people call you wise, you were already wise as a child. The hurt I felt when Kelly rearranged *my* things in *my* house was a hurt seeded deep into my mind by Jessie, to whom nothing about me was off limits—so you see, nothing was ever *mine* when I was growing up, not even my own body.

When Ashley came, she didn't rearrange as much—probably because she lived here before so she was used to where things went. She did put a few things back in the kitchen drawers in places I wasn't used to—I couldn't find a hand towel for a week. She arranged the porch chairs so that they had something to do with the vernal equinox and perfect stargazing.

"You know, Leo the Lion is only visible in the northern hemisphere for six months out of the year."

"What's Leo the Lion?"

"You don't know what Leo the Lion is? Mom! It's a major constellation—didn't they teach that at school?"

"No, they didn't, so it's a good thing you're here to teach me. People didn't know as much back then."

"The constellations were around since like *before Stonehenge* so don't give me this crap."

Ashley gave me this funny and dismissive look.

"*Astronomer.* An astrologer tells your fortune."

"Whatever, Einstein. Want to smoke a bowl?"

"Just..sit..please. We're gonna smoke that stuff from Cali..next time. But I've been saving something special for *you* to smoke with *me*, here, since I knew you were coming.

"Oooh, what is it?"

Ashley's reaches out and I slap it away.

"Now look, this isn't going to be as potent as I know you can get out there, and we don't have smoke shops with like ninety zillion flavors..but it's something I like, and it's something I want to share with you."

"Aww, Mom. *Thank* you."

So I smoked her out, and she looked more relaxed than when she got here.

"A three-day Greyhound is a long ride," I said.

Ashley said, "*Long* motherfucking ride."

And she took a huge hit.

"It's like the settlers," she said, with smoke still in her mouth. "Except we go both ways across the country, east to west *and* west to east. And it only takes us five days instead of like five months and shit."

"It did. But while I was cramped into a window seat with some tired-ass grandpa breathing off an oxygen machine, *sweating on me* even though I aimed both the overhead air jets *on him*, I honestly was feeling sorry for myself for having to ride among *the tired, the poor, the huddled masses yearning to breathe free*, you know? *The wretched refuse of our teeming shore. The homeless, the tempest-tost..*wait, I lost my thought."

"Something about your Greyhound trip taking three days."

"Right! So I looked up other routes you can take on the Greyhound to make myself feel better. Philly to Tucson?—Five days. New York to LA—*Seven days.*"

"*Fuuuck.* That is truly insane. That means there are people on the Greyhound that haven't showered in *seven days*."

"Those must be some stinky motherfuckers."

"No doubt. You wanna smoke another bowl?"

"No thanks. I think I'm going to clean the bathroom and take a shower."

It offended me that she thought my bathroom was so dirty that she would have to *clean* it before it was safe enough for her to take a shower. But I didn't dwell on it—I smoked a bowl and looked at the stars and wondered if somewhere out there, there was a Davina constellation that no one had discovered yet.

When Ashley came, there was a slight change in the house—and I'm not talking about her moving the dish towels or making minor changes to the location of utensils in the silverware drawer. It was the new logistics of having one more person in the house. I was ok with it—I invited Ashley to come here, but that came with an element of increased stress. It was small at first—then it grew. It was hard because there were only three bedrooms: Zach had one, Phillip had one, I had one. Zach and Kelly treated their room like a little apartment—the door was always closed and the rest of us hardly saw them come and go.

She would love him, and hug him, and squeeze him like a teddy bear. "I think I already know," Phillip said.

"Of course you know," Ashley says. "I hear you jerking off at your computer every night to *Extreme Redheads*. What's so extreme about redheads?"

I just laughed. I was chewing and a little chunk of ground beef feel from my lips onto my plate.

"I just like redheads, ok?"

"Like whatever you want but don't leave your damn *tissues* on the dining room table when you're done jerking off your little fourteen-year-old cock. How long have you been jerking it?"

"About three years."

"Ever fucked a girl?"

Phillip looked so red in the face and he stopped chewing and looked around at everyone at the table, especially Aaron. Then he swallowed his food and got up from the table and he went into the other room and flipped on the Playstation.

"I SUPPORT YOU AS WHOEVER YOU ARE, PHILLIP."

Of course not. We are animals, after all. The reason I held back wasn't morals or a respect for Ashley or anything value-based or generous or caring toward the rest of the world—it was complexity. I want my life to be simple. Fucking Aaron would make my life more complex. Not fucking him would keep things simple—at least as simple as I had gotten them so far.

So no—there was no sexual tension between me and Aaron.

Besides—and you're probably too stoned by now to remember this, sitting here as you are breathing me and Aaron's second-hand smoke—but Aaron was my pot dealer. Is, was, and ever shall be. Amen. That's how the relationship started—hopefully, it will never end. And as anyone who has two Abe Lincolns to rub together knows: *you don't fuck your pot dealer*. It's just not done, Old Sport. The dealer-buyer relationship is a business relationship. And even if you *wanted* to fuck your pot dealer, your pot dealer would never fuck *you*, because underneath the fact that you *are* also friends or maybe relatives or you work together, your pot dealer views you quite rationally (and maybe primarily) as a stream of income. If they fuck you, and things go wrong, that's their fuckin' *money*.

And while we're on the subject of telling the *truth*, that story Aaron told earlier about how he got discharged from the Army—that's about as true as *Cinderella* and *The 9/11 Commission Report* combined. Aaron never fucked no guy in a plastic effing shithouse. He's not even gay—he's not *bi*, either. I guess he doesn't mind people thinking he is, though, since he tells that shithouse ass-fucking story to anyone dense enough to believe it—but the real story is a lot less entertaining: he was discharged for failing PT. He never would say the exact details, even to me or Ashley, but he failed some aspect of the physical requirements. Like he was too skinny and couldn't gain weight or he couldn't run fast enough or do enough push-ups or something stupid like that. That's not what he wanted to be known for, though—being the only private in Basic who couldn't do a single pull-up—so instead he told that ridiculous story about fucking guys in the Army's portable toilets..and we let him do it because it hurt him less than him just saying he wasn't physically fit enough to be a soldier.

## 98
Do you want to know a secret?

*Yes.*

I just did a line of coke that's my secret.

*You did?!*

I know right I'm a bad girl.

*A* very *bad girl.*

Don't tell on me.

*Did you do another line of coke or just keep it to the one?*

I did another one!

*Haha do you have more?*

Nope. Aaron is kind of stranded here and he's sharing with me.

- - - -

On the surface, Aaron and my daughter lived together and behaved as friends, family, and lovers for the nine months before Christopher was born and for some months after that. Aaron's sense of responsibility to his kid is why he joined the Army—it's the only way a boy with no skills can make a dime in this country—that or become a drug dealer (which in my opinion would have been the safer, more financially sound, and morally higher grounded of those two choices—*but who the fuck am I?*). Ashley sincerely tried to work it out with Aaron—I believe it was out of some sense of obligation to societal rules—but she was never really satisfied with him as a mate, so three months after Christopher was born she broke up with him. Ashley had her baby now and she basically thought Aaron was a loser—I mean, he got fired from *Servpro* for a second drug violation after only eight months (that's the suicide/murder/corpse cleanup job). Obviously, that's a company where they're hiring from the bottom of the barrel—to clean up blood and guts from murders and gang fights you really must have no other options. Like most of us, Aaron is never going to work for NASA. Also—and I'm just being realistic here, but from a job-placement point of view—Aaron needed a job that didn't drug test him. He needed to be high *in order to work*..so he had to find a job where that was cool.

Ashley never thought of Aaron as mature and responsible enough to be her husband, start a family, do all the stuff that people do. She always felt he was too young for her. Also, he annoyed the shit out of her every time he opened his mouth. He would come over to visit Christopher and the rest of us and Ashley would shout him down when he was talking so that all the stupid shit he said didn't ever reach her ears.

Then she'd tell him he was the dumbest guy she ever dated:

"I've dated some dumb guys—I mean *dumb* motherfuckers—but Aaron, you take the cake. Mom. Did you know that Aaron didn't learn to read until the *fifth grade*?"

"Ashley be nice. Different people learn at different rates. Einstein—"

"Don't tell me about Einstein. If you're *Bill Gates*, it's *ok* to drop out of Harvard. I mean, probably anyone who gets into Harvard doesn't *need* to go to college in the first place! Aaron, would you *get me a drink*? I'm *thirsty*. *Fuck*."

Scientists now know that there's a correlation between intelligence, drug use, and mental illness. Based purely on our Scrabble games, I know Ashley is smarter than me. It's no wonder Aaron bored her.

After Aaron was fired from the crime-scene cleanup job, and after he finished his thirty days of outpatient rehab, he found a guy who owned and bought hotels. Aaron and this guy would refurbish the properties: every room, every closet, every sink, every tub. They'd refinish the pools with new tile and this goopy blue paint that protects your feet from the concrete and makes the pool look like a *pool*, instead of just an ugly concrete hole in the ground. They put in all new carpet, painted every wall—they made these junky places *beautiful*.

And the best part for Aaron: no drug test. His boss was high, too, from sun up to sun down. Aaron was a hard worker—and that's all his boss really cared about.

I guess Aaron didn't feel like coming over to my place all the time to hear Ashley yelling at him and telling him how stupid he was just to smoke out with me till 2am and crash on the couch. Nor did he want to pay the rent on what used to be his and Ashley's two-bedroom apartment in Binghamton, so he broke their lease and started staying at his work sites—it was all empty hotels—he could stay for free in any room he wanted.

- - - -

Ashley dated this guy Chuck—she met him at this house down the street where we all used to go and party. One night Ashley was super drunk and me and her got into a fight. Ash was trying to confront me about flirting with Chuck (which I was not) but I refused to converse with her while she was drunk—if I did, she wouldn't even remember it the next day. This made Ashley super pissed, that I was calling her out on how drunk she was.

I went inside the house to get a couple shots of Jameson. When I tried going back outside to the porch, Ashley kept me from coming out of the house by putting her whole weight on the screen door. I shoved the screen door as hard as I could—it knocked the screen door open as wide as it could go and the screen door knocked Ashley off the porch. Her "whiskey and water" spilled all over her and everybody on the porch was laughing at Ashley and just the situation in general. Ashley never came back to the party that night—I imagined her curling up in bed, feeling sorry for herself, wishing she had more alcohol. Especially when her crush, Chuck, didn't follow her home to lick her wounds for her. Guess those two weren't as close as I thought.

The next day I told Ashley that Chuck wanted to have a threesome with her and Aaron but that Chuck was kinda nervous about being with another guy.

Ashley says, "Who's Chuck?"

"That guy..from down the street..last night..the one you were flirting with..Ashley you had your hand down his pants and were practically getting him off on the back porch with everyone watching—"

"Oh!!! Chuck!!" Ashley says. "Maybe we can talk about that *later!*"

"Of course," I say. "You want some pancakes?"

"Duh! Mom, you make the *best* pancakes."

"I'll take that as a yes."

It was crowded that morning while I made pancakes. I kept almost running into Ashley, my boys, and extras from Chuck's party who made it from his house to my house to crash. I wanted to yell, "Everybody out!" but I was too chickenshit. It wasn't just the kitchen where we crowded up in unworkable bunches. It was the living room. It was the dining room. We were moving chairs from room to room so everybody had a place to sit but it still wasn't enough—the dining table is only so big..ditto the living room (we have a big TV but it's sorta hard to pay attention to the show when there's five people on the couch and you're the one in the middle).

There were simply too many people for that house to hold. I had hoped Chuck and I—or even better, Chuck and Ashley—could have a respectful, loving, stick-to-the-facts conversation about Chuck inviting people to my house and telling them I would cook pancakes. I mean, come on, it's one thing for your neighbor's drunk guests to crash on your couch but it's another thing when they wake up expecting you to make them breakfast! All Ashley had to do was tell Chuck: *Look, no more house guests at my Mom's or I take away the pussy.* That way Chuck and I could be neighbors without being up each other's fucking butts.

But of course that didn't happen in *our* neighborhood—that was the type of shit that would happen in Mr. *Rogers'* Neighborhood.

After I was done making pancakes for seven or eight *very* hungover thirteen- to sixty-five-year-olds, I politely kick everyone the *fuck* out of my house, I clean up the entire first floor—*pissed and fucking yelling*—then I manage to chill out. I'm finally drinking my morning coffee, I'm sitting in my comfy chair.

Then what?

Stuff starts *flying* down the stairs—blankets, chairs, a bar of soap (unwrapped, scuffing the walls). I get up and go to the bottom of the stairs. Ashley has a floor lamp in one hand and a bottle of gin in the other. The gin is half gone.

She starts to open her mouth but I preempt that shit.

"I'm not talking to you drunk, Ashley—you're a different person, you don't remember jack—I'm just not gonna do it."

"FUCK YOU!!" my daughter says to me.

She throws the gin bottle at me and it doesn't even make it all the way to the bottom of the stairs.

"Ashley, it's eight in the goddamn morning!"

"Oh please, I've seen you drink all night and keep on drinking all through the next day."

"Not when I had a kid to take care of."

"Fuck you *doubletime*."

"Doubletime? What the fuck is that even suppose to *mean*? Is that some military thing or like a dance move or something?"

"Davina."

She comes downstairs.

"Hand me my gin."

I laugh.

"You passed that gin on the way down the stairs, sistah! Do you see it now? You're close..now you're getting cold..bingo!"

Ashley stares at me out of one eye while she takes a giant drink.

She wipes her lips with the back of her hand—it reminds me of a scene in *Lolita*.

"We're moving out," she says.

"I gathered."

"I said we're moving *out!* Me and Christopher are getting out of this hellhole."

"I'm very happy for you."

"Don't you want to know why?"

"No," I said—and this made her very angry.

"I'm moving out..because *you*..don't *appreciate* me and *all the things I do* for this family."

I got right in her face.

I told her: "*I* take care of your kid *all the time* even when you're at home and drunk. So yeah, Ashley, thanks for doing me such a *favor* for moving in here."

"Oh, come on, Mom, you couldn't even pay the *rent* before I got here."

"We didn't have any problems with money before you got here."

"Mom, you're kidding yourself—living off you and Phillip's *disability*? How long is that gonna last? What happens when Phillip moves out?"

We look at each other for a long time.

"You haven't thought about it, have you? You're living in a *fantasy land* where Phillip lives with you forever and disability checks keep coming and coming and coming until you die. Mom, you have to have a *plan*. Did you think you'd never have to work again?"

I took in—and let out—a big sigh.

I take the gin from my daughter. And I drink.

"Ash, you know I love you. But everything changed when you got here—we changed our whole way of life for you."

"Je-*sus Christ*. If I had known that you helping your kid out by letting us sleep in an empty bedroom was *changing your whole way of life*, I never would have asked. You just don't know how to say no, do you? If Bigfoot walked through that door, right now, and I told you to suck his cock..you would do it, wouldn't you? You'd do it, then regret it later—that's how *you* work."

I was crying now.

Ashley hugged me and I hugged her tight—I still loved her.

She got out two souvenir shot glasses—one from the Grand Canyon and one from Hollywood. She gave me Hollywood and her the Grand Canyon. I briefly wondered if she meant it as a sleight—giving herself the majestic, jaw dropping, eternal one and me a chintzy, touristy coffin for dead movie stars. But when she poured the gin, for one second I stopped worrying and imagined this as one of our wonderful drinking days, where the adults get hammered and keep a lazy eye on our children—just making sure they don't fry themselves with a toaster in the bathtub (that sort of thing).

I almost drink but Ashley says, "Toast, toast!"

"Ok," I say. "I toast you, my daughter, little Ashes, now grown-up Ashley, that you figured out when you'd outstayed your welcome and knew that it was time for you and your kid to *get the fuck* out of this small-ass house."

I was again about to drink when Ashley said:

"And to my mom: thanks for the fucking childhood. A pack of wolves would have raised me better."

She smiled, I smiled back, and we took our shots.

## 99
Ash moved in with that guy down the street, Chuck. I have no idea why. He was a total fucking crazy motherfucker and he and Ashley had nothing in common—I guess he wanted her tiny hot bod as his daily fucktoy.

Chuck was the kind of drunk who drinks from the moment he wakes up to the moment he goes to sleep. Ash would leave the baby with Chuck while she went off to meet Aaron in the Burger King parking lot, Aaron's job sites, sometimes even *my* house to fuck her former boyfriend—Ashley's pussy was getting fucked three, four, five times a day. I worried that she would get pregnant by Chuck but mostly it worried me that she was leaving Christopher with Chuck, given the type of drunk he could be. That's my *grandson*. Of course I offered to watch him, but Ashley didn't want my help—she wanted to be *independent* (which of course she wasn't anyway since half her stuff and most of her kaleidoscopic supply of alcohol still lived in my house).

Ash broke up with Chuck a month later. She moved back into my house and all the moving out and moving in was too much chaos for me and Phillip. Zach and Kelly finally got their own place, so that helped a little bit. Ashley kept reverting back to Chuck, then Aaron, then Chuck. The last time Ashley moved back in, we switched around which rooms people were staying in. It was a whole big mess but basically I was trying to get Ash the best room for her job schedule at Dunkin' Donuts. She worked early and I wanted her to be able to go to bed early so she could get enough sleep without being bothered by the rest of us who were staying up till midnight watching movies and playing video games and everything else we did that made noise. As it turned out, I asked Phillip to sleep on the living room couch—which is a sucky deal since the only bathroom in the house is on the first floor, through the kitchen. Every time someone went to the bathroom, it woke Phillip up. There might have been a better arrangement but I couldn't think of one. I put a sheet over the doorway to the living room so Phillip had a place to put his computer and other things. I know how important it is for a person to be alone sometimes.

I still get mad every few days given that Ashley displays this monster-queen bitch-all attitude towards Phillip. She's always bitching at him. Phillip will be eating breakfast and Ashley will go over to him, stick her nose inside the rim of his cup, lick his juice, and say:

"Don't drink my baby's juice, alright!? Do you know how much this fuckin' shit *costs*?"

"Ashley," I say, "get your tongue out of Phillip's cup—it's a health hazard."

Ashley keeps talking to Phillip with her nose inside his cup—even though she's really talking to me:

"Don't *drink* the baby's juice, don't *get* into my stuff, and don't *eat my fucking cereal!*"

"Watch your tone of voice," I say.

Ashley steps to me.

"What??"

"Stop talking to him like that. Phillip needs patience when you talk to him."

"Well I don't have the patience to talk to him."

"Then I guess you better find a new place to live—one that's rent free so you can afford to drink twenty-five hours a day. Are you still breastfeeding Christopher?"

"Fuck you. Look!"

Ashley points to Phillip's cereal.

"That's my Special K."

"I'll buy more," I say.

"He could at least be polite and *ask*."

"Ashley, I know you know *I poured* that bowl of cereal."

"But *he's* eating it."

"GODDAMN BITCH HE GAVE HIS FUCKING ROOM TO YOU!! This is gonna be tough, but why don't you show some gratefulness now and then?!"

"To you?"

"*Yes. Whose goddamn house do you think you're living in?*"

"Well I'm moving soon."

"Good."

I turn away but I can't resist one last comment.

"You know what the crux of the situation is here?"

Ashley says: "I've never heard that word before—it must be an old-person word."

"It means the most important point in the issue being discussed."

"Ok."

"The *crux* of our house issues, right now, is that you have the attitude that *you* are the most important part of this house."

Ashley immediately went upstairs to drink—I *clean* this house, I know where all the hidden liquor stashes are.

If you want to eject from the conversation and not talk about your own issues, instead deciding to drink alone in your bedroom, fine—but you're not going to hang around the kitchen verbally abusing my son, your brother. Phillip is too important to us.

I get upset when I think about how much everything changed when Ash got here. Before Aaron and Ashley got together, I would hang out and watch movies and play darts and be friends with Aaron—he was my weed dealer!—but now because Ashley hates Aaron so much, me and Aaron can't even be friends. I can't just invite people over for band practice or to come over just to play music for me, or have a minor fucking party, because of Ashley. Once Ashley moved in it was *no parties* and *no dancing* and *no band practice* and *no private performances* just for me.

I feel like she's judging me when I watch TV, like she would never watch the *smut* I watch. Well fuck me with a masonry drill, but I don't like to watch "the news"—it's mostly lies anyway. You've got your *own* TV, bitch, in your *own* room—go watch Disney movies or *Meerkat Manor* or whatever gets you off.

I feel like because this is *my* house and *I'm* the mother, I should be able to do what I want—but I do not feel that way at all. I have to tippy toe around my own house almost twenty-four hours a day—the work and school schedules dictate that someone is sleeping in my house at all times. I try to be considerate—I clean once a week, I straighten up after Christopher every morning when he's done playing with the *army* of plastic toys we somehow amassed for him. I'm glad he has them because he gets these looks of delight on his face when the dump truck dumps imaginary dirt all over our floor..but I hate those toys, too, knowing that all that plastic is burning holes in the ozone layer and is one of the reasons that the next extinction-level event is coming faster than any of us let ourselves believe.

Also I cook every night—forgot to mention that because it's just part of my ritual. I buy the ingredients with food stamps and disability cash—and if you've ever tried to feed a family on food stamps then you *know* that it is both an *art* and a science. But I love cooking and I would always cook for my family, even if I had to dumpster dive every element of the meal. So I cooked every night at the Hallstead house—and I cook there still. It doesn't matter if it's one, two, or eight of us—we may be poor but no child of mine will ever go without at least one big meal a day. (So there, young mothers, young fathers: you want to be better parents to your kids than your parents were to you? Just pick one thing, like cooking, or saying "I love you" or giving them hugs. Not everything can be fixed in a generation. But if you're conscious, a whole fuckload of things can.)

Did I also mention that I clean the whole house? Sometimes I get help, like Ash and I will crank Sublime and get it all done in like an hour. You should see us do it—especially the way we dance. It's not club dancing where the whole point is to show your stuff to find some stranger to go home with. We call it "freak dancing" or "idiot dance" and it's basically the most ridiculous thing two grown women would come up with as a way to make the cleaning go faster. When the cleaning's all done, Ash and I sit on the couch smoking pot and watching episodes of *House*—that's my show.

Also I wash clothes for the whole house. Even for people who don't live here—relatives and friends of relatives who don't have their own washer and dryer. I wash, I dry, I fold, I sort. Sometimes when I'm putting clothes into the washing machine I find socks and underwear stiff as a diving board. These are Phillip's, and when I come across them it pleases me—any loving mother wants to know that her son is cumming good. It's only right for a parent to wish sexual pleasure for their kids.

Oh and I forgot to mention the dishes—I wash the dishes too.

- - - -

Ashley and I were in the kitchen one day—I was making pizzas with custom toppings over individual slices for each person who wanted to participate. I was at the stove, Ashley was sitting on the kitchen counter like she always does, and Ashley says:

"What are you going to do when Phillip leaves?"

This topic was a can of worms in my brain—and it was an emotional topic too—I'm not sure if Ashley understood that when she asked this particular question.

And before I could answer the first one, Ashley showed that she had more questions that came from the same place as her first one.

"I guess I'm saying," Ash continued, "that if you're leaving to go to California—that if you did could I just stay in *this* house and take over the rent."

That makes me feel weird.

It makes me feel like my own daughter is kicking me out of my house—right? Is that what she's trying to do?

"You said you wanted to wander."

"Yes, Ashley, I did say that."

"And I mean, you can't really *stay* here since you can't pay the rent."

"Ashley, how many months, since you've been living in this house, have you paid rent?"

"I thought our agreement was that every other grocery trip I pay for it."

"That is our arrangement," I say. "I'm just asking you to appreciate that you're not the only person here and you're not the only person with money problems. And about the house, I don't know what I'm going to do."

And I didn't. I had no idea. I felt so *selfish* that I couldn't just leave the house to Ash. I kept talking openly with my kids about wanting to wander—just grab a backpack full of stuff and hitchhike my way back to California, where I belong. I didn't want to give up the house, though—I mean, I'm forty-two, I have back problems—what if I find I'm not the ideal candidate for hitching around the country? I'm a grandmother—I want to see my grand babies grow up and be able to talk with them when they can talk and all that other stuff that seems trite until you've actually done it—actually held your grandchild in your arms and seen him look up at you and smile.

I think: what if I come back, what if I find I can't wander like I used to? If I come back it's going to be Ash's house now and *I'm going to have to live by Ash's rules?*

I hate that I have both feelings of being upset and missing the way it was before Ashley moved here—she *is* my daughter and I should help her, but I feel like Ash should be more considerate to me and to her own brother. She just moved to Hallstead, came in my house, and took the fuck *over*. I kick myself for getting mad about that—I mean Jesus-fuck, I should give her anything she wants, everything she needs—but I resent my own daughter for never giving me the tiniest piece of acknowledgement that when she needed a place to stay, I let her stay in *my* house—I found it, I rented it, and I've been paying for it and raising Phillip in it all these years.

Ashley comes in the picture and not only does she act like she owns the place, but she's working with me on when I might be able to move out (for her convenience) into what? Homelessness?

Not long ago she needed a place to stay so she wouldn't become homeless in California—so I let her stay with me. Now that she's here, her needs outweigh mine, and my daughter is being as polite as she can in saying that if my idea to wander became reality, it would be much better for her than if I stayed.

## 100
This is how we pay the bills:

I pay the rent and utilities and Ash and I take turns buying groceries. If I need extra money Ashley will give it to me—like if I'm behind on a bill.

If you're wondering how a woman with no job can pay to have a place to live, and eat, and partially support grown children who have their *own* children..it's disability and child support, *baby*, disability and child support.

The disability settlement was from my back problems—it wasn't much: a few lumps sums and one of them I used to buy Ashley a car. I was going to buy Zach a car first but Zach wouldn't get his driver's license. It was kinda cheap but it was good for driving around town. Ash was getting used to driving it and she was even picking up the groceries more often.

But one night Zach got drunk and drove Ashley's car to McDonald's in the snow and ice and he went the wrong direction to even *get* to McDonald's and he crashed into a giant boulder and totaled Ashley's car. There's friction between Ash and Zach *to this day* because he wrecked her car. But it goes both ways: Ashley has borrowed money from Zach that she hasn't paid back. And to increase the tension even more, Ash (as you recall) is Kelly's boss at Dunkin’ Donuts.

- - - -

During Zach's next birthday I hide in the corner (in my comfy chair) while you interview me.

*What kind of party is it?*

It's Zach's birthday and it isn't a party anymore we're all old!

*You can still party when you’re old!*

I just feel bad that I don't have a gift for Zach. He came over to eat a piece of cake and I wanted to order him a band shirt online and I asked him which band he liked and he said, "I don't even *listen* to music," and it *really* hurt my feelings and I feel like a dick that I didn't get him anything and I feel like an asshole that maybe *he* feels bad or has hurt feelings about it—I just feel like a total fail.

*You're not, though. I think it's harder to get gifts for people when they're older.*

I just think Zach hates us. He and Ashley aren't on good terms rn and his girlfriend works for Ashley and she took some management decisions personally so she and Ashley don't talk well either and it's this whole big cluster fuck. I mean Zach doesn't even talk to his dad anymore idk when he got so jaded against his family..or why..it makes me sad and hurts my feelings but what can I do, you know? I text him that I love him so he doesn't forget—I guess that's the extent of what mothering has come down to in this day and age.

*Check out this steak I'm cooking. That's as rare as they fucking come!*

Nice baby! I'm having a drink Ashley says I should.

*Whatcha drinkin’?*

I don't even know! One of Ashley's crazy concoctions! Lol!

*Lol.*

- - - -

Ashley was too busy with her job and Christopher that she never came along with us, but me and Zach and Phillip used to fill our backpacks with water and food and we would take half-day hikes up the sides of mountains, just wandering on and off the official trails, sometimes following the river where there were no trails. It's so dense in there, it almost feels like you're in Vietnam or something. In spots, there's so much shrubbery and foliage you can hardly move.

I got into this meditative state when we'd go far from the trail and rays of sunlight were shining down on our heads where the trees and leaves had gaps between them. Sometimes I couldn't control my mind and my head was speaking the way the people from our Louisiana church spoke when they spoke in tongues. Then the trees would speak to me—no lie—they had solemn things to say but also silly things to say. I walked behind my sons so they couldn't see how I danced with the trees above and the mud below. Those walks changed me—I felt like I was moving into a third chapter of my life or something.

Finally my boys turned around to see me. I was doing the kachina dance that I had learned by watching my ancestors when I was five. I was raising my hands to touch the invisible stars. My sons stopped 'cause I was singing..singing some morphed-up version of what I had heard those half-men, half-gods singing on the reservation that had stuck with me all this time..through girlhood, through being a teenager..through everything I did and everywhere I've been. In the baptismal when I had gotten baptized Pentecostal—the white robes that the Pentecostals had given me to wear that day turned into the fabric of the kachinas. Jessie would never come back for me because for now and for *ever*, *I* hold the power of the kachinas—a power I never knew I was even *allowed* to have! Even my visions of dogs were visions created for me by the kachinas, as warnings, sent to me through time and space for no less a purpose than *to save my life*. And I knew, dancing Hopi dances in a Pennsylvania forest, with my sons watching me, that I was *connected*—for a spare moment—connected with ancestral knowledge so old that it pre-dated this *planet*.

My sons never tried to stop me. They made no comment. They stood still in the forest keeping watch over their mama.

Next thing I know I'm lying on the ground, my sons helping me up, and I said:

"Trees are the only upstanding citizens."

They laughed at what I said and called it nonsense. But that wasn't my first trip to the rodeo when it came to nonsense. One girl's nonsense is another girl's wisdom—even when you speak *pure* nonsense, it might make perfect sense to someone else (and you might never ever know that it did).

Anyway my boys got me standing again. We walked off the mountain and in, through the town, back to our precious home. My revelatory visions faded back into the gold dust that surrounds us, that we breathe in and sweat out every minute of our lives. That special feeling of connectedness that I felt on the forest floor..left..slowly, but completely..and I even had some of those human emotions like jealousy, like hate. All that is normal came back to my consciousness like thieves sneaking into my brain to steal my mountaintop experience. I held onto what I had seen and heard and felt and known as tightly as I could, but they were stronger than me—or I was too poorly trained—and all I kept from that day was that *Trees are the only upstanding citizens*. Every other insight washed away like a dream.

- - - -

So I've lived without a lot: some people live in newly built condos, buy their clothes at the mall, eat out nightly in pricey restaurants—leave a hundred-dollar tip. I'm 42 and I've never had a pedicure—but mostly that's 'cause I don't like people touching my feet. My feet are a very sensitive area and I think all they'd end up doing is tickling me to death.

When we had cars we did things—when we didn't have cars we stayed at the house and watched like four movies in a row..every single day..until the snow melted and they plowed the streets and the oil man was able to get fuel to our house so we didn't freeze. I think of the fun we had when we did have a car on a spring day: We'd get in the car and take random trips—compulsively—just drive and drive and drive and end up in weird places. I can think of a few times we went to New York City (that was a three-hour drive one way). There was a river we found once that you could never get back to..some lake that was the same way..once you went there, you might never be able to get back to the same place by taking the same route.

This is the point in the interview when I tell you that not everything I say is *true*. Oh it's true to *me*, but it might not be *factually* true. Of course this is true of everyone to some degree: A couple has a fight but each one has a different memory of what was said. The magic river exists in my mind—maybe nowhere else. But she comes to me as either memory or a dream. And I don't question her, because the important part is that she gives me *pleasure* and *power* when she comes.

Anyway, we had so much fun when someone had a car—we had a lot of fun.

I really got along with all of Zachariah's friends—I could really relate to them, I felt comfortable with them. I was part of their crew even though I was older. That's just the way it was and nobody ever thought that it should be different—because I *am* very young at heart and I don't hang out with people my age. I don't have much in common with people my own age. Me and those boys went to concerts, watched music shows on TV, and we watched *thousands* of movies over the course of a single summer.

It was like this: ten of us walking down to the video store and buying tons of movies (I refused to pay for cable at the time). We bought movies out of the old movie bins—we'd go through them for like an hour, come home with five or six movies, get pizza or crust and ingredients to make pizza. It was a carefree life, no doubt—one of the best times of my life.

I never got rid of some childhood habits—they're part of me forever. One time walking home from the video store Zachariah said:

"Mom! Stop that!"

I was digging in a trash can and all the boys were waiting, looking on with wonder and interest and disgust. But once you've been homeless, you always look at trash cans differently.

"What?" I said. "Half of what is good and pure in this country is in the trash."

And I meant that. Even when I had a job and money and a home with a refrigerator full of food, I still ate out of trash cans. And to give a little credit where credit is due, it didn't help that when us kids were growing up with my mom and her various boyfriends, there was never anything in the kitchen. Cabinets bare. Refrigerator had one stalk of celery that had turned black.

So I mean, growing up poor, spending time homeless—I just never got over the feeling that I couldn't let anything go to waste.

My sister Jamie's response to these same experiences from childhood was the polar opposite: she bought the highest-quality ingredients—steaks and shit, the most expensive she could find—and she'd always buy more than she needed. She'd cook these lavish dinners and by the design of her subconscious there was always more food than they could eat. When I went to her house, instead of one black stalk of celery in the refrigerator, there were ten pounds of rotting meat—the most beautiful steaks you've ever seen—wasted, inedible. That was her plan, to always have food go to waste, because spending her money on food she was never even gonna *eat* was my sister's way of convincing herself there would never just be one stalk of celery in the refrigerator ever again. That waste made her feel secure.

After the Summer of Infinite Movies, Phillip was back in school terrorizing the kids, the teachers—he even terrorized the fucking janitor.

I got a call from Blue Ridge High almost every single day.

And even though from one point of view, Phillip was weekly, sometimes daily in trouble with the school—from another point of view we just decided, wordlessly but together, that those events no longer needed our constant, frenetic focus. Out of raw necessity—out of a need for our mental survival—Phillip and I learned to laugh at his outbursts (*after* he calmed down and apologized). But at some point, either Phillip mellowed out or I got used to his disruptive behavior, because we got to a place where all that junk was no longer the center of our lives—it was a series of wild behavioral events and arbitrary disciplinary actions that were simply bumps in the road that Phillip and I got through, step by freakin' step.

- - - -

One more thing before we finish: My best friend Calum decided to move to Texas and Calum's friend Frank decided to go with him. At first I was mad because my entire social structure of people my own age was leaving the state. Then I remembered all the trips I had taken across this beautiful country (I'm referring to the land, mostly). I had driven across it several times and I am a veteran of needing to wander. I know that urge like my lungs know the urge to breathe—and when a person stops following those urges that make a person happy, that person is dead in all the ways that matter.

Me, Frank, Calum: all in the kitchen talking. We had known each other for *years*..nothing like my nomadic childhood. As a kid, I hated moving: from one podunk Louisiana town to another, always changing schools, living in every motel ever constructed in the state of California. It was nice for once to have all these good memories all in one place. I mean all we ever did was talk smoke weed drink beer laugh our asses off and go drunk driving up north where the new construction was. Just blank roads with no one on 'em 'cause the only place they went was to empty neighborhoods with half-built homes—they hadn't even painted the lines on the roads yet.

I haven't been up there in a while. Now it's probably all whitebread women wearing Spanx to the grocery store..one kid with a retractable Velcro leash..the only thing in her cart when she gets to the checkout is seventeen cans of dog food with real meat in it, a bunch of single-serving snack packs containing crackers and a cheese food spread (for the kid—Oh my god! Make sure the kid is still on the leash!! There he is—safe, reading the tabloids. He kisses the image of a woman's tit.), and—

"Josh!"

She yanks on the chain.

"Josh!!"

She yanks on the chain.

"Will that be all?" the checker asks.

The woman sees the checker checking her body and imagines a scenario where she gets the checker to come to her bedroom and she turns off the light and undresses herself really quickly so the checker never sees that she's really forty pounds heavier without the Spanx.

"Will that be all?"

"Just these, for me."

She hands over her Amex and a bottle of Diet Pepsi.

The checker is doing a visual count on the remaining bottles in the woman's cart.

"There's twenty," the woman says.

The checker rings her up and waves bye to the kid as they go through the sliding doors. In a few minutes that bitch'll have the kid downstairs feeding the dog cheese food while the kid opens a can of dog food and eats it with a spoon. Upstairs the mom has a two liter of Diet Pepsi in one hand, her clit in the other, and she's watching porn about a husband licking his wife's asshole because getting off on this vid is as close as she'll ever get to having her husband do that for her.

- - - -

"D."

Someone's touching my arm.

"Davina."

I look at Calum.

"I was just imagining..you know that road we drive up with the new houses?"

"Yeah."

"I was just imagining what the people will be like, who live there."

"You mean what the people will be like who can afford to live there."

"Yes, that too. Do you think I have dissociative personality disorder?"

"No. I just think you have a big imagination. And now that you're back from the Divine places that only Davina can go—"

"And only Davina can choose to come back from—" Frank said.

"Yes, right," Calum said. "Now let's have a little toast."

Calum had already poured three shots of whiskey and he handed me one. Then one to Frank. Then Calum picked up his own shot.

"Make it quick," I said. "I'm ready to get drunk."

"Ok," Calum said. "A *quick* toast at Davina's request..I'll try to get in the essentials."

Everyone raised a glass.

Calum said: "To Davina, Frank, and Calum. To all the fucking amazing times we've had together. And all the fucking amazing times we'll have to come, no matter the paths we choose to follow."

I downed my whiskey and wiped away a tear.

I was filled with love. That might not sound like me but right at this moment that's the best and only way I can think to express my feelings, being there with my two best friends. And as Frank and Calum and I drank, and danced, and worked ourselves onto a second bottle of whiskey, I was overwhelmed with strange feelings of satisfaction and contentment. And I saw that my two friends were on the same page, and that they shared those same feelings, those same memories, with me.

I feel like I have a lot of memories of stuff I went through by myself or with people I don't talk to anymore, so it's really cool to have shared experiences that are good—happy times as an adult with other adults who I can trust and love (even though they were leaving me, possibly forever).

- - - -

Calum knelt in front of me. I was sitting on the couch.

"Davina."

"Yes?"

"Come back to me, ok?"

Calum put his arms on my legs.

"Come out of your mind, my friend, and dance with me—it's our last day."

Calum took my hands and pulled me up from my sad couch and Frank was in the background dancing and then Calum and I danced too and we turned the music up so loud that it woke the babies. Then we turned it up so loud it woke the neighbors and the neighbors came out in their sleeping clothes and slippers and they shook their fists and threatened to call the police and I was like:

"My uncle Jimmy is the deputy sheriff to the Chief of Police of Hallstead and if you call the police right now you won't be *able* to get the Chief out here. And you won't get no knicker-knocker rookie either—you think some cop's been on the force three months is gonna take a loud party noise disturbance call? He'll prob'ly do something illegal and you *know* we'll have cameras on that fucker before he gets out of the squad car. So if he takes your call, he'll have to resign tomorrow for whatever stupid cop shit he does when he comes out. I guarantee you that rookie cop ain't taking your call. So here's what I suggest: One, get your sleepwear-wearin' asses off my front porch before my uncle Jimmy slaps restraining orders on each and every one of you motherfuckers. Go. Just go. Put your head under your pillow and *pray* that we don't open another bottle of whiskey."

I went inside my house and closed the door.

Calum said: "What's a knicker-knocker rookie?"

Frank said: "I never knew you had an uncle Jimmy!"

"Figments of imagination, gentlemen. Figments of my fucking mind."

Calum gave me a shot and kissed me but I pushed him away (gently). If I hadn't pushed him away, we probably would have had sex that night. I don't know—that's just my feeling.

- - - -

The next thing I remember I wake up on the couch, fully clothed, with an actual pillow (as opposed to a couch cushion) under my head. Before me, on the coffee table, is a shot glass filled to the tippy tippy top with whiskey, and it's set on top of a scrap of paper.

I sit up, bring the glass to my lips, and shoot it in one motion.

I set down the glass and pick up the paper. It says, in Calum's hand:

"To Davina: You are the baddest bitch alive. Love, Calum and Frank."

I cried so hard.

I went to the kitchen sobbing and tried to hug the refrigerator—I ended up on the floor, still hugging the fridge (just its bottom feet). I was howling and moaning like a dog who has lost her mom—or her mate.

The day that Frank and Calum left was one of the saddest days of my life.

## 101
I've done weed, speed, coke (snorted it and put it in joints). I've done acid, mushrooms, and with Eddie I've done sherms (that's a cigarette dipped in formaldehyde). I've done crack, OxyContin, Adderall, Vicodin. My favorite lately is Suboxone. Also: Xanax, Molly (snorted it), and finally salvia. Those are the drugs I've done.

Now all I do is smoke pot. And I smoke it *every day*.

I did salvia at the house in Hallstead with this girl I met online—we were in the same chat room, hit it off, then we were online friends for about three years. She lived in North Carolina but she came up to Pennsylvania to visit. I picked her up from the bus station in Binghamton. She brought salvia with her. It came in a flat disc compact—looks like oregano or loose tea. My friend I worked with at the Family Dollar came over. The online friend surprised us both by bringing out the salvia but my Family Dollar friend didn't want to smoke it.

"Why not?" said the salvia friend.

"Because..reasons," said the Family Dollar friend.

It turned out not to matter what her reasons were for not wanting to smoke salvia—my online friend snuck some into a bowl of weed. As soon as my Family Dollar friend hit the pipe she was like:

"Oh..what's in that?..it makes me feel weird."

"That's salvia."

My Greyhound-riding friend got a huge smile on her face. Then she was laughing her head off.

"I think that worked."

"So do I."

My Family Dollar friend, who a few seconds ago had her anti-salvia morals in place, was now extremely glad she'd tried it—that's how it is with drugs: the less experience you have with them, the more prone you are to judge the people who use them. It's a weird pseudo-symmetry: Sober people critique the judgement of people who use drugs. Drug people (perhaps unfortunately) have a wider range of experience: They know that drugs kill you, but the feelings they give of bliss and amnesia and incredible magnification of sexual sensations are so good that they're willing to do them again and again and again until they come to their senses, run out of money, or die.

My Family Dollar friend was lying on the floor, flipping through the bottom of my bookshelf, saying, "This is good and this is good. This is good—"

"They're all good," I laughed. "That's why I bought them."

"Mmm."

"Ginger, you feeling good?"

She nodded, looking at the ceiling.

Ginger was my North Carolina friend and I tried to keep her talking—I love that southern accent. Corinne was my Family Dollar friend.

We called a quick session of the Hallstead Parliament and the votes were all "Yay" on taking a deeper dive into the salvia.

The first time I ever did it, I took a big ol' hit and held it.

Ginger said, "Let it out let it out you're not supposed to hold it that long!!"

So I held it in a few seconds longer—I'd done *way* worse drugs than these two and I wanted to get the full effect. I mean *salvia*. *Salvia?* I've got salvia growing in my yard—I just never had the ingenuity to try to smoke it. When I let out my first ever puff of the now-sacred salvia leaf, my body shook with a gut-wrenching laugh—this laugh kept going on and on (forever it seemed). It was so loud. Inside my head I kept thinking, "What am I even laughing at?" I thought that I was laughing so loud the whole neighborhood could hear me—so I shut the window. We were all laughing uncontrollably. Corinne was rolling around on my bed saying, "I'm gonna piss in my pants. I'm gonna piss in my pants."

Other times smoking salvia were much different than that—they weren't even lucid. It makes you feel like you're being choked out. When I smoke salvia now, my inner experience is that I'm throbbing and spasming even though it's not really happening according to those around me. I'd read about DMT experiences and then later smoked salvia and the salvia experience reminded me of what I had read about DMT. I'm definitely light on hallucinogen experience—if I can travel someday I'd like to change that.

- - - -

I remember this one time I smoked salvia—it was me and Zachariah. Phillip was really young—he was supposed to be in bed but he came downstairs right after I took a hit. Zach was watching me trip..I don't know what it looked like from the outside—it probably looked like nothing, like me just sitting on the kitchen floor—but to me, it was an entirely different world, much stronger than the first time I took it. The first time had been slumber-party hilarity—just a girl party. This time, these people came to me through the salvia and whatever gates it opens in your brain—they were in the shape of people but with no discernible features. Mine were made of colors, as if each one of them was a stack of ribbons. I can't explain it any better than that: each one was a solid stack of colors—shaped like people.

One was gently pushing my head down. Even though they were talking in a language that was totally different than any language I've heard a human speak, somehow I could understand what they were saying—the one pushing my head down was soothing me and comforting me and telling me to let it happen. I didn't know what she wanted me to let happen—my best guess was death of this body and transition to another world.

The one with her hands on my head lifted my face and she had turquoise eyes—like stone.

Her face was pure love and pure light—like nothing I'd seen or felt before.

She cradled my face in her hands and said:

"We've been waiting for you."

It was the sweetest thing..I've ever felt..in my lifetime. For the time she held me, I felt like I was being held by my mother—not Lisa, but some of cosmic mother, I swear. And this mother was overflowing with love for me, and I felt it, and I knew it was the kind of love that could stop time, or make you whole again, and I had visions of being a child, and playing with this alien mother in a perfect version of Earth, before there was such a thing as toxins in our oceans and our lakes and our streams and mountaintops. She and I were walking in the middle of a small creek and holding hands and feeling the rocks under our feet that had worn smooth over millions of years.

She asked me to stay with her.

I said I'm sorry—I have to take care of my kids.

She said that's fine.

I didn't feel any guilt about going—I wouldn't have felt any guilt about staying, either.

So she let go of my hand—my cosmic mother—and through that metaphor she sent me back to the borough of Hallstead, to the kitchen where Zach and Phillip were looking at me like: *Ok, where did* she *just come back from?*

- - - -

I read about someone on DMT who also encountered beings who said they were waiting for her. I always thought of salvia as a drug that helped you transition into a different plane of existence or helped you get in touch with—or become—one of your alternate selves.

It's a ten-minute high then you're in a fog for two minutes then you come out of it. One time Zach did it in my room with Ginger—my North Carolina friend? It was only three minutes into Zachariah's trip and he was staring off into the distance.

"Can you hear us, Zach?"

He couldn't—he didn't hear a thing. He told us later he was living a whole other life as a butcher in another city where he was very well loved and his butchery was celebrated far and wide across the country.

When you take a drug like salvia it opens up those parts of your mind that help you go somewhere else. Are they "real" places that you go, or is it all in your head? The way I see it, *everything*, including normal consciousness, is all in your head—so everywhere you go is real—some drugs or practices like meditation are like switching the channel on your radio. All channels are always happening—our brains just filter it down so it's not too much or us to handle at once.

For a while I was so interested in salvia that I spent days looking up stories online from doctors who had used it in experiments and people who had tried it. Whenever I share them, my friends think my salvia theories make me a raving lunatic—but I'm trying to tell them something important.

"It's like this," I say. "What if you're a brain in a jar—what if you have no body and you're *not* sitting on my couch. How do you *know* you have a body anyway?"

"You mean like *The Matrix*."

"I guess. I'm just saying: How do you know the difference between a mental trip and a physical one?"

"You don't."

"Exactly," I say. "So maybe there isn't one."

Then whoever I'm talking to checks out of the conversation—their eyes glaze over and I can tell they haven't made the leap with me that as far as we know, there's absolutely no difference between a mental trip and a physical one. Anyone who's read an intro to philosophy book will consider this common knowledge.

- - - -

Salvia. When I have it, I don't know if I want to do it—but once I do it, it's like *oh my god*, I want to do it a bunch.

Sitting in a circle and going one at a time, then listening to the person's story of where they went. In the kitchen, my friend Jordan took a big hit, sunk down on the kitchen floor in front of the sink and says:

"Guys..guys..get on the blue tiles. Get off the while, I'm serious. Guys, somebody turn off the fan—it's blowing me away."

(We turned off the fan.)

When somebody's on a salvia trip and needs help, you help them. In fact with any type of hallucinogen, a trip guide—someone who's not using the drug—is ideal. It's like in the beginning of *8½*: The director is floating in the clouds—surely he's in a dream—but there's a rope tied to his ankle and when someone on the beach below pulls on the rope he plunges toward the Earth.

You're gonna think I'm some sort of loon, just as my friends do, but sometimes you can be in two dimensions at once, and all it takes is a nudge from your trip guide to get you completely back to the one you started in. In the same way, it seems to me with the family of hallucinogens who make you see otherworldly creatures (who say they've been waiting for you and tell you to come with them)..it might only take a nudge in the other direction and you could find yourself dead in this dimension and alive in the other.

When I was a kid, bored, I would bend over, take a lot of breaths, inhale the last one then come up and someone pushes on your chest—I had Janine do it or whatever friend was around. You pass out for a couple of minutes—spasm out—flopping around on the floor in the bathroom with Janine watching me choke myself out, trip, get high—*whatever*—before I ever tried drugs. I was changing my state even back then.

*What's your current drug state?*

Clean and hating it.

*Haha.*

I'm feeling like I need drugs it's all I've been thinking about for like four days. I want Suboxone so bad. I've been asking Aaron for like a month but I think he just blows me off like for my "own good." I guess it's kind of a scumbag drug—like no one wants to associate with the people you can get it from so fuck me. I've asked a few people lol it's truly hard for me to come by. It's fine I don't need it but I sure do crave it.

It's an escape, you know?

Sometimes I like this life—sometimes I don't. So far when I've seen the salvia trip creatures I've managed to stay on this side—in the world we all agree is the world—but I'm seriously considering, the next time those creatures say they've been waiting for me, and they welcome me to their realm..I'm considering following them into their dimension and never coming back.

## 102
*Davina, tell me more about the gatherings at the house down the street. Who was there? What did you do?*

The guy whose house it was is Chuck. He had a roommate who was his cousin, his name was..I forget..I forget ok!..it's not as if you'd remember anyway. So Chuck's roommate's cousin we'll call Mr. X. My friend Loren was living there for a little while. They would all be there, the people I just mentioned, but a month later it would be the guy who owned the house, plus his relatives and friends—but beyond that the whole cast of characters would change.

Slacker was the one who took me over there in the first place. He introduced me to Chuck..we were over there for a couple of nights in a row. Yeah, no, I didn't care at all. I could see why he went for her instead of me and had no shame about it. The first time they fucked, when they came downstairs, Chuck's eyes and my eyes met. There was no animosity there. We're friends—*mi casa es su casa* and I wish you every happiness.

We all loved going to Chuck's house, though. We would go there in the evenings and we would go there in the afternoons. I would go first and Ashley would come over when the baby was sleeping. Ashley would give solemn instructions to my boy Phillip to keep an eye on Christopher and make sure he didn't get *out of* his crib and *into* some trouble.

You might think of that as a bad thing—leaving one little kid to watch over *an infant*—but it wasn't really. I mean we wouldn't go over unless it was nap time or bedtime and it was always Phillip *and* Zach and we were *right* down the street—five *minutes* walking time—*and* we had our phones on so I felt comfortable leaving. Not that the boy was my responsibility. I mean his *mom* is the one who should feel bad if anyone.

You gonna judge me on that? Say that I'm a neglectful parent? Fuck you. You think you could have done better if life dealt you a different set of cards? Not likely. Life gave you a full house. I got three two's, an eight, and a queen.

And you know what?—I'm skinnin' ya.

A friend of mine that Gary introduced me to (her name was Jennifer Combs) started going to Chuck's house that summer. Another girl that was a friend of Loren's (who went by the name of Bridget) also came almost every day—she was always selling Xanax to everyone. There was a girl from down the street named Amber and she brought "spice" to sell and give away.

Spice is known as legal weed, synthetic weed—that stuff's crazy. You get a really intense high like your head is turning into a balloon and it's going to lift off of your head into the sky. It's almost like hallucinating. People get seizures from it. They sell it as incense—you buy it at the adult bookstore and it has cool names like for the different flavors..never any indication that you could use it as a drug. It's illegal in Pennsylvania now, but not in New York.

Amber was making daily trips to New York for more spice. She was addicted to it—she smoked a whole big package every day.

That stuff is really bad for you. I try not to smoke it, but secretly I like the way it makes me feel. You have to know a drug that causes seizures is not something to tangle with..but I love the way it makes me feel.

One time Amber was in Chuck's bathroom and I convinced her to unlock the door and I went in there and she had been smoking spice—she had this big bag of it open set down beside the toilet. As soon as I came in, she had a seizure. She got stiff as a board and I was kneeling on the bathmat holding Amber's head in my lap and I was thinking, "We should call an ambulance." But that didn't seem like a good idea with all the drugs we had in the house. So I sat it out—I waited for that girl's seizure to stop and when she came to she was mad at me for being in the bathroom with her.

"Are you high?" she said.

"I came in here to smoke with you," I said.

"Well. Let's get craculating," Amber said.

She set us up with spice in twin bowls and said:

"Ok, when I light this shit, you light that shit, and we be *gone*."

So she lit her shit and I lit my shit and I took a *deep* motherfucking inhale approximately the size of Chicago and I have no clue how long Amber and I were in that bathroom but people came in and out to pee while Amber and I sat on either side of the toilet and rated their pee streams on a scale of one to six (don't ask). Ultimately we dubbed ourselves *The Spice Rack*—which we thought was hilarious but never caught on with the rest of the house.

I loved my little Amber girl—I wonder where she is now. Prob'ly in a psych hospital. Or dead.
