When we had wood, we would make big fires out in the back of Chuck's house. We drank tons and tons of beer—Chuck lived right across the street from the beer distributor and that was our beloved hobby and solemn profession: buying beer each day and drinking it.

Loren never had a job. His cousin never had a job. *Jimmy* never had a job. Chuck was a worker so he had money. They would all try to get together a few bucks hustling pills, cigarettes—*anything* that would get you high. Chuck's house was a tiny black market ecosystem—a never-ending circle of people trading narcotics for booze.

I'd love to say that we were doing something useful for the country, for the world, but that's obviously not the case. Most of us couldn't even *get* jobs—as in, no one would hire a bunch of tatted-up drug freaks who never went to college. This country is for the few—those whose parents were rich and *their* parents were rich. Basically, if you don't inherit money in this country, you're fucked.

So we did what we could—if not for the country then for ourselves—we smoked a lot of weed, got super drunk, and generally acted like pirates.

One time I woke up in Chuck's back yard, covered in dew—it was early morning with the initial oranges and reds swelling where the sun was coming up. I came home all barefoot and shit. I didn't remember anything about the previous night—maybe I was in Chuck's back yard because I passed out drunk there and nobody had the heart to wake me. Maybe there had been a fight and I left for my own house and only made it that far. But nothing mattered, you see, because I woke up in dew to a shy sunrise and when that happens, *everything* in the coming day can't help but be perfect.

## 103
Ashley mostly stayed over there because she and Chuck were fucking like the Easter Bunny on crack. (To be more precise, their sex was the Easter Bunny versus the Energizer Bunny on *Celebrity Deathmatch*. I can't tell you who wins—I don't want to ruin the ending.) Ashley would come home every three days to wash her stinky little body and then she would walk her clean body down to Chuck's house, go inside and upstairs to the bedroom, lie on the bed face down, strip off her "JUICY" sweatpants and stick her ass in the air for Chuck to fuck her—he could choose which hole.

Do you ever imagine cum dripping out of your daughter's asshole—because I imagine that a lot.

Sometimes Chuck would cook for us—he had a huge grill and as our host he would flip the burgers and turn the dogs..all the while, Ashley suffocating him with kisses, twirling her body around his with her legs spread, rubbing her pussy all over him like she was trying to put Lolita out of a job.

Of course Ashley was much older but as her mom she's always little to me. Also, she throws her pussy around like it's a goddamn Tootsie Pop, throwing everyone a lick, trying to see how many it takes to get to the center of her puss/candy.

Mostly at Chuck's everyone got stupid and drunk and the neighbors complained and nobody cared and we'd listen to music *really* loud and generally be happy and partying—it was awesome.

I ended up having a few rolls in the hay with Jimmy—it was kind of ok, kind of alright, not awesome but it was at least *something* for a girl that hadn't gotten laid in a while. Jimmy and his girlfriend Bridget were broken up at the time Jimmy and I were fucking. Then they got back together. Bridget was mad at me because I fucked Jimmy while the two of them were broken up. She came to me at Chuck's house. She said:

"I got a problem with you, bitch."

"Yeah? Well now I got a problem with *you*, bitch."

"And what is your problem with me?" this bitch asked.

I informed her: "I got a problem with bitches I don't even *know*..callin' *me* a bitch!"

"Well I got a problem with you sleepin' with my man."

Now, here came what—at the time—was a fairly major conundrum for me: Bridget—besides being Jimmy's sometimes girlfriend—was also the girl who sold Xanax pills to Chuck's entire house. Pissing Bridget off was not an option.

I asked her if she had any Xanax on her.

She told me to stop sleeping with Jimmy and I said:

"Well, whatever happens." As in: *I'll fuck him any time I want to*—because apparently my brain was wired to be a horrible, pathological, murderous, psychotic bitch to this bitch. Something about that whore bag just *made* me into a bitch. Around Bridget, I automatically *transformed* into a bitch *just for the sake of* being *a bitch*!

Girls will understand this.

Guys: get a girl to explain.

Sometimes people would come over to my house too—but mostly not because the baby was there and my landlord lives behind us. Even so, I can think of plenty of times last summer when everyone was over at my house hanging out and getting loud.

Slacker—the one who introduced me and Chuck in the first place—was drifting on the road between Pennsylvania and Florida..also between Pennsylvania and California because he could get seasonal jobs with weed farmers, clipping buds. You get to smoke all day—*plus* you get weed as part of your pay. Slacker and his friends are all filthy dirty and road hardened. They're cool..crass..boisterous..drunk. They're Loren's friends and a couple of them are in bands. I love Loren because Loren is a) rowdy b) drunk. Everybody was drunk and for me, everyone had a drunk animal alter ego. When all the different animals came to my place, stuff kept getting broken and with our sizable zoo it was hard to keep everybody fed.

We brought the outside table into the house just so everyone had places to sit. We played drinking games with a deck of cards from 6:30am till 3am. No one actually slept—we just made out with randos on whatever couch, bed, floor, recliner, porch, grass—wherever. I tried to keep people Phillip didn't know out of his room but—as you surely know—people have a voracious appetite for getting it on with people they've never gotten it on with before.

It was really so much fun.

- - - -

The whole time Ashley was wiping up Chuck's cum with the inside of her vagina, baby daddy Aaron was in Fort Jackson, Columbia, South Carolina, lying in his bottom bunk in an Army barrack, imagining he was standing inside a shit-smelling Port-O-Let, scrubbing the interior cavity of Private Wilson's rectum with his cock. Ashley pretended Chuck was Aaron. Aaron pretended Private Wilson was his baby momma and that he didn't mind fucking Private Wilson *in a pinch*—but ultimately he was going to have to convince one of these nineteen-year-old Army cuties to risk it all to play with him the Port-O-Let game.

"Keeping a man without pussy is a sin."

Aaron confided this to me when he returned from Basic. With what Ashley told me about Aaron's new library of sexual positions that appeared out of nowhere when he came back from the Army, and with this one-line summary from Aaron of his experience at Fort Jackson, I wondered if he really had been fucking guys up the ass in the Port-O-Lets of Columbia, South Carolina when he was supposed to be doing push-ups and getting yelled at by drill sergeants for not having his headgear properly angled and so on and so forth.

But for all their cheating, and all their fights, Ashley and Aaron loved each other. Ashley stayed home in their Binghamton apartment and took care of Christopher—which Aaron's BCT was paying for—rent, food, car, gas, baby formula, and alcohol. They wrote letters to each other the whole time Aaron was at Basic Combat Training—real letters, with pen and paper (which, because I was born before the internet, makes them seem more romantic to me than email). Ashley was proud of Aaron for what she thought was growth or maturity—taking financial responsibility for her and Christopher by joining the Army, which was definitely not something he wanted to do.

And Ashley told me, in more than one of her frenetic rants, how *excited* she was for their future. You could tell, by her voice, by her eyes, that the mad energy inside her *knew* that everything was going to work out between them.

- - - -

One time Lauren left the house and he said:

"I'll be back later."

I never believe him when he says that—coming from Loren that could mean: *I'll see you in two years*. I was waiting and resting on the couch and I fell asleep and woke up to a tapping on the bottom of my foot. I opened my eyes to Loren and a big group of people behind him and he said, "You'll be alright," in this mystical way that would have annoyed the hell out of me if it came from someone else.

We resumed what was now an almost round-the-clock cycle of drinking games (heavy on the drink—light on the games).

Phillip brought down his set of Bose speakers—a bass and two tweeters that you can hook your devices up to. Someone would play from their phone, then a YouTube playlist..it became a big deal whose turn it was (to make sure everyone got their turn). Also, you had to be careful not to pick a shitty song—you would get booted off your turn if you did. You know any playlist has a certain vibe, and it can fuck up everyone's spiritual and mental state. Our group was tight—if you fucked up the vibe, not just one or two people knew it..*everyone* knew it. A volunteer would unplug your device, hand it back to you, and say:

"You can't fuck up the vibe."

Then you—the guilty party—would drop your head in shame and for *minutes* you would question your misjudgment of the vibe. Sad times.

## 104
Slacker would come to my house some mornings and say:

"Do you wanna go?"

My answer was always, "Yes." Hell yes.

Slacker would drive, going through and through the back roads of Pennsylvania, deep into the mountains. We took turnoffs, looked over outlooks, snuck into abandoned houses. Slacker showed me the house he was currently squatting in—it was a brand new, completely furnished house. The doors were unlocked—poor criminals from Allentown or north Philly..no offense but low-class thugs just steal from their own neighbors. People like that lack the *imagination* to drive outside of town and steal crap from the model home in a new subdivision. And there was tons of stuff that could be stolen and sold, but Slacker wasn't into that—to him this was just a rent-free home with a comfy bed to sleep in. A back porch overlooked the river. We'd sit out there all night, lounging in these plush chairs in our bare feet, enjoying a glimpse of the views that America's small-time millionaires wake up to every day of their lives. In the morning we'd go back to Chuck's house and barbecue. Then we'd go back out again.

Slacker sold weed so there were always people calling him and we'd go deliver to them at their various locations. Slacker laughed out loud (at nothing) and he talked to himself when he drove. In his loose soup of phrases was the shocking and the divine, and occasionally one of these served as a starting point for a conversation—but mostly Slacker talked and I listened..or else I had silent conversations of my own, in a brain which had grown into a tangle like the rose gardens where Lisa had taken me as a child. I was no longer gentle all the time. I was no longer welcoming all the time. I could only hope I was capable of carrying a blossom or two..because—believe me, I know—I am a bush of thorns.

This is me. This is Slacker. Slacker is driving his truck. I am listening to everything that comes out of his mouth as he waxes unpoetic:

"So I gotta tell you something—right? Right?"

"Right."

"Right. You know your friend Idea, right—what was her other name?"

"Iris."

"Iris the Idea. Right!! Your cute little friend."

" 'Cept she ain't cute anymore—"

"What??" Slacker shouted over the road noise.

"I *said*: She ain't cute no more after she got jumped—"

"By that guy named Woody."

"Wait—you know about this?"

"Oh yeah. What's not to know? We fucked that guy up."

"Because he gave Iris a bloody lip?"

"*And* because she's your friend, and *we don't let* anybody mess with your friends *or you*..so that's the short reason we had to bust that motherfucker up."

"Tell me the long version."

Slacker looks at me, then the road we're speeding down, then at me again. He says:

"So it's like this: me and Zach and Constance—"

"*Zach* Zach—like my son?"

"That very one you gave birth to, I believe his real name is Zachariah—does that clean up the matter a little bit?"

"Uh..yes, the matter is crystal clear."

"*So*, as I was *saying*, before being *rudely* interrupted—"

"Sorry."

"No problem. So me Zach and Constance found this guy Woody and told him to get his ass in the fucking truck before we blew his brains out in the fucking OddLots parking lot, you know what I'm saying?"

"Did you really have a gun?"

"Sittin' right behind ya, sister."

There were two rifles behind me—a shotgun and a .22—I had noticed them before but never really thought about it.

"So what happened next?"

"Woody got his ass into the fucking pickup, is what happened next. Constance put an OddLots bag over his head—a paper bag—so the motherfucker couldn't see *shit*."

"I'm sorry who's Constance?"

"You know that zipperhead bitch with half a shaved head be *mad* interested in teenage pussy? That girl with skeletal implants? She got her *tongue cut in half* so she can give girls better head."

"Oh! *Constance!*"

"Yeah, now you know her. So me Zach and that dyke Constance go way out in these woods—way past anywhere I've ever driven you. I stop my truck on the side of the road. Then we get that guy who busted up your friend's lip and we walk him into the woods. All the while me and your boy Zach are talking like:

"Is this far enough?"

"No, this ain't far enough—someone could still hear a shotgun blast from here."

"*Zach* said that?"

"Yeah. Your son Zach's a mean motherfucker."

I felt such a swell of love for Zach right then. I knew in school he went too far, but I didn't know he would go this far just to avenge a casual acquaintance of mine.

Slacker continues, and when he gets to the exciting parts, he accelerates.

*"Did you kill him?"*

"No, no, nothing like that. We kicked his ass. Constance pushed him down next to a pine tree, in all the needles around the base of the tree. I mean what can I say? What the fuck more do you want me to tell you, Davina? You wanna know that your son was kicking this guy in his testicles, using a loaded .22 rifle as a club and beating Woody in the face and telling him never even to *look* at Iris again or we'd bring him back out here again but *next time* we were gonna make him dig his own motherfucking grave?"

I was beaming.

"I think your son watches too many movies, Davina."

"He gets it from me. Please, continue with your story."

"That's pretty much it."

"*Bull. Shit.* Tell me everything."

"Oh, right, well there *was* the part where we took turns shoving my loaded shotgun down his throat. Like, not *just the tip*, where his lips and teeth are and shit—we stuck that shotgun barrel past that little punching ball in the back of your throat and all the way down *into* his throat, like it was some kind of feeding tube or something..or you know those sword swallowers..what I'm saying is two of us had to hold him by his head to get his neck straight so we could get the barrel down farther."

"How far did it go?"

"I'd say to his stomach, 'cause he puked up all this gray/brown stuff like the stuff that's inside a deer when you cut it up..the stuff that's between the organs?"

"Yeah, I know about that gray/brown stuff."

"Yeah, I'm guessing we tore a hole in his gut."

"Yeah, I'm pretty sure you did. Where is he now?"

"In the hospital. In intensive care."

"Did you take him there?"

"Fuck no! We left his ass on the side of the road about 50 miles from Hallstead."

"It's called an epiglottis."

"What are you talking about?"

"That little punching bag in the back of your throat—it's called an epiglottis."

"Well, *I* just learned *my* new thing for the day!"

Slacker hits the steering wheel.

"Let's get some beer!" he says.

We drive all day, drinking on the back roads, sticking my bare feet out the window and feeling the wind blowing in my hair. Those were the best days, with Ashley watching the kids and no matter how late I came home, my daughter always yelled at me like she was the parent and I was the child.

When Ashley was done yelling, I went to Zach, where he was at least pretending to be asleep. I brushed his long, black, punk-style hair so his ear was uncovered and I imagined him sticking Slacker's loaded shotgun down Woody's throat just because Woody had punched Iris in the lip. I said one thing to Zach:

"I'm proud of you."

He stirred and mumbled something I couldn't understand. Then I realized what it was. What he said to me, through his sleepy brain, was:

"We don't call 911."

I said, gently, "What does that mean?"

And Zach mumbled again: "It means we handle our own business right here in Hallstead."

He turned over, and I smiled big. I was beaming at my son.

I hope he remembered enough of our interaction to know that I was proud of him, even if it was only in his subconscious. And I hope he knows what I'm proud of him for: standing up for right, even if the way you do it is wrong.

It's people like that—people who understand loyalty—that I want as friends. And I forget sometimes that I live in backwoods Pennsylvania, where a guy making a girl's lip bleed isn't something the police get into. No, no—things like that we handle ourselves. Maybe it's illegal, maybe it's immoral, but it makes me feel right at home.

## 105
I've never been a fan of the cops, boo. If I was poorer or angrier or had less of a brain, I'd prob'ly be one of those cop-killing motherfuckers who goes to the biggest police station around with some assault rifle they got off the internet and blows away like 20 or 30 cops, just popping their heads off like grapes. Fucking cop motherfuckers—when's the last time any of them did anything good for *you*? All they do these days is kill little black boys—how am I supposed to be happy that some of my tax dollars are going to support *that?* As far as I can tell, eighty percent of 'em are racist, gun-toting assholes granted the right to kill unarmed civilians and get away with it every, single, time. I thought the power to execute lay with the judicial branch, but no—really—we give that right to cops: the right to execute unarmed people *who haven't committed a crime*. This country makes me *sick*.

One time I lost a cop who was coming after me. I had the boys in my car—I was so proud of myself that day! Did you get down that I've never been a fan of the law?

*Yes, I have it: Wannabe cop killer, hates fucking cop motherfuckers.*

Ok, add child molesting in there because I'm pretty sure the cop profession either attracts or creates perverted, lowlife, child-molesting motherfuckers.

*Right. Child-molesting motherfuckers. Got it.*

Are you making fun of me?

*No, not at all. I think—and Oscar Wilde would agree—that on the whole cops do more harm than good. I say roast ‘em all and pass the bacon.*

Lol bacon is right boo and I like mine crispy! So this donkey-fucking nineteen-fifties-looking motherfucker with the fucking *aviator* shades and the silver star pinned to his shirt—that was probably his prized possession—I mean you can *see it in his eyes* that he's fucked donkeys at least fifteen, sixteen times—this motherfucker is speeding down the other side of this divided highway and you can see he's doing like *eighty*, *ninety* on this road that's supposed to be fifty-five. That jack rabbit-eating motherfucker passed me going the other way and at the time I had PA plates and New York stickers and shit was expired and I saw this Muppet-fucking cheese-head cop look at my car..and..I watched him turn around in my rear-view mirror and there was a line of cars between us so I said, "Hold on, boys!" and I just ran straight off the road..we were in a field of wildflowers and they were like hitting the car and we were knocking off their buds and some of the buds came in through our open windows and the boys were in the back screaming.

We went over the top of that hill where the wildflowers were and at the bottom I made a few quick turns—just typical shit you learn from watching *The Bourne Identity*—stuff like spies do when they need to avoid the local cops. That's what I was pretending anyway. We zipped into a neighborhood I had never seen and made a few more turns for good measure. Then we came back out on another street and we saw the cop driving slowly like looking for us. I let his dumb ass drive right by—the fucker must have been blind 'cause all three of us were holding our arms out the windows with our middle fingers dialed up, telling him to go to hell.

"Zachariah, Phillip. You see what just happened here? WE DON'T LIKE PIGS IN THIS MOTHERFUCKER. CAN I GET AN OINK? OINK, BOYS—PIGS ARE FOR PUTTING ON A SPIT. YOU LET THEIR FAT AND THEIR BLOOD RUN INTO THE GRASS AND YOU KNOW WHAT'S LEFT? *YOU KNOW WHAT'S LEFT?"*

"What, Mom?"

"MOTHERFUCKING *BACON*, THAT'S WHAT. WHAT DO YOU DO IF A COP EVER ASKS YOU ANYTHING?"

"Lie."

"Run away."

"NOPE, YOU DON'T RUN AWAY BECAUSE THEN THE PIG WILL SHOOT YOU IN THE BACK. YOU DON'T LIE BECAUSE THAT PIG WILL SHOW UP IN COURT AND USE THAT LIE AGAINST YOU. AND YOU'LL END UP IN FUCKING JAIL. UNDERSTAND?"

"Yes."

"Yes, Mom."

"IF A PIG EVER STOPS YOU, YOU SAY NOTHING. YOU CALL ME. WE'LL GET A LAWYER."

I was imagining all this in such exquisite detail that I'm sweating from nervousness. I almost couldn't drive. I looked around the car and saw all these wildflower heads, and I just thought about how many flowers I had killed to get away from that cop. By fleeing him I greatly increased the sentence I would have gotten if he had caught me. My mouth was dry and I had flashes of my kids going to foster care because of the wildflower stunt and because I was born a goddamn infernal maniac a lot worse and a lot more subtle than anything Dante ever thought up.

I watched the pig go, then I pulled out and jumped on the freeway back to PA—I used the normal freeway entrance ramp this time—we were in New York where we got back on the highway, but the Pennsylvania border is really close.

The boys were screaming and shouting and throwing wildflower heads out the windows. I guess my stunt made them crazy 'cause they were making up songs about me being some kind of a hero who had vanquished the New York City cop. It was Phillip who first introduced the word "vanquished" into their songs.

"Where did you learn the word 'vanquish?' "

"I don't know."

I shake my head. Probably every twelve year old in the world knows that word and I'm just light years behind.

I guess my little run-in with the New York popo doesn't seem like a big deal but it was thrilling for us at the time. I mean, nothing ever happens in Hallstead so after a while you tend to get starved for the dramatic.

## 106
For the rest of my life I'll always be paranoid that every father is molesting his daughter. Last summer we had a fundraiser here for a friend of the boys he shot his head off—

*Shot his head off?*

Yeah, poor guy. Schizophrenic poor guy. Dead.

But at this fundraiser there were bands and booze and tons of people. The fundraiser was for his family, you know, to help the wife and kid make a smooth transition after the guy who brought home the bacon couldn't live in a schizophrenic head anymore. I have some mental illness—not schizophrenia—but I'm amazed that guy lasted as long as he did. Normal people rarely kill themselves—90% of suicide is attempted or completed by people with a major mental illness. Read the articles—it's all there.

So I'm at this fundraiser and I don't have one-hundred dollar checks to drop on this kid's college education or his mother's Lortab addiction. I drift out of the grievance line once I'm done giving the mother a hug. Didn't seem right to do much more or to say much at all to a woman whose sick husband turned his head to a stump with the family shotgun.

I wanted to see what it looked like—to make it more visceral to me. We're all crying over a dead body that isn't even here. I've learned to keep my mouth shut around culturally normal human beings *because they're very easy to frighten*.

I hung out at the cash bar and drank a mixture of drinks that would stagger the digestive tract of a baby horse.

Me and the bartender talked. His name was Raffaello or some other bullshit name he was using to get more tips. I didn't tip him once. He even put on a fake accent that sounded slightly Italian.

"Tips are welcome!" He shouted this even though I was the only one buying drinks and I was standing three feet away.

I asked Raffaello a bunch of questions like: Did it hurt squeezing that big head through your mother's vag? Are you trans? Are these tips going toward your college fund? But he answered nothing so I just ordered drinks from him and wondered what the world had come to if you couldn't talk to your bartender. I mean a bartender needs to be a bartender *and a therapist*, in my opinion.

But there was this teenaged girl there at the cash bar with me and we talked for a long time and I got very drunk and I sensed some tragedy in her eyes—something she and I had both been through and on my tenth mixed drink I decided she'd been raped by her father and she was thin like a Disney character with a flowing full-length dress with bits of lace classily fixed here and there.

I leaned in to her ear and cupped my hands around what I had to say—which was:

"My daddy used to pull up my dress during nap time and then he'd pull down my panties and I can still smell the cum—can you smell it?? *Can you smell it??!!*"

I put my fingers under her nostrils so she could smell Jessie's cum.

She pulled back from me.

"What are you talking about?" asked this perfectly reasonable girl.

"I won't tell anyone," I say.

"There's nothing to *tell*," she says. "Excuse me."

Then her dad came and said:

"Who's your friend?"

"Her name is Davina and we are not friends and I would like us to go now. Please."

But I was onto him—I had this motherfucker's *number*. I followed this guy and his beautiful daughter and the ten drinks inside my blood plus every wrong thing I learned in childhood spewed out of me verbally and for about a minute, everyone at that fundraiser stopped imagining a mentally ill father blowing his head off with a shotgun and they listened to *me*. And this is what I said:

"You *rapist* motherfucker. You think no one knows? I can see it in your *eyes* what you do to her at night. I know what you do!! I'M TALKING TO YOU, YOU STOCKBROKER-LOOKING MOTHERFUCKER!! Does your *wife* know? Did you convince your pretty little girl that this was all *her* idea, from the very start? So now you've convinced her that it's all *her* fault—that you can't help yourself when you fuck her cute little white pussy? Do you make her do it without protection? I *know* you stockbroker types and you *always* do it without protection—it's not real sex unless you do it without protection! I bet you *love* fucking little Beth there without a condom—I bet your droopy little dick knows every ridge, every bump of that girl's puss."

Everyone in the entire square was silent.

The guy turns to me.

"I have never raped my daughter. Or touched her inappropriately. You are extremely drunk. *Extremely drunk.* I suggest you go home as you are disrupting these people who are mourning an incurably sick man who has left behind him a wife without a husband and a son without a father."

The now non-rapist stockbroker and his daughter Beth were staring at me intensely.

My kids found me: Ashley, Zachariah, Phillip—holding my hands, standing between me and this guy.

"Are you saying that you never raped your daughter?"

"Never."

"Never touched her inappropriately?"

"No, and *why are you asking me these questions*?"

"Just a look in the eye."

"What?"

"Just a look in your daughter's eye. I saw something. Maybe it was a glimpse—"

"We're going," the man said. He grabbed his daughter's hand.

"Just one more thing," I said. "All this time, when you were raising up your cute little baby Beth to the beautiful girl she is now, *not* touching her inappropriately when she was young, *not* raping her when she got older..did you not do it out of a sense of right and wrong, even though deep down you really wanted to—or did you not do it because you really didn't want to?"

Beth came to me and gave a little hug. I couldn't tell if it was her upbringing or she really wanted to hug *me*.

Beth and her dad left.

Ashley dragged me and the kids away from the fundraising crowd and off the square in the direction of our home.

That girl having hugged me launched a million thoughts which launched a million questions which landed as a million theories which consolidated into a single answer which is that I *am the crazy one*.

Observation: *Everyone else* that I see seems crazy to me.

Conclusion: I *am the crazy one.*

- - - -

I had seen that guy around town and I didn't like him anyway but I felt bad for embarrassing everyone. Really my kids mostly—they never know when Mom's gonna have a major meltdown. They never know when Mom's going to *invent* something that doesn't exist, then cuss it out in the middle of Hallstead.

My point is I'm a paranoid fool.

That afternoon Ashley set me up a chair on the porch and she made me mojitos. I sat there and processed me going crazy on that guy at the fundraiser. I need therapy—I know that. I'm willing to go, even *want* to go—the problem is paying for it.

Weather-wise, it was beautiful that day (I remember that day pretty clearly). Every summer that I've had in this house has been great—the winters though, are so dreary, so bleak, that they make me want to kill myself.

That fall, Chris (Zachariah's dad in case you forgot)..Chris and me and his wife had a little fun. I came in his wife's mouth before Chris let us all sleep together. His wife came in my mouth too. We planned it for like a month and I bought new sheets and we had a date and everything. It was sweet.

That was the high point of the fall.

Then it got cold again. Sometimes I had money for the fuel truck and sometimes I didn't. The days the truck came—I mean we *celebrated*. When the fuel was out, I heated our house by putting the oven on broil and leaving its door wide open. I did the same thing with the clothes dryer. We'd all just stay in bed cocooned under quilts, wearing gloves, wearing hats.

In this cold, dark state, cuddled in bed, the weather and our lack of fuel got together with my childhood mind (which was heavily conditioned by my parents' words and actions) and their combined entity said things like: *I deserve to be freezing. It's my fault that my children and their children are living in sub-zero temperatures. It's my fault that we don't have enough income to pay the heat. I deserve for bad things to happen to me. I deserve to be uncomfortable in every way possible. I'm disgusting physically—no one will ever have sex with me again. It's so cold.*

*It's* so *cold.*

*You know, it wouldn't be that bad if Ashley and her little Christopher, and me, and Phillip, all died in our sleep tonight—froze to death in our cute little house.*

*It's not death I wish for us—it's an end to the struggle.*

*So sometimes, yes, I pray to lie down in bed and never wake up.*

But then I remember: that's not me. It never has been.

Sometime in my youth, I got out a clean sheet of paper and I wrote at the top: How to Become an Unstoppable Force:

That's all I wrote!

## 107
Meeting a new friend doesn't happen all the time. I mean of course people are constantly flowing through our lives, but very few stick—and all you people on Facebook with 800 "Facebook friends"..those aren't your friends. And you damn well know it.

Friendship now is reading a post about your high school boyfriend's dog's birthday party with a cake made out of dog food and in the background you see your old boyfriend's current wife and you can tell that they live in a rich neighborhood—one where the cops stop you for not being white—and you wonder how a tatted-up, pot-smoking social idealist married some vanilla-looking bitch prob'ly never did a line of coke in her life.

That's Facebook friendship, Twitter friendship. I'm talking about old-school friendship where you met someone face to face and weren't sure if you were gonna fuck 'em or kill 'em. You might think that sounds terrible, but you have to know something about me: For me, it isn't really real unless some blood is drawn.

So I'm gonna tell you about this friend I met—who has everything to do with the book you're reading now.

In my life story that you're reading, we've gone from before the time there were cell phones (when it was land lines and then cordless phones), then to the birth of the internet and its limitless chat rooms, to now, the age of Twitter.

At this point in my life I was crying myself to sleep at night. I know why—it's because relationship after relationship was falling apart, leaving my heart split open, bare, used, forgotten. You might not be able to see it on my face, but I do have emotions sometimes..I get hurt..I'm not *quite* indestructible.

I was going through a really hard time with a relationship that lasted almost a year. Then we broke up. Then I had a relationship with a guy that was really intense: we had been friends for seven years—beyond friends—just day-to-day deep love talks, like pulling at my heart from every direction. It was all long distance—I never even met him. But we talked all day, every day. Then all of a sudden it ended abruptly by his decision. I felt abandoned. His breakup speech sounded like it was written by a third grader—which was a real possibility since he had a brother in the third grade. I couldn't get over it because I felt left, dropped, like everything we said and everything we shared *meant* something to me—but not to him. He could just walk away fine and I'm left holding onto as much of our love as I can with two arms and my head, everything bleeding out like sand. But he was gone, and soon all of our confessions and shared dreams and gentle comforts faded away for me, too. His loss was especially hard to adjust to because it was so sudden, and given how involved we were, to go from that to nothing was very hard.

Cut to: crying myself asleep every night.

Cut to: the invention of Twitter.

Stir in one helping of the Madonna lyric that says: *Nothing takes the past away like the future.*

I was mourning when I met you, basically. I was in a really hard spot. But I'm past that now and you helped me immensely and I am very grateful.

I'm telling you, boo, before I met you, I had to change my pillowcases every morning—they were drenched in tears and snot: my body's way of telling me to get a new friend.

So I did. I did the thing you do to make new friends: I put myself out there. I didn't go to any parties (I wasn't even sure there *were* parties that real-life people my age went to anymore). So I did the modern thing—I got a Twitter account.

It works on my phone so I don't need like an iPad or something like that to be connected. Plus my phone is always with me so when I'm washing clothes or washing dishes or even while I'm vacuuming I can Twitter—I can be *on* Twitter—I can *Tweet*? I don't know, I'm 42 fucking years old..I'm a *grand*mother..I can't be expected to remember all this lingo.

What I can do is find new friends on Twitter. I follow painters, I follow poets, I follow people in England, people in Georgia. Some people I talk to..talk back. Some never do and I get the idea it's because they think they're too cool to accept a compliment on their poem by lowly little me. So there's ego (especially among the marginally talented) but there are other people who are doing work—lots of work. There's a trans boy who has written *seven* books, who is also a painter and a costume maker—and a fiend for vintage Nancy Drew computer games. And his paintings are *phenomenal*—like this trans boy is *really* talented..the kind of talent you only find in a handful of people in the world at any time. I follow the most amazing people I can find and I talk to anyone who will talk back. Pretty soon I'm on Twitter 24/7 just like I used to do with chatrooms in Yuba City.

I meet this one guy, goes by the name of *clownfysh*.

He posts links to a document he's writing. I click through. The document's title is *Untitled document* and I start reading *and it's good*. Pages and pages of nonsense, some loosely connected threads, personalities, alliteration.

It's just weird.

That's what I'm thinking: *It's just weird.*

Or is it? Maybe there's structure *and* weirdness.

I watch the document being written on my screen—it's shared between clownfysh's computer and the rest of the world. But I can tell by the icons that I'm the only one watching.

He's a slow typist. Totally unafraid of the taboo. He seems to be writing down whatever comes into his head and I think: *How simple. What a simple and genius thing that is to do.*

I say into the chat window on the document how highly I think of this technique he's using.

He says: "The surrealists used it all the time."

I say: "Really?"

"Yes, and they used to write poems where they could only ever see the last word written—they'd cover the rest of it up, then they'd wait like a month to look at that last word and whatever single word came to their mind, they'd write *that* word down. Then they'd cover up the previous word so the last word was the only one showing."

"That sounds like a difficult way to write a poem."

"We could do it sometime, if you want to!"

"We could?"

"Yeah, sure, if you want to."

"But wouldn't it take away from your document?"

"*Untitled document*?"

"Yes."

"Not at all! I'm working on *Untitled document* right now. I'm typing everything we say in chat into *Untitled document*!"

I looked and it was true: he was typing the content of our chat into his *Untitled document*.

I got nervous. "Isn't this messing up your project?"

"Not at all. You can't mess up *Untitled document*. It's implicit in the formulation of the project that everything that happens there is correct. I'm not even correcting spellling..see?"

At that moment with clownfysh, I pictured him as the Cheshire Cat, erasing everything about himself with that bushy tail and leaving me stranded. I looked at his Twitter profile—his name was Matthew Temple. Then I went to his website, and that was the real rabbit hole.

Multiple interests, multiple websites, things called cellular automata which made absolutely no sense. They were beautiful, but..I didn't understand where they were coming from. I had to set parameters on my notifications so I wouldn't get any after midnight—every morning I was watching Christopher so every morning I had to be up at six, taking care of the baby so his momma could go to work. I complimented him through @s and he always responded—though sometimes several days later. I followed him and it was a *big deal* for me—I was like a seventh grader with a crush.

He followed back and I guess you'd understand this even if you don't know Twitter, but it was a big deal to me that he followed me back. Some people are easy to get to follow you, and then there are some who are the medium or big fish and you're not going to get a follow from them unless *they* think you're on their level. I learned that he was almost always drunk. He'd be sitting on the front porch of his grandmother's house drinking gin from a coffee cup, texting me. He drank every day, but I held my tongue because we were definitely not close enough for me to tell him that I thought he had a drinking problem.

Matthew got himself an allowance (this is a thirty-six-year-old man living in his grandma's house, not working, after a suicide attempt in Vermont, losing his apartment, bipolar diagnosis which I agree with sight unseen, etc. etc. etc.)—so he gets an allowance and the first thing he does is upgrade his alcohol. I worry about the shape of my body.

In fact I hate my body!—that's why I like scarring it.

Let's do a shot!—well, I'm doing one. Keep track for me so I don't get butt fucking wasted. Is that a measurement of drunkness? Oh yeah, it is: it comes right after plastered and right before *fuuuuucked up*.

It's ok, though—she's my daughter and I would do anything for that bitch.

I guess I have one last thing to say and that is that I really do think that I've been a decent mom on the whole.

One week later:

I'm embarrassed to say that I'm just not getting along with my daughter (or agreeing with her parenting) and I'm feeling trapped in my own house. Every day I look around at all the new clutter and baby toys and odd food in the refrigerator—this used to be *my* house where I could do what I want. Now I spend every day cleaning and taking care of her baby—not that I mind taking care of him but I feel like I'm *raising* my daughter's kid. I love him to death but I didn't *want* another baby. Now the situation is like I'm the live-in maid-slash-babysitter. Like even when she's not working she barely takes care of the kid—it's me in the living room watching Christopher and Ashes in the kitchen with a bottle of DeKuyper Hot Damn!

And like the other night when I told her my friend was coming over, she complained that *her* boyfriend was coming over. Matthew why didn't I just say *shut the fuck up bitch this is* my *house!* And the worst part is that I feel like a shitty person for 1) raising a person who doesn't respect me and 2) what kind of spineless person puts up with this treatment? I don't even belong in my own house anymore. And how embarrassed am I to tell you I'm feeling this depressed and suicidal over that situation?

I feel literally like I'm dog shit—I know it's petty right? I should be a big girl and take care of my shit but I'll tell you one thing and that is: if I let this turn into a confrontation it will not be good because I will black out and lose my mind. (I don't blame you for thinking I'm ridiculous.)

Me and Ashley, we *will* fight sometimes boo! Like physically :( I mean we're drinking and playing Scrabble and I'll get pissed as fuck because that bitch is smarter than me. I cannot beat this girl at Scrabble, boo—I was winning through the whole game then she just pulled ahead and won smdh. And yes I am proud of that.

I just thought your kids were supposed to be about as smart as you—not too much smarter and not to much dumber—but Ashley has at least 10 IQ points on me, I can just tell by a lifetime of observing that crafty little slut.

She gives me black eyes, too—we take Scrabble very seriously in this house. For us, it's not just a word game—it's more like MMA with alcohol.

*D, we've done a lot today. What do you think about pausing our interview right here?*

Ok, I think that's for the best. I just had to comfort my very drunk daughter about my eventual death—she's been sobbing all night.

*I’d like to hear about that.*

Right now?

*If you don't mind.*

You just said you wanted to stop!

*Yeah but then you teased me about this thing about Ashley.*

Ok, I'll tell you what happened.

Ashes puts both hands on my shoulders and says:

"I want to talk about *you*. I do think it would be good for you to wander around for a while. I'm going to get a house with my boyfriend and be with him..like, just him."

I was telling Ashley how I don't really *want* to be away from them—that I'll miss them but I want to be away from this area.

"I feel so lost right now," I said. "And I'll miss Phillip when he leaves to go to Washington to be with his dad. I've never been away from him."

Ashley kind of grabbed my knee.

I continued talking: "I'm scared—I'm scared for myself, scared of missing people. I mean Phillip leaving—it's kind of a big event in my life."

Ash chugged a bunch of whiskey standing in front of me, rubbing my knee like she was polishing the motherfucker. Then she said:

"What are we going to do with you?"

She was crying and I couldn't tell if she was feeling guilty about the move out with her boyfriend that, along with Phillip leaving—and with him the child support payments—that would make me no longer able to afford this house *and hence become homeless*..or if she was talking about something else (which she was) and when I finally figured it out I said:

"Ashley, you mean *when I die?*"

"Yes."

Ash was getting hysterical, I mean like when you leave a three year old at preschool for the first time. Total separation anxiety and I'm standing right before the kid and she's not even a kid..she's a grown-ass adult that's had too much to drink and she's in random emotional mind.

"Just cremate me."

Phillip was there—he heard it.

Ashes' boyfriend was there—everyone hanging out in the fucking kitchen while we talk about what the kids are going to do with my body when I die. I wanted to scream, "I'M ONLY FORTY-TWO!!" I'm not about to die!! And I especially didn't appreciate that the only reason we were having this talk at all was because Ash was maudlin drunk. The right thing for her to do at that moment would have been to crawl under her covers and sleep it off. But did I *dare* say that to her? No. Because somehow she had taken all my power away—*she* was the parent, *I* was the child.

I really didn't do a good job raising my kids—I try to tell myself I did but I'm not a good mother. This is what I deserve: everyone leaving me..I'm alone..I'm fucking homeless, giving up my movie collection I collected over years and my bed, the only bed I've had that doesn't bring back my back pain that makes me unable to walk..I'm fucking disturbed, I wake up from dreams that everyone is bleeding and I hold the knife, I mean crazy dreams where I'm cutting off boys' genitals with an electric turkey knife meant for Thanksgiving..and this line of boys waiting goes back forever and I'm doing a real rough job—sometimes I get their cock and sometimes I get their balls—a Zorro swing gets me both in one motion. Then I just wake up clutching Phillip and he isn't there..he's moving to his dad's house and I'm going to have to get used to probably my closest relative I've known not being here. Then I'm lying in the dark on top of my covers, and there's no one left in the Hallstead house but me. No one to smoke with, no one to laugh with, no one to help pay the rent. Deep down I'll always suspect my kids left me not to pursue their own lives, but because they couldn't stand to be around my eccentric, crazy, fucked-up, child-molested self.

I shouldn't be left alone with adults—let alone children. The only good things I ever did for my kids is never molest them and teach them how to read—otherwise I'm a fucking scumbag bruh.

My boys just found out their gramma has liver cancer.

I feel sad for them.

It's not Lisa, it's their dad's mom. I feel bad for them to have to go through that. It's sad for them and their dad. Chris's mom is not my favorite person—we never got on well. To be honest she's the most hateful person I've ever met but I feel sad for the boys.

I remember the day the kids' gramma was scheduled to be taken off life support. All day I was nervous about what the state of my kids would be when they get home. Ashley took it worse than all of them which is curious to me since that's not even her grandmother.

In the afternoon I came downstairs to Phillip throwing things around :( I was hugging him and he's so much bigger than me I actually felt small. He sobbed into my chest and I hoped, even for a big boy, that between his mother's breasts would be a comforting place to rest his head.

His tantrums got worse after Chris's mom died. Instead of twice a week it was every day that Phillip had one of these fucking megalodon outbursts where he stomped around slamming doors and saying not-nice things and every day I have to be so calm and internalize my own thoughts and sometimes I just get really tired of my son yelling at me like he is *possessed*. I'm crying in my bed at 9pm, hoping Phillip will wear himself out and just *go to fucking sleep*.

It's like living with a monster in the house. It's a monster you gave birth to and know and love—but he has so much energy that he has no idea who he's hurting when he gets like this. He says and does things he doesn't remember once he's calm. You remember his diagnoses, right? Asperger's. ODD. ADHD. And you should know by now that I don't even *believe* in psychiatry—not wholeheartedly—but I'm at the point where I'm willing to believe in different things if they will just *help my son*.

I woke up close to 11 today and Ashley was just getting the baby out of his crib and Ashley was already drunk so that was not a pleasant way to start my day. I'm telling you it's hard to keep my shit together sometimes. It just sucks because the baby is on such a routine when I take care of him—he doesn't understand the change of Ashley's day off or staying in his crib all morning 'cause no one's looking after him. I feel so bad for him like he's mine I get angry as fuck..I mean I'm all for drunken days off—you know that.

*[Yeah. But neglecting the baby..](#)*

Yeah that's shitty. How did this happen?

I've been thinking about how having a child doesn't make another self of you—it's a whole other person as much as any stranger.

Matthew?

*[Yeah, sorry. I was catching up. I fell behind.](#)*

Do you want me to talk slower?

*[No, just do your thing. I'll type faster.](#)*

Hah. I have been measuring my speech and giving you pauses so you can type it all.

*[I noticed. That's very conscientious of you.](#)*

Well. Ok. So Ashley is getting drunk as fuck right now while baby is napping, so I can count on me being the one to take care of him when he wakes up since Ashley will be passed the fuck out.

An hour later I came downstairs and no one was watching the baby..everyone's sleeping while Christopher runs around doing whatever. *Fuck my life.* I just shook my daughter by her shoulders and told that bitch:

"Your baby is running around without supervision and starving!"

She's just like, "Oh, I know!!! I guess I'm being a bad mom!"

*No fucking shit.*

To help Ashley's just fucking insane drinking, she goes out sometimes with this guy she broke up with a while back and this fucking upstanding citizen likes to drop my daughter off at our house falling down drunk. When she gets home I hear her stumbling up the stairs, laughing loudly to herself at whatever she found *so hilarious* inside that drunken head of hers.

It occasionally goes the other way, though. I remember this day when Ashley had the day off. I slept in that morning and when I got up, Ashley was cleaning and doing laundry and she got up with her own baby for once—which to me was a miracle of galactic, fucking *Star Wars* proportions. Normally she would let him lie in his crib for two or three *hours* while she hit the snooze button over and over without ever actually waking up—so Christopher's cries woke up everyone in the house *except* his own mother. I would eventually take him out of his crib and make my coffee and watch him until Ashley woke up—typically around noon. So, yeah, days like that one, when I woke up to Ashley cleaning—those were kinda cool :)

*[Do you think what you said to her the other day has sunk in?](#)*

"Yeah I mean she didn't even drink last night! That wasn't a thing I mentioned but I guess that's part of getting up with her kid. She's even going around picking up after the baby! I barely know what to do!!"

And on days when Ashley did go in to work, she called me once an hour, barricaded inside her boss's office, just hating her job. I listened—that's all I could do. Me and Ashley are different here—I always kind of liked my jobs. Then again I never worked for Dunkin' Donuts. Ashley's a smart girl—even as a manager, a donut shop is not utilizing her brain. Some people won't understand this, but I say it for those of you who will: Doing work that is beneath you, even socializing with people beneath you, isn't just *tiring* for the expansive person—it's actually *torturous*, *depressing*, *deadly*. It wears such a person down, because they're surrounded by momos and it paints a picture of the world for them that says the world is *all* momos—that's a suicidally depressing thought for someone like my daughter.

She deadened the pain with alcohol and (ironically) more work.

She would work a closing shift after she had already opened that same day, and she'd bring her comforter and protector with her: vodka and OJ in a silver Thermos. Thirty-five minutes later she'd call, asking me to bring her more vodka at work. She sounded desperate:

"Mom, I just *need* a little more to get me through the *day*."

I tried to figure out the best way to show my daughter love in this situation.

I said to her, "Do you think that's really a good idea? Baby, you know it's the stress you have that's making you drink."

"What stress?"

"How about working two shifts in one day?"

"But they *need* me—Carlos didn't show up and he was supposed to be managing this shift but his dumb Mexican ass is high on pain pills."

"What kind?"

"Why, you wanna buy some?"

"No—"

"You know you're not the best person to give me advice on drinking."

"It's not really about the drinking, Ash. It's the stress of working two shifts in a day..when you're also a mother of a young kid."

"I know!" Ashley says. Then she cries, "I, honestly, can't, do this anymore. I feel like I'm dying, and I want to kill someone, but I don't want to end up in a mental hospital.."

"Like me?"

"I didn't want to say it—but yes."

"You might not! I want to help set you up so your life can be *better* than mine—not worse, not the same."

"So what are you saying? Are you bringing me more vodka or not?"

"Baby, what I'm telling you is *quit your job*. The stress isn't worth it. We'll find another way, Ashley."

"But Mom..don't we need the money?"

"The money isn't worth you being stressed. Stress is the number one killer now—they know that. It isn't cigarettes or alcohol or even crystal fucking meth—it's stress..about your job, about your partner, about the end of the world."

"I'm not afraid about the end of the world."

"Baby you can't get through an eight-hour shift without four refills of vodka and OJ. *I'm worried about you*—and that worry isn't a judgment, it's a momma being afraid for her baby that she'll hurt herself!"

"I won't. Will you bring me the vodka."

"I really think you should think about quitting that job."

"But the money!"

"We'll find another way."

"Well," Ashley says, "I gotta go, so..I guess you'll either bring that vodka or you won't."

I don't say anything.

Ashley ends the call.

I can't ever remember being as worried about Ashley as I was that moment.

But I took her the fucking vodka.

I put the whole bottle into a paper grocery bag and Christopher and I took a walk "to visit Mommy!" It was a grand ol' charade where I handed up Ashley's baby over the glass counter and set the paper bag down gently but it still made a *clink*—I wondered if any of her coworkers saw what we were doing. Ashley hands Christopher back to me and she looks inside the bag then leans over the counter and hugs me.

- - - -

I didn't see Ashley until 2pm the next day.

When she came in the house she was dead, stupid drunk. The girl could hardly stand.

"Why don't you ask your friends at work to drive you?" I said. "Or—I dunno—call me."

"You don't even have a car."

"I could borrow one!"

Ash lies face down on the couch and says:

"If you're going to get mad at me, get mad at me now because I'm straight up 'bout to pass the fuck out."

"Go to your room! I don't want you passed out on the couch and I'm trying to watch TV. Get a puke bucket."

"I don't need a fucking puke bucket."

She stands up from the couch like a zombie—no control over her movements. She starts up the stairs and I put the handle of our pathetic mop bucket in my daughter's hand.

She looks down at it.

"This isn't my puke bucket! This is one of Christopher's toys! It's from his sandbox!!"

"Ashley. This is the same bucket we always use for your puke bucket and so far Christopher doesn't seem to mind."

Ashley makes it to the top of the stairs, turns, sits on the top one, looks down at me.

She says, "So you don't have any hateful things to say to me?"

"I have nothing hateful to say to you. You're my daughter. I love you. I am worried about your health like your liver and shit."

"STAY OUT OF MY LIVER YOU FUCKING HO."

Yes, that is exactly what my daughter said to me—and yes, she sounded like that little girl from *The Exorcist* when she said it.

"Between you and me," I said, "*I'm* the fucking ho?"

"You know what you need to do?" Ash says to me. "You need to get yourself off with that fucking turkey baster. Don't deny it—I've seen you do that shit. You sneak into the kitchen when you think everyone's asleep but sometime's I'll be perched up on top of the kitchen cabinets where you'd never think to look. And there you are, taking that rubber bulb off the turkey baster and sneaking back to your room. The first time I saw you, I sneaked up those stairs and pulled open your door *like a half a centimeter* and I watched you take off your clothes so you were down to your big-ass momma panties and then you were sucking on your clit with that turkey baster and it was like: *errrrsssuuuucccckkk* every time you fucking plunged yourself with it and it fucking made you cum in like one second. So don't think I don't know *your* secrets, Da-*vi*-na. You're a fucking sick piece of shit."

"Yes, yes—I'm a sick piece of shit. Would you please go to your room and get some sleep."

She went that time, and it's a good thing she did—I'm not sure how much more of her mouth I could take.

That night I figured it would be cool if I didn't watch the baby since his dad was here but it turns out he put the baby to bed without dinner (how awesome) and I'm just like *what the fuck*. Can I ever *not* worry about this baby you know I'm not the fucking *parent*!! Are you guys *this* fucking irresponsible *and* this fucking stupid??!!

I can't take it boo I'm going to lose my mind.

Yesterday Ashley is crying to me about how every guy she dates is a loser and I said:

"Maybe you should try being happy with yourself then you attract a different kind of guy."

And she says, "I AM HAPPY TO HANG OUT OUT AND DRINK BY MYSELF."

Jesus *that's* what she said!!

And I said, "That's not what I meant."

But she was drunk anyhow so who gives a fuck. I'm not heartless and I'm not insensitive but *take care of your shit* like *this is not an option*!! If the reason you're not getting laid is that you're falling down drunk twenty-four hours out of every day—well, there's your answer. And one more thing: you're my daughter and I want you to be happy but I truly don't lose a wink of sleep over you not getting laid as often as you'd like. Welcome to the grown-up world—if you're lonely, try the turkey baster.

Maybe I'm making it too easy for her by letting her live in this house and bringing vodka to her work anytime she asks for it. I feel like she doesn't know what it's like to struggle—not that that's a life requirement—but nothing was ever as easy for me as it is for her as far as having a live-in babysitter and not having to worry about paying all the bills. I don't even think she knows how much I do to make her life easier—or what the cost is to me: I never have time to myself. Ashley didn't raise Christopher—neither did Aaron. I did. I love that little kid but it was probably the mistake of my lifetime to let the three of them live here as long as they did.

You don't realize what a bad parent you've been till your kids grow up.

## 113
But..on the flip side..we have days like today where it's so very nice outside that we all can't help but wander our way out of the house. Me and all of us were outside—it was a miracle.

I took baby out and got his little slide and he played and Phillip pulled him in the wagon!

Phillip raked for me. He was philosophizing the way teenagers do when they have that second awakening in life (the first being language). But it was cool to listen to all those thoughts come together in his mind as he starts to understand *who he is* within our world.

That day I'll always remember and I say it is *The Coming of Spring*. I don't think you understand how wonderful it feels to be outside right now—I have no jacket on and I'm wearing flip flops. It's fucking sixty-nine degrees out I could die happy right now.

The next day I had a phone conference with some of Phillip's teachers and it went really well! Shame on me for thinking I always know people's character—or what they'll do next, their level of evil, things like that. They are going to change his schedule a bit to give him more time around less people so he can catch up on some work. I admit I was dreading the call but it went well.

The second thing is I got a letter today from the Children and Youth worker who came by our house that day. It said they investigated claims against me but they closed the investigation because the claims were untrue or did not meet the standard of neglect the state of Pennsylvania finds serious lol..*standard of neglect!!* Lol! Yeah, it was cool, that letter—it was like a big *fuck you* to that school 'cause I know they got a copy, too! :)

*Marching through the house with baby! We're chanting march march march march!* It's cool marching—more like sashaying really lol. See! *See!* I can have a good time when things go my way! I even love taking care of my baby's baby—I just need a good word and someone who's not working against me *some percent of the time*. A sour-apple school board who *wants* your kid to fail—that's not really in the spirit of the job, is it? But eighty percent of these people are just in it for the money. They don't care if your kid goes to college—some of them truly want your kid in jail. I mean, in some places in our country you actually get kickbacks for every kid you send to jail—tell me that's not fucked up.

And in the middle of my revolutionary thoughts, Phillip just saved me from becoming an old crab grandmother who's always reminiscing about "the way things used to be." Phillip just ran by, in the arc of some circle game he's playing with baby Ashley's baby Christopher..and he said, so gently,

"You're an amazing gramma!"

He's so sweet he's all:

"You're so kind and understanding and good and giving to the baby and you're really good and understanding to me too."

He's so nice—you have to wonder if his outbursts in school aren't the direct result of immature teachers and students not behaving in a loving way towards him. Because I'm watching, right now, this "problem child"—when put in nature in a lovely back yard with people who love him—he's not only keeping the baby happy but he finds time to build up his mother with some of the kindest affirmations anyone's ever given me.

That's part of the second awakening of a person. Around fifteen, they start to be capable of everything an adult is capable of—or better. They beat you at Scrabble, they make love with their friends, and *they* encourage *you* when they see that it's needed. They love you back just as well as you love them. At least mine did—if yours didn't I suppose you must have missed an ingredient or two that the recipe clearly called for while making—

*You're an amazing gramma!* Those are words that will ring forever in my ears. When your kid tells you that, it's worth life, it's worth death, it's worth all the other affection and all the money in the world.

At the moment he tells me this, I know without a shred of doubt that the richest person in the world is a mother whose children love her—I feel that richness now and I know there's nothing greater because this feeling makes me feel an overflowing love—I feel, if it were any greater, I would break.

*You're an amazing gramma!* I know!—wasn't that so wonderful of him? To come around to where I sat during his circle game with baby Christopher and slow down just enough to say such lovely things to me. He said it all so matter of factly and so generously and so simply it put me in shock—is *this* the child I raised? In such a crazy house, with Phillip *sleeping on the couch* getting woke up every night with his alcoholic sister throwing up in the toilet in the next room. Going into that school and out of this school so many times I lost count. And don't forget me!—a crazy momma who would still, to this day, suck up any street drug you placed before me, break up prescription pills to snort, and not always drink—but when I do drink, I sometimes cross the line that a mother shouldn't cross in front of her kids.

And still, after he moved, Phillip sent me this text—the last one he ever sent:

"I really do love you Mom you have been the best and greatest mom ever and I love you more than anything and you'll always be the biggest role in my life thank you for always being such a great and amazing mother even when I was a bad kid you stuck through it and still were such an amazing and perfect mother I love you so much thank you for everything that you are."

I locked that text so when I hit *erase all texts* that one stays.

I will keep it forever.

And..

That's about all there is to tell!—I mean *what more* do you fucking *want* from me? I told you things that if *you* did them, *you* would never tell your mother. Or your sister. Or *the whole world*—present and future—by putting it in a book.

But I have a little bit of a different view on those sorts of things than perhaps you do. I believe that only a fraction of what we learn, we learn in school. See, most of my education didn't happen in the schools I went to—it happened by watching the stories that unfolded around me: the stories of my parents, my siblings, my children, my friends..and of course the stories that happened to me.

There's this cool quote from Plato—you've probably heard of it? He says, "Come then, and let us pass a leisure hour in storytelling, and our story shall be the education of our heroes."

Well, *you* are my heroes, and *these* are my stories—and if you can make time, I will make it with you, for I have a few more stories to tell.

Does that give me reason to be unstable now or suicidal or to self-harm? Sometimes I feel that I'm acting like a big baby—my mom says I should just get over it.

And I did—I tried to do that for a very long time. It worked at first, I guess. Every time a thought about Jessie came into my mind I would overwrite it with some favorite music, a counteracting positive thought, or I would just make a cup of coffee and tell myself that I wasn't allowed to think that Jessie thought until the cup of coffee was through. My hope was that by the time I got to the bottom of the cup, I would forget the the thought entirely. It worked at first but then my obsession kicked in. Stacks of thoughts I had pushed aside would all come at me simultaneously before I was even halfway through my cup. Then everything I pushed down—odd things like waking up to the smell of Jessie's cum, my head stuck to the pillow—it all came up like invisible vomit. Every closet, every cooking smell—it all led to thoughts from childhood and I couldn't distinguish what was real from what my psychotic mind was adding in at the last minute before I had the experience of remembering.

That was when everything started catching up with me. Hiding was no longer an option.

But you can't.

Because they have the power.

And you don't.

Lisa as an adult is just as scared as me as a child. She never developed. She's useless.

Kids aren't supposed to have to stand up to abusive adults.

But adults *are* supposed to do that.

And here we get to the scary truth. There's something intrinsic that we learn as children—that adults we know are people we can trust. So spring the lock on the carnival clown—for the devil is coming out to play.

Surprise! You can't trust the friends you had as a kid. You can't trust the adults at church. You can't trust your parents—Lisa and Jessie, the people who buy you food. You can't trust one to keep his hand off your vagina and you can't trust the other one to stop him when he does.
