## 117
The night that Ashley got stuck in Philadelphia, I was smoking pot with Phillip and we were listening to our jam (a Ludacris remix)—this song that Phillip and I play when it's just us in the house because it especially suits us. It's wild, it's a romp—it's music that breaks the rules. Plus it gets you twice as high as you would have been otherwise. We got our high and I took some of Ashley's pot. I felt bad 'cause I actually had to go in her room to get it. I hated to invade her privacy but she was gone and we were here and Ashley's the one who buys pot for the house—I didn't think she'd even notice the theft, I took so little.

You were online, hovering for an interview, when I posted a picture of the glass pipe Phillip and I were smoking from.

> *[Pretty pipe!](#)* you texted.  
>   
> "Yes, it reminds me of the beach! Calum gave it to me."  
>   
> *[Calum is special to you?](#)*  
>   
> I never answer.  

I am closest with Phillip of all my children. I don't let him drink alcohol—I don't think he'd be able to handle it—but pot is fine for him.

When our jam is over, I pour myself shots of tequila while I'm in the shower singing the lyrics of the Ludacris jam at the top of my lungs. I post a picture of the skull shot glass I'm drinking from.

You ask me about it.

I hear the notification and know it's you so I lean out of the shower, half wet/half dry, and text:

"Oh Ashley sent it to me one year for Halloween when she still lived in Berkeley."

I take a few more shots in the shower, singing, feeling high off the Ludacris and the pot and buzzed off the alcohol.

"Is drinking in the shower a thing yet?"

*[Yes. Oh yes. That's a thing.](#)*

"Cool."

I take like my seventh shot.

"You wanna see a picture of my pussy, boo?"

You say you do. But due to my heavy degree of intoxication all it ends up being is a poorly framed image of my dripping pubic hair.

You write back and claim it's a work of art.

When you say things like that I never know if you mean a work of art like it could end up on the walls at MoMA..or if you just mean that *between us* it's a work of art. I sort of hope the latter.

A few minutes later I'm watching movies all cozy in my cozy chair—dressed in my pajamas with an afghan over my lap. I have my device and I'm looking up "how to get help with my electric bill."

There are some possible state funds that can be used for households like ours which don't have enough money to pay our electric bill each months but who presumably deserve for a light to go on when we flip the switch, just like you'd expect in the homes of the rich or semi-rich. There are four classes in our country right now: super rich, semi-rich, poor, and homeless. Thankfully right now we're just poor.

I got distracted from my task of finding help with the heat and electric by my own bookmarks and seconds later my spirits had lightened due to the fact that I was no longer thinking about how fucking poor we are when there are people out there with private jets..or people who *own islands*—I mean that's depressing shit to think about when you only have heating oil about half the weeks of winter.

Some adjectives for those people: rich, luxurious, glamorous. Some adjectives for me: useless, poor, and ugly.

It's very hard on the old conscience when your kids are freezing to death in their own home. We use fingerless gloves so we can type. Phillip wears his jacket in the house. It all adds up to me thinking of myself as a fucking loser. Which I am. I'll probably kill myself soon.

After a series of cold nights like that, I hallucinate, just like a thirsty girl in the desert. From my chair I thought I saw someone's shirt sticking out from around the corner of the door to the kitchen as if someone was trying not to be seen but didn't realize their shirt was sticking out. I was convinced someone was there—I had to get up and look. I scared myself to death approaching the kitchen. With every step, I was surer and surer I was about to be murdered by someone who had snuck into our house. But when I got to the kitchen there was nothing there—just light and air.

Then I thought the edge of a rug that's coming off was a little snake—it was wiggling and everything! That shit made my heart jump 'cause I was startled there was a fucking *snake* in our house!—but nope..just a rug..just the frayed edge of a rug.

It's scary to me what my mind makes up.

Anyway my bookmarks were looming, offering me a taste of my latest fascination: post-mortem photos from Victorian times. Back then it was very expensive to have your photo taken so a lot of times a post-mortem photo was the only one a family had. They would pose the dead person to look peaceful or even alive (with their eyes open) and siblings or parents or whole families would be in the photo with the dead person. I posted a few on Twitter. They are fascinating and beautiful to me.

And, really, I can only hope my own death will be half as beautiful as theirs. But my own family would never plunk down a week's wages for a photograph with my corpse. That's not the style anymore. When I die, I'll be taken in some anonymous ambulance with no lights and no sirens—what's the hurry? And I'll be cremated by some medical student whose night job is to run the Barnes-Kasson hospital morgue. He will be Chinese. His name will be Chen. In the moments before my head burns off Chen will speak to me in Chinese and I'll magically understand. I'll strain to hear him as my face and eyes and ears burn in zillion-degree heat, thinking Chen is offering me at least *one* of the secrets of the universe. But he's not, he's just reciting some mnemonic that will help him with tomorrow's quiz in cell biology, and that recitation is the last thing I'll hear.

## 118
We had an interview that night, my memoirist and I. That meant a formal interview over the phone, which I dreaded every time. You tried to make it easy for me—and you kept your promise to be nice and gentle while we raked through the gory facts of my existence—but it was still hard.

"My book should end with my suicide how cool would that be?!—That would be a big seller I bet."

You're silent.

"I'm just kidding—I think."

"Look, I can't stop you from committing suicide if you want to do that, and you can't stop me from doing the same—"

"Please don't."

"I won't, Davina. I tried it before and I know you have too. It's a hard life and I certainly accept suicide as a rational response in some people to a horrible world. Neurotypical people with less conflict in their brains about the evil we're all complicit in *just by paying our taxes*..people like that have less reason to kill themselves because the system works for them. It puts them in fancy houses or *at least* it puts them in houses *at all*. I have relatives who work in the oil industry. They make good money, their girls each get a new car when they turn sixteen, their college is paid for, and they're generous when they can help the poorer members of my family like me and my mom. But with the oil spills—and *oil spill* is such a euphemism—but with BP's gushers that are hardcore *destroying the ecosystem*, with Exxon's climate disinformation campaign, I say if you help the oil industry in any way, you've got blood on your hands. So..you're probably not going to like this, but given that I've already had a circumstance in my life where I feel I am partially responsible for someone else's death—someone I loved—and given that you're talking about suicide, I have to ask you to promise me that you won't harm yourself as a result of this book."

"I can't promise that."

"Then the project's off. I'm sorry, baby, but I'd rather have a friend and no book than a book and no friend. And I can't let myself do something that might contribute to your death—think of how horrible that would be for me to be a part of."

"Are you listening to how selfish you are? You're talking about *my* suicide but all it's really about is how it would affect *you*."

Then you ask me to sign a contract, and god, how vacant, how vacuous became the space between me and you. I cried for days just that what was once a project with someone I loved had become a legal matter, with you specifying what percentage of the profits each of us would get if this book ever made any money. *I don't care about the money!* and I told you that. You were worried that I might sue you for damaging my character—don't you remember that *I asked you* to write this fucking thing! If I was worried about what you would do with it I never would have come to you in the first place.

But the kicker—the real kicker—is that you made the contract specify that I wouldn't harm myself or kill myself in any way that was predicated on you having written the book.

I was sobbing when we talked about this:

"I can't *believe* that you are worried about getting blamed for my suicide!"

"I'm sorry, Davina, but I don't own your life story and we need some kind of contract—just a tiny, one-page one that says I have the right to tell the story based on your interviews—"

"And that I won't kill myself."

"Yes. And this is coming from a sympathetic person—someone who's felt suicidal and even acted on those feelings. What if I write your story and you kill yourself for whatever reason and then someone in your family decides to sue me and blame your suicide on the book?"

I didn't say to you what I thought next, but it went something like this: *You piece of shit. I have never treated you legalistically. We have said we love each other—even said we were* in *love (when you were drunk). No one in my family would ever* sue *you based on my death—what kind of people do you think we are? The fact that you can't just* trust *me on this, in my mind, this could be the end of our relationship.*

But I did say this one piece that day:

"You really hurt me today, Matthew."

"Davina, I am extremely sorry that the contract hurts your feelings. I can understand how it does. But I need us both to sign it, now that we're getting close to the end of interviews..because..I'm not going to start writing the text of the book until that contract is in place."

"Ok."

"I'll mail it to you."

"Fine, whatever."

"It'll have two copies, you can keep one."

"I think I'll burn my copy."

"Please do whatever you want with it."

"I might smear my period blood and blood clots all over your copy."

"Please do whatever you want to it as long as you sign it and send it back to me. I'll include a stamped envelope."

*You'll include a stamped envelope? You sound like a cheap lawyer—the kind who rents office space over a laundromat. Yeah, I'll send you your fucking contract. Jesus, this always happens—you think you've found a friend and then about three years into knowing them you find out some weird shit about them that just makes you wish you'd never met.*

Anyway we got through our contract hurdle mostly by me just going along with whatever you asked from me. The contract was humiliating—it protected you, not me. I was still operating under the assumption that we trusted each other—and I did trust you.

But I stuck my tail between my legs. I signed your fucking contract. We continued with the interviews. Months later I saw the copy of our contract that I kept for myself and it didn't seem evil. It was actually quite pleasantly written and was just a couple of sentences long. You specified we split the profits 50/50. At the time, I told you I didn't want the money and you said you weren't doing this for the money either, but you said splitting it 50/50 was a way to say we were equals in this project. It took a few months, but I gradually forgot about the contract and got back to the business of telling my story.

"So what do you want to know? I'll tell you about Phillip.

That's what all the shows on TV and the mainstream movies are teaching us—they're teaching us to fear."

"You were supposed to sip it."

"You never told me to sip it!!"

Ashley heaves once, then twice, then throws up in our flimsy little mop bucket.

I hold her hair back while she's puking—mostly liquids. Looks like whiskey to me.

With puke still at the corner of her mouth, she says:

"You fucking did that to me on purpose. Who drinks lemon water for a hangover?"

She wipes her disgusting mouth with the bottom of her shirt. I can see her sexy panties and bra and I think of her riding Benton's cock all night long, in Benton's bedroom in Philadelphia, with a puke bucket beside them in case his cock thrusting made her throw up again.

"Mom, I might not be as intuitive as you, ok? I admit, you are the most intuitive person I know. But I am intuitive, too, and sometimes I feel that you want me to be with a certain boy or girl or one of your friends. It's things you do, yes—like things I hear you say on your phone with your friends and that guy that's interviewing you. Just tell me the truth. Are you planning a threesome between him me and you?"

"No, I'm not."

"Ok. Well. Thanks for answering that. Does he have a crush on you?"

"I don't know. We're using each other—that's what adults do, instead of having real relationships."

"Bullshit. *That's so much bullshit!* I know he likes you because I hear you playing his sexy videos at night. Do they get you off?"

"Royally."

"Is he gonna move up here?"

"I don't know. Probably not."

"Do you wish he would?"

"Ashley, I'm tired. We're writing a book together. It may never be anything but that."

"If he does come up here, I'm going to fuck him before you."

"Why would you even say that?"

"Because I'm trying to teach you a lesson, Mom—that you're old and who's gonna want to fuck your tired-ass pussy when they could fuck mine—"

I slapped her—slapped the *shit* out of her.

She said, "That actually sobered me up a little."

I said, "Ashley, you are a fucking psychopath."

That's all I had to say. I went upstairs. It was so fucking late I fell asleep instantly.

In my dreams, I was being chased through a neighborhood by a group of people—someone trying to hurt me. I go up to people's doors, asking for help—but no one will help me.

## 120
The next morning Ashley argues with me until the clock says ten minutes till twelve, then she walks out in the middle of a sentence—the Harmony's bar opens at twelve. I guess our argument was just a pointless exchange—Ashley's way of entertaining herself while she was waiting to drink.

Sometimes she comes home, sometimes she doesn't—sometimes she meets random guys and brings them home and fucks them on the way up the stairs with Phillip sleeping on the living room couch and her baby in the upstairs bedroom. If Phillip is out late they'll fuck on the couch, fall asleep there, and when I come downstairs I'll find Phillip sleeping on the linoleum tiles in the kitchen.

"Hey, buddy, did you get kicked out of your bed?"

Phillip nods. He stands. He makes the coffee.

"I'll have a talk with Ash," I say.

Now the morning coffee ritual is plagued with awkward looks between Ashley and the guy Ashley just fucked, between Ashley and Phillip—between everybody. And there are cum stains on my couch.

"I'm Davina," I say to this guy who was just rubbing genitals with my daughter.

I reach out my hand, to shake.

He stands there, looks at my hand, and doesn't shake it.

He brushes past me to pour himself a cup of coffee.

When Phillip and I offer him kindnesses, he pretends we're not there.

When Ashley tries to talk to him, he insists they go outside.

To my reading, these are all very bad signs.

Within the space of a week Ashley meets, falls in love with, and marries this guy who doesn't like Phillip or me and who by the way likes to hit my daughter in the face. He goes by the name of David. Ashley moves in with him. The first night he hits her, Ashley comes to stay with me for a few days' recovery.

Her face doesn't look like someone punched her—it looks like someone came at her with a baseball bat.

"You know he's going to keep on doing this."

"We just had a fight, Mom."

"A fight..is yelling at each other over some issue you disagree on. This is physical abuse, domestic violence, whatever you want to call it. Mature adults don't hit each other..*ever*. How can you live with him knowing this might happen again?"

"Oh, it won't, it won't," she told me.

I kept my mouth shut. She wasn't listening. She went to the sink and I watched my daughter drink directly from the tap like a rhesus monkey or some kind of wonderful, primitive animal from the oldest of the old worlds—I felt pride swell in my breast. She wiped her hands on the ass of her shorts and I put my girl to bed.

I burnt sage in my room that night—it needed a cleansing.

Then one morning Ashley was gone—she moved right back in with that asshole. Didn't even tell me she was going.

That's not the end. Oh no. Ashley comes over maybe three days later so I could spend some time with Christopher. From a block away I could see that Ashley had a black eye.

"Hi Mom!"

"I don't even want to talk to you right now. Christopher, you wanna walk around the block with me?"

He smiles real big and says, "Yeah, Gramma!"

I'm walking with Christopher around the block and seething about my daughter's black eye. I guess that's her life now. She got all pissy with me when I asked about it so fuck me. She doesn't want her mother to protect her. And either David is such a monster fuck between the sheets that Ashley's making a rational decision by staying with someone who *hits her in the face*..or (more likely) I somehow raised a daughter whose self-esteem is the size of a pea—which, by no accident, is the same size as mine.

But don't worry..it's ok..because I am going to kill my daughter's husband.

You think I'm kidding.

I've seen every episode of every show on the *Investigation Discovery* channel—*twice*. *Deadly Women*?—I got that shit *down*. *Women Who Kill*?—that's practically *me* if David hits my daughter again.

Ashley goes back home with Christopher.

The next day David beat Ashley in the face and she came screaming back to my house. We agreed she and Christopher would stay with me for a few days but the next morning I woke to an empty house. I couldn't even call her—check out *this* wife-beater tactic: Every time Ashley gets a new phone, David breaks it. He throws it against the wall, stomps on it, snaps it into pieces like a Kit Kat bar. That way Ashley is isolated—she can't call for help.

But you know, as much as I love my daughter, she wasn't the one on my mind—the one on my mind was Christopher. I hate to see Ashley beat up by her husband (especially when it's bad enough that I should have taken her to the hospital) but that's just what happens when David gets mad at *Ashley*.

What happens if David gets mad at Christopher?

- - - -

I go up the stairs crying.

I would do this many times, after talking with Ashley and then her weakly disappearing so she wouldn't have to look me in the face and admit she was going back to her crazed husband to get beat up again.

I guess my fear for her put me in childhood mode: scared, wanting to hide, running upstairs after our latest fight and flopping on the bed. My body ached, yes—but something much deeper in me was wearing too thin for safety.

My mom couldn't stand it when we cried. She always gave us hateful looks and shook her head at us and said to shut up. I still feel sad and hurt when I think of that look on her face—like she hates me. When a child cries, you comfort them—is there any other answer?

My tears are making Mr. Bunny damp. Mr. Bunny is a stuffed animal I've had for twenty-two years. I think he's my oldest possession, and there's magic in that for me—I trust him and at times he is my savior. I'm hugging Mr. Bunny right now and he comforts me. I apologize for getting tears all over him and he says: *It's ok. It's part of my job.*

I keep thinking about my mom and wondering why she hasn't ever loved me and it hurts so bad even still. I think that's why I feel so lonely at my core and I'll never get over it: It's because you only get one mom and if nature twists yours into a sociopathic meth head..well..that's just your luck. The only thing you can do is get used to it, and I can tell you—four decades into this process—that it's pretty fucking hard to let go of the idea that your mother will someday love you.

I remember one time I was mowing the lawn and I was singing at the top of my lungs 'cause no one could hear me—I guess I was having fun—and when I went inside, Lisa was like:

"What were you singing?"

I was embarrassed and I was scared—I didn't want to tell her. I thought I would get in trouble because it wasn't a church song so I just stood there, dumbly mute. And she kept asking me why I wouldn't speak and I couldn't say anything—I don't know why!

She slapped my face so hard.

I just kept thinking about how I shouldn't let anything show—my mother didn't deserve to see my emotions. *Be stoic Davina so Mom doesn't get mad.*

No one *loved* me when I was a little girl.

I mean my siblings did but I had no core value inside me—maybe I had no love for myself because there was no place to internalize it from.

I think that's why I'm lonely forever.

- - - -

*What are your plans for the fall? Traveling? Staying in the house? Are you going to be a drifter, homeless?*

I don't know.

I'm in the living room adding up my bills over and over lol.

I'm going to try to fill out some papers for public assistance—I need to come up with $550 in two weeks for rent. Either that or I'm out of here, I guess.

*Wow babe.*

I don't even give a fuck anymore.

Maybe I'll just burn the house down.

I'm not sure my landlords even have insurance.

I feel weird because I don't want anyone else to touch my stuff but I can't take it with me if I go somewhere, so I kind of want to burn down the house. It feels dangerous not to care about your life too much—like the part of me that doesn't care if I go to jail. It would suck—I would hate it (I hate authority and institutions)—but part of me is like *I just don't fucking care*.

Ashley gets on a schedule of staying at my house during the week and going to David's house on the weekends so she can get her pussy fucked and her face beat in like she's on the motherfucking playground. Christopher stays with me all the time. I feel like I should be able to handle this but instead I'm this disgusting mass of *don't touch me I hate everything all I do is bleed and cry fuck the world fuck you fuck your love*. You don't know how hard it is for me to keep my mouth shut when I'm angry and so I just let tears fall down my face instead..well..not like I could help it. I just have to keep it together until Ashley works her shit out.

Ashley and Aaron were planning to move, not as a couple, but as a family unit, to Syracuse. Ashley wants to move to a city. She and Aaron don't get along but neither one wants the baby to be without his parents. I'm not sure what the state of that plan is but probably something similar to the space shuttle Challenger about 70 seconds after launch.

We go through a phase where Dave stays over but he won't even come out of Ashley's room to *eat dinner*—she takes it up to him and they eat up there smh! What the fuck *ever*. It makes me think less of him to be honest—I try not to but what am I, an ogre? I'm saying I have a house guest *who wants to avoid me* so *badly he goes* outside *to piss so he* won't have to pass me *to get to the bathroom!* Something about that feels disrespectful to me but maybe I'm just taking it too personally. Or maybe that *fuck* just can't stand to look in the eyes of the *mother* who *birthed* the *daughter* whose *face* he turns into a peanut butter and jelly sandwich every night.

Either way, I don't think I can live here anymore.

I just wish I knew how to make a living.

Oh, sweet: Zachariah is texting me to see what's happening and I feel like he's mad at me..and I just feel..like a big problem. He just texted me that he has to *explain* to Kelly and her family about my bad habits and "the company I keep." What am I hanging out with Al *Capone*? Now he's texting me I need to go to the unemployment office. I just feel like I must be the biggest scumbag.

*How would you describe yourself currently?*

As a scumbag.

*I got* scumbag *already.*

*I would describe myself* currently as: anxious, angsty, and perpetually on the verge of running off at the mouth about this *fuckwad* who has taken up residence in my *father*fucking *house!!!*

I wish there was some way I could die tonight.

*I'm sure you can find a way if you really want to, but I ask that you give it one more day before you act on that desire. Because I like your company <3 and you mean the world to me.*

Yes I can find a way you're right.

*I know you can.*

I'm very sad. I feel useless and betrayed and unappreciated.

*Well. I don't know about useless and betrayed but you're definitely under-appreciated.*

Thanks, Math.

We did movie nights where we'd alternate picking a film and then watch the same one at the same time, like a date night but with six states between us. One night, I remember, you were too sleepy and we had to skip the movie. I felt like I was away from you—how weird is that?? And I don't like feeling away from you if you want the truth.

## 121
So check this out: There's just weird shit going on around here plus I feel like I'm crazy..like maybe I should keep my mouth shut I don't even know. I just feel like I don't belong here anymore I'm so uncomfortable.

*What kind of stuff is making you uncomfortable, boo?*

Well, I came downstairs to see why Ashley wasn't tucking in Christopher and had Dave doing it—like I admit that's probably not my business but I just didn't see a reason why she couldn't do it and I thought it must be weird for the baby I mean he doesn't even know Dave that well—none of us do!

Then Ashley comes in from outside and a giant kitchen knife falls out of her coat.

I was like, "What the fuck is going on?"

She just kept saying, "Mom stop. *Mom.* Please just stop."

I'm like, "Stop *what!!*" and I'm yelling like, "What are you doing with a *knife* in your coat and why aren't you tucking in your kid?!"

And she's drunkish of course and she's all like:

"He doesn't *want* me he wants *Dave*!"

And I said, "Oh, *yeah*, okay," all sarcastically.

I just *feel* weird around here. There's a weird vibe and it's making me anxious and scared and *I hate it*. I wish it was warm still so I could leave easily. I'm pissed off at myself for getting into this situation.

*I'm so sorry baby that sounds extremely weird and scary. The knife thing especially.*

Yeah I feel like I'm going crazy Matthew I'm really scared and upset.

*You don't sound crazy to me. Where is Ashley and the knife?*

I put the knife away and they just went outside. I don't want to get pissed off..I don't want to lose my temper..it's scary. I'm just scared.

Yeah I cried a lot.

Getting in now. I feel like I've been through an emotional wringer.

Ashley told Dave that if he came in to talk to me I was going to punch him in the face or stab him in the throat :(

*I don't understand.*

I don't know! I guess I'm a savage!! I was yelling at her for having a knife in her coat before so she thought I was going to be angry and Dave told her to come in and talk to me because he thought I was feeling sad and she said no so he said *he* was going to and she told him I was going to flip out on him and I was like: *If you can all get out of my emotions this will go a lot smoother.*

Of course not. It was a good talk actually. Dave said he's going to buy a new washing machine and he said to ask for anything at all that I need. But you know, the whole time this guy is spinning this kiss-ass story to me all I can picture is Ashley coming in from outside and that giant kitchen knife falling out of her coat.

Ashley and my eyes meet.

I say: "Are you you gonna divorce Dave?"

"I want to but I'm scared."

I'm scared too. Dave seems like the kind of guy that if you divorce him you'd end up with a gangsta-style shootout-slash-hostage situation at your house and CNN would show it to the whole world and that would be your 15 minutes: COPS SHOOT ALCOHOLIC MOTHER AND CHILD IN HOSTAGE SITUATION—TWEAKER FATHER COMMITS SUICIDE IN BACK OF PATROL CAR. MIRACULOUSLY, GRANDMOTHER ESCAPES POLICE FIRE.

I'm thinking of going back to California.

I'm a sensitive person—I can't live with this shit. Like this is the type of stuff Dave does: He comes over to my house using a key I guess Ashley gave him. I'm sitting in the living room in my comfy chair. Dave does not *look* at me, he does not *talk* to me, he does not *ask me if he can borrow my property*—he just goes to the movie shelves and starts picking out DVDs. I want to stab this motherfucker in the face.

*Do you need him to respect you?*

I don't need him to respect *me*—I need him *not to beat my daughter*! It just disgusts me that he can be around me and not feel ashamed. He's in, he's out—he got his DVDs and didn't say a *word* to me. Maybe, yeah—maybe I want respect. I mean I *gave birth* to that beautiful girl you're fucking and *dude needs to learn* that marriage isn't a fucking MMA bout. I don't hate people often but I do hate him. And as Ashley's mother I feel I have the right—not the legal right, but the celestial right, the universal right, given to me by the stars and the motherfucking *moon*—to fucking *murder* someone who beats my daughter so she comes home looking like she jumped in front of a train. You remember how Slacker, Zach, and Constance fucked up that guy Woody 'cause he punched my friend Iris in the lip making that bitch bleed? That can happen again, my friends, to my son in law Dave. Lovely Dave. All I have to do is make one phone call and that motherfucker is staring down the barrel of a shotgun.

- - - -

The next morning Ashley came in my room crying and hugging me in bed saying *she was sorry* and that *she's quitting drinking*. She was sorry, she said, just for *acting stupid* and she said *she doesn't want to act that way anymore*. I feel glad that she's seeing that she's been irresponsible and hopeful that she'll try to at least not get SO drunk SO often. But tbh I feel skeptical that she will quit drinking completely especially working at a liquor store. I know it's possible it's just gosh *why did she have to get that particular job??*

*How is she doing tonight?*

Oh, she's fine. She thinks her new husband left her.

*She does?*

Yes, because she had a drink this afternoon.

*WHAT???!!! Did they have an agreement she wouldn't drink?*

I don't know baby but if so that is a ridiculous agreement. You don't marry an alcoholic based on them not drinking. What a cock scab. He was just here. She walks out the door leaving me with Christopher, doesn't say a word. Like: *Hey, can you watch Christopher for* five minutes *while I go conjugate with my cock scab husband?* What am I—the fucking live-in nanny? Then it's the two of them out in his truck, talking—of course—because he can't be anywhere near *me* like god forbid he come in the house. Then I look out and it's the two of them *fucking* in the cab of his truck! The whole neighborhood could see her getting fucked. They could have at least gone around the corner. I don't know—you're a guy—I guess that sounds sexy but for me it's just annoying.

*No, it sounds annoying.*

Then she got off his cock and came in crying.

*Where is she now?*

She's upstairs bawling and I'm sure I can't say anything helpful so I'm keeping to myself for now. Oh wait. He just pulled up again, we'll see what happens.

(I'm painting my toes with little hearts.)

I wish they were doing this in the privacy of their own home so I don't have to feel like I'm supposed to be involved somehow. I know that's selfish but I don't do well in this kind of environment. He's outside honking—he can't even bring himself to come inside I guess. I'm liking this guy less and less every day.

*For real! Just sitting outside honking for someone you love??!!*

I KNOW RIGHT?? What a fucking pathetic asshole. I finally told her he was out there. Jesus Fucking Christ. You don't even know how hard it is for me not to go outside and call that guy a piece of shit loser like my pulse is *racing*.

- - - -

*How's your pulse now?*

It's ok I'm doing some cleaning but do you think it's awful of me if I say I don't want him around here anymore? I probably won't in the interest of keeping peace between Ashes and I but if they don't move out soon I think I will say that.

*Be good to my baby!!*

Yeah that's what I'm thinking and believe it or not what Ashley does right now is going to affect more people than Ashley—it's me Christopher and Zach and Kelly too! Of all the things I've been through, this stretch of time since Ashley met Dave has been one of the weirdest.

*That's saying something coming from you.*

No shit. You would know, too, since you've been up my ass with a microscope.

*Sorry about that.*

No, it was necessary. It's ok. I'm just worried about keeping Zach and Kelly hanging. They are *very anxious* about this whole moving thing too, you know. Ashley has moved in and out of this house about three times now with different guys and I can't let her do it anymore. Every time she does that everything gets fucked up and I want to help her but she has to stop this. And it hurts my feelings an awful lot that she doesn't seem to take my difficulties into consideration.

*So what's going on with Ashley's husband? Did he leave her?*

Ashley's husband didn't leave her but he's mad at her for drinking so I'm sure everything will work out fine—and that *is* sarcasm. Ashley and I talked about this earlier. I had so many opinions but I kept my mouth shut. I did ask what their status was.

She said, "It's complicated."

And I just said, "Ok."

- - - -

Ashley has said in the past, drunkenly, that she would leave Dave and she and I would live together forever, that she wanted to live with her mom. All that means is that Ash wants me around to take care of her baby. I don't think Ashley sees me as someone who could have a mate—someone besides her—to live with. She doesn't think of me as someone who might have plans of my own. I'm expired to her—an old person with no sex and no possibilities.

When Phillip leaves (assuming Ash leaves Dave) and it's just me and Ash and the baby, what if I want Matthew to move in? Do I have to run that by Ashley? I don't want to have to ask my daughter if I can have someone come stay with me. Plus I would want whoever was staying with me to have their own room and right now Ashley and her baby take up most of the house.

If Ashley moves, what would keep me here? Pennsylvania isn't the place I want to live—it never has been—it was just an accident on top of an accident that landed me here.

What happens when my disability is up for renewal in August? If they don't renew, I will have to work. Where in the fuck in Hallstead am I going to find a job?

If I go to California, I'll be farther away from you, Matthew, and I don't want to get farther away from my baby.

Part of me wants to stay in this house alone. It's the place I've been the longest..the state is beautiful, moving is hard. I have a couple of friends here. I do get excited about working and taking care of myself and not having to take care of anyone else—my kids, their babies, their boyfriends, their husbands. I feel like I would be young again! If I had the place to myself, I could have people over, do our band parties, set up the strobe lights, piss off the neighbors..just like we used to.

Maybe I'd be alone and unhappy, but I doubt it! The idea of being able to go to the bathroom in the middle of the night without getting dressed..that's worth a million points right there. The more I think of having my own place and doing my own thing, the more amazing it sounds. Not waking Phillip up when I go downstairs—because I feel a tiny bit bad every time I wake him up and all those tiny bits of bad roll up over time into this huge ball of bad that I'm carrying with me constantly. The most annoying part of the way it is now is cleaning up after another adult. My daughter may think otherwise, but I'm not a fucking maid. It's always the little things that add up to make you completely unable to stand the people you live with. In my case it's Ashley's hair all over the sink. *Pick up a washcloth, wipe down the sink so that not a single strand of hair is left, then throw the washcloth in the laundry basket and you're done.* But no: Ashley causes the damage and I clean it up. That's the way we've worked together as long as she's been here.

One possibility that's been discussed about *what to do with me* is that of me going to live with my daughter's family in some apartment she would pick out. I don't want to be a third wheel, though, and Christopher will be in school soon—he's not going to need a full-time grandma to watch over him.

My life is uncertain—that stresses me out. One minute I think I'll somehow be able to keep this house and live here alone and the next minute I start to get realistic: I have no money, no job—my only real option is to become a drifter.

Or homeless, if you prefer to say it that way. I read an article somewhere that said that one of the ways America has changed is that there used to be ways to be dignified and poor at the same time. But now people think there must be nothing valuable about the poor—all college kids care about these days is picking a major that will put them in a career that makes a lot of money. I think it's sad that training for a career is considered education.

So let me sum up my situation:

At 42, I'm looking at the possibility of being homeless again.

I am not afraid of being homeless—I *want* to wander—my soles are itchy and miles are the only thing that can scratch them.

I envision sleeping in dilapidated houses on my way to California—I need to go back to my home.

When I'm homeless in Cali I'm carrying a box cutter and I hope someone jumps me. I hope they just *try*.

And yes, this all takes place in Cali. I don't want to be homeless on the East Coast, I can tell you that.

I wish—what I really wish—is that I lived in the dirt. I wish I *was* the dirt..I wish I was blood and dirt mixed together. I want to be those tiny clumps of dark dark soil stuck to roots when you pull up grass.

*Where are you now?*

I'm in my bed, looking at the window through the holes in my afghan.

*What are you thinking about?*

I was just wondering if you could make blood out of elements like sea water and iron from mountains.

*Do you think you'll feel at home once you're back in California?*

I won't be home till I'm decomposing.

Then I Tweet, for everyone to see:

"One day you will not know me anymore and all you will have is this moment."

I decide that might be the last thing I ever Tweet—it seems like a good one to end on.

And when Ash and Dave *did* finally move, they emptied the cabinets. *Cleaned* my shit out. Kitchen was *empty*. Things we bought *together*. Things I bought *myself*. All that was left was a box of rat poison and a Cheerio. If they had looked closer, they would have taken those too.

I think about myself as one of those old-time post-mortem photos—I'd be dead, and my children would stand around me and we'd all be smiling 'cause Phillip would have taken the time to mold my dead cheeks, and my dead mouth, and my dead eyes into a smile that looked just like my smile when I was alive. My children would be sad, but they would also be relieved—people weren't made to suffer, and I had suffered too much. The more you suffer, the earlier a death you deserve.

I didn't even think about the fact that I was homeless when I was—it was just part of life. I guess part of being homeless actually agreed with me because I kind of want to be homeless now, you know? I like the thought of having zero responsibilities—probably because I've been responsible for a really long time starting off as a kid with keeping family secrets: not telling anyone about daddy touching me 'cause *we'll both get in trouble and daddy will be taken away you don't want that do you*? It's a lot of responsibility to keep that secret. Of course I feel bad for little girl me but one good thing that came of it is that I'm pretty good at keeping secrets now.

If I'm honest, many good things have come of my life and my experiences. And if I'm honest, I know that I can change the course of my life *at any minute* if it feels right to me. That's how I see it—I'm a tiny ball of flesh in an infinite multiverse of possibilities. Our lives have access to vast opportunities, Matthew. The choices are infinite really.

## 122
I wake up in the middle of the night, alone.

This happens often.

It's 3am. The first thing I remember is that Ashley asked me if I would babysit and I said no because I wanted to drink and now I feel so sad that I missed the opportunity to be with Christopher and I'm worried I won't get to again. Last time Christopher stayed the night we were cuddling and he was SO tired he keep saying *I love you* and I would say *I love you too* and he says *I love you more* and I say *nuh uh* and then we laugh and he pushes my piercing like a button and touches my nose and says *boop!* Lol! He cracks me up. He's like a wild animal, reacting like a wolf playing with his wolf grandmother—paws on paws—and I am so proud of my part in his genetics. Part of *me* is in *you!* *Boop!* on the nose! That wonderful child, sweet little wolf, and I left him in the nest with his alcoholic mother so I could do *what? Drink*, alone, at the house by myself. My hands go to my phone and Tweet:

"I'm terrified."

A reply comes back—it's Matthew.

"What are you doing up?" I ask.

I'm always over-polite. Like if a tree fell on my leg and you were sawing it into little pieces to get me out, I'd be asking you about *your* feelings.

"I am..smoking..and I am sitting outside doing some editing."

"What are you smoking?"

"Kamel Reds. But let's talk about you. Why are you terrified?"

"How did you even know I was up?"

"I knew you were up, baby. I saw the number of Twitter notifications I had and I knew one of them was for you. I can't sleep when you can't sleep. I knew that something was up with you."

"Thanks for checking on me! I woke up and started thinking about Phillip leaving this summer which I've been trying not to think of..but he was talking about it a lot today and I'm just so scared."

"You'll miss him."

"*Yes* I'll miss him and I'm worried for his mental well-being! His dad has never cared for him long term so I'm worried, too."

"Sounds scary, boo."

"Yes it's scary from every angle for me."

I keep typing.

"I also feel guilty that I moved him so far away from his relatives :( That's not normal is it? To move so far away from family? I feel like a bad person for it."

"You're not a bad person, D. Think of how often your parents moved *you* as a child."

"You moved a lot, too."

"Yes, but most of mine was self-inflicted."

"I guess I just woke up in the middle of the night and started worrying about what I'll do with all my stuff if I just walk away. I don't think it would be very nice of me to just *leave everything in the house* for my landlords to deal with. I know I won't be able to rent a U-Haul without a valid license and no credit card. I don't feel like anyone would want to buy my stuff in a yard sale. So I guess that just leaves throwing everything away. I worried about that for about an hour when I first got in bed—in complete darkness I'm worrying, worrying—about stuff I shouldn't even care about. I'm a wanderer—right? I've wandered before. I shouldn't even be worried about *stuff*. But years pass, and you cease to wander, and you collect all this stuff that is really nice to have—I mean my movies..maybe I'm copping out on my ancestral nomadic genes but I like to have my movies. I like to be able to watch them at any time. That's part of my soul, too."

"Yes, the stories we carry around with us."

"Exactly."

"But you can always replay them in your head, when you're out at night, sleeping under the stars, those stories are still with you—like every myth—they power us when we're weak, they regulate our morals by telling stories of people less moral than us. You always have your formative stories with you, even when they're not in high resolution."

"You're brilliant. Thank you for being my friend."

"Thank *you*. You're brilliant too."

"You're just saying that."

"No I'm not. I wouldn't go out of my way to lie to you, especially on this keyboard."

"Are you on your iPad?"

"Yes, I am. And typing three extra words *with an apostrophe* just to build your ego..I'm sorry..isn't worth it =P"

"Haha."

"This of it this way: Do you respect me?"

"Yes, very much."

"Do you respect my opinion? You said you think I'm brilliant, remember, so do you respect a brilliant person's opinion about you?"

"Lol. I guess I would have to."

"Lol I guess you're right and I say you're brilliant *so believe it*, would you?"

"You make me cry."

"Shit, Davina, I make *myself* cry."

A pause. Then I decide to tell you:

"I went to the hospital today."

- - - -

"You did?"

"Yeah I thought I might be having a mild heart attack but don't worry I wasn't! They said my blood pressure is *outrageously* high and that my heart has an abnormal rhythm and they FINALLY checked my sinuses and wrote prescriptions for everything. Ashley was with me—she got us a ride."

"Is the irregular heartbeat something to worry about?"

"I guess not since I've always had it and they sent me home."

"Right, ok."

"But the high blood pressure *is* a problem. And it's been a problem for a few years now. Maybe more than a few. From now on I'll be trying to curb my salt intake since my blood pressure is so insanely *high* it sent me to the emergency room. And while I was lying there thinking—you know—*is this the way I'm gonna die someday*, I remembered this thing the kids do..which is..they pour salt on their skin, then hold an ice cube on top of it."

"Why, what does that do?"

"It burns you really badly—it's just awful."

"Why do they do it?"

"Because it's like a group thing smh see who can stand it the longest. I'm pretty sure all three of my kids have done it at the expected time in their childhood."

"Did they learn it from you?"

"I've never done it! I never heard of it till I saw this disgusting sore on Ashley's leg one day about ten or eleven years ago. Then on Phillip a couple years ago and I'm sure on Zachariah a couple years before that."

"I would love to talk to the first kid who did that and ask them how they thought of it!"

"Lol me too! I guess it's natural to be self-destructive at some point. Actually, maybe it's more like an innate need to test yourself, you know?"

"Yeah, I get that theory."

"Anyway that's my salt story. And the moral of the story is *curb your salt intake* or you'll end up in the hospital."

"Jesus, baby, I'm glad you're ok."

"I am too. I'm even glad I went to the doctor today—though it was a fiasco—and I'm thinking maybe I should really put some effort into being nicer to myself because maybe I have more to offer the world than self-loathing. I mean maybe I'll never be *perky* and *filled with light* but I could be less full of doom."

"Baby, I think that is amazing and in honor of your new philosophy I am going to have another cigarette."

"I love it when you smoke."

"That's so funny to me: you love when I smoke and I love when you drink. So does this new doom-free philosophy affect your drug regimen?"

"I never said doom-free. I said *less* doom."

"Oh."

"And no, *nothing* affects my drug regimen, bruh."

"Not even going to the hospital?"

"I'll have you know I drank wine with breakfast, switched to whiskey at lunch, went to the ER, got my prescriptions, then my friend came over and we did a line of coke—well, a few lines—then my *other* friend came over and I did some meth."

"You did *meth* today?"

"Yes baby I snorted *drugs*."

"Did you?!! That sounds fun!"

"I hope you aren't disappointed"

"Not at all!"

"Yeah it was fun tbh."

"Nice =)"

"Thanks for not thinking ill of me."

"Oh no, not at all. Did he leave you any for later?"

"No and guess what?"

"What?"

"It wasn't even regular meth!"

"Oh shit! What was it?"

"Some fucking kick-ass meth called glass."

"!@##$%##@^$%!!

"He's really tight with his money so one thing I know is whenever he has drugs *they good*."

"Oh my god I am *jealous!* I've had glass!"

"Yeah babe not to rub it in but it's awesome."

"Oh man I bet =) Enjoy that shit."

"I am baby and this is the first time in days I haven't had pain in my face so it's euphoric."

"Tell me you're feeling good..let me live off your feels!"

"Mmm baby I'm feeling SO good! I feel stoned and wired just slightly too much and it's perfect. The only drawback is Aaron is here but leaving soon. He's standing over me talking while I text this."

Our dialogue stops. You text me later when I'm in bed.

"Was that glass like pushing you a little past the edge?"

"Just a little. Why, could you tell??"

"No, you told me!"

"Yeah, for a second it was. It was never more than I could handle. But it's had me wired for the rest of the day so right before bed I smoked a bunch of pot and I've been a little foggy since then. Help me out—what the fuck was I saying before?"

"You adopted a new philosophy of love and light?"

"No, not that. Oh yes!!—*shoes*!! Earlier I was wondering what the best kind of shoes would be for walking hundreds of miles and if I can order the right size online. Was I talking about shoes earlier?"

"No."

"Well I'm'onna tell you anyway."

## 123
I told you about the shoes. You listened intently. Or, at least, you were responsive in text—you never know what else someone is doing when you're texting them but it *feels* like they're paying attention if they at least respond quickly with something relevant to the conversation.

"So guess what?" I say.

"What?"

"I just found out my sister is in jail."

"What for?"

"For robbing banks over the last few days."

"*What??!!* Banks..*plural*? How many banks *was* this?"

"Two that we know of."

"Why? Who with? Did she have a partner?"

"I don't know, Math. It was just her I guess and she's six months pregnant."

"Jesus *fuck* I'm sorry."

"Thank you. I don't know how to feel. I'm actually feeling pretty upset."

"That's understandable."

"I wouldn't think I would but I am. I guess my mom dropped in on Ashley and told her. Ashley called me. There's a news article—I Tweeted it if you want to see it. There's a split second of my sister in a wig at the bank on surveillance camera. Did you look at it?"

"Yeah, I'm looking at it now."

"Do you see her?"

"Yeah."

"Apple don't fall far from the tree."

"Damn, baby. I mean there's crazy but that's *really* crazy. Doesn't she have kids? I forgot but I thought she had at least like two kids."

"She has four kids but she's not with that guy anymore. She was engaged to another guy and pregnant with his baby."

"What are you doing now?"

"Just crying I'm really sad. It's everything you know?—It's everything at once. I know I shouldn't but I'm even thinking about my mother and wishing I loved her. It's weird because I feel so sad for her but I just can't be around her—it's very conflicting."

"Where are you?"

"Sitting at my desk listening to music. Taking shots. Gonna smoke a little."

I pick up my devil-skull glass and do a shot. Between my eyes and the screen is just tears, tears for my little sister who has now fucked up her life worse than I fucked up mine.

I put down the shot glass between a jar of colored pencils and some paintings I made with my menstrual blood.

I type to you:

"I'm feeling bad that I didn't call my sister after she stopped here last week."

"What are you drinking, boo?"

"Tequila. What if I could have prevented this?"

"No no no. Don't put that on yourself. Did she come to you three days ago and say, 'Davina, should I rob a bank today?' No. No you could *not* have prevented this."

"Maybe she can get help now?"

"Maybe. What kind of help do you mean?"

"She told me a long time ago that she was diagnosed bipolar and I begged her to take meds but she said she never will. I don't know if it's even true because she lies about a lot of things—but that in itself indicates a problem, don't you think? I just know she needs help."

"With bipolar."

"And with her addictions."

"Yeah, well, those go together, I happen to know."

I take another skull shot and type:

"I feel like I might explode to be honest. I feel dangerous."

"It feels funny for me to say this, given that *I'm* the alcoholic between the two of us, but maybe feeling dangerous and drinking tequila don't go together."

"Ok. I'll stop. Will you time me 30 minutes and I'll drink more then?"

You agree.

I tell you more about the bank robbery situation. I tell you that yes, my sister's children are having a hard time with it—or so I heard (of course they would). I tell you that I got really upset this morning because Ashley came over and while she was here she was telling me I should talk to my mom because her daughter just got put in jail and she could use the support.

There was a quiet moment when I surveyed my daughter and tried to gauge—given her obvious smarts—how it was possible she understood so little about the dynamic between me and Lisa. Then I said:

"HAHAHAHAHAHAHAHAHAHAHAHA!!"

Ashley was scared and left the room.

A couple days later she came to me with another status:

"Oh..um..she got out of jail at like eleven. I guess she's fine except her kids can't live with her now."

"Uh-huh. She's fine. Does that seem *fine* to you, Ashley? Her kids *can't live with her* and it's fine?"

"Mom. Chill. She robbed a couple of banks. It's no big deal."

I was going to give Ashley a big long speech about the foster care system in America and how it's like a 50/50 chance that instead of some nice family who serves crescent rolls with dinner every night, my sister's kids will end up with some sick child-molesting motherfuckers. I wanted to tell her that human trafficking is real, and that a lot of people's brains are configured such that they have no qualms about shoving an adult-sized cock into a child-sized ass or vagina. I was going to recommend a list of documentaries for her to watch on Netflix and *then* have her come back and tell me those kids are going to be fine without their mother.

Instead, I calmed myself with a couple of breaths.

I looked at Ashley, my sweet daughter.

I looked her in the eyes.

And I said:

"Whenever a kid can't be with their parent it's a big deal."

I've been typing all this to your text messages, my steady biographer, and I assume that in addition to conversing back with me, you're also copying every word I say into some document called "Davina—notes." No one could ever say about you, Math, that you're not thorough.

So there it is. My sister Jamie went to jail for robbing two banks—I told you she was a crazy demonic bitch and how many of you believed me? You know how they say that fact is stranger than fiction—I think this book is strange enough to qualify as fact on that strength alone. Why did Jamie rob those two banks? Jamie, my little sister Jamie, was seven months pregnant at the time—not six as I had originally heard. Her relationship was unstable, which meant her financial situation was unstable. And she was strung out on meth. Maybe that's a good enough reason to rob banks.

I'm done telling you about my crazy life for tonight—and Math, I might be done forever. I type my goodnights to you and we do our emoticon handshake that means we are happy within ourselves and that we love each other dearly:

:) <3

=) <3

I put my phone down, thumbs sore from typing.

## 124
For hours I lie awake, in a trance, thinking desperate things:

*I am my past and there is nothing in my future.*

(Stuff like that.)

I heard the droning of a cargo plane fly outside my window against the constant background of freeway traffic.

Either of those sounds takes me back to childhood—both of them together took me back to childhood *hard*.

So much had happened to me—so much happens to *everyone*—and no one ever knows the secret parts or the mundane parts of your life unless you *tell* them.

I respect that most people like to keep their secrets to themselves. Maybe they're scared, maybe they don't think their life story is worth telling, maybe they just have no desire to share with others in such a profound way.

But that's not me.

I wanted my story to be told. I wanted there to be a *witness* to my life—and that's *you*, Dear Reader—that's you.

What does my life *mean*, at 42? Was there *anything* of meaning in the story I just told you? Have I loved enough? Have I raised my kids right? Looking through these pages, reading in print many of the exact words I said in our interviews, I feel like I have nothing to show for my life: I'm no great talent. I'm not rich. I'm not famous. And I'll probably never be any of those things.

But..if you reverse my life: reverse the constant arguments with Ashley, reverse me taking her vodka at work because she's my daughter and I'd do anything for her, reverse my friend Gary going into a coma and never coming out. If you reverse Jamie constantly making me feel bad about how I raised my kids, if you reverse the daily headache of calls to Phillip's schools to get them to simply *educate my kid*, if you reverse me driving from west to east and east to west across this country looking for something I still haven't found. Reverse my stays in mental hospitals..reverse all the questions psychiatrists asked me about myself that I would rather have never considered.

Reverse Zach and Kelly's huge loud scary fights after they moved in to help with the rent—I'm trapped in my room, terrified of leaving, waking up at 1am to the two of them outside the house screaming bloody motherfucking murderous threats at each other. Reverse that.

Reverse my son Zach telling me how great his life has turned out in terms of his career and his relationship and his sanity..and that compared to him I'm a chronically suicidal psychopathic shitstain and that I should go to my room and kill myself—"get it over with," he said.

Reverse my fears about where I'll live in the future, whether I'll be homeless, whether I'll die homeless in some city out west where no one will be able to identify my body—all I'll be is a fat, failed poet with kids who hate me and a mother who hates me even more. A naked body on a slab with some coroner looking over my tattoos and burns and scars and self-mutilations. There will be no goodbyes..just an abandoned blog and a Twitter that I'll never touch again. It's too much for a person to comprehend. I just hold on to the fact that everything passes into something else and I hope it's something better for everyone.

Reverse a day and a day and another in a series of self-loathing and depression. Reverse the usual obsessions and sadnesses.

Reverse the time I bought Ashley a car with my disability check and Zachariah drunk drove it into a boulder, trashing one of the few nice things I was ever able to do for my daughter.

Reverse the Christmas Eve when Ashley got beat up by her new husband and tossed on the street. He yanked out a bunch of her hair and tried to light her on fire. I wasn't feeling so merry that night.

Or what about the day Ashley's husband split her head open with a table leg? I mean there's *domestic violence*, like slapping your wife around a little, maybe punching her in the face, you know, giving her a black eye—and then there's *ripping a leg off the goddamn dinner table* and going after your wife's skull like you're trying to *kill* her. I stayed up all night with Ashley in the emergency room. I never went to sleep. *My daughter got stitches in her fucking head!!* BECAUSE OF HER LOVING HUSBAND!! You better believe we pressed charges against *that* dick-waving cocksucker. Definitely reverse that day.

Or what about the day when I'll actually have to *testify in court* against the aforementioned cocksucker. If I do, the baby will be safe *because they'll take him away* but then my daughter will hate me because the baby won't just go away from Dave, he'll go away from Ashley, too. Might seem like an easy decision but I assure you it is not. That day hasn't even happened yet. Reverse it anyway.

Reverse the day my cat went outside and never came back.

Reverse the day when Phillip left to be with his dad in California and the emptiness crushed me like a plastic bag, sucking all the joy from my lungs, and I was alone in a way I had never been alone before—without my little boy.

*This place is going to be a lot less lively without my baby*, I thought (or maybe said) at the closed door Phillip had just gone through. I touched the door with my fingers. That's the last time I've seen him.

Reverse the very next day after Phillip left when baby was in the kitchen holding his arms out asking, "Uncle? Uncle?" and I had no way to answer him.

Reverse the day, a year later, when Phillip and his dad stopped responding to my texts, my emails, everything. There was never any explanation—they just cut me out of their lives. I texted Phillip every day anyway, just to say *I love you*. But he never replied.

Reverse that Jamie's kids were taken from her after she robbed those two banks. Or that when she was sentenced, she was sentenced to six years in federal prison. You know what I hope she learns in there? *Not to rob banks.*

Remember the night Ashley told me I'm her best friend. That was something to hear. Don't ever reverse that night.

Reverse my pregnancies and reverse my children (just for a minute). Reverse my suicide attempts. Reverse all the men my mother pimped me out to. Reverse Louie, who took my virginity in exchange for giving my mom one month's rent and some crystal.

Reverse the first time I got raped *and* ass raped in the same night by the father of my first child.

(Memories like this will try to enslave you so be careful.)

Reverse me being homeless, sleeping on the floor of a public restroom in a park.

Oh and don't reverse this: Shout-out to the junkie who brought me a plate of his mom's cooking when I was a homeless teenager. Your place is reserved, friend. Don't ever reverse that shit.

Reverse living with those two guys who made me their sex slave.

Reverse the drugs I did to protect my mind from the horror of reality.

Reverse the first time I tried to kill myself. When I was twelve and I took that whole bottle of Aspirin.

Reverse. Reverse. Reverse.

Reverse all that and I'm still smiling, still showing off my infectious dimples, still sitting in the branches of a fig tree with my brothers and sister, tasting its glorious fruit.

Even though half a lifetime has passed, I'm still there.

Still lying on the floor during our Sunday nap—scared, pretending to be asleep—while my stepfather lifts my dress and pushes down my panties..and..you already know the rest.

Reverse the physical abuse, the sexual abuse, the incest I endured—reverse the psychological damage I may never fully comprehend. But know that whether I understand it or not, that damage has colored and will continue to color the way I handle every moment that life brings me, up to and including the moment I take my last breath.

Reverse all the way back to when I was a little girl.

Reverse to when my dad kidnapped me from Lisa and we went to live on the reservation.

I was five—reverse all the way to there.

You can add in all the defilements that came later—you can throw them at me like stones—but I'm still just a girl standing on the edge of a cliff, holding my father's hand, screaming my name at the wind that's rushing up the side of an Arizona canyon.

The wind blows back at me, raiding my black hair, and she tells me many secrets that only the wind would know. Secrets modern people cannot comprehend even when spelled out for them in all the languages of the world. She gave them to me when I was five years old and I've been using them all this time—*that's* how I survived.

So yes, it is true that the world tried so so *so* hard to break me.

And it is true that it didn't work.

It will never work.

You can't follow someone else's path (or read about it) and expect to learn the same lessons they did, so it would be useless for me to tell you what the wind said or what I've figured out so far about becoming an unstoppable force. All I can do is wish you well on *your* path.

Even though I have endured many defilements, I am not defiled.

I am and always will be just a kid standing on our roof, brought home by that particular smell of adobe mixed with desert rain. A kachina feather brushes my skin. I'm back in Arizona, looking at the stars, climbing the cliffs, playing with the other kids—it's an innocent life—laughing, running, my sneakers hitting the sand, spinning up like Mario grabbing a blinking star, landing on two feet—invincible—forever running the rez.
